```
overwritten.

>'CMemoryException' which will need to be caught at some point.

-- quotes --
: However after certain limit the program assigns value NULL to the
: pointer. I am not sure why this is happening. Please let me know if
: anybody has any idea about this phenomenon.

Pre-standard behavior, e.g. in Visual C++ 6.0.
http://www.talkroot.com/archivee/index.php/t-49495_Memory_Allocation_Problem.html

:My program needs a lot of memory and after allocating some using NEW
:statement, it returns NULL for any new object created. 
http://www.talkroot.com/archivee/index.php/t-49549_How_to_increase_HEAP_size_in_VC++_6.0_environment.html

:Why do you say that "VC++ 6.0 was a disgrace by all means even since it was released?
*operator new doesn't throw std::bad_alloc but returns NULL
http://forums.devx.com/showthread.php?threadid=135074

By default, Visual C++ 6 doesn't throw an exception when new fails, it
returns a null pointer just like malloc. 
http://www.daniweb.com/techtalkforums/thread14010.html

>I suppose it is possible for some lame class author to catch all
>exceptions from the base class and then not throw these on, returning
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>clueless about (based on things you have previously said as well).

 void * operator new ( int size ) { return malloc (size); }
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T11:14:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412021114.4eb4f9e7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411291056.aef03fa@posting.google.com> <t3lnq059jf64nne48sbrccm3120j6tc07o@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411301115.2cc1a50e@posting.google.com> <c4jsq09smqlfe21phiuv3hf6jb8h1gk66f@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >> >In other words OO _does_ protect against this with exceptions.
> >> 
> >> Not true for VC++ 6.0, which returns a null pointer. 

> Pre-standard behavior, e.g. in Visual C++ 6.0.

I have no particular interest in MicroSoft anything, but I do happen
to have a complete set of MS Visual C/C++ Manuals - version 1.0 dated
1993.  At the time I was sometimes using Zortech (later Symantec) and
trying Watcom.

The manuals specify that a CMemoryException will be thrown for the
classes that MS provided, presumably from the base classes used by
those.

You may also note that a typical reply to the problem is:


""" -------------
Sounds like your C++ implementation is broken. 'new' may not return a
null pointer. Some implementations incorrectly return a null pointer
when the request cannot be fulfilled.
----------------- """

So, while "OO", and in particular "C++" _does_ protect against this
with exceptions, even with the classes provided by MS itself, the MS
implementation appears to be broken in that it allows incompetent
third party classes to bypass this.

"""------------
My advice is to get rid of VC++ 6.0 and switch to the latest Visual
C++ version available. 6.0 was a disgrace by all means even when it
was released so you want to make a fresh start with a decent compiler.
---------------"""

"""------------
I don't remember all of the awful bugs it had back in 1997 but let's
start with a sample:

*namespace resolution issues. For some reason, the compiler couldn't
cope well with identifiers declared in a namespace when certain
members weren't public.

*very poor template support.
*many functions of and features of the Standard Library aren't
implemented
*no support for cavaraint return types
*scope of variables declared in a for-loop wasn't confined to the loop
itself
*operator new doesn't throw std::bad_alloc but returns NULL
*operator dynamic_cast throws bad_cast instead of std::bad_cast (now
try to figure out such a bug!)

and many others.
-----------------"""


A bug in MS's implementation is not a feature of the language, nor of
the class of languages.
```

```

In article <c4jsq09smqlfe21phiuv3hf6jb8h1gk66f@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 30 Nov 2004 11:15:26 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> overwritten.

Not in C it won't.  It's Undefined Behavior, and has been in every
version of the Standard.  Once a C program invokes Undefined
Behavior, anything can happen.

In some cases, a program that invokes UB will behave as the author
expects.  That should not be confused with "working", and certainly
does not imply that the UB in question "will work" in any general
sense.
```

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
<snip>

> Yes like you I *do* set objects to null - dammit if they appear as a set
> of 'Globals' or even occurrences, it's no big deal to code them :-
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I believe the last line above is not valid COBOL.

But there have been so many changes to the language to accommodate shoddy
programming I no longer know whether it is or it isn't and I don't really
care... <G>

It has irritated me over the years to watch the purity and simplicity of
COBOL being eroded because programmers insist on having it conform to what
they think it OUGHT to do rather than actually learning what it does.

It's all pretty irrelevant now and certainly not worth starting another
'infinite loop' thread on...

My understanding is that you cannot use 'value' with 'occurs' , but they've
probably changed it so that you can,  because people kept doing it and
grizzling when it was disallowed... (DIVIDE ...BY is another example of
this. INTO was too difficult for many people so it was changed to allow BY.
No-one seemed to worry about why it was INTO in the first place - it was
because MULTIPLY had already grabbed the BY clause - and the history of
COBOL seems to have been pandering to the LCD until we reach a point where
programmers can't even distinguish between arithmetic and data
manipulation... oops! sorry, I was starting to rant there... I'm calm
now...<G>)


> Something I did pick-up on that you wrote, which I have never used -
> Invoke and End-Invoke - did that subtle scope-terminator tend to resolve
> problems with COM ?
>

I wish I could find the case where it did solve a problem that I had a
couple of years back, but I can't. It was subtle and resulted in the
parameter list to the object being considered incorrectly.

This led me to draw the following conclusions (all are totally one man's
opinion, but it works for me. I have very few problems getting COBOL to
work...)

1. Scope delimiters in general are a 'GOOD THING' (used in the sense of
'1066 and All That') in COBOL, no less in OO COBOL.
2. Passing parameters to objects is not the best way to keep a stable
interface that does not require maintenance. The fewer parameters in the
list, the better. If I am writing OO Classes and objects for my own use, I
minimise the parameters to be passed. Instead, I set what would have been
parameters, as properties. So, for me, using objects involves:

1. set the properties.
2. invoke the method.
3. check or get the properties back into the current process.

At the time I build the objects/classes I try to consider ALL possible
properties (key data fields) that might EVER be associated with this piece
of code.  (This is a useful part of the analysis and is fun to do...)

Example:

Xmas tree object.

Properties:

maxHeight
maxWeight
typeOfTree
ageOfTree
decorations (object reference to collection)

Methods

BringTreeHome
Decorate
Relocate
Recycle

(The above is not as comprehensive as I would do if it were for real, but I
know you like concrete examples (ever see a concrete Xmas tree <G>?) so it
will serve...)

Take it the tree has been created with 'New' or 'CreateObject' and we have
an object reference to it in 'XmasTree1'

Now, when I come to write the 'BringTreeHome' method, the height and weight
of the tree might be relevant (to see if it can fit in the car, for
instance)

instead of :   invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome" USING maxHeight, maxWeight
end-invoke

I would use;

move weight to maxWeight of XmasTree1
move height  to maxHeight of  XmasTree1
invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome" end-invoke

There could be a number of code modules that use the XmasTree object and
invoke the "BringTreeHome" method.

(The BringTreeHome method accesses details on the luggage capacity of the
car, checks height and weight of tree, checks if other properties are set,
initialises them if they are not. If the typeOfTree and ageOfTree properties
are set, it makes sure the tree meets these; if they are not, it sets them
after it has determined whether the tree fits in the car.  In other words,
the BringTreeHome method is written to be as "broad" as possible and to
consider all the factors that are likely to be important in bringing the
tree home. It is encapsulated and we really don't want to have to maintain
it, so we ensure it does what it does...If we later use a different car, the
car database will be updated and the method will still do exactly what it
always did, without amendment.)

Later, it becomes clear that under some circumstances, the tree we bring
home must be a real tree, in other cases it needn't be.

If the type of tree is added to the parameter list, the interface is changed
and every program that uses it must be recompiled.

I simply set the typeOfTree property in the modules that need it...

move weight to maxWeight of XmasTree1
move height  to maxHeight of  XmasTree1
if some-criteria-TRUE
   move 'real' to typeOfTree of XmasTree1
end-if
invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome" end-invoke

The interface doesn't change, coding impact is minimised and so is
regression testing. Furthermore, the behaviour of the object has been
changed selectively, without violating encapsulation or changing the code in
the object/class.

The bottom line is that when thinking about Xmas (as a system), the process
of bringing home a tree is covered. This means that system design can be
thought about at a more abstract level without worrying about the details.

I find brainstorming objects (components, for me) as part of OO system
design to be amusing and entertaining. The above object could be used as a
"building block" for an overall "Hey, it's Xmas" system or a completely
different "Hey, let's have a pagan festival to clebrate the equinox" system,
or a "Let's practice making pine trees look pretty" system. The beauty of OO
is that objects are exactly that, objects.

<snip>
>
> "belt and braces...".  Remiss of me if I haven't written it before.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Given Bill's stated sexuality, creeping to his room in the middle of the
night might have sent entirely the wrong message...(or then again...<G>)
You have to admit, Jimmy, "I only wanted to steal his braces..." is a pretty
lame excuse...

Pete.
```

```
In article <217e491a.0411301810.345c989@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> outside a very British group of (now) OAPs.

Though I, too, have a dogeared copy of _1066 and All That_ ("This
History was written to console the reader"), I note that the Jargon
File / _New Hacker's Dictionary_ has an entry for "Good Thing", and
it acknowledges Seller and Yeatman.[1]  It lists "Good Thing" as
"very common".

This is the Jargon File 4.4.7.  Based on the archives on the JF site,
it appears to have been introduced with some release of JF version 2,
sometime between 1987 and 1990.  It's also in ESR's 1993 edition of
the NHD.  The acknowledgment was added relatively recently, in JF
4.3.1, 29 June 2001.

So while _1066_ may not be especially well-known in the US (though
ISTR several US posters to alt.folklore.computers said they'd read
it, in a conversation some years back), "Good Thing" is apparently
well-established in the US hacker community.


1. http://www.catb.org/~esr/jargon/html/G/Good-Thing.html
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

Gotta print it out to digest - some really good stuff. As it's not yet

>Given Bill's stated sexuality, creeping to his room in the middle of the
>night might have sent entirely the wrong message...(or then again...<G>)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Good one :-). Ahh. But I have greater respect for Bill. I knew before 
Newbury his sexual preference and noticed at one breakfast he had to pop 
considerably more pills than I do. No I'm not dying on you yet, just 
fibrilation like George Snr., and that old fogey celebrated his birthday 
by doing yet another parachute jump.

There are three 'states' I believe, heterosexual, AC/DC and homosexual - 
I'm in Number 1. There was a young sweetheart, about eighteen on the 
hotel desk at Nerwbury, slim, reasonably pretty - now if I had wanted a 
bit of tail.............

I will be responding to Bill's statement, regardless of you trying to 
put me off, and hopefully indicate I have tolerance, and am not 
interested in making any plugs. I'll try and illustrate how difficult 
the problem has become up here tied into replacement of the British 
North America Act by the Canadian Charter of Rights.The latter has 
become a dollar printing machine for the lawyer sharks.  Let me hint, I 
think my own circumstances are reasonably unique - and the word is 
'predator'.

Truly it was only his braces I would have taken, generously leaving his 
belt to keep his pants up :-)

Jimmy
```

```
The Highwayman - Alfred Noyes 1880 -1958 :-

The wind was a torrent of darkness among the gusty trees,
The moon was a ghostly galleon tossed upon cloudy seas,
The road was a ribbon of  moonlight, over the purple moor,
And the highwayman came riding
Riding-riding-
The highwayman came riding, up to the old inn-door.

But back to OO because Pete made some interesting observations.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T05:06:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gpxrd.408865$%k.51956@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<col8so$ahn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net> <cward.398112$%k.72731@pd7tw2no> <cok64l$pm6$1@panix5.panix.com> <wsord.388600$Pl.321384@pd7tw1no> <col8so$ahn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

>>The Highwayman - Alfred Noyes 1880 -1958 :-
>>    
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
To reply to two in one. I certainly knew Doc's title 'The Wreck of the 
Hesperus", but the very first time I read it was just now googling :-

http://www.blupete.com/Literature/Poetry/Wreck.htm

Sure has its own cadence.

Your memorizing poems. Me, 8 in 1939 at a Christian Brothers school - 
learning English under the tutorship of a Brother Austin. Some thirty of 
us had to learn a poem about a Chinaman with a pigtail - don't now 
recall title or contents. We all blew it, so he had us all line up to 
get a whack on the palm of our hands with a ruler. Miserable sod ! (Not 
that I would have thought that back then). I saw him around in 
subsequent years, but never as a class master. School location Southsea, 
Portsmouth.

Age 16, I spend last year of schooling at another Christian Brother 
school, Southbourne, Bournemouth as a 'boarder'. For you Kiwis, of an 
evening, particularly Sundays, we could here the bells of Christchurch 
Abbey across the bay summoning Anglicans to Evensong.

Leave eventually, do my bit in RAF, get married and two kids. Very late 
sixties nostalgically visit Bournemouth with my wife and children and 
make a call on my old school. Meet two young men, Christian Brothers in 
mufti (civvies) and ask, "Anybody around who was at St. John's, 
(Southsea) around 1939 - '47. I'm Jimmy Gavan ?". One disappears to 
enquire. He returns followed by a bubbly little old man with white hair. 
"Of course I remember Jimmy Gavan" says Brother Austin with a beaming smile.

Wasn't such a miserable sod after all - he never forgot his small charges.

Wish I'd stayed enlisted, the de la Salle, still fairly active in 
California, used to make a decent drop of white and red vino in the Napa 
Valley, sold with a 'Christian Brothers' label when I first came over here.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT : Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T07:17:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tqUrd.415620$nl.148197@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<conb8l$jlt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net> <cward.398112$%k.72731@pd7tw2no> <cok64l$pm6$1@panix5.panix.com> <wsord.388600$Pl.321384@pd7tw1no> <col8so$ahn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Gpxrd.408865$%k.51956@pd7tw2no> <conb8l$jlt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

>On  1-Dec-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Each to his own - (although I really liked the actor (?) - I loved his 
performance along with Jeremy Irons in "Longitude" or whatever it was 
called) - Yes saw 'Singing Detective', but not my cup of tea.

I think you hit the right word with 'rounded'. They were a religious 
order founded by a French aristocrat, Jean Baptiste de la Salle who 
became a priest - around late 1600s. But the members of the order are 
brothers,  just like you have with the major religious orders, priests 
and brothers - the latter do not perform priestly functions. (For 
comparison consider them the male equivalent of nuns). Probably as a 
result of the French Revolution they spread around the 'catholic' world, 
some 4 schools n England, across to Eastern Canada and no doubt includes 
Quebec and the USA, plus places like Sri Lanka. We have Sri Lankan 
friends here , and all four brothers were educated by the Christian 
Brothers in Ceylon.

So they had a 'religious' vocation - poverty, chastity and obedience, 
but far more importanrtly their vocation was to teach boys - and so far 
as I'm concerned, in the latter, they came up in spades.. There's a 
saying reference the Jesuits, "Give us a child at the age of 7 and you 
have a catholic for life". I somehow doubt that applies to-day - just 
like other groups, without knowing statistics, there are many catholics 
who are non-churchgoers, although they show up for baptisms, weddings 
and funerals, and perhaps Christmas and Easter.

So chances are - I got indoctrinated./brainwashed. Yes and no. Certainly 
I have a continuous belief in the church's main doctrines, and the real 
stress is on Charity/Love/Tolerance. Bearing in mind they were teaching 
young kids, how do you put religious ideas across. Now an atagonistic 
aethiesit could consider the following just hokum, but if temporarily 
for the sake of discussion, they accept that this is based in a belief 
of a 'God'  :-

!) Certainly back during the Reformation if not a catholic you finished 
up in Hell, fire and damnation. That is certainly not what I was taught 
all those years ago. I met a very rigid Afrikaans woman, taught by a 
narrow-minded group of nuns - you ain't a catholic - you're gonzo. Not 
specifically directed at aethesits but more what the church would have 
called 'pagans' - if you are of a sincere belief,  lead a good life 
according to your own code of morals - then you are just as acceptable 
to God as a regular church-going catholic. (They don't loudly proclaim 
this otherwise the numbers might drop off, with less bucks being put in 
the collection plate !).

2) Hell - how do you explain that to kids - think of all those ugly 
paintings with devils sticking forks into the damned. So this is how it 
was put across to me - (bearing in mind you have to accept the concept 
of faith) - We have this image of an elderly bearded man sitting on 
clouds surrounded by angels - Michaelangelo, Rapahel etc.  But as 
explained by my teachers, we really don't know what God is, and we 
translate him to an image -a man-like figure. However, condsider the 
concept of God as being the ultimate beauty. Where the Hell bit comes 
in, even assuming anybody is 'cast down to Hell' , the so-called guilty 
are given a very fleeting glimpse of that beautiful God, and then it is 
removed from their vision. Hell is 'agonizing' over having lost the 
opportunity to be with this incredibly beautiful being for the rest of 
eternity.

3) Creation and Evolution - they can quite happily co-exist in 
catholicism. You have to be out of your mind to think God worked 24/7 
and created the world and universe in 168 hours. There are most 
defintely scientists of a catholic persuasion, plus the Vatican who 
accept the dualism between creation and evolution. Where the divergence 
occurs as it relates to homonoids, at some stage, (again you have to 
accept the concept of faith),  God said, I've taken this species far 
enough, let me give it a soul, Ecce Homo.

Balderdash and poppycock - I don't buy that says a disbeliever. Well, 
it's good enough for me, and I see it as a compromise, and can happily 
live within it's context. I will only find out for sure when I bite the 
dust.

So much for religion - but somehow the Christian Brothers  managed to 
shape our minds too in the 'rounded' sense. Whether they worked at it 
deliberately, (and I don't think they did), individually so many of them 
as educators were so dedicated and enthusiastic, encouraging us to learn 
and search. Bro. Anthony enthused about history - we were all sending 
off to the National Potrait Gallery in Trafalgar Sqaure for photos of 
paintings of Britain's famous historical figures. Each of us in class 
given a peice of balsa wood to shape a galleon, put a a stick in and add 
a main sail. We were to colour them blue and red to represent the French 
and British fleets at the Battle of the Nile - in - oooh - I had to 
google on that - 1798. (Next year is the 200th anniversary October 5, of 
the Battle of Trafalgar. Nelson made his last steps down Sallyport at 
Porstmouth that long ago October).

Same teacher, with a then post-war Labour government, sneered at the 
Labourties for giving away the Jewel in the Crown - India and Pakistan. 
Yours truly (16)  -  I'm not sure I buy that, why is he so upset ? My 
mentor, but the pupil is starting to question the mentor. Subsequent 
historical facts show that there was no choice - I've no idea of British 
Army numbers in India at the time - but all those zillionas of 
Pakistanis, Hindus, Brahmins with a taste for democracy from the 
peaceful teacher Mahatma Ghandi ?

Another political observation by the pupil, and bearing in mind I have a 
very great love of Birtish history, i.e., prior to the Victorian era 
when, to my mind, it gets somewhat dull.. Around about this time 
'47/'48, George VI,  the now dead Queen Mum and the two teenage 
princesses,  Elizabeth and Margaret,  trot off to S. Africa for a PR 
tour. They return via Porstmouth. Hooray get all the schoolkids out to 
line the pavements(sidewalks) to cheer. Needless to say we wsere waiting 
there some 60-90 minutes. Eventually excitement, the royal limo is 
coming ! Expectantly kids peer forwards. Being one of the taller ones 
I'm stood at the back. Made waves and hoorays as they zip past in an 
open limo - we get no more than a fleeting glimpse. (I did pick-up on 
the fact that George was wearing stage makeup, not uncomon in that era 
for the black and white newsreel photography). But I didn't wave - 
asking myself, "Why are we waving. Whom are we waving to. What have they 
done ?".  Not an anarchist, but a non-monarchist was born - even with a 
love of history they had no relevance in the post-war world. 

Mathematics - we'll give that one a complete miss. We had a genius who 
only got his message to the six kids in the front row. He designed and 
wrote, provided clues and answers for a quarterly magazine - all in 
LATIN ! I'm still not clear whether or not you are supposed to read a 
book of log tables rotating it north, east, south or west.

English Grammar and Literature - Ahhhhh.........Bro. Bernard, definitely 
effeminate, and in all probablility, on reflection, a latent 
homosexual.. Summer, some kid in the front row flatulates - don't all 
little boys ? With a flourish he draws a handkerchief from under his 
robe, (Forgot to mention -  their religious uniform is a black gown with 
a crisply starched,  white,  two-pronged French lawyer's collar), 
holding it to his nose disapprovingly. He can stand the methane no more 
and with deliberate steps makes strides towards the sashed-window. With 
dramatic theatrical gestures, he 'thrusts'( not 'opens' ) the bottom 
part of the window, with one final dramatic gesture and breathes in the 
cooling outside air.

I loved that man. He brought literature to LIFE. He was at both the 
schools I attended.  A book of essasys, the first being Bacon's 'On 
Truth', others I've forgotten, but I think extracts from Robert Louis 
Stevenson's 'Travels with a Donkey" and somethng from DeQuincey's 
"Confessions of an Opium Eater". He paraded and strode around the 
classroom uttering the famous words and phrases from some of the 
Shakespearan plays we did, Julius Caesar, Macbeth, Hnery V. We read a 
cross-section of poets. He explained, cajoled, encouraged.

I must stop - I could carry on forever.

Yep they did me proud and hopefully I am a reasonably rounded citizen. I 
loved them, (Ahh Ha, now it comes out). Negative !. Have you picked up 
on Pete Dashwood's messages with reference to his father. Pete LOVED his 
father dearly, and at his knee picked up wisdom - well, let's not go 
overboard - some wisdom :-)  . I loved those Christian Brothers in the 
same way.

Jimmy
```

```
In article <tqUrd.415620$nl.148197@pd7tw3no>,
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>There's a 
>saying reference the Jesuits, "Give us a child at the age of 7 and you 
>have a catholic for life".

I recall reading a bigraphical reminisce - not nearly as enjoyable as this 
one, Mr Gavan - written in the Sunday Washington Post magazine where a 
fellow recounted an incident from his youth, spent at some manner of 
Jesuit-run school in upstate New York.  He'd committed some kind of 
infraction and Father O'Leary had caught him and was about to administer 
a bit of... corporeal redirection.  The kid bleated something like 'I'm 
sorry, Fadduh, I made a mistake an' it won't happen again... show a little 
mercy, willya?'

O'Leary's eyes blazed as he rolled up his sleeve and he responded 'Mercy 
is for the Lord our God, my son... *we* are Jesuits and *we* dispense 
*justice*.'

(perhaps not as much nowadays as when they were the Secular Arm of the 
Inquisition... but the tradition seems to survive)

DD
```

```
Richard wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I find it truly amazing how our lives have crossed, given the distance 
and separation of years.  Firstly in the RAF at Northolt when your 
father was there as a squadron leader, and now - I was educated close to 
your wife's domain.

You may not remember, but your wife might. It was St. Peter's 
School/College run by the Jesuits until the de la Salle took it over in 
'47. Location, awfuly vague, but the road going past would have taken 
you down towards Hengistbury Head  - with views across Christchurch Bay 
and looking east towards the Needles on the western extremity of the 
Isle of Wight.

Seeing as we are name dropping.........:-) (Lily Langtry). I was 
desperately in love with a girl in Southsea - it just didn't work out 
for me. I "suffered" for over a year before I got over it - then I met 
my wife 'the girl next door',  professed my love second day and have 
never looked back since. But just like Daniel said, he is happily 
married but still retains a fondness for an earlier love who is now a 
manageress in a restaurant. Same with me, I love my wife dearly, but 
there is still that romantic fondness for Jill. Thoughts are easily 
triggered when I hear 'Unchained Melody', her favourite at the time. Not 
the vocals but it was Norrie Paramour did an orchestral with an 
accompanying male/female choir.

Above  merely sets the scene for name-dropping. Jill's great-aunt was 
the actress Sarah Bernhardt. What I didn't know in all those years that 
I was going to St. John's in Southsea, was that a little girl called 
Jill lived in a house directly opposite to the school entrance. And back 
at the beginning of the last century, that same house was occupied by 
Conan-Doyle when he was a  GP  before creating Sherlock Holmes. I never 
knew the above at school - got the info on Bernhardt and Conan-Doyle 
from Jill.

Jimmy
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<col4a2$7v1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <SHSqd.384805$%k.209871@pd7tw2no> <312ppvF360hkqU1@uni-berlin.de> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net> <cward.398112$%k.72731@pd7tw2no>`

```

On 30-Nov-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> There are three 'states' I believe, heterosexual, AC/DC and homosexual - 
> I'm in Number 1. There was a young sweetheart, about eighteen on the
> hotel desk at Nerwbury, slim, reasonably pretty - now if I had wanted a
> bit of tail.............

I don't believe they are states, nor are they inclusive.   A state is quantum,
where there is no doubt what its characteristics are - and whether that state is
on or off.

Some recent studies show that there are more people who are asexual than
homosexual - at least in England (or maybe Britain).    If you are primary
asexual with some homosexual leanings, it is easy to let the asexual part take
over in our heterosexual friendly world.

And everything is relative.   To some people weekly sex is extremely asexual -
to others it is extremely sexual.    Some societies (prisons and otherwise), the
person who plays the "female" role in sodomy is homosexual, but the one
performing the "male" role isn't.   Some societies expect a bit of same-sex
sexual play among youths - to be outgrown by heterosexuals, and to others that
means they are homosexual.

I hate having to put state statuses on things I can't measure.   My cardiologist
and his assistants keep asking me to measure my pain on a 1-10 scale.    What
happens if my dial goes to 11?
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

Top post.

Well note I did wrap the word states in quotes to indicate a separate 
meaning, I wasn't thinking of dials,  or state values. Am I confused by 
your response - yes, you betcha. I think I'm a 'boy' - well I was last 
time I looked.

Does this help ? As the saying goes, "I wouldn't crawl over Raquel Welch 
to get at you in bed" :-)

My commiserations on your heart condition - mine is negligible. Shhh. I 
can do you a deal on cross-border prescriptions. They used to call one 
pill I took Coumidin, (I think that was the spelling). Now the 
pharmacists have gotten honest and call it Warfarin - and we all know 
what that does to our delightful little four-legged friends. Could 
always send you some. Scratch that. Go to a local hardware/gardening 
store and you can get a Kg/Lb of the stuff real cheap.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T03:52:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
If it goes 11, either you'll never know, or you wake up in the hospital.

Among other things, I have sleep apnea, and have had trouble in some
hospital visits getting them to provide me with the Bi-pap I've used
for years. Until, they had a code on me, and moved me to a better place.
With O2, with bi-pap.  I never nap, I go to bed and turn on the O2 and
bi-pap.

All so that I can enjoy the mutual state we live in.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T17:41:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3176lhF385lflU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <SHSqd.384805$%k.209871@pd7tw2no> <312ppvF360hkqU1@uni-berlin.de> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> 11/30/2004 4:32:04 PM >>>

>instead of :   invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome" USING maxHeight, maxWeight
>end-invoke
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome" end-invoke

I don't do much OO programming, and no OO-COBOL, but wouldn't the following
be more in following with the OO paradigm?

invoke XmasTree1 "Set_maxWeight" using weight
invoke XmasTree1 "Set_maxHeight" using height
invoke XmasTree1 "BringTreeHome"

Obviously Set_maxWeight and Set_maxHeight would be simple one line methods,
but from all I've read about OO this way is preferred.

Just thinking...

Frank




--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T04:12:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NCwrd.408614$%k.83695@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3176lhF385lflU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <SHSqd.384805$%k.209871@pd7tw2no> <312ppvF360hkqU1@uni-berlin.de> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net> <3176lhF385lflU1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

>Pete Dashwood<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> 11/30/2004 4:32:04 PM >>>
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Good thinking Frank, nothing wrong with what Pete is doing, but it is an 
alternative. I was going to make the same comment in passing - saves me 
doing it. Just one itsy-bitsy correction; in keeping with other 
languages J4 have fairly recently determined method names will be 
CamelCase. Your  :-

invoke XmasTree1 "Set_maxWeight" using weight, becomes

invoke XmasTree1 "SetMaxWeight" using weight

I've only switched "max" to "Max" for clarity.

Haven't got N/E V 4.0 but it will generate the code for "set.....", methods, adding supplementary source files. I'd have to dig through the N/E OO on-line manual, but it may also generate "get....." methods.

Still checking over my reply to Pete.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T23:35:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3189fuF385rv5U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <SHSqd.384805$%k.209871@pd7tw2no> <312ppvF360hkqU1@uni-berlin.de> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net> <3176lhF385lflU1@individual.net> <NCwrd.408614$%k.83695@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:NCwrd.408614$%k.83695@pd7tw2no...
<snip>> >
> >
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, it it sure is a pity that J4 don't keep themselves better informed on
things OO. Frank's usage is exactly in line with the conventional usage of
most of the OO community (who have probably never heard of J4). The "itsy
bitsy correction" suggested, would result in the invoke not functioning
correctly...but, I guess that's J4 for you.

In the Fujitsu case, the GET and SET MUST be upper case and separated from
the property name by a hyphen (in deference to COBOL; the rest of the world
use an underline, as Frank showed.) Again, the "corrected" invoke would
simply fail to work.

For a fuller discussion on this, please see my response to Frank's post.

> I've only switched "max" to "Max" for clarity.
>
> Haven't got N/E V 4.0 but it will generate the code for "set.....",
methods, adding supplementary source files. I'd have to dig through the N/E
OO on-line manual, but it may also generate "get....." methods.
>
> Still checking over my reply to Pete.
>
I look forward to seeing it, Jimmy.

In particular, whether you use parameters to Methods as a matter of course,
whether you agree or disagree with the setting of properties instead, and if
you do, how you would go about getting and setting them (if you would).

I'd also be interetsed to know what the implications are for early and late
binding in NE, and which you mainly use?

Cheers,

Pete.



Pete.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:G4Krd.396592$Pl.112617@pd7tw1no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Well, it it sure is a pity that J4 don't keep themselves better informed
on
> >things OO. Frank's usage is exactly in line with the conventional usage
of
> >most of the OO community (who have probably never heard of J4). The "itsy
> >bitsy correction" suggested, would result in the invoke not functioning
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jimmy,

appreciate your honesty. Thanks.

Early binding means that the compiler can resolve much of your OO code when
you compile it. Late binding means it is resolved at run time. It is similar
(in concept, not in practice) to static and dynamic linking.

The difference is that the SYNTAX changes  (well, it doesn't have to, but
you have more choices) if you use early binding, and it is, obviously, more
efficient because there is less to do at run time. If you use the :: in-line
syntax I believe it is only available with early binding. (To my eye, it is
ugly in a COBOL program anyway, but that's just me...)

The REPOSITORY (or prototyping) allows the compiler to have acces to all the
properties and methods of the classes you are using, so it can syntax check
your invokes and make sure that the right number of parameters, of the right
type, are declared for the methods.

This feature is of little use to me as I try to minimise the number of
parameters to methods anyway, for the reasons I outlined in my original
post.

As I am a late binding fan, it is also pretty irrelevant (for me).

The main advantage of late binding is that you can change the class without
having to recompile all the programs that use it. If the class is early
bound it is "integrated" nto the application.

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

Pete - Good info - interjections below

>Early binding means that the compiler can resolve much of your OO code when
>you compile it. Late binding means it is resolved at run time. It is similar
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
 >>>>>>
Well that's got to be me - EARLY binding. Now :-

"If you use the :: in-line syntax I believe it is only available with early binding. (To my eye, it is ugly in a COBOL program anyway, but that's just me...)".

No it's not. Click your memory cells, back in softawaresimple, I wrote to the effect, I didn't like it, and was that what Bill referred to as "C-ifying" the language". You replied something like "Well it's OK Jimmy as long as we don't have to use it....".

Now here's the rub - there's only about three of us "dare" to post OO code here, you, Fredrico and me. Donald is a fourth, but reticent - maybe he's the smart one :-). Now I'm not imagining it, but say roughly 7 to 14 days ago, I'd swear I saw you post something which had those dreaded double colons :-)

Just checked the N/E V 4.0 on in-line invocation, plus as it is lumped together, it covers the next topic. I've just done a copy/paste so I hope it shows up OK your end :



      In-line Method Invocation

You can invoke a method in-line, as long as it has a returning 
parameter, by coding the object reference followed by the method and 
separated by two colons in place of an operand in a statement, for example:

move anAccount::"getBalance" to current-balance

This is equivalent to the following INVOKE statement:

invoke anAccount "getBalance" returning current-balance

**** There's no reference to early/late binding here - so I'm assuming 
it is kosher for both


      Invocation Using Object Properties

You can invoke get property and set property methods using object 
properties. Get and set property methods are methods that are either 
generated automatically by the Compiler in response to object property 
syntax in data definitions, or coded by you using special syntax in the 
Method-ID paragraph. To invoke a get or set property method, you use a 
statement such as:

move balance of anAccount to current-balance

This statement invokes the get property method balance of the account 
object referred to by the object reference anAccount.

For more details see the section /Get and Set Property Methods/ 
<http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/opmeth.htm#u040> 
in the chapter /Methods/.


    Conformance

Conformance is a quality that applies to interfaces. The term interface 
is used in two ways in OO COBOL:

    * Every object, whether factory or instance, is said to have an
      interface consisting of the data and methods that have been
      defined for the object in its class source element.
    * An interface can also be a collection of method prototypes,
      specifying some behavior.

Part of an object's interface (in the first sense) can be a set of 
methods that it provides because it implements an interface (in the 
second sense). Interfaces in the second sense are explained in more 
detail in the chapter /Interfaces/ 
<http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/opinte.htm>.

It is essential in OO programming that, when your program is executed, 
each object conforms to the interface that you defined for it. To help 
to ensure this, the Compiler provides conformance checking. In other 
words, as far as possible it ensures that your objects will conform at 
run-time to the interfaces you have described for them. If any 
conformance checks fail, the Compiler displays error messages. The more 
rigorously you use the OO COBOL syntax, the more thoroughly your program 
will be conformance checked.

Conformance checking relies on the existence of an external repository 
file, containing details of all the interfaces. The use of this file is 
controlled by the REPOSITORY Compiler directive, as follows:

    * To request that your program is conformance checked, compile it
      with the setting REPOSITORY (CHECKING ON)
    * To make sure information about the object interfaces in your
      program is added to the external repository, so that other
      programs can be conformance checked against it, compile with the
      setting REPOSITORY (UPDATE ON).
    * If you want both checking and update, compile with the setting
      REPOSITORY (UPDATE ON CHECKING ON).

Here are some examples of the kinds of checks included:

    * You invoke a method of an object of class A using a typed object
      reference. The Compiler checks that the method exists in the class
      and that all the using and returning parameters in the INVOKE
      statement match the parameters defined for the method in the class.
    * You define class B as inheriting from class A. Class A's factory
      object has two methods, and so does class B's. The Compiler checks
      that the methods in class B's factory object have the same names
      and same using and receiving data definitions as the methods in
      class A. (The methods in class B override the methods in class A.)
    * You define classes B and C as inheriting from class A, and you set
      a typed object reference for class B to a typed object reference
      for class A; the Compiler rejects this, because at compile time
      the typed object reference to class A might actually point to an
      object of class C. (You can get round this restriction by using an
      object view, which delays the checking until run time. For more
      information see the section /Object Views/
      <http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/opusob.htm#u005>.)


The Compiler uses the information in the external repository to perform 
conformance checking.

**** Now when it comes to Repository I was going to ask you to confirm , over and above parameter checking, doesn't it check methods - the answer above is "Yes". That to me is far more important than parameter checking. 
Most times you will pick up on typos in method names when testing, but it is irritating to have to go back, change and re-compile. Even more irritating as part of the particular tests you are doing, you may hit a 'Private' method that you haven't used before !

(For others reading this - unlike other OO languages COBOL does not distinguish between 'Private' and 'Public' - the difference between the two being, 'Public' you, the external coder, know about them. 'Private' is invoked within something you can't see, (encapsulated) - might be current 'utility' or the super utility above that it inherits from. That's not an insurmountable problem in a large installation - assume the DBA is responsible for both control of the (R)DBMS and installation utilities - he *only* issues you a list of Public methods which you can use and sending/returning parameters required).


>The REPOSITORY (or prototyping) allows the compiler to have acces to all the
>properties and methods of the classes you are using, so it can syntax check
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No don't quite agree with that, but see what you are getting at. Let's 
say I've got my own super class - in fact I have - DBIMain (Database 
Interface Main) - contains common methods applicable to collections. 
Assume I already have 1,000 program that utilize invokes, perhaps 
through their own separate DBIs (say CustomerDBI is one) - they each 
pick up on methods available in DBIMain such as :-

 invoke os-CustomerDBI "getKey" using os-Collection returning ws-CustomerKey

That method is addressed to CustomerDBI, doesn't find it and shoots 
straight up to find it in DBIMain. After some five years of happy 
OO-coding, we hit a new condition which just doesn't quite fit. We 
really need a method which needs to be 'tweaked' - we don't want to 
upset the apple-cart, and as you point out, re-compile all the 
'senders'. So we have the ability to add an entirely new method to 
DBIMain, one compile,  and assuming there are three new "senders" just 
compile those three.

Not quite the same as I'm illustrating above, but shows how it can be 
done. I use the Dialog Editor to "visually" design screens. I use one 
copyfile of passing parameters regardless of whether it is a listbox, 
dropdown or entryfield etc. I'll stick with entryfield. With the M/F 
dlgedit.exe I design, set positioning and tick (Hey ! That's OK in the 
USA, heard an American use it on one of the TV channels last night), 
against a limited set of properties, "numeric", "disabled", whatever. 
Rather than laboriously go through each control as generated in the 
....rc file, (Resource File), I set up a sub-parameter class identifying 
each control, and the sequence they appear for tabbing, (this contains 
fixed format for entries regardless of control type and is "occurs 
MaxControls"). Each of the individual table parameter rows are now 
passed to an instance of a 'generic' dialog to create them. Fine it works.

Think back some years ago I sent you a screen shot of the 'Readings' - a 
two-dimensional table.
No can do with the Dialog Editor, even on a small table it would be 
LABORIOUS identifying each 'cell' to a unique name. But I can still use  
a row parameter passing technique from that parameter class for dynamic 
creation - and for these in the common parameter copyfile I have to 
identify x,y, w and h  to get positioning.

The obvious and easy way is two separate methods :-

PUBLIC METHODS - "createEntryField" and "createDynamicEntryField" both 
receiving lnk-DialogParameters.

 Then I look at the code - wait a minute they are both doing the same 
thing for an entryfield, except for positioning , where I provide x, y, 
w and h for the latter. So still two PUBLIC methods accessing one 
PRIVATE method :-

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

        invoke self "getObjectFromId"
               using lnk-ID returning lnk-object
        invoke self "zCreateEntryfield2" using lnk-Params

       End Method "zCreateEntryfield".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield2".          *> PRIVATE
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
        *> some local-storage fields
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

       initialize ws-MethodRec
       initialize ls-length
       invoke lnk-object "accessSystemEvents"
       *> ****** lots of other coding for any entryfield, as appropriate 
......

       Exit Method.
       End Method "zCreateEntryfield2".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "zDynamicEntryfield".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       copy "\copylib\dlgparams.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

        invoke TextEntry "new" using self returning lnk-object
        invoke lnk-object "SetRectangle" using x y w h
        invoke lnk-object "SingleLineEntry"
        invoke self "zCreateEntryField2" using lnk-Params

       End Method "zDynamicEntryfield".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

Over and above what I show in "zDynamicEntryField" - it makes sense to 
get it right just the once by invoking self. So far I've never had 
errors resulting from the above - any errors occurring result from what 
I missed in setting up the pre-defined parameter list. (As background to 
that - I did have a half-assed attempt to read the dialog resource file, 
which obviously is line-sequential, fixed 80 chars, but needs some 
fiddling to identify entries for individual controls, depending upon 
line-lengths and that's dependent on which properties have been set. 
.Let's say, as is, it works some 75% - but with patience I could reach 
100% - then I wouldn't inadvertently even get parameter errors).

More on your other comments later - 'cos this was easier to answer.

Jimmy


>Pete.
```

```
news:3176lhF385lflU1@individual.net...
> Pete Dashwood<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> 11/30/2004 4:32:04 PM >>>

maxWeight

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't do much OO programming, and no OO-COBOL, but wouldn't the
following
> be more in following with the OO paradigm?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obviously Set_maxWeight and Set_maxHeight would be simple one line
methods,
> but from all I've read about OO this way is preferred.
>
(Don't believe everything you read...this is a very controversial aspect of
OO programming and there are heated debates about it. I just use what works
for me...)

> Just thinking...

And nothing wrong with your thoughts, Frank.

I would CERTAINLY use GET and SET methods if XmasTree1 was a COM or ActiveX
component, OR it was being late bound.

If XmasTree1 is simply an OO Class and it is NOT being late bound, then
there is no need to use GET and SET methods because the OO COBOL compiler
can resolve the moving of values to properties at compile time, using the
REPOSITORY. (or a prototype...)

I didn't want to introduce that (GET and SET debates have been raging in OO
for some years now, and there are good arguments on both sides) because my
post was about DESIGN and NOT about the nitty gritty of OO, which has been
thrashed to death here many times.

The irony here is that personally, I NEVER use early binding, so all of my
components implement GET and SET. (The ones that are COM compliant inherit
it from the Fujitsu *COM class and I don't even have to write the methods,
the (very few) others have their own 1 line methods as you describe.)

Pete.
```

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9M6RLwoPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  02.12.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
>      on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
>      in  3187ruF381dqtU1@individual.net
>   about  Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data
descriptioni
>
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, Leuko, it is not wise to take hold of something and decide it is so
before you have had some real experience of it.

It's like first year philosophy students who encounter Aristotle and
evaluate everything else using his logic system. When they get to the third
year they find there is more and, while Aristotle is not wrong, there are
better systems. Had they kept their minds open, instead of going into
agreement with the first thing they encountered that looked understandable,
they would have had much more richness from their course.

The concept of encapsulation in OO is a very important one and I certainly
endorse it. But there is much more to it than a black and white statement
that properties should only be manipulated by the methods of the object.

I said it was controversial and it is.

(And I have never been one to slavishly adhere to "rules", simply because
they were passed down from Authority, without thinking  about or questioning
them. It's a Kiwi thing...<G>)

Theory is one thing; practise is quite another.

Why would you specify a property to be PUBLIC if it was only ever available
inside an object?

If the property exists ONLY within the object you can argue that it is
encapsulated. The scope of the variable extends ONLY to the use of the
object. Any application manipulating the variable WITHOUT invoking a method,
has no effect on anything. Encapsulation integrity is not breached.

What difference is there between changing the property by passing a
parameter  that replaces it, in the invocation of the method, or directly
manipulating the property immediately before invoking a method of the
object?

As soon as you declare an object property to be PUBLIC (and these are the
only ones you CAN manipulate outside of the object) you have indicated that
the object can tolerate having it manipulated. In effect, the PUBLIC
properties are part of the interface to the object.

I use them as such, and the point of my post was that there are advantages
to doing this, rather than manipulating them through parameterization of
method invocation.

Having said that, I would not try and enforce this viewpoint on anyone else.
I use what works for me. I have been developing classes and components now
for around 6 years. I have tried many different ways of doing it. Some of my
early attempts were pretty abysmal. My current ones are not. As my knowledge
and, more importantly, UNDERSTANDING, of OO has increased through Java and
OO COBOL programming, I have reached a state of competence where I am able
to consider the "rules" and decide how much value there is in them, within
the context in which I wish to use the OO paradigm.(Primarily for component
development).

My code works; my components don't fail; my applications reuse them in many
different ways, without problem.

I reckon I must be doing something right.

Pete.
```

```
>      in  319jmgF37lp8kU1@individual.net
>   about  Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data
descriptioni
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are right, too <G>.

I re-read my previous posts and I think I reacted  bit too strongly. I don't
want to give the wrong impression here. Properties of objects are best
manipulated by the use of GET and SET methods (and I do that... I said so in
my post). But whether you use GET and SET or you don't, the fact that the
properties CAN be manipulated is what is important. If you use tongs to pass
the salad or you simply grab a handful of it, the result on your plate is
the same... <G>.

OO COBOL does not specifically provide for PUBLIC and PRIVATE properties,
but it does do so implicitly. (.NET OO COBOL allows methods to have these
attributes, and I think it may also allow properties to be described
specifically as such, but I'm not sure about that...)

The real point here is that you can pass parameters to methods, OR you can
manipulate the properties (directly in early bound code or using GET and SET
in both early and late bound OO COBOL).

I have found that there are certain advantages in minimising the number of
parameters passed to methods. (Ideally, zero...). The alternative of
manipulating object properties to change behaviour, is more flexible and
much lower maintenance.

This then has some beneficial effects on the design process. Objects
(classes, really) can be designed to include all the attributes that are
part of that object and all the methods that are likely to be applied to it,
rather than just considering the data and the process steps for the current
goal, as is usual in non-OO design. (Then we go back later and add more data
elements and more process steps and grizzle about what a pain "maintenance"
is...).

For me, considering what an object MIGHT need to do, is a fun exercise that
requires imagination and the ability to build scenarios. I use "paper
models" of objects before I ever start coding them. When the component
design is complete, I then go back to the problem at hand that originally
sparked the need to design the object, and make sure that the designed
component meets this need.(I have never had a case yet where the original
requirement was overlooked in designing an object, but there could always be
a first time...)  Having regained the focus for the problem solution, I then
move to the next component.This design process iterates until I am ready to
glue my objects together into the final solution.

I never think about maintaining these objects. If there was a flaw in the
design, and a future requirement comes up that pertains to that object and
was not considered in the original design of it, I scrap the whole object
and repeat the design process. (this is partly to reinforce where I went
wrong and partly as a kind of "punishment " for not doing it right the first
time... <G>) In just over five years, I have had to do this twice... If a
future requirement comes up that is related to that object but introduces
new properties and possibilities I would create a new "co-operation" object
that co-operates with the existing one.

For me, the essence of design is to make it simple, with non-complex but
comprehensive building blocks for applications.

The XmasTree example is a contrived one, but it serves to illustrate the
concept. There might be uses for Xmas trees that have nothing to do with
Xmas. The object should handle them. It manages this by considering the Xmas
Tree as an object and noting its physical characteristics  (deriving the
properties), then what you can physically do with a Xmas tree (deriving the
Methods).

Obviously, this is a very different approach from that which we
traditionally employ in application design. However, the two are not
completely mutually exclusive. The traditional methods are good for getting
an overall design. Things like the flow of documents and what happens to
them. I still use data flow analysis to derive all the data elements and
their source and disposition, for complex in-house processes. The overall
system flow becomes the basis for the top down Object design in large
systems.

In smaller systems it is sometimes enough to simply design and build the
objects.

Very often the same objects keep cropping up in different circumstances.

For example, I have a single component that handles anything to do with
people. It can interface to databases and it has properties like their
addresses, date of birth, phone number, hair and eye colour, distinguishing
marks, skills (can vary by DB attachment), etc. (there are around 60
properties to this component, some of which are simply DB attachments that
can be varied. NONE of them  (except the keys - Lastname and ID) are
mandatory. You can add a person with just a surname (the object assigns the
ID) and you can then update the information later. Duplicates are detected
and choices offered. Soundex is encoded automatically when a new person is
added.

This component currently is used in situations where the "people" are:
1. Clients.
2. Members.
3. Patients.
4. Prospects
5. Staff.
6. Subscribers.

All different application systems, all using exactly the same component to
deal with people. In the old days, it would have meant 6 different database
designs with 6 different sets of attributes, hard coded processes to add,
change, delete, search, retrieve, and configure the "people processing".

Nowadays when I am considering a new application, I don't even have to think
about the people processing. I can take it as read. All I have to do is
check that the component can handle the requirements of the new system... it
has never failed to, and I have never amended it since it was first written.

It can run on a Web Page or on a desktop, without amendment. (You need to
install the fujitsu runtime on your web server or point to it via a virtual
directory in the web server. Obviously, with .NET Cobol using managed code,
this requirement is removed.)

There are advantages in OO design and development that are just HUGE when
considered against traditional methods.

It is a tragedy that mainstream COBOL shops have failed to embrace them.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T00:08:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ojssd.430834$nl.280387@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <u37gq0lei1hedphaeenk8uqt8dhfj7f2a2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411271254.908feeb@posting.google.com> <lmiiq01vgp5ik17h4vme2nj27md7evhcr7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272343.538720d@posting.google.com> <5k0lq0hnnat0lg15m9d3ifqb6glo2v5ker@4ax.com> <SHSqd.384805$%k.209871@pd7tw2no> <312ppvF360hkqU1@uni-berlin.de> <KH2rd.393755$%k.252681@pd7tw2no> <314e7sF387olhU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

Just a quick reference to the OCCURS with VALUE as a separate topic and 
then next message will address your "Parmeters" and "Properties"

>>01 Object1      object reference value null.
>>01 Object2      object reference value null.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry but you blew that one - been around since COBOL 85 - so almost 20 
years.
My 'bible' recommended to me back when I was using Microsoft COBOL, 
(that's *theirs*) not the re-badged MF, was Jerome Garfunkel's COBOL 85 
Example Book.  In addition to history, tables etc., he runs though each 
significant RESERVED word in alphabetical order with coded examples. 
Great. That's what we need an updated version of. For my money the 
shoe-in would be WMK - providing Bill had the interest, stamina and 
health to do it. If he took up the challenge, I would be more than 
wiling to help.

But to be honest, I don't recall referencing Jerome on this one, maybe I 
saw it in some 'concrete' code rather than 'abstract' :-). Anyway 
objects with occurs is a logical parallel to the old '85 feature. So I 
must at some time have written :-

01 MyDialogs.
    05  a-Dialog occurs 6 object reference.

Bearing in mind M/F doesn't have auto garbage collection, I have a 
copyfile which handles the various 'finalize' methods which at class 
close-down does like the following (not what I actually have - but for 
simplicity)

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 6
  invoke a-Dialog(n)  "hide"
  invoke a-Dialog(n)  "finalize"
end-perform

I'm in the doggy-doo with both the above "methods", if I haven't got 
values for the subscripted objects. OK - one way around that as initial 
houskeeping, use an in-line perform to set them to null values. That 
seemed unnecessary, as tucked away in the back of my mind was you could 
set intial values for pic 9/x. So did a suck-it-and see and came up with 
the :-

occurs 6 object reference value null.

It comipiled OK and worked in  practice.

This is what I actually do, and we'll assume, I only use, in this 
particular run, resulting from user events, Dialogs 1, 3 and 6. So on an 
event for EXIT the control program has a finalize method :-

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "finalizeObjects".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-object                     object reference.
       Procedure Division.

       *> firstly I repeat my global objects for quick reference

       *> os-Parent (M. Menu) - this and next are NOT finalized
       *> os-Resource           by this class. They are only
       *>                       destroyed when exiting Master Menu
       *> os-Collections        10 droplists
       *> os-CusTable           SQL CustomerTable
       *> os-Dialogs            6

       perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 10
         set ls-object to os-Collection(n)
         perform FINALIZE-COLLECTIONS
       End-Perform

       set ls-object to os-CusTable
       perform FINALIZE-SINGLE

       perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 6
         set ls-object to os-Dialog(n)
         perform FINALIZE-DIALOG
       End-Perform

       invoke self "finalize" returning self
       .
       EXIT METHOD.

        copy "\copylib\finalize.cpy".

       End Method "finalizeObjects".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       *>------------------- finalize.cpy -----------------------------

*** Note in following copyfile, invokes to objects with "finalizeObjects"
*** is where the object (class) invoked is doing its own internal - like 
*** the preceding method above

       CLOSE-FILE.

       if ls-object <> null
          invoke ls-object "closeFile" returning ls-SqlResult
          perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
       End-if
       .
       FINALIZE-CLASS.

         if ls-object <> null
            invoke ls-object "finalizeObjects" returning ls-object
         End-if
         .
       FINALIZE-COLLECTIONS.

         if ls-object <> null
            invoke ls-object "deepfinalize" returning ls-object
         End-if
         .
       FINALIZE-DIALOG.

         if ls-object <> null
            invoke ls-object "hide"
            invoke ls-object "finalizeObjects" returning ls-object
         End-if

    *** The method "hide" here is 'belt and braces'; I've probably
    *** already "hidden" this dialog
         .
       FINALIZE-MENU.

         if ls-object <> null
            invoke ls-object "finalizeObjects" returning ls-object
         End-if
         .

       FINALIZE-SINGLE.

         if ls-object <> null
            invoke ls-object "finalize" returning ls-object
         End-if
         .
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

Above is just for info to get the OCCURS clarified.

Jimmy
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I *know* Robert will be itching to have a go, anybody else got 
suggestions ? As I typed this I realized that, although the inclusion of 
the 'copy routine' only marginally increases compile-time, to be an OO 
Reuse 'Purist' it would appear to make sense to have a separate class 
MyFinalizer which includes the copy routines.

    set ls-Object to os-Collection(n)
    invoke MyFinalizer "destroyCollection" using ls-Object

    set ls-Object to os-Dialog(n)
    invoke MyFinalizer "destroyDialog" using ls-Object

The previous Paragraph-Names are now indicated by the new methodnames - 
Hey Pete - one prameter :-)

And of course, should I find a need for an additional 
Paragraph/MehodName down the road, I only need add it to the new 
MyFinalizer, which is a single re-compile, and then only refer to that 
Paragraph/MethodName, for new classes where necesssary, NOT all existing 
classes.

Going back to my original method above, I really don't want to do this :-

(a) either
if os-Dialog(n) <> null
   set ls-Object to os-Dialog(n)
   perform FINALIZE-DIALOG
End-if

(b) or :-

if os-Dialog(n) <> null
    invoke MyFinalizer "destroyDialog" using os-Dialog(n)
End-if

Seems 'crisper' just to set the object and let 'elsewhere' determine

One downside to using the separate class MyFinalizer - EVERY 
program/class utilizing "finalizeObjects"  would need a reference  in 
Class-Control/Repository, at the top of the program/class   :-

 Class-Control.               ThisClass            is class "thisclass"
                                       AnotherOne       is class "another"
                                       MyFinalizer        is class 
"myfinalz"
                                        .
I've been assiduously avoiding quoting  classs names where they aren't 
needed.

How about you Bill - you are damned good at spotting the obvious.

Any takers ?
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:

> Top Post Only..
>
> One developer I know generates Java using a cobol system. I'm
> not quoting him, but I believe he said Java is for the birds
> or something like that, and that he can do more with cobol.

Ok Warren - but ask him to give us a clue, not his name, rank and serial 
number - but *which* compiler is he using ? That matters. I'm out of 
touch but there were/are COBOL compilers devoted to Linux OS.

My own situation - application is very keyboard intensive - so I really 
have doubts it could be 'Webbed' effectively; very much a desktop 
application. I could very well be wrong, but if I was to try it, I'm 
suspecting a logjam between Client/Server, purely related to the fact 
that  current web technology will be  light-years behind what could be 
available in another 5 or 10 years. Couple of weeks back was talking to 
a Radio Shack guy, and he was enthused about 'wireless' communication, 
but still something comparatively new. Funny thing about the word 
wireless, been with us since you were a boy, but we never dissect the 
word into WIRE LESS. (Think back to DEC introducing the first CDs, as 
mentioned in one of the PC-magazine articles - now their original CD 
capacity is a flea bite).

Having got clobbered royally with viruses and even introducing Norton 
Anti-Virus on a Win 98 - it made the situation even worse. NAV 
conflicted with their own Norton Utilities, plus Win 98, and having to 
access the MS Knowledge Base for fixes. Screens designed to show in a 
full screen Windows frame now appear as only two thirds shown,  the 
bottom third is out of sight below the screen, so I can do bugger-all 
with my Push-buttons in the lower third, which don't show. The point is, 
reluctantly, and a family saviour provided the money, I have to upgrade 
to an XP, (sometime within the next week). The point of the above tale 
of woes is regardless of which current machine I buy, it outstrips my 
old Win 98 on both speed and disk capacity, plus of course extra goodies.

At this juncture, I haven't needed to jump out of Net Express to do 
anything non-COBOL, and that I would suggest is because I have 
collections built into the total product, allowing me to manipulate 
strings - can be done in Procedural but the  routines (methods) are 
already written for me. Additionally, (not a sales pitch), the *superb* 
ESQL module which formats my SQL statements. Java-wise  I *could* using 
specific Micro Focus support classes, to access Java - but as yet no need.

I'm going to get real vague here, because I'm not going to spout about 
that which I know nothing of. After his book on OO, Will Price wrote a 
book on using M/F, "Elements of COBOL Web Programming", published by 
Micro Focus, and thanks very much, we received a freebie copy along with 
Net Express V 3.1. This second book is *not* about OO but is devoted 
primarily to the N/E Forms feature to prepare web pages,  using all 
those every-day terms like HTML, CGI and God knows what else. A quick 
read through, I absorbed what say HTML stood for, but by the time I got 
to CGI or something else, had completely forgotten the meaning of the 
previous acronym. The simple fact is, you don't get a handle on the 
terms or techniques until you actually use them. Hence my caveat. Still 
that also applies to other forms of technology.

In addition to all the good stuff on DBMS and SQL fed to me by Pete, 
Will's book strengthens the case and drew my attention to taking a 
second look at the ESQL module.

 Frankly I haven't seen much comment on the Forms module in the M/F 
Answer Exchange, so I don't know how it has been taken up by developers. 
The only quote was something like, "Well it's a bit clunky to use" - but 
I'm in  no position to interpret that comment.  But I do have my 
suspicions - I'm inclined to think folks look at the bare bones module, 
hoping it will fit every contingency. It wont; there will always be 
exceptions.

 As I said I can use COBOL classes to access Java features - but without 
any proof, this is where I would go as a first shot into Webbing - look 
at that basic Forms module and see where, (just like our buddy Richard 
does, with umpteen tools), using templates, I can make that 'sing' for 
me. I might fall flat on my face, but first shot I'll give it a try and 
I'm still totally in COBOL, or at least COBOL is still in control. 
Second option if that fails - OK - "talk" to Java from COBOL.

I think OO adds the mortar to the bricks making it possible to do many 
things in COBOL which may not initially be apparent.

PS: One of these days Richard is going to get real brave and gives us a 
catalogue of all the languages, tools and God knows what else that he 
uses :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T10:27:50-05:00
