_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T23:33:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<315kviF38c8esU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <9Lekk3FPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no> <joe_zitzelberger-459E88.16395028112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <qqSqd.370172$Pl.108431@pd7tw1no> <312q8lF33eq06U1@uni-berlin.de> <hN2rd.389026$nl.33743@pd7tw3no> <Nxard.3809$0H.3156@fe12.lga>`

```

"Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:Nxard.3809$0H.3156@fe12.lga...
> Top Post Only..
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Warren, you probably wouldn't be familiar with Jackson Structured
Programming, but not long after Djkstra's famous paper on the elements of
programming (where he shows there are only three requisites for a sequential
process implemented on a Von Neumann processor), a hustler (oops...gotta
watch for law suits here...) a "consultant" named Michael Jackson (no
relation...and just as mentally sound) came out with a system that would
enforce structured programming. Programmers indicated which of the three
functions they required  and the sequence they were to be applied in, and
JSP generated a COBOL program that was then compiled.

Now, this system had everything going for it. It could be proven to be
mathematically "pure", it generated COBOL code, which everyone could read
(this was in the days when Source and COBOL were still King). I worked on
one site in North London were it was "mandated". I refused to use it for a
particular job that required something more than batch processing and was
told my job was on the line. (I was a contractor). I said fine, and started
packing, (It was in the days when COBOL contracts where abundant), the
senior manager heard about it and, after hearing both sides gave me
dispensation for the specific programs I was writing. The manager who had
enforced it on me vowed to see me off the premises. (A year later they had a
party when he left and I signed his leaving card with the comment "I'm still
here..." Small victories are often the sweetest, but I digress...

One of the major criticisms about JSP was that it generated TERRIBLE COBOL.
It was full of GO TOs (very anti-structured for a product that was
Evangelical about structured programming...) and even used ALTER. (I liked
it actually, but I never let on...and it was the concept I liked not the
product...)

So, anyone who used JSP could quite legitimately say (after looking at the
generated code) : "Wow! This COBOL really sucks as a language. I could do
things in RPG much better than this."

In case I've lost you, the point was that your friend's experience of Java
was as a COBOL programmer viewing Java that was generated from COBOL. This
is no way to form an informed opinion about Java or anything else.

Warren, the fact that Java is good, in no way dimiinishes COBOL, and it is
silly to have a contest. They are both good for certain tasks.

Java is more successful because the proponents of it are more dynamic in
support of it (you don't find "infinite loop" threads in the Java forums
<G>), it is virtually free (as opposed to several thousand dollars for a
commercial COBOL compiler), has proliferated because it is pretty easy to
learn (provided you don't approach it using "COBOL think"), implements OO
(in my opinion, it does this even better than C++, but I don't profess to in
depth knowledge of C++; this opinion is based on admittedly superficial
reading and I will change it if evidence to the contrary becomes available),
and is virtually platform independent. (Quite a lucrative market is emerging
in writing JAVA games for WAP and 3G phones... I might even have a go at
this next year <G>)

So, irrespective of your friend's opinion, Warren, Java is successful and
looks like being with us for some time to come.

Procedural programming is on the way out (there are sound reasons for this)
and COBOL is a procedural language. A fantastic effort was made to provide
it with OO facilities, but these were not picked up by the COBOL community
at large, and it has,  largely, missed the boat.

Even OO languages like Java do not have unlimited lifespan, but it will be
longer than the languages which implement the procedural paradigm.

I have to be honest here: the main reason I am still using COBOL is because
it  (well, I should really only speak for the Fujitsu implementation of it,
as that is the only OO COBOL I have ever used) implements OO so well. (The
other reason is because I am attached to it after making my living from it
for 40 years...)

Pete.
<snip>
```

```
Top Post only...

Peter, I understand. And perhaps I misled. This person knows JAVA and 
OO. He uses them, but finds that for his work, a lot of which is 
conversions, he can create the new programs faster with his system of
COBOL programs designed to create either COBOL, or with another 
converter, JAVA. Someone has a conversion they want done, he will do it.
Using his programs. The output source may not look that much like you
might expect, but they are generated using his COBOL programs. They
work, and it apparently is cost effective. I believe he also creates
new applications with his COBOL generator. Of course there has to be
some beginning work to do that. Oh, and as I was born in Gary, IN.,
I know who you were talking about. There are some other Garyites that
I believe are more revered that the Jackson your referred to.

Warren
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> Warren, you probably wouldn't be familiar with Jackson Structured
> Programming, .... , and
> JSP generated a COBOL program that was then compiled.

Jackson Structured Programming was a design methodology in the early
70s as in his book 'Principles of Program Design'.  His company made
its money primarily on consulting and courses on program design.  I
recall being taught the methodology, or at least being told what it
was in a formal setting.

JSP-COBOL was a code generator, but it should not be confused with the
design methodology.
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> In article <co268i$jaj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
>  "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> omnipotent being create people to watch them suffer?

That wasn't how He created them.  Check out Isaiah 14 for how Lucifer 
fell and became the driving force for evil, and the first few chapters 
of Genesis to see how he interfered with the then-perfect Creation. 
It's also important to remember that while suffering is bad, good can 
come out of bad things.  It's not guaranteed, but it happens quite a bit.

A recent example from my experience - in traveling to Greenville, SC 
from Montgomery the day before Thanksgiving, I stopped in Newnan, GA to 
get some coffee.  The Dunkin Donuts where I stopped had a line back to 
the door, and I was quite frustrated that I'd have to wait so long. 
(I'd already spent 15 minutes in a drive-thru in Auburn, AL, and I was 
only the 3rd in line...)

When I get almost to my father-in-laws house in Greenville, there has 
been a 3-car wreck that happened at that intersection just a few minutes 
prior.  If there hadn't been such a line when I stopped, would mine have 
been one of those three cars?  The slow drive-thru and long line 
suddenly didn't bother me - I was just grateful to have made it there 
safely.
```

```
On  2-Dec-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > I tend to be of the opinion that even if God exists, he isn't very nice.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> come out of bad things.  It's not guaranteed, but it happens quite a bit.

Our experience on Earth shows that good things can come from bad.  Strength can
come from suffering.  Enlightenment can come from suffering.  I don't *want*
perfection.    I don't want to play heavenly golf and score 18 every time I
play.   Please let me die instead.    But even worse than the Heaven we see many
worshipers wanting for themselves, is the Hell many worshipers want for the rest
of us.   They are looking forward to watching us being tortured beyond all
understanding forever and ever without hope of parole - for being fooled into
not believing.  And eternity is a long, long, time.

Of course they worship a being who they believe can stop this.    How can people
mix up love and hate so greatly?
```

```
I think I addressed this answer in the "Bowing Out of Debate" message I 
posted.  I'm running behind on my messages here, but I can assure you 
that the name of the website you reference above has no basis in truth - 
if God hates them, why did Jesus come to die for them?  Far-right 
nutjobs help my side the same way Howard Dean helped the Democrats in 
the last election...
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Incredibly tragic what you relate Chuck. Can you appreciate why much of 
the world is currently disturbed by American attitudes, beliefs. Here's 
me thinking that men in white pointed hoods, with holes poked out for 
eyes, and uttering , "We don't need no niggers, Jews or catholics around 
these parts", was just an historical aberration carried over from the 
Civil War. Obviously a lack of tolerance is still a deep festering wound 
in American society.

I've had a very private thought which has formulated over about a 
ten-fifteen year period, and recent events triggered it in my mind. 
Economically and historically events appear to occur in cycles. While 
most members of 'groups' are quiet majorities, there are the vocal 
activists who wish to push the envelope. I'm thinking of say Gloria 
Steinberg (if that's how you spell her name - the one who did a stint at 
a Bunny Club); she and her friends pushed the envelope for feminism - 
not wrong, but to-day I don't think the concept has much female support 
- young women accept as is, not realizing what Steinberg and company 
actually achieved.

The same with homosexuality. I have no idea of numbers, but somehow 
feel, that given decriminalization of the sexual act and equal 
property/equity rights under the law, coupled with some civil 
recognition of same-sex marriage/partnerships, then again a silent 
majority could live with such a status, quietly leading their own 
lifestyle. Unfortunately there are the activists, who again want to push 
the envelope, 'in your face'.

Now here's my thought - religion, and specifically Christianity in the 
Western World, is going to turn around and bite, with "Enough is enough 
!".  Might seem far-fetched, but I see that we could have something as 
horrific as the Reformation, re-cycled, times a factor of  'n'. Then add 
to that the possibility of it  triggering a confrontation between 
Christianity and Islam.

Note, agnostics and atheists - you don't even get consulted - in all 
likelihood, after the homosexuals, you could be the next targets. 
Reflect on Nazism - first target Jews, but then add mentally-disabled, 
gypsies, Slavs,  homosexuals et al......

(Pause at this point and ask yourself where is atheistic China in all 
this confrontation).

God forbid. And I sincerely hope my thoughts are skewed.

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-972C5B.09071317112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net> <coj41t$1nlm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Dlcrd.399066$%k.100230@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Dlcrd.399066$%k.100230@pd7tw2no...

> Incredibly tragic what you relate Chuck. Can you appreciate why much of
> the world is currently disturbed by American attitudes, beliefs. Here's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> in American society.

When Walt Disney decided to build Disneyland in Anaheim in the early 1950's,
he made it a condition of doing so that the City Council could no longer
wear their Klan garb in Public.

I've heard from several relatively reliable sources that as late as the
1980's there was a police code "NIN" in Newport Beach; the last two letters
stood for "In Newport".  I leave it to your imagination what the first "N"
stood for.

I don't disagree with the points at all.  Stonewall was in 1969, and I think
in general the gay community has quieted *way* down since the early 1980's
(after the decimation -- and devastation -- of the AIDS crisis).

As to the point of "in your face":  Why is a family photograph of wife and
kiddies on one's desk appropriate in the workplace, while a family
photograph of oneself with one's lover a political statement?   "Quitely
leading their own lifestyle" tends to evolve very quickly into "unless we
get a hint that your lifestyle might differ from ours".

The concept of privacy as to sexual activity applying only to heterosexual
activity was confirmed by the US Supreme Court in Bowers v. Hardwick back in
1986.  This decision was reversed by Lawrence and Garner  v. Texas just one
year ago.

The police had entered Lawrence and Garner's apartment without permission to
investigate a (false) report of an armed intruder (having broken into the
wrong apartment), found them "engaged in sodomy" and arrested them.
Lawrence and Garner appealed their conviction on the grounds of equal
protection and right to privacy (basically challenging Hardwick) and won
their appeal.  At the time of that decision, Texas, Kansas, Oklahoma and
Missouri banned "sodomy" between same-sex couples but not between
opposite-sex couples; thirteen more banned such acts outright.  These laws
were overturned by that decision.

> Now here's my thought - religion, and specifically Christianity in the
> Western World, is going to turn around and bite, with "Enough is enough
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christianity and Islam.

Well, as I said, I see *much* less activism from the gay community now than
I saw in the early 1980's.

> Note, agnostics and atheists - you don't even get consulted - in all
> likelihood, after the homosexuals, you could be the next targets.
> Reflect on Nazism - first target Jews, but then add mentally-disabled,
> gypsies, Slavs,  homosexuals et al......

I don't disagree.

> God forbid. And I sincerely hope my thoughts are skewed.

They may well not be.  But I am not convinced it's the job of the targets
(of any stripe or ilk) to make themselves invisible and accept whatever the
targeters want to dish out!  The Stonewall Riots in 1969 stemmed from the
police repeatedly raiding the gay bars in Greenwich Village.   I do reflect
on Nazism and similar views; as I indicated elsewhere in this thread the
City Council of the town in which I live was appearing as a group in their
Klan robes until Walt Disney convinced them that this might repel the
tourists he was hoping to attract with Disneyland! And this ain't hardly the
deep south.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>Oddly enough the Dashwood family motto is "Pro Magna Carta". (no, really,
>I'm not joking...)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Get outta here ! What does that translate to - Roger the Dodger from Ash 
Wood - just shoving a "D'" in front doesn't make it sound very 
Gallic/Normanesque. They were a bunch of Viking-Norman cut-throats and 
robbers who joined Willie the Conker to seek their fortunes. And you 
know bloody well  that by slipping one of the monks at Battle Abbey a 
few bucks, years after the Battle of Hastings, you could be added to the 
roll-call  as though you *actually* had been around in 1066. By the time 
you get to your Hellfire Club relative - some legitimacy given to the 
'claim' - nobody could really check after some 600-700 years.

Now my brother when he first came over here had the four family crests 
put on to plaques, Maternal - Walsh and McDermot, Paternal - Gavan and 
Dunne. There are outfits in N. America, for a buck,  will print you out 
a computerised family tree, even if granddaddy was a chimpanzee. (There 
just aint no such animal as family crests in Ireland, they weren't into 
heraldry). Really impressed his new Canuck friends. When they were gone 
he would grin and slyly tell me, "Well I tell them we are descended from 
the Kings of Ireland". Quite possible, and there were hundreds of 'em - 
chieftains of small nomadic herdsmen clans drifting to and fro across 
Ireland with their goats, sheep and cows.

Not too many years ago I got a blurb from the promoters assuring me they 
could trace my family tree, using a template letter :-

    "John Gavan was the first Gavan to arrive in New York in 1801. *She* 
did this........".

I was intrigued by the bit that you did on checking accuracy for 
duplicate names and addresses, rejecting, what was it, some 12,00 
records.  Those silly buggers that sent the above couldn't even get his 
sex right !

You bet you've got something in your jeans - probably bovine. I believe 
your fairy tale about as much as you believe there's a God :-)
 
BTW - I do *not* store stuff, (i.e. e-mail) - I keep it in my memory 
box, that's why my quotes are only approximate.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:wxbrd.393951$nl.202429@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> robbers who joined Willie the Conker to seek their fortunes.

 A "bunch of cutthroats" who carried out the last successful invasion of
England ....

I spent a Summer afternoon on the field in Battle and visualized the whole
thing. The total numbers on both sides were less than 10,000 men and in one
afternoon they changed the course of Western History. When you think that
100,000 men died on the Somme in one day without making the tiniest bit of
difference, it is a sobering thought.

Despite what you might think, my sympathies were with Harold and I think the
whole thing was a tragedy.

But you missed the whole point of my post, Jimmy. It was about how
ridiculous this thread is becoming.

I certainly did not intend to add fuel to the fire.


> And you
> know bloody well  that by slipping one of the monks at Battle Abbey a
> few bucks, years after the Battle of Hastings, you could be added to the
> roll-call  as though you *actually* had been around in 1066.

Well Jimmy, before you dismiss the research of other people, you should do
some of your own. I understand it is DeBrett's who document Roget D'Ashwood
although the connection is, admittedly, tenuous, and it is over 30 years
since I was told about it. As I don't have a copy, I can't check, so you may
be right.

Personally I neither know nor care whether the Dashwoods were at Hastings,
any more than I care about the properly documented history of the family.
You have obviously been to West Wycombe, gone through the caves and decided
that was the start of the family. It wasn't. The baronetcy was granted by
Queen Elizabeth the first, and the Dashwood family were prominent before
that.  Francis and the Hellfire club (the Mad Monks of Medmenham) are
probably the most interesting, but we had a Lord Mayor of London (1702),
several admirals, a Chancellor of the Exchequer, and an explorer in
Victorian times who found a pass through the Southern Alps of New Zealand
which is named after him. We also had a number of rogues and vagabonds, just
like any family.  Then there is me... <G>

I place no store by any of this stuff and mentioned it in passing only
because the family motto IS "Pro Magna Carta" and there IS a long history of
defending civil rights. I might just as well have mentioned that Francis
introduced the Black Mass to England and was the first British Satanist, so
it is hardly any wonder I am an atheist. While all of these things may be
true, I attach no importance to them and I don't believe they were
instrumental in making me what I am today. I hold the views I do because of
my life experience in THIS lifetime, no other, and certainly not because of
what some ancestor did or didn't do. I am what I am and I make no apology
for it.


> By the time
> you get to your Hellfire Club relative - some legitimacy given to the
> 'claim' - nobody could really check after some 600-700 years.
>

The claim was legitimate long before that, see above.

> Now my brother when he first came over here had the four family crests
> put on to plaques, Maternal - Walsh and McDermot, Paternal - Gavan and
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That is both our prerogatives. I did not post in the hope of persuading
Jimmy Gavan, and disparaging  my personal beliefs to support your argument
could be seen by some as a cheap shot. Have I ever sneered at your religious
beliefs? (or those of anyone else here?)  Please accord the same respect to
mine. I hold my views just as firmly and with the same reverence you do
yours, Jimmy. Although I recognise you meant no harm by it, your analogy is
out of order.
>
I will not be responding to any further posts here regarding the family. It
was wrong of me to mention it and I realise I shouldn't have. It is
personal, and it is off topic.

Pete.
```

```
hot and the food is very spicy, but the Audio Visuals are good...)

makes it unattractive to me.

respite from the marriage). Now I collect Frequent Flyer points for each
visit.

The irony here is that I actually have the utmost respect for women (as for
all non-human species) and my wheelbarrow comment was not meant tobe taken
seriously...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T23:01:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net> <tC9sd.2$Yq6.1@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Fri, 03 Dec 2004 20:50:55 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:


>One can make a pretty good argument for that, particularly in this 
>country.  I'm not saying that we should go back to those "good old 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>might happen, and who will pander to them by promising "more security"...

You can say that again. The American public's response to danger is
ignoble. Three incidents come to mind:

Jacksonville, Florida, hasn't been hit by a hurricane in more than 20
years because it sits in a 'dent' in the Atlantic coast. Hurricanes
slide by, 100 miles out in the ocean. The main part of the city is 15
miles inland and somewhat elevated. In the unlikely event a hurricane
did hit, the only flooding danger was to beach towns such as Ponte
Vedra and Jacksonville Beach, not the city of Jacksonville.

In 1999, Hurricane Floyd was predicted to make landfall at
Jacksonville. Stores were quickly depleted of plywood, milk and bread
(why do they always go for bread?) The night before it was to hit,
half the city's population, 300,000 people, decided on their own to
evacuate, even though there was no official evacuation order. I-10
westbound became a massive traffic jam. It took 8 hours to go 100
miles. 

It was a false alarm. The hurricane slipped by 50 miles out in the
ocean. By 8am it was north of Jacksonville, yet businesses remained
closed all day and PEOPLE CONTINUED BUYING PLYWOOD. Home improvement
stores were selling it as fast as emergency shipments arrived .. AFTER
the threat had passed.
------------------

The Watts riots, in 1965, were confined to the Watts district in
south-central Los Angeles. I watched their week-long progress from my
high-rise office on Wilshire Boulevard, 2-3 miles to the north. Fifty
miles out in the San Fernando Valley, suburbanites were blockading
streets and running militia patrols to repel 'invasion'. Geez, talk
about over-reaction.
-----------------

Security screening of passengers on the tourist boat to the Statue of
Liberty this year was tighter than any airport. People and their shoes
were searched, cameras and cellphones examined, etc. Yet the statue
was not open. Visitors couldn't get closer than 100 meters from its
base. Why were they looking for bombs concealed in shoes and
cellphones? 

So I took an informal poll. I asked a dozen people whether they
thought such security was overkill. They all shrugged and said "It's
for our own protection."

Protection from what? From things that go bump in the night? From
psychological demons? It certainly doesn't protect us from loss of
civil rights nor from government harassment.

When the US Constitution was written in the 18th century, it was based
on the premise that Americans were self-reliant and proud. That
premise is no longer true; people have become sheep and cowards.
They're like profligate children pissing away the family fortune they
inherited. I question whether they're worthy of liberty, leadership
and money. Much of the world seems to feel the same way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T16:40:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31feaaF3atelrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net> <tC9sd.2$Yq6.1@fe40.usenetserver.com> <k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 03 Dec 2004 20:50:55 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> and money. Much of the world seems to feel the same way.

Robert, I enjoyed your post and I understand your points.

Except the last two sentences.

ALL human beings, no matter how bad or misguided are entitled to live in
freedom. Even the ones who we put in jail have certain "liberties" and their
rights are respected.

When we start to question this fundamental axiom, because we are frustrated,
disappointed in, or generally pissed off with our fellows, then the entire
structure of a democratic free society is being eroded from within, more
effectively than any terrorist could ever do from without.

BECAUSE you have liberty you can do something about changing the attitudes
you despise.

You despise the timidity and ovinicity of people. But that doesn't mean they
don't deserve to be part of the society.

If everyone was a hero who would tend the wheat?

Since people started forming communities there have always been the timid
relying on the warriors to protect them.

It is a symbiotic arrangement that works. (Ants find it useful, too...)

America is diverse. It is unlikely that every person will "get along" with
every other person.

Settling differences and resolving conflicts with mutual respect and
acceptance of the underpinning ground rule that ALL have value and the right
to a viewpoint, is fundamental to the success of the society. When someone
questions the "worth" of others, or decides that they are "superior" to
others (even though all of us are "superior" - perhaps "more adept" is a
better description - to others in certain areas), when someone questions
whether others DESERVE the same rights as the questioner enjoys, then the
whole history that bought freedom is invalidated. The lives that were lost,
the cruelty and torture that was endured, the blood that was spilt... all
for nothing.

You are heading down Orwell's road of "Some animals are more equal than
others"... It is not a good place to go.

It is a long time since any of us in the western democracies had our freedom
taken from us or our liberty proscribed. It is easy (for us) to forget the
ones who bought it for us.

What we have, cost us dear and it is worth preserving.

Don't give up on your fellow man because he behaves badly sometimes, or he
doesn't meet your expectations, or he isn't as smart as you are.

As for the opinion of the rest of the World....

There are many around the world who don't get to vote in America but must
still "live with" the results of the US elections. That certainly breeds
resentment, and it is very hard to resolve. Sometimes America takes
positions that are suspect to people in other cultures. That also breeds
resentment. Sometimes blatant mistakes are made by the world's most powerful
nation. That pisses everybody off... And they show it.

But despite all of this, America still has many friends around the world
too.

I'm not happy with the idea of one nation having total power, but I wasn't
happy with the Cold War and the threat of nuclear annihilation that I grew
up with, either. If we have to have one nation with unlimited power, I would
rather it was a nation based on democratic ideals and commitment to the
rights of human beings, even if they get it wrong sometimes...

Besides, popularity is a fickle thing and can change very quickly.

Whether we agree with the politics or not, there is no denying that the USA
is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.

Pete.
```

```
On Sun, 5 Dec 2004 16:40:48 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:


Thank you for the sanguine article. Reading it raised my spirit until
the last paragraph recalled a news report I had just read elsewhere. I
copied to rebut "ideals on which it was founded".

One of my brief assignments in the Marine Corps was Forward Observing
for an artillery unit practicing in Japan, using Mount Fuji as an
impact area. In protest, young Japanese would run into the target area
waving flags, knowing we'd stop shooting (part of my job was to spot
them and call cease fire). We responded by sending military police to
drag them away, kicking and shouting. Then we resumed fire. 

Internal reports called them "Communist agitators".  I knew this
wasn't about Communism, it was about protecting a symbol of the Shinto
religion. We could have put the impact area anywhere. Using Mount Fuji
was the height of insensitivity. 

When I walked in nearby rural Japanese villages, people spit at me.
Two deliberately swerved their cars and tried to hit me. When I tried
to enter a Japanese bar/restaurant, the owner saw me coming, came out
waving his hands and yelling (in Japanese) "No Americans. English,
German, anything else ok, but no Americans." His attitude didn't
change when I answered in semi-fluent Japanese that I wasn't a typical
American (they call us gi-jin, which means barbarian). I said I was on
his side and just wanted to enjoy a Japanese meal without causing
trouble. That wasn't good enough. I was still an Ugly American (The
only thing worse is Korean. Japanese totally despise Koreans.) Some of
my colleagues passed themselves off as German, talking in
German-sounding nonsense they knew Japanese wouldn't understand
either.

At the tender age of 17, I knew the Japanese peasants in those small
towns were right and we (the US) were wrong. 

A few years later I saw millions in Southeast Asia  lose a tradition
of quasi-democracy to be brutalized by well-intentioned but misguided
ideaology. 

>Whether we agree with the politics or not, there is no denying that the USA
>is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
>remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.

WASHINGTON - Evidence gained by torture can be used by the U.S.
military in deciding whether to imprison a foreigner indefinitely at
Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, as an enemy combatant, the government concedes.

Statements produced under torture have been inadmissible in U.S.
courts for about 70 years. But the U.S. military panels reviewing the
detention of 550 foreigners as enemy combatants at the U.S. naval base
in Cuba are allowed to use such evidence, Principal Deputy Associate
Attorney General Brian Boyle acknowledged at a U.S. District Court
hearing Thursday.

Some of the prisoners have filed lawsuits challenging their detention
without charges for up to three years so far. At the hearing, Boyle
urged District Judge Richard J. Leon to throw their cases out.

Attorneys for the prisoners argued that some were held solely on
evidence gained by torture, which they said violated fundamental
fairness and U.S. due process standards. But Boyle argued in a similar
hearing Wednesday that the detainees "have no constitutional rights
enforceable in this court."

Leon asked whether a detention based solely on evidence gathered by
torture would be illegal, because "torture is illegal. We all know
that."

Boyle replied that if the military's combatant status review tribunals
"determine that evidence of questionable provenance were reliable,
nothing in the due process clause (of the Constitution) prohibits them
from relying on it."

Leon asked whether there were any restrictions on using
torture-induced evidence.

Boyle replied that the United States never would adopt a policy that
would have barred it from acting on evidence that could have prevented
the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks even if the data came from
questionable practices like torture by a foreign power.

Several arguments underlie the U.S. court ban on products of torture.

"About 70 years ago, the Supreme Court stopped the use of evidence
produced by third-degree tactics largely on the theory that it was
totally unreliable," Harvard Law Professor Philip B. Heymann, a former
deputy U.S. attorney general, said in an interview. Subsequent high
court rulings were based on revulsion at "the unfairness and brutality
of it and later on the idea that confessions ought to be free and
uncompelled."

Leon asked whether U.S. courts could review detentions based on
evidence from torture conducted by U.S. personnel.

Boyle said torture was against U.S. policy and any allegations of it
would be "forwarded through command channels for military discipline."
He added, "I don't think anything remotely like torture has occurred
at Guantanamo" but noted that some U.S. soldiers there had been
disciplined for misconduct, including a female interrogator who
removed her blouse during questioning.

The International Committee of the Red Cross said Tuesday it has given
the Bush administration a confidential report critical of U.S.
treatment of Guantanamo detainees. The New York Times reported the Red
Cross described the psychological and physical coercion used at
Guantanamo as "tantamount to torture."

The combatant status review tribunals comprise three colonels and
lieutenant colonels. They were set up after the Supreme Court ruled in
June that the detainees could ask U.S. courts to see to it they had a
proceeding in which to challenge their detention. The panels have
reviewed 440 of the prisoners so far but have released only one.

The military also set up an annual administrative review which
considers whether the detainee still presents a danger to the United
States but doesn't review enemy combatant status. Administrative
reviews have been completed for 161.

Boyle argued these procedures are sufficient to satisfy the high
court.

Noting that detainees cannot have lawyers at the combatant status
review proceedings and cannot see any secret evidence against them,
detainee attorney Wes Powell argued "there is no meaningful
opportunity in the (proceedings) to rebut the government's claims."

Leon suggested that if federal judges start reviewing the military's
evidence for holding foreign detainees there could be "practical and
collateral consequences ... at a time of war."

And he suggested an earlier Supreme Court ruling might limit judges to
checking only on whether detention orders were lawfully issued and
review panels were legally established.

Leon and Judge Joyce Hens Green, who held a similar hearing Wednesday,
said they would try to rule soon on whether the 59 detainees may
proceed with their lawsuits.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:iia7r05ancbggahq429pg0dkh9jr4va7uc@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 5 Dec 2004 16:40:48 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

USA
> >is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
> >remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.
…[95 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Robert, it is quite apparent that you are genuinely concerend about these
issues.

(I would be too, if my government was doing the things you describe. We have
a known terrorist here who entered the country illegally, is wanted by
Interpol and a dozen other states, and has been incarcerated without trial
for just over a year now. He has been given access to lawyers, well wishers
etc and is not confined in chains in a dungeon. There has never been any
question of torture (mental or physical) yet there are demonstrations in
Wellington (our Capital) for his release and the grant of refugee status.
Why? Because it isn't the ""Kiwi" way to lock people up without due process.
We require a "Fair Go" for EVERYONE. I'm sure in my own mind we would be a
lot better off without this guy in our society, and so are many of his
supporters, but it just "isn't a fair go...". Funny little country this. I
can't help but love it, even though I know patriotism can cause wars...)

The behaviours that Don and yourself have alluded to are certainly cause for
concern. When the head of state takes absolute power it is time for the
people to start thinking about changing the head of state. When soldiers
indulge in brutality using the need for information as an excuse, it is time
to look at who is leading the soldiers. (I was a (at first reluctant)
soldier. It is an honourable calling. Soldiers are not murderers or
torturers, like terrorists.) When you can't tell the good guys from the bad
guys any more it is a pretty fair bet you are losing the war.

What to do about it?

I know from my own experience in America that there are people there who
will be as concerned about this as the rest of us. Surely there must be
opposition to these measures. What of the Supreme Court? Is there no
possible calling to answer for these actions? We have seen Congessional
enquiries before. What if millions of people wrote to their Congressmen and
demanded these practices be stopped? There must be right minded people even
in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Are there no Senators or Congressmen who would act as a rallying point for
putting in some checks and balances?

The war against Terrorism is something that affects all of us. Strong
measures are necessary.

But if, in combating scum, we become scum, then the scum have won.

Pete.
```

```

In article <31il6sF3b3l5dU1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> I know from my own experience in America that there are people there who
> will be as concerned about this as the rest of us. Surely there must be
> opposition to these measures.

Indeed.  A great many of us voted, for example.

> What of the Supreme Court?

It takes a while for things to get to the Supreme Court.  The SC doesn't
just issue rulings on any matter that comes to its attention; it's not
the Papacy.  There must be a case appealed through the Federal court
system to the SC, and then the SC must decide to consider it, and then
it must be added to its docket, which of course is scheduled well in
advance.

> Is there no
> possible calling to answer for these actions? We have seen Congessional
> enquiries before. What if millions of people wrote to their Congressmen and
> demanded these practices be stopped? There must be right minded people even
> in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Sure.  There isn't currently a sufficient vocal mass to persuade a
majority of Congress that its political future depends on restraining
the Executive Branch.  Members of Congress are probably correct in
that calculation - their jobs are more secure, at this point, in not
challenging the Bush Administration's power grab.

You must remember that most of the US electorate is gruesomely ill-
informed (as poll after poll shows) and easily manipulated.

> The war against Terrorism is something that affects all of us. Strong
> measures are necessary.

The "war against Terrorism" is a hobgoblin.  There is no such thing,
and there can be no such thing; terrorism is a strategy, not a group
of people.

There are a couple of wars against specific groups being carried out
by the US at the moment.  Exactly what measures are necessary for them
are highly debatable.

Similarly, exactly what measures are necessary, or desirable, to
promote the security of the US against terrorist tactics, by whomever
might elect to use them, is highly debatable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

```
On Tue, 14 Dec 2004 15:20:05 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 13-Dec-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Che Guevara.

All three fought for a National Army, which includes revolutionary
armies. Present-day terrorists don't belong to a nation, they belong
to a movement. They do not attack a nation or coilition but rather
individual  targets of opportunity. Their goal is not to replace one
regime with their own, it is to destabilize many institutions which
they view as a bloc i.e. The First World. 

>But there have been war-like activities between the powerful and the
>close-to-powerless forever.   Those who don't have power have always used
>tactics which could be called terrorism.    Those who have the power say anybody
>who won't stand up and fight mano a mano are cowards.   I suppose Goliath's
>supporters thought David was a coward for using a sling.

The word 'war' means hostile conflict between states or nations.
Phrases like 'war on drugs' , 'war on poverty' and 'class warfare' are
etymological perversions. What terrorists are doing is outside the
definition of war in the Geneva Accord, the UN Charter, any UN
Resolution, or any other public international law.

They're not guerrillas because they're not engaged in 'irregular
warfare'.  They are not insurgents or rebels because they're not
fighting their own governments. They don't have enough popular support
to legitimize the rodomontade that they represent The Nation of Islam.
They are simply anarchists, which is defined as:
1 : one who rebels against any authority, established order, or ruling
power.
2 : one who believes in, advocates, or promotes anarchism or anarchy;
especially : one who uses violent means to overthrow the established
order.

Some will disagree by pointing out that anarchy means the absence of
government. Don't they want a state based on Islamic law? Sure, for
their people. But Muslims are not their primary target. What do they
want for the First World? The answer is disorder and anarchy.
```

```
>(I would be too, if my government was doing the things you describe. We have
>a known terrorist here who entered the country illegally, is wanted by
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>can't help but love it, even though I know patriotism can cause wars...)

The purpose of torture is to get information, not to torment or punish
someone. (Remember, I used to be in the information gathering
business. :) It doesn't sound like this guy has secrets to hide, so
there'd be no reason for torture. Give him a fair hearing, then kick
him out. It should take days, not a year.

>The behaviours that Don and yourself have alluded to are certainly cause for
>concern. When the head of state takes absolute power it is time for the
>people to start thinking about changing the head of state.

They just had a chance, and they re-elected him. Next time: Hillary
vs. ??? Here's a clue:

 12.03.2004
The front page of USAToday's Fri/Sat/Sun issue is "Should the
Constitution be amended for Arnold?"
The resulting avalanche of hits took our website down. Having handled
5 million hits on this website a few weeks ago, we'll be fascinated to
find out how many visitors it took to take us down today. (We won't
know for a few days how many people checked AmendUs.org out.)

> When soldiers
>indulge in brutality using the need for information as an excuse, it is time
>to look at who is leading the soldiers. (I was a (at first reluctant)

>torturers, like terrorists.) 

Some are. Our prisonerf interrogator was fired as a drill instructor
for being too sadistic. That's like firing an accountant for being too
conservative.

Actually, the worst torture is self-inflicted ... the fear of what
might happen. He understood that brilliantly. He cracked people in
hours, by detecting their weakness (everyone has at least one) and
issuing threats against it.

>When you can't tell the good guys from the bad
>guys any more it is a pretty fair bet you are losing the war.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Neither the Supreme Court nor Congress has jurisdiction outside the
US. Right minded people are abandoning the administration. Three
high-level officials left last week, including the head of Homeland
Security.

>Are there no Senators or Congressmen who would act as a rallying point for
>putting in some checks and balances?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>But if, in combating scum, we become scum, then the scum have won.

We're the ones trying to change the other side to be like us.

I'm not convinced Arabs planned the 9/11 attack. I think they were
fall guys, in the same way McVeigh and Oswald were set up as solitary
villians. There are too many inconsistancies in the official story.
For instance, cellphone calls from 30K feet are a physical
impossibility. Steel framed buildings do not collapse from the heat
produced by a kerosene fire .. especially the number seven building,
which wasn't even hit.

Who would plan such an event? Look at who benefitted. Certainly not
Arab countries.
```

```
On Mon, 06 Dec 2004 22:19:29 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>What Robert suggests above is monstrous. Would the Arab nations really 
>have let that slip by and take the blame for an 'American Plot'. 

It wasn't blamed on any nation; it was blamed on Al Qaeda, headed by
former CIA-operative Usama bin Laden who was based in Afghanistan, run
by the US-funded and about-to-be-ousted Taliban. By coincidence, the
former operative met with the CIA in July 2001.

I don't doubt Al Qaeda was involved. They may have even thought they
were the prime contractor. What happened to their 'vast organization'
after we no longer needed a bad guy?

>Come on Robert, name just one in the current Administration who would have had 
>the *intellect* to plan  something so incredibly sophisticated and not 
>have details seep out.

When details do seep out, people refuse to believe them. For instance,
in Oklahoma City, the bomb squad was on the scene a half-hour before
the bomb went off. It was reported on TV and newspapers. Then they
went into the rubble and hauled off two unexploded bombs _inside the
building_, one in each of the ends that didn't collapse. Hundreds of
witnesses saw it. Survivors said there were two explosions: one from
the outside that broke windows, and a second that brought the building
down. 

The chief of US Air Force munitions research, a general and PhD
chemist, said it was impossible for an ammonium nitrate bomb to bring
down that building. Its velocity is much too low. They called him a
nut. He set up a demo with a similar building and bomb. Sure enough,
all it did was break windows. Then he put small demolition charges in
the right places and brought it down.

In spite of all the evidence, people believe McVeigh did it with his
truck bomb. 

Intellect? No more than a second-unit (special effects) movie
director. They fool millions every day.
```

```
wrote:

>On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
```

```
>That explains a lot about you.

It explains that when there is a conflict between facts and the
government's explanation, I begin to doubt the government's story.
Then, when I see inconsistencies in the government's account, I think
we're being lied to. For instance, on two occasions the President said
he learned about the first airplane crash from watching television in
the hall outside the Saratoga classroom. In fact, there was no TV
showing news in that hall. Why did he make it up?

>> Who would plan such an event? Look at who benefitted. Certainly not
>> Arab countries.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>long as we're the bad guy, he wins.

He didn't make us the bad guys in Iraq; we did it to ourselves. There
was no connection between Iraq and 9/11. We invaded to eliminate
weapons of mass destriction, then couldn't find any.

>And it is obvious to all that not everybody in a country benefit by the same
>things, especially if they measure benefits by religious terms.

There is no benefit to being invaded, by any terms. 

Beneficiaries are Halliburton, Bechtel, Fluor and the Carlyle Group,
headed by Frank Carlucci. Some of its partners are James A. Baker III,
George Soros, Fred Malek  and George H.W. Bush.
```

```
Why do you think they are denied due process in America?

US Supreme Court, Rasul -v- Bush and Hamdi -v- Rumsfeld are good 
references.

(Granted, those things take time -- any routine SC case needs to wait 
for an October/November hearing then six months for opinions to be 
released.  Courts of appeals tend to be just as slow.)

But the various incarcerated terrorists do have access to courts and due 
process.
```

```
AFAIK, only HHamdi is an American Citizen -- captured carrying arms 
against 'his country'.  He is one of the ones the courts have heard.  

(It is worth mention that the SC heard a case in the 40's about some 
Nazi sabatours in the US -- some of those were also American citizens.  
I think they were hung.)

"Rasul Bush" returned 30,800 hits from Google.  "Hamdi Rumsfeld" 
returned 24,000.  Some media, some law, some blog.

I can only say that I read about these two cases in American media 
outlets, but I do agree that they don't get the coverage one would 
expect.

Perhaps to cover them would take away from the time the American media 
has to beat the "Iraq is a worse quagmire than Vietnam" drum...or the 
"Bush loves to torture muslims" drum...or the "moral values run amuck" 
drum...or the "Condi is just a 'yes ma'mmy'" drum...

But those would just be wild speculation on my part.  Perhaps is is just 
as simple as Supreme Court cases aren't as sexy as a car chase or a 
celeb wardrobe malfunction.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com> <cov4ej$pdj$1@panix5.panix.com> <36e7r0t654ggf1n67p5cecu8l49fuflhkv@4ax.com> <cp0g1f$rct$1@panix5.panix.com> <4L3td.446333$nl.49532@pd7tw3no> <d%3td.4209747$yk.635246@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Mon, 06 Dec 2004 21:16:57 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>The  "Brooklyn Bridge defense"
>is the oft attributed to "all" mothers (at least in the US) answer to "Well 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Golden Gate Bridge, or any other high place>, then would YOU jump off it?"

How do you know ALL mothers say that? How big was your sample? Tell us
about the few you heard say it, IN FACT, and don't assume it's
universal. 

Why, just the other day I heard Mexican mother say "If everyone else
ate at Taco Bell, would you want to go there too?"

A blonde and a redhead are having drinks after work. On the 6 o'clock
news they see a man threatening to jump off the Brooklyn Bridge. The
redhead says, "I'll bet ten dollars he jumps." The blonde responds,
"You're on." He jumps, and the blonde pays up.

The redhead says, "I can't take your money. I confess to seeing it on
the five o'clock news." The blonde answers, "I saw it too. I didn't
think he'd jump a second time."
```

```
DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 50)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T21:00:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I19td.5377$Yq6.4560@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4L3td.446333$nl.49532@pd7tw3no>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com> <cov4ej$pdj$1@panix5.panix.com> <36e7r0t654ggf1n67p5cecu8l49fuflhkv@4ax.com> <cp0g1f$rct$1@panix5.panix.com> <4L3td.446333$nl.49532@pd7tw3no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> background - and don't you DARE write, "Do your own homework...." !

It's a defense used when someone uses, as the basis for their doing 
something, someone else doing the same thing previously.  It goes 
something like...

Well, if [aforementioned party] jumped off the Brooklyn Bridge, would 
you jump too?

This claim also assumes that the party one has cited with providing a 
good example does not actually provide an example.  To some, if this can 
be used as a retort ("Hey, that's just a Brooklyn Bridge defense"), the 
argument has no merit.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

I read your rather longish but informative message about abuses. But to 
me the following really, really does sum it up.

>Contempt for the American people is evident every day in Marketing
>circles, and in political consultants.
>
>  
>
While I understand the point you are making - remove the words 'the 
American' and you are even closer to the point.  I wasn't going to 
mention it, but it is relevant. From to-day's Calgary Herald cartoon :-

Shows a Billboard - "Finally.... a solution to Canadian participation in 
U.S. missile defence !".
Below are catapults, Roman ballista, with Canuck soldiers holding a 
string to eject the missiles. The missiles are cows with a big arrow 
pointing at "Alberta Cow". At the foot of each wooden ballista is 
another arrow pointing at "Softwood catapult".

The cartoon is NOT about our lack of military personnel or spending. 
Canucks will get it with a wry smile. My bet 99.9% of US readers here 
wont get it. (Hints - "beef" and "lumber").
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I may have hit the nail on the head, but you then come along like a 
master carpenter, ensuring it is square and gets firmly hammered into 
place with your own observation.

I hate to keep on picking on them, but Wal Mart - they don't *realize* 
what they are doing to their home country, because although motivated by 
rapaciousness, they don't see the effect down the road. Aggressive 
buying policies. One Hong Kong entrepreneur wont deal with them. They 
try to line up 5 or 6 Chinese manufacturers to bid against one another, 
sometimes with no more than a 1 or 2 cent differential. Naturally they 
are big time in China, (a radio quote on labour costs - Chinese 20 cents 
per hour, US $20 per hour). US home-based industries close down in small 
communities, as Wal Mart goes overseas. When those factories close, then 
a Wal Mart store pops up on a vacant lot next to the empty factory. 
"Sure,  come and work for us in retail - $8 per hour".

Meanwhile - don't know which East Coast port authority - the female 
manager states, "From this location alone we export $6 billion to China. 
We import from China $36 billion per annum".

I wrote above, "they don't *realize* what they are doing to their home 
country...". Commerce no longer has a home country.

Jimmy
```

```
In article <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> certificate?

Maybe a few hours with Quicken Family Lawyer and a trip to the local 
Notery.


> > The why?  Well, 800 years of english common law has failed to offer
> > legal recognition of same sex unions.  That is the source of such
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> .

That is not my position, just the reality.  A hundred years ago you 
would have gotten the same answer to the "why can't women vote?" 
question.

You (and others) seem to be of the impression that it was just last week 
when a bunch of southern christian redneck KKK types rode into town and 
invalidated all the happy same sex marriages that had existed forever.

They are quite new and quite unusual in the historical scope of things.  
AFAIK, only Vermonters have only (slightly) addressed this question with 
their legislature.  Why not actually try to change the law before 
accusing your neighbors of being intolerent and insensitive in the 
tradition of great-great-grandpa?


> > As DD asked earlier in this thread, why limit it to a single species?
> > Why not have automatic interspecies inheritance rights?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> CONTRACTS.

There are many places where society sets arbitray critera for licenses.

One is denied access to the bar for lack of a JD degree -- that does not 
mean one is denied legal advice.  One is denied medical license without 
equal certification, regardless of possible competance -- that does not 
deny one medical care.

A marriage license is but one way to secure some automatic rights.  And 
lack of a marriage license, does not in most cases, deny one other means 
of access to the rights it might confer.


> > Somewhere a
> > line must exist or everyone would have automatic rights to everyone
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to sign this one.

Automatic right of inheritances.
Automatic implied power of attorney for lots of stuff.
Etc.

Two people who have rights to enter into contracts can enter into the 
above contracts through slightly different means.  Such a separate 
contract option is also available to hetrosexual couples with the 
passing of the Commonlaw spouse in some states.

Why insist on the specific contract when the results are otherwise 
available.  Common law, as you noted, evolves VERY slowly.  Specific 
acts of legislature trump that and can do so VERY quickly.

Why waste your time with the slow common law approach instead of 
addressing this problem to your legislature for correction?
```

```
> Notery.

And that's going to suffice to convince the people in the hospital that you
*really are* another guy's spouse?   Does a married couple have to carry
their marriage certificate around with them to prove their relationship?


> You (and others) seem to be of the impression that it was just last week
> when a bunch of southern christian redneck KKK types rode into town and
> invalidated all the happy same sex marriages that had existed forever.

Figuratively it *was* last week when KKK types were running the town I live
in.   The times, they are a'changing, though; we now have one (out of six)
US representative from our county that's a Democrat, replacing the
much-loved B-1 Bob Dornan, who I believe replaced William Dannemeyer ...

> They are quite new and quite unusual in the historical scope of things.
> AFAIK, only Vermonters have only (slightly) addressed this question with
> their legislature.  Why not actually try to change the law before
> accusing your neighbors of being intolerent and insensitive in the
> tradition of great-great-grandpa?

My personal opinion is that laws that prevent discrimination on the basis of
sex are fundamentally in conflict with laws that prohibit same-sex couples
from entering into a marriage contract to begin with.  I don't understand
the convoluted thinking that allows both to coexist in the same
jurisdiction.

> A marriage license is but one way to secure some automatic rights.  And
> lack of a marriage license, does not in most cases, deny one other means
> of access to the rights it might confer.

It does deny *automatic* rights because they are automatically conferred
with the marriage license.  One can address each of those rights
individually -- with varying success compared to that which comes with the
marriage license -- but that is no longer an automatic process.

> Automatic right of inheritances.
> Automatic implied power of attorney for lots of stuff.
> Etc.

But this isn't automatic.

> Two people who have rights to enter into contracts can enter into the
> above contracts through slightly different means.  Such a separate
> contract option is also available to hetrosexual couples with the
> passing of the Commonlaw spouse in some states.

Not all states recognize common-law marriages (California for one).

> Why insist on the specific contract when the results are otherwise
> available.  Common law, as you noted, evolves VERY slowly.  Specific
> acts of legislature trump that and can do so VERY quickly.

And as I said above I think laws that preclude two people with matching
plumbing from entering into this particular contract are in fundamental
conflict with civil rights laws based on gender.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T23:13:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <cokuq5$2vua$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-7FC25A.09402001122004@knology.usenetserver.com...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> their marriage certificate around with them to prove their relationship?

No, and parents do not have to carry around birth certs to prove that 
they have an equal right to visit.  All one usually has to do is to 
simply assert a reasonable right to visit.  For serious decision making 
for an incapacitated patient, one will need to prove next-of-kin status 
-- via marriage license, or perhaps power of attorney.


> > You (and others) seem to be of the impression that it was just last week
> > when a bunch of southern christian redneck KKK types rode into town and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> much-loved B-1 Bob Dornan, who I believe replaced William Dannemeyer ...

It was Loretta Sanchez under a cloud of serious voter fraud that replace 
Bob Dornan.  For you to claim that Orange County was run by the KKK 
until last week is way off the deep end and well into surealistic 
nonsense.


> > They are quite new and quite unusual in the historical scope of things.
> > AFAIK, only Vermonters have only (slightly) addressed this question with
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> jurisdiction.

Really?  Do laws the prevent age discrimination prevent a 40+ father 
from wedding his adult age daughter?


> > Two people who have rights to enter into contracts can enter into the
> > above contracts through slightly different means.  Such a separate
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not all states recognize common-law marriages (California for one).

Perhaps I wasn't clear.  Reading that back I see that I used "passing", 
which in a legislative context means 'codifing into law'.  But I was 
thinking of the many states that have revoked the concept of common law 
marriage in the last few decades -- e.g. "passing" as in "dying" or 
"passing into history".

Georgia is one such that in 1997 explicitly stopped recognizing common 
law marriages because 'people living as husband and wife' were, most 
often, not really 'living as husband and wife'.

Those hetrosexual couples that are now denied common law spouse status 
can, if they desire, receive all the benefits by exersicing the 
QFL+notery approach I outlined above.


> > Why insist on the specific contract when the results are otherwise
> > available.  Common law, as you noted, evolves VERY slowly.  Specific
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> conflict with civil rights laws based on gender.

I think you are viewing this incorrectly.  The marriage laws in question 
apply to individuals, as all laws do.  You are trying to create a law 
that applies to a group.  The persons you claim are denied marital 
contract rights have all of the marital contract rights any other 
citizen has -- that is, they cannot marry someone too young, or 
incapible of understand the contract, or their parent or child or 
sibling, or someone without whom the have taken a blood test, or any 
number of other exclusions that have been placed upon the institution.  
And one of those exclusions has indeed always been that they cannot 
contract with someone of the same sex.
```

```
In article <cokuq5$2vua$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-7FC25A.09402001122004@knology.usenetserver.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> much-loved B-1 Bob Dornan, who I believe replaced William Dannemeyer ...

I just have to throw in an extra comment on this one.

It really is beyond all reasonability to try to slight Bob Dornan as 
being KKK type.  There has never been a credible accusation that he is 
in any way involved with the KKK, or indeed, that he is racist at all.   
And there is much to the contrary.

He is a member of a party that stood against slavery, segregation and 
socialism for all of its 150 year history.  Somehow it has become 
acceptable to slander and slur anyone that stands athwart socialism as 
supporting segregation.  But it is just wrong.

Lets not forget that the KKK was always the taliban wing of the Democrat 
party in their efforts to support slavery and segregation.  Even today, 
the Democrats activly support an officer of the KKK in sitting Senator 
Robert Byrd (D-WV) and have done so for half a century.

Contrast that against the Republican involvement with the KKK, that is a 
single, crazed, David Duke running for Gov, Sen & Rep in the open 
primary state of LA.  The GOP quite throughly repudiated him and 
rejected all of his awful ideas, even to the point of endorsing the 
Democrat in the runoff.

IIRC, it was in Bob Dornans early years that the KKK wanted him 
assinated for his work on black voter registration in the Democrat 
south.  

It is totally reasonable to attack B-1 Bob for his politics and his 
personal stands.  But to associate him with the KKK just because you 
don't like is politics is just wrong.
```

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-610F1B.09433406122004@knology.usenetserver.com...

> > Figuratively it *was* last week when KKK types were running the town I
live
> > in.   The times, they are a'changing, though; we now have one (out of
six)
> > US representative from our county that's a Democrat, replacing the
> > much-loved B-1 Bob Dornan, who I believe replaced William Dannemeyer ...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> And there is much to the contrary.

The "KKK types" was a reference to the City Council of Anaheim, not to Bob
Dornan.

I would, however, regard both Bob Dornan and William Dannemeyer during their
respective tenures in the House as being the feathers at the end of the
conservative wing!

> He is a member of a party that stood against slavery, segregation and
> socialism for all of its 150 year history.  Somehow it has become
> acceptable to slander and slur anyone that stands athwart socialism as
> supporting segregation.  But it is just wrong.

Did I say that, about either Dornan or Dannemeyer?

> Lets not forget that the KKK was always the taliban wing of the Democrat
> party in their efforts to support slavery and segregation.

Until relatively recently, the Republican party was pretty much unwelcome in
the Deep South because of their role in Reconstruction.   The Democratic
party was the only game in town.  In Texas where I grew up, this changed
most visibly when John Connolly switched parties in '73 and when Bill
Clements moved into the governor's mansion in '79.

> Contrast that against the Republican involvement with the KKK, that is a
> single, crazed, David Duke running for Gov, Sen & Rep in the open
> primary state of LA.  The GOP quite throughly repudiated him and
> rejected all of his awful ideas, even to the point of endorsing the
> Democrat in the runoff.

From what I gather, David Duke sought candidacy in the 1988 presidential
election *as a Democrat*, was barred from running, and only then campaigned
unsuccessfully under the Populist banner.  He won election in 1989 as a
Republican to the Louisiana house, and was elected chairman of the
*Republican* Executive Committee of St. Tammany's Parish in the late 1990's.

> It is totally reasonable to attack B-1 Bob for his politics and his
> personal stands.

Where I live, Republicans regard supporters of women's rights as
baby-killers, anyone who thinks domestic partnerships are a good idea as
outspoken advocates of NAMBLA and obviously in favor of child molestation,
and anyone who drives a Prius is a Commie.  I live in the Traditional Mover
and Shaker neighborhood of Anaheim (what used to be Mayor Charlie Pearson's
house is next door, the Ganahl and Penhall homes adjoin our back yard, Carl
Karcher's (that's the Carl of "Carl's Jr." restaurants) home is four houses
away, and the Fluor homestead is maybe two blocks away) and such is my
experience with that neighborhood.  Bob Dornan and Bill Dannemeyer received
*a lot* of financial support from my neighbors.

> But to associate him with the KKK just because you
> don't like is politics is just wrong.

I don't know the timeline between the documented involvement of the KKK in
Anaheim *city* politics in the 1950's and the support for conservative
(ultra-conservative) political agendas in *national* politics in the 1980's
and later within the area of Anaheim between Pearson Park and North street.
But I can say that the Dornan banners, and before that the Dannemeyer
banners, were large and many come election time in that particular
neighborhood!

Because the two different topics appear in the same paragraph does not
necessarily mean they logically follow.  And whatever Dornan's civil rights
record with respect to people who have a different skin color or ethnic
heritage might be -- including his support for Martin Luther King -- I would
have to say that I doubt many gay people feel he has lobbied as vociferously
on their behalf -- using phrases "screaming transvestite" and "Lesbian
spearchucker" does not strike me as indicating quite so much sympathy for
that group as for those who were targeted by others with the "N-word" and
worse some twenty years before!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-972C5B.09071317112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> writes:
> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> certificate?

For that matter, how much work is it to dissolve that relationship?
Before we were married, my wife and I had (present, durable) medical
power of attorney, (present, durable) financial power of attorney,
joint bank accounts, wills and insurance policies specifying the
other as the beneficiary, and considerable property owned jointly
with right of survivorship.  When people suggested we get married "to
show our commitment", we pointed out that marriage would make it much
easier for us to split up.

Dissolving all of those piecemeal arrangements is a lot more work
than divorce.  Easy divorce is also a civil right, accorded only to
couples that are permitted to marry.

(That wasn't the main reason we weren't married, of course.  The main
one was that we don't like joining exclusionary clubs.  That hasn't
changed, but Vermont, Massachusetts, and Ontario took some of the
sting out - one of my wife's former graduate students was among the
first men in Toronto to marry another man - and eventually my wife
decided she'd like to be married to me.  I was planning to stick
around anyway, so our legal status didn't concern me terribly one way
or the other.)
```

```
.    On  30.11.04

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

r>>> Aren't 'reference notation' and 'STRING' a "quality of Cobol" ?

LW>> for COBOL, rather a "me too".

HB> So?

   So what?

   I'm not sure what your "so?" asks for -- as to reference notation  
(like in variable-x(startpos:length)), which is expressed in most  
other languages by a funktion SUBSTRING(var, start, lenth), I do use  
it the database development system which I do currently use as my main  
tool, VDP. I'n sure if this particular form of SubStr() is implemented  
in other langauges, too (slice of an array).

   String concatenation is also a normal thing in such programming  
languages.

   For UNSTRING, though, there are not so straightforward equivalences  
in other languages, but pattern matching has been around in SNOBOL for  
a long time, and in REXX I have the PARSE statement, and the WORD()  
function. For my VDP applications, I have written my own procedure to  
parse a string into words, with the separators handed over as a  
parameter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T12:00:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<col7th$4h9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <9Lr69p99flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coi19j$4rq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9M2F-czeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <col5ao$8jq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:col5ao$8jq$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Dec-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Being late additions, and being not at all distinctive for CoBOL does not
make
> them any less qualities of CoBOL.
>
> (add the COMPUTE statement to this as well)

COMPUTE doesn't seem to have been a LATE addition to COBOL, but rather an
"optional" capability specified in the standard that not all implementors
elected to include; it's Nucleus Level 2 in '74.  It's been in the spec from
COBOL's earliest days; all evidence I have at hand shows it as part of COBOL
'60.

STRING (likewise Nucleus Level 2) was (with UNSTRING) introduced in the '74
standard and was included in the '69 version of the JoD.  That doesn't, in
my view, qualify it as a "late addition".

Even reference modification has been around for nearly twenty years now.

    -Chuck Stevens
```
