###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T00:36:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 6 Dec 2004 21:57:15 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>> In practice, that style is being mandated by shop standards.
>
>So which mandated standards hurt the bottom line?

Ones that make enhancements slow and expensive. For instance, file I/O
in a program containing business logic. When the record layout
changes, you have to re-test many programs. I believe files should be
encapsulated in their own callable program; database tables should be
accessed through logical views.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T14:10:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com>`

```

On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >So which mandated standards hurt the bottom line?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> accessed through logical views.

Most mainframe CoBOL shops would first have to accept the lowered standards for
testing that OO shops have.    Right now, if I change a record layout in a
called program, I have to re-test all the programs that call that program.

The reason that people don't do this testing for widely used objects and called
programs is because it costs too much.

I have lived with a system that used a program to handle I/O with a KSDS VSAM
file - that had a bug in it (It passed a "7" back to the called program whenever
the file status started with a zero - including "97".    We lived with this bug
because testing all of the programs that called it was too expensive.    If we
had used the "obsolete" standard that each program did its own I/O, they would
have been fixed long ago.
```

>On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

The condition was self-inflicted. You have a testing standard intended
to make software MORE secure/accurate/reliable. In practice, it makes
software LESS so, as you just demonstrated. Who's the bad guy: the
program bug or the standard that prevents it from being fixed?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T11:46:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102448787.753840.241750@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com> <cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
>> Most mainframe CoBOL shops would first have to accept the lowered
standards for
>> testing that OO shops have.    Right now, if I change a record
layout in a
>> called program, I have to re-test all the programs that call that
program.

> The reason that people don't do this testing for widely used objects
and called
> programs is because it costs too much.

I have seen it advocated that if a class requires a change for only a
limited set of its callers then a new class should be derived with that
change so that most programs could continue to use the old class and
thus need not be retested, only those ones using the new revised class.

This, of course, leads eventually to 'inheritance hell' where there is
a tree of derived classes with entwined branches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:23:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ziotd.454893$nl.175966@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1102448787.753840.241750@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com> <cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1102448787.753840.241750@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>I have seen it advocated that if a class requires a change for only a
>limited set of its callers then a new class should be derived with that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Last comment - not sure about the entwined branches, but the approach of 
inheritance as you outline, is somewhat like designing an annual diary 
then sticking loose bits in the back with a staple. As a short-term it's 
acceptable - obviously better if you can anticipate the condition in the 
first place. But in the real world such an approach is likely to fail.  
Inheritance is a very useful tool - but shouldn't be abused - i.e., 
don't try to artifically create a 'super'  when there is no legitimate 
need. Some of the comp-obj crowd dispute the usefulness  of inheritance, 
and from studying C or C++, our good buddy Ken Foskey from Ozzie, 
(remember him from 2-3 years back),  made somewhat the same point.

Now to the first part -  take a file template class. Appears to cover 
all you need. Then along comes you know who with, 'Only test the first 
"0", particularly with an Open Extend'. It really is no big deal to add 
a method "openExtend" to the file template class - compile to get it 
clean, then test say against two new modules X and Y in your business 
application which need the new method. There's no necessity of 
re-testing existing  programs/classes, say G and H, because their code 
doesn't acknowledge the new method.  If you are introducing Open Extend 
into either G or H - then for starters, you are considering an 
application re-design - naturally they would *then* have to be tested.

Jimmy
```

The much touted savings in reusability is much less than anticipated because
interdependencies that are created can be very expensive.

- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com> <cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cp4dks$s4h$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Most mainframe CoBOL shops would first have to accept the lowered
standards for
> testing that OO shops have.    Right now, if I change a record layout in a
> called program, I have to re-test all the programs that call that program.
>
If the record was PUBLIC within an Object you would need to do the same. But
that is why you make data PRIVATE and return (or PUBLICise) only the
properties that are needed. If you change a record layout that is private
within an Object (and I wouldn't do this anyway, because I don't believe in
maintaining Classes, only extending them...) then why would it need
regression testing? There is no change to the interface.  There is no
"lowered standard" for regression testing when OO is used; rather the object
behaviour remains the same for applications that already invoke it, and
changes ONLY for the applications that want the new behaviour. I have tried
to communicate this here and failed consistently because it is a completely
different concept from standard COBOL.

The proper thing for you to do would be to give us the benefit of the doubt
(in your mind) until you actually have used and truly understand the OO
paradigm. At that point you would see how incorrect your current conclusion
is.


> The reason that people don't do this testing for widely used objects and
called
> programs is because it costs too much.
>

Nope. The reason people don't do it with Objects is because there is no NEED
to. (If there WAS a need, the cost of doing it certainly wouldn't stop it
being done...)

> I have lived with a system that used a program to handle I/O with a KSDS
VSAM
> file - that had a bug in it (It passed a "7" back to the called program
whenever
> the file status started with a zero - including "97".    We lived with
this bug
> because testing all of the programs that called it was too expensive.
If we
> had used the "obsolete" standard that each program did its own I/O, they
would
> have been fixed long ago.
>
And if you used a multi-tier sytem design you would never have had the
problem in the first place because your programs would only see data
buffers. Handling VSAM return codes would be done in the data access layer.

If wishese were horses, beggars would ride.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T20:41:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >> Sections are just disguised perform .. thru.
> >
> >Not necessarily.
> 
> When are they not?

When they are not the subject of a PERFORM.  I have used SECTION
labels in programs and then only PERFORMed the paragraphs without THRU
within those SECTIONs.

In older systems that were originally used with DOS the SECTIONs had
been used to create overlays while pargarphs were PERFORMed.

There is also no reason that a program cannot be written using only
SECTIONs as the labels, with no paragraph labels and thus a PERFORM
SECTION is no more nor less than a PERFORM paragraph may be in another
program - no labels with the scope.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T01:41:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com...
> Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I completely disagree with Robert and partially agree with Richard.

Sections are NOT disguised PERFORM...THRUs whether they are performed or
not.

PERFORM ...THRU delineates an explicit range of paragraphs within the scope
of the refencing perform.

A SECTION that is performed is an IMPLICIT range of paragraphs, within the
scope of the referencing perform.

However, there is a severe difference between the use of PERFORM..THRU and
the use of PERFORM... SECTION.

Without arguing the value (or otherwise) of paragraphs (more importantly,
labels), within SECTIONS, it is certainly true that SECTION can adequately
describe (and document as a descriptive header) a piece of related,
contiguous code, that implements a given function. If you subsequently add
paragraphs or remove paragraphs from it you do NOT have to check every
reference to it as you would if it was PERFORM...THRU.

Richard mentioned the requirement to use SECTION when overlaying and that is
no longer relevant. We have discussed this before and I am fairly persuaded
to Richard's argument that there is no technical need for sections in modern
code. I still use them because they are familiar to me, the benefits I would
derive don't warrant changing (I have NEVER had a program bug caused by
sectioning), and because they appeal to me as part of the language hierarchy
that is being lost sight of.
DIVISION.
     SECTION.
          PARAGRAPH.
                 SENTENCE.
                         STATEMENT.
                                 CLAUSE.

That makes sense to me and always has done.

But then, I read "Zen and the art of motorcylce maintenance". <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T22:44:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net>`

```
On Mon, 6 Dec 2004 01:41:27 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:


>Sections are NOT disguised PERFORM...THRUs whether they are performed or
>not.
>
>PERFORM ...THRU delineates an explicit range of paragraphs within the scope
>of the refencing perform.

The PERFORM ... THRU we're discussing looks like this:

PERFORM some-paragraph
       THRU some-paragraph-exit.

some-paragraph.
    ....
some-paragraph-exit.
    exit.

>A SECTION that is performed is an IMPLICIT range of paragraphs, within the
>scope of the referencing perform.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>labels), within SECTIONS,

What are interior paragraphs used for? They are either GO TO targets
or comments. Assuming the latter, comment lines would be clearer
because they don't give the false impression they're being used for
something else.

> it is certainly true that SECTION can adequately
>describe (and document as a descriptive header) a piece of related,
>contiguous code, that implements a given function.

A flower-boxed comment works better. It's not limited to 30
characters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T19:00:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> The PERFORM ... THRU we're discussing looks like this:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     exit.

This is just another of your 'I meants'.  It may be what you _thought_
you wanted to talk about but it was not what you _said_ at all.

RW >>> Sections are just disguised perform .. thru.

You made no qualification at all that you were only discussing 'the
subset that appeared to fullfill your claim', in fact you didn't even
specify that PERFORM SECTION was what you were referring to.

CS >>Not necessarily.
RW >When are they not?

What _we_ were talking about was those that were 'not neccessarily'
like a PERFORM THRU.  ie we were answering your actual question.

Now it seems that you want to 'selectively' 'pass over' the bits 'that
you don't like' (ie that show your assertions to be wrong).

(Note reuse of your complaint).


It may be that when there is a SECTION with paragraph names then the
equivalent amount of code is executed with either a PERFORM
section-name or PERFORM first-paragraph THRU last-paragraph.

However, it was pointed out that is not necessarily the case that
"SECTIONs are disguised THRU".  You seemed confused.

Just because there are SECTIONs does not mean that there are also
pragraphs. This means that PERFORM section-name is not a THRU at all.

Just because there are SECTIONs with paragraphs does not mean that the
SECTION is being PERFORMed, it may be just a way of collecting
paragraphs into a logical group, or it may have been done for
overlaying, or even because the compiler needed to use them for
segmentation (which sometimes occurs with MF).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T00:28:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com>`

```
>>This is just another of your 'I meants'. It may be what you _thought_
>>you wanted to talk about but it was not what you _said_ at all.

>I assumed we all understood normal practice.

It is 'normal practice' to use things for what one wants to use things
for, not to fit into your pre-conceived ideas about what those may be.

>Just to be sure, I spelled it out, only to be accused of (?
something).

You spelled out one restricted meaning for the generalisation you had
made and then was surprised that other meanings existed.

> I didn't know SECTIONs had any function except target of PERFORM.

Exactly.  There are many things that you don't know.

> Now it seems their main attraction is to serve as comments.

No.  The SECTION may be PERFORMed without there being any paragraphs at
all.

SECTIONs also are a way of creating a logical group, just as FILE
SECTION is, or WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.  In fact they can also be used
to 'overload' because paragraph names need only be unique within a
SECTION.  So one may have:

PERFORM Read-Next OF Product-Processing
PERFORM Read-Next OF Customer-Processing

Not that I would recommend that, but it is a use that SECTIONs could be
put to _without_ this being another way of saying 'THRU'.

> I did lots and lots of Cobol under MS-DOS, without once using
> segmentation. It wasn't suppored by Realia. CALL/CANCEL offered the
> same functionality .. better. It was better because it freed data
> areas as well as procedures.

'Better' is in relation to what was being done.  You always think that
your ideas are 'better' or 'best' _regardless of criteria that others
may need to cater for.  In fact how would you _know_ that it was
'worse' if you never used it ?

Of course not using segmentation is 'better' if your compiler doesn't
do that.  I used MicroFocus on CP/M and other 8bit systems where there
was 64Kb RAM.  CALL/CANCEL was used, but also segmentation was
required.

I have occasionally used segmentation on DOS systems where the total
required program size grew beyond that available, even when much was in
CALL/CANCELed programs.
Of course 386s + XM catered for much more memory, but that was later.
```

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

- **References:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 00:28:24 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>>>This is just another of your 'I meants'. It may be what you _thought_
>>>you wanted to talk about but it was not what you _said_ at all.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>made and then was surprised that other meanings existed.

Everyone here knows that PERFORM ... THRU was and still is used as I
described: PERFORM nnnnn-name THRU nnnnn-name-exit. If you claim it
was used otherwise IN PRACTICE, IN LARGE SHOPS, provide something to
support the assertion.

>> Now it seems their main attraction is to serve as comments.
>
>No.  The SECTION may be PERFORMed without there being any paragraphs at
>all.

Then they're functioning as paragraphs. Deleting the word SECTION
would produce the same result.

>SECTIONs also are a way of creating a logical group, just as FILE
>SECTION is, or WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

In that case, they are being used as comments.

>  In fact they can also be used
>to 'overload' because paragraph names need only be unique within a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>put to _without_ this being another way of saying 'THRU'.

If one insisted on exit paragraphs, they could all be named
SECTION-END.  GO TO SECTION-END would implicitly be qualified with the
section name.

Most people who advocate sections don't understand how to use them.

>> I did lots and lots of Cobol under MS-DOS, without once using
>> segmentation. It wasn't suppored by Realia. CALL/CANCEL offered the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>'worse' if you never used it ?

CALL/CANCEL was not my idea. It's in the language standard.

>Of course not using segmentation is 'better' if your compiler doesn't
>do that.  I used MicroFocus on CP/M and other 8bit systems where there
>was 64Kb RAM.  CALL/CANCEL was used, but also segmentation was
>required.

>I have occasionally used segmentation on DOS systems where the total
>required program size grew beyond that available, even when much was in
>CALL/CANCELed programs.

Segmentation frees memory, same as CANCEL. Whatever you were releasing
with segmentation could have been put in a callable program. Then its
working-storage would have been freed, in addition to procedural code.
Perhaps you couldn't figure out how to pass files between called
programs. That can be done with EXTERNAL, or the system can be
designed so it's not necessary.

>Of course 386s + XM catered for much more memory, but that was later.

I was talking about 8088 PCs with 64-256K.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T10:32:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102444322.949946.192540@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com>`

```
> Everyone here knows that PERFORM ... THRU was and still is used as I
> described: PERFORM nnnnn-name THRU nnnnn-name-exit. If you claim it
> was used otherwise IN PRACTICE, IN LARGE SHOPS, provide something to
> support the assertion.

Now you want to provide further restriction to your previous sweeping
generalisations.  It is now only in 'large shops'.  Certainly it is
used there in that way, but your original claim made no such
distinction about how and where it was used.  In fact you admitted some
ways in which you did not know it could be used.

When I worked on mainframes I did use SECTIONs for overlaying and
certainly did perform the paragraphs within the SECTION.  One reason
for that was that if one used UNTIL as in:

PERFORM Process-File UNTIL EoF

Process-File SECTION 60.
blah blah

Then the overlay was reloaded for each iteration.  So to avoid this:

PERFORM Process-File-Until-EoF

PRocess-File SECTION 60.
Process-File-Until-EoF.
PERFORM Process-File-Record UNTIL EoF
.
Process-File-Record.
blah blah.

While PERFORM SECTION and PERFORM THRU _may_ be used to perform the
same set of paragraphs, that doesn't mean that they are equivalent.
Both require care to ensure that they work correctly but the checks are
different and the faults are different.

Also I don't see the point that you are attempting to make.

> Most people who advocate sections don't understand how to use them.

Now you are just making your usual personal attacks.


> CALL/CANCEL was not my idea. It's in the language standard.

So is segmentation and overlaying.  In fact it was a standard part of
Cobol before CALL/CANCEL was.

> Segmentation frees memory, same as CANCEL. Whatever you were
releasing
> with segmentation could have been put in a callable program.

No. That is not true.  Typically a program goes through several stages
and may, for example be:

Initialise data (open files, set up data items)
Process files (read into arrays)
Print report
shut dows, close files etc.

By overlaying each stage the memory used will only be the largest one
of those.

> Then its working-storage would have been freed, in addition to
procedural code.
> Perhaps you couldn't figure out how to pass files between called
> programs. That can be done with EXTERNAL,

Yes, they are _NOW_ done with EXTERNAL.  That was not a lot of use to
me on the mainframes or on small machines in the late 70s and early 80s
when it was most needed.

> or the system can be designed so it's not necessary.

Is that right ?  You are so omniscient that you can tell how my system
was designed and come up with a 'better' one just by my mentioning that
I had one ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T01:58:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p8mcr0hnsj46rd03c25emfsqdm6ecqm0ru@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <1102444322.949946.192540@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 10:32:02 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Everyone here knows that PERFORM ... THRU was and still is used as I
>> described: PERFORM nnnnn-name THRU nnnnn-name-exit. If you claim it
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>ways in which you did not know it could be used.

I added large shops only to limit examples to realistic usage. Saying
PERFORM A THRU C followed by PERFORM B THRU C followed by PERFORM A is
possible but not realistic.

>Also I don't see the point that you are attempting to make.

I forgot.

>> Most people who advocate sections don't understand how to use them.
>
>Now you are just making your usual personal attacks.

The only real 'feature' of SECTIONs is paragraph qualification,
explicit and automatic. You can tell I hit a nerve, because it pissed
them off.

>  Typically a program goes through several stages
>and may, for example be:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>of those.

That's one way .. called 'temporal cohesion'. You put processes that
occur in close time proximity together. Process or print will be the 
biggest; it doesn't take much code to open a file.

CALL/CANCEL encourages 'functional cohesion', which is better design,
because of its smaller granularity and greater flexibility in managing
memory. For instance, you might put the I/O, including open/close, for
the report file into Print Report. There's no reason to allocate
buffers for the report file in Initialize. Similarly, the source of
data in the array can be released once the array is loaded.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

```
> Everyone here knows that PERFORM ... THRU was and still is used as I
> described: PERFORM nnnnn-name THRU nnnnn-name-exit. If you claim it
> was used otherwise IN PRACTICE, IN LARGE SHOPS, provide something to
> support the assertion.

Now you want to provide further restriction to your previous sweeping
generalisations.  It is now only in 'large shops'.  Certainly it is
used there in that way, but your original claim made no such
distinction about how and where it was used.  In fact you admitted some
ways in which you did not know it could be used.

When I worked on mainframes I did use SECTIONs for overlaying and
certainly did perform the paragraphs within the SECTION.  One reason
for that was that if one used UNTIL as in:

PERFORM Process-File UNTIL EoF

Process-File SECTION 60.
blah blah

Then the overlay was reloaded for each iteration.  So to avoid this:

PERFORM Process-File-Until-EoF

PRocess-File SECTION 60.
Process-File-Until-EoF.
PERFORM Process-File-Record UNTIL EoF
.
Process-File-Record.
blah blah.

While PERFORM SECTION and PERFORM THRU _may_ be used to perform the
same set of paragraphs, that doesn't mean that they are equivalent.
Both require care to ensure that they work correctly but the checks are
different and the faults are different.

Also I don't see the point that you are attempting to make.

> Most people who advocate sections don't understand how to use them.

Now you are just making your usual personal attacks.


> CALL/CANCEL was not my idea. It's in the language standard.

So is segmentation and overlaying.  In fact it was a standard part of
Cobol before CALL/CANCEL was.

> Segmentation frees memory, same as CANCEL. Whatever you were
releasing
> with segmentation could have been put in a callable program.

No. That is not true.  Typically a program goes through several stages
and may, for example be:

Initialise data (open files, set up data items)
Process files (read into arrays)
Print report
shut dows, close files etc.

By overlaying each stage the memory used will only be the largest one
of those.

> Then its working-storage would have been freed, in addition to
procedural code.
> Perhaps you couldn't figure out how to pass files between called
> programs. That can be done with EXTERNAL,

Yes, they are _NOW_ done with EXTERNAL.  That was not a lot of use to
me on the mainframes or on small machines in the late 70s and early 80s
when it was most needed.

> or the system can be designed so it's not necessary.

Is that right ?  You are so omniscient that you can tell how my system
was designed and come up with a 'better' one just by my mentioning that
I had one ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T21:17:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z5ptd.435295$SM5.25518@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <9MRAQo7uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko,
  Have you tried this?

The '85 Standard (and all standards that I know of - past and present) 
explicitly state that paragraph names need NOT be qualified if referring to a 
uniquely named paragraph WITHIN a section (even if the same paragraph name 
appears elsewhere).

I am *NOT* saying this is a "good" technique, just one that the Standard allows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T13:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MVFs4DPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9MRAQo7uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <z5ptd.435295$SM5.25518@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  07.12.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  z5ptd.435295$SM5.25518@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


WMK> The '85 Standard (and all standards that I know of - past and
WMK> present) explicitly state that paragraph names need NOT be qualified
WMK> if referring to a uniquely named paragraph WITHIN a section (even if
WMK> the same paragraph name appears elsewhere).

   Oh yes, you are right, and I was wrong.

   Either I never knew it, or I had forgotten about it (I should try  
to find my comparative study of Univac COBOL compilers done in 1980,  
and the recommendations for programming I made in them; maybe that  
fact is mentioned in there).

   So it is "even" in the 1974 standard, as reflected in the manual  
for the ACOB compiler of ole Sperry Univac, Section 1.3.3.3.8.1  
"Qualification", rule No. 4:

   "A paragraph name need not be qualified when it is being used  
within the same section."

    Under
    1. Introduction
    1.3. Overall Language Considerations
    1.3.3. Language concepts
    1.3.3.3. Concept of Computer Independent Data Description
    1.3.3.3.8 Uniqueness of Reference

    Numbering in the Standard 1974 may differ. IN COBOL-85 "Uniqueness  
of Reference" is Section 4.3.8 (page IV-17 or 163 of 976),  
"Qualification" is section 4.3.8.1 on page IV-18 or 164 of 976. The  
above sentence is in rule 6.

   And this rule makes really sense, since within the scope of  
uniqueness, the label name does not need to be qualified.

WMK> I am *NOT* saying this is a "good" technique, just one that the
WMK> Standard allows.

   Well, I see it as strengthening my case for using SECTIONs as the  
sole object of PERFORM, and paragraphs as labels, only (and  
exceptionally) being the target of GOTO.

   So, by using standard labels in every SECTION, like "BEGIN", or  
"END-SECTION" as target of a GOTO without qualification, can make sure  
that this remains within the confines of that one section.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Eine ganze Milchstraï¿½e von Einfï¿½llen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T08:28:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpkfvb$26r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9MRAQo7uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <z5ptd.435295$SM5.25518@news.easynews.com> <9MVFs4DPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Virtually identical wording, with insignificant variations, appears in the
following reference manuals at hand (in order of increasing age).  None of
the user manuals mark it as an extension to the applicable standard:
    ANSI X3.23-1985
    ANSI X3.23-1974
    Unisys A Series COBOL ANSI-68
    IBM DOS Full American Standard COBOL (this is also ANSI-68)
    Burroughs B6500 COBOL (this is COBOL-60 with some '68 extensions)

Given this, and given that SECTIONs within the PROCEDURE DIVISION seem to
have been a feature of COBOL from its earliest days, I tend to believe this
ability rather predates the 1974 standard.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9MVFs4DPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...

> to find my comparative study of Univac COBOL compilers done in 1980,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

>RW> If one insisted on exit paragraphs, they could all be named
>RW> SECTION-END.  GO TO SECTION-END would implicitly be qualified with
>RW> the section name.
>
>    You will have to qualify it explicitly.

Here is an example:

perform section-1
perform section-2

section-1 section.
    if  ....
        go to ende
    .....
ende.  exit.

section-2 section.
    if   ....
         go to ende
    ....
ende. exit.

The use of PERFORM ABC  THRU XYZ *was* common in pre'85 Standard source code - 
on IBM mainframes - from WITHIN a SORT Input or Output Procedure.  There were 
other ways to do this, but it did exist.

Many (some?  a few? - I don't know) applications that have been "maintained" 
upwardly from that compiler still use that technique.  It is NOT something that 
I "like" or personally would recommend.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:31:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <6mntd.4272434$yk.643601@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Tue, 07 Dec 2004 19:18:26 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
>news:hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>I "like" or personally would recommend.

The Input and Output sections were like two programs packaged into one
source file. There was no more reason to use PERFORM ... THRU in them
than there was in a program without a SORT. The only difference was
that one had to GO TO the section exit when done, rather than STOP
RUN.

For some reason, the Cobol SORT verb got a bad reputation. I've seen
many shop standards that prohibited its use. When asked why, they said
SyncSort (run standalone) is faster. That's an invalid excuse. When
SyncSort is installed, the Cobol sort uses it. 

As with many mainframe standards, there was probably a valid reason
for the prohibition at some time in the distant past. Now everyone has
forgotten why, and follows the 'tradition' out of habit.

Cobol SORT is a good thing. It saves two writes to and two reads from
temporary files. Also, its availability deflates the sails of those
who would use an indexed file to sort data.
```

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:55:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp55d3$c9a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <6mntd.4272434$yk.643601@news.easynews.com> <ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com>`

```

On  7-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> The Input and Output sections were like two programs packaged into one
> source file. There was no more reason to use PERFORM ... THRU in them
> than there was in a program without a SORT. The only difference was
> that one had to GO TO the section exit when done, rather than STOP
> RUN.

I usually had code like this:
MAIN-SECTION
     sort...
SORT-OUTPUT SECTION.
     perform sort-output-paragraph.
GET-RID-OF-THE-DAMN-SECTIONS SECTION.
SORT-OUTPUT-PARAGRAPH.
     ....

> For some reason, the Cobol SORT verb got a bad reputation. I've seen
> many shop standards that prohibited its use. When asked why, they said
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> forgotten why, and follows the 'tradition' out of habit.

One reason I've seen is "We can change the sort fields without modifying the
program".

> Cobol SORT is a good thing. It saves two writes to and two reads from
> temporary files. Also, its availability deflates the sails of those
> who would use an indexed file to sort data.

It can be very efficient.   But sometimes efficiency isn't the #1 goal.   Where
the advantage isn't high in having an internal sort, there is something to be
said in having jobs with small component-like programs.  
Sort/extract/report/sort/extract/report/sort/extract/report.   Restart is
easier, for one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Sort verb getting bad reputation was Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-18T21:45:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<32ld2tF3mc4ttU2@uni-berlin.de>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cp55d3$c9a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <6mntd.4272434$yk.643601@news.easynews.com> <ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com> <cp55d3$c9a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On  7-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

The real reason dates back to sorting in real memory partitions.  I was 
used to 22 - 28K partitions on DOS and 100K - 160K regions on MVT.  This 
meant that you really didn't want to sort a large file using the COBOL 
SORT verb because of the limited amount of memory available to the SORT. 
  This lessened with the 4 - 8 megabyte regions in MVS/SP and still 
further in MVS/XA.  Times change and now the major reasons to have 
separate sorts may well be history and the greater ease of getting a 
change to even complex SORT changes (INCLUDE/OMIT, multifile etc.) into 
production versus changing a program.
>>As with many mainframe standards, there was probably a valid reason
>>for the prohibition at some time in the distant past. Now everyone has
>>forgotten why, and follows the 'tradition' out of habit.
> 

 >> rest snipped
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T14:44:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102459467.190663.75360@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <6mntd.4272434$yk.643601@news.easynews.com> <ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com>`

```
> For some reason, the Cobol SORT verb got a bad reputation.

One missing feature of the Cobol SORT is an 'equal key' procedure.
While records can be added together in the output procedure to produce
a summary file (ie totals) this is only done _after_ all the records
have been sorted.

A proprietry sort that allows access to matched keys in every phase of
the sort can outperform a Cobol SORT by orders of magnitude.

> Also, its availability deflates the sails of those who would use an
indexed file to sort data.

Sorting is such a 'Batch' way of doing things.  I find it much more
useful to use alternate keys so that data is available in the required
way instantly in whatever subsets are necessary.  This data is also
available in a shared manner such that extracts can be done by many at
the same time, primarily for different subsets of the data.

Using SORT is usually restrictive in that work files cannot be shared,
this may be restrictive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 68)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T01:58:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1kcr0l370jcbrvs52g4fdgcj2trhssajp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051900.38197226@posting.google.com> <i209r09ihvqcaf1ha0aaih7tqgqjd108sa@4ax.com> <1102408104.873565.195510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <hphbr058kcq0jdse35rkmoim8d0q8uhton@4ax.com> <6mntd.4272434$yk.643601@news.easynews.com> <ru3cr0hiiabt1t5vsirfji3n09bgp7m57a@4ax.com> <1102459467.190663.75360@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 14:44:27 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> For some reason, the Cobol SORT verb got a bad reputation.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the sort can outperform a Cobol SORT by orders of magnitude.

Proprietary sorts such as SyncSort let you specify summation from
within the Cobol program. Granted it wasn't done with Cobol syntax;
you called the sort tool before issuing the SORT verb.

Unix offers a better 'internal' sort through the use of pipes. You're
not limited to single sort, you can do this:

proga -> sort -> progb -> sort -> progc
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 6 Dec 2004 01:41:27 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >PERFORM ...THRU delineates an explicit range of paragraphs within the
scope
> >of the refencing perform.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, so what exactly is your point?

> >A SECTION that is performed is an IMPLICIT range of paragraphs, within
the
> >scope of the referencing perform.
> >
As you demonstrated above.

> >However, there is a severe difference between the use of PERFORM..THRU
and
> >the use of PERFORM... SECTION.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No Robert, I specifically said I wouldn't argue the use of paragraphs within
sections. So you immediately go and do so. It is a separate issue, it has
been discussed here many times and I'm not commenting on it.

> > it is certainly true that SECTION can adequately
> >describe (and document as a descriptive header) a piece of related,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Neither is a flower boxed comment referenceable from somewhere else in the
program.

Perform calculate-pay
(it is a fair bet to any English speaking programmer that THIS section will
calculate how much someone should be paid. There is no requirement to go and
lookup a flower boxed comment somewhere down the bottom of the program...
that would be the comment that was never updated when the paragraph was
changed... <G>)
....
*=========================
calculate-pay section.
*>
*> This section sets the current pay field in WS to contain what we owe
*> the current employee for this pay period
*>
get-hours-worked.
       yad yada yada
get-current-rate.
       yada yada yada
cp-exit.
       exit.
*> Yes, Robert, the exit is extraneous... at least it is this week. (And I'm
not discussing it further <G>)
*===============================

Later we decide there are more possible rates and the hours processing needs
changing to cater for part hours on the different rates. We add more
paragraphs. No change to the referencing performs in the main program.

Note that if this was an Object Method  (and I wrote it) it would contain
Properties (PUBLIC) for the employee ID and the current pay field. All the
other fields it needed (derived database access keys, foreign keys,
relations, tables, semapores, calc fields, etc.) would be PRIVATE.  It is a
black box where you simply give it the employee you want paid and it returns
the amount to pay him. All of his various rates would be accessed from
databases, along with the hours or part hours for each rate. Tax and
deductions, FICA, pension fund, whatever, would all be found on databases
and manipulated by this one Object Method using rules based processing,
implemented by soft rules, that can be changed outside the object.

The Class that this Method is part of might also include some other methods;
that is beyond the scope of what I'm trying to illustrate here.

I would not expect to ever maintain this code again, once it was written,
and, as the interface to it would never change either (linkage was not used
to pass data in and out), there would be no impact on code that invokes it
if other innate features of it were activated later.

In effect, the whole payroll system would be dependent on proper database
design. The behaviour could only be changed by changing the database. I
believe that is a good thing.

In fact, I am coming to the conclusion that there may be a good case for
designing proper databases and using stored procedures on them, to implement
the system, with virtually NO application code at all. The team I last
managed tried to persuade me in this direction, and they were very clever
people who had spent their whole professional lives working on this kind of
technology for COMPAQ. Guys in their 30s with 15 years experience, all on
Database driven systems <G>. For COBOL this is not a good solution and I
still believe that  embedded SQL should see the DB as purely a repository.
with no application code embedded in the database. But OO solutions may well
use things that COBOL is not good with, and there could be a case for
Extended SQL stored procedures and triggers, in conjunction with transaction
processing, and no COBOL (or ONLY  minimal OO COBOL...)

Pete.
```

>Perform calculate-pay
>(it is a fair bet to any English speaking programmer that THIS section will
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>changed... <G>)

Tax calculation is very often done in third-party code that's out of
your control. Suppose the entry point is named VERTEX-ITX. You might
want to document the call with a flower-boxed comment. 

Suppose your shop has an ancient mainframe naming standard that says
programs must be named ssbbbpnP (just in case we have to go back to
VSE). You think I'm making this up, don't you?

>Note that if this was an Object Method  (and I wrote it) it would contain
>Properties (PUBLIC) for the employee ID and the current pay field. All the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>implemented by soft rules, that can be changed outside the object.

OO is not required to do that. For decades, people have done it with
called programs, remote procedure calls, etc.

>I would not expect to ever maintain this code again, once it was written,
>and, as the interface to it would never change either (linkage was not used
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>believe that is a good thing.

I design large systems the same way. For instance, at aforementioned
supermarket company, a frequent activity was determining the cost of a
product on a given date. I completely re-engineered the tables
containing that information without touching, recompiling or
re-testing the many programs that called for cost. Before the Big
Rewrite, dozens of programs would have needed substantial logic
changes.

>In fact, I am coming to the conclusion that there may be a good case for
>designing proper databases and using stored procedures on them, to implement
>the system, with virtually NO application code at all. 

Stored procedures ARE application code.

>The team I last
>managed tried to persuade me in this direction, and they were very clever
>people who had spent their whole professional lives working on this kind of
>technology for COMPAQ. Guys in their 30s with 15 years experience, all on
>Database driven systems <G>. For COBOL this is not a good solution ..

Sure it is. On Oracle, at least, stored procedures can be written in
any language, including Cobol. The technical term is External
Procedures. They work just like stored procedures, with an entry in a
Library table pointing to a .dll or .so.

>.. and I
>still believe that  embedded SQL should see the DB as purely a repository.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>processing, and no COBOL (or ONLY  minimal OO COBOL...)

No Cobol?! Are you trying to put us out of work? :)

No, people have TRIED to do it with those devices and had varying degrees of

preclude other solutions.

> >I would not expect to ever maintain this code again, once it was written,
> >and, as the interface to it would never change either (linkage was not
used

it

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Good for you. That was probably forward thinking to the people on the site.

> >In fact, I am coming to the conclusion that there may be a good case for
> >designing proper databases and using stored procedures on them, to
implement
> >the system, with virtually NO application code at all.
>
> Stored procedures ARE application code.
>
Actually, I do know that... (been in this game a while now) <G>. I should
have said "non-database resident " application code. I made the mistake of
thinking it  was obvious... Even after absorbing the Doc's discourse on the
subject. Must be getting old...

> >The team I last
> >managed tried to persuade me in this direction, and they were very clever
> >people who had spent their whole professional lives working on this kind
of
> >technology for COMPAQ. Guys in their 30s with 15 years experience, all on
> >Database driven systems <G>. For COBOL this is not a good solution ..
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The difficulty is when you get into extended SQL which has facilities that
are database dependent and not available in COBOL. So you embed this SQL
into COBOL. What for? It is perfectly capable of standing alone on the
database without a COBOL wrapper. I understand you are talking about writing
stored procedures in COBOL and I know this facility has been available for
some years now. But with extended SQL you simply write the procedures in SQL
and there is no requirement for any other host.

> >.. and I
> >still believe that  embedded SQL should see the DB as purely a
repository.
> >with no application code embedded in the database. But OO solutions may
well
> >use things that COBOL is not good with, and there could be a case for
> >Extended SQL stored procedures and triggers, in conjunction with
transaction
> >processing, and no COBOL (or ONLY  minimal OO COBOL...)
>
> No Cobol?! Are you trying to put us out of work? :)
>
No, Robert, the COBOL community has largely done that for itself. I am
simply looking at solutions and trying not to be biased. For years now I
have been advising COBOL people to expand their skill sets.  This is an area
that might be worth investigating. These triggered procedures can be driven
from web based code and scripts as well.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T15:57:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkjbr0lb912fbn6qap0h2c36p19bblmsh6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <31ipg1F3c0tspU1@individual.net> <l029r0990r1d0116d8nihu34cgrbhs9i0b@4ax.com> <31loiiF3d1893U1@individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 8 Dec 2004 02:12:43 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:l029r0990r1d0116d8nihu34cgrbhs9i0b@4ax.com...

>> >black box where you simply give it the employee you want paid and it
>returns
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>preclude other solutions.

I agree that OO lends itself, but people were doing it quite well
before OO. Typically, it was seen in large-scale projects, not demo
programs or simple application systems.

>> Sure it is. On Oracle, at least, stored procedures can be written in
>> any language, including Cobol. The technical term is External
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>are database dependent and not available in COBOL. 

This is FUD. You're not talking to management here. First, the syntax
for calling an External Procedure is exactly the same as calling a
stored procedure. The caller doesn't know or care how it was
implemented. Second, a Cobol program can issue any valid SQL
statement. It is not limited to a subset just because it's Cobol.

>So you embed this SQL
>into COBOL. What for? It is perfectly capable of standing alone on the
>database without a COBOL wrapper. 

Such procedures are normally tested standalone, without a Cobol
caller. One brings up a general-purpose SQL tool such as Toad, Msquery
or SQLPlus to call it and see the result. The procedure doesn't know
or care whether it's being called by Cobol or something else. It could
just as well be called by a Web page, script or another database
procedure such as a trigger.

>I understand you are talking about writing
>stored procedures in COBOL and I know this facility has been available for
>some years now. But with extended SQL you simply write the procedures in SQL
>and there is no requirement for any other host.

One cannot write procedures in ANSI Standard SQL. It's a
non-procedural language. They are usually written in a *proprietary*
language supplied with the database, for example PL/SQL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T10:41:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102444906.757582.6120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bkjbr0lb912fbn6qap0h2c36p19bblmsh6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <31ipg1F3c0tspU1@individual.net> <l029r0990r1d0116d8nihu34cgrbhs9i0b@4ax.com> <31loiiF3d1893U1@individual.net> <bkjbr0lb912fbn6qap0h2c36p19bblmsh6@4ax.com>`

```
> Second, a Cobol program can issue any valid SQL
> statement. It is not limited to a subset just because it's Cobol.

It can do so using the database's direct call library, but if it wants
to use embedded SQL (EXEC SQL) then it is limited to what the
pre-processor allows for.  For example Fujitsu only caters for a
subset.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:36:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bu4cr05gmtjrg2sfop7ip8lvcr2fodcf9c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412042041.7765058e@posting.google.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com> <31ipg1F3c0tspU1@individual.net> <l029r0990r1d0116d8nihu34cgrbhs9i0b@4ax.com> <31loiiF3d1893U1@individual.net> <bkjbr0lb912fbn6qap0h2c36p19bblmsh6@4ax.com> <1102444968.715411.246440@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 10:42:48 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Second, a Cobol program can issue any valid SQL
>> statement. It is not limited to a subset just because it's Cobol.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>subset.

The Fujitsu pre-processor supports PREPARE. Programs using dynamic SQL
can issue any valid SQL command. They're not limited by the
pre-processor.
```

Robert,

I was going to respond to each point below, but the sun is shining here and
I think I'd rather be at the beach.

You seem to see discussion as attack. It isn't always.

I have no problem with you using COBOL procedures if you want to. My point
was that there is no NEED to with modern datbases and modern implementations
of extended SQL.

Oh, and I am perfectly aware of whom I'm talking to, don't need or use FUD,
because I limit my discussions to things I know something about; I find that
tends to make my arguments more cogent. (Works for me...)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T14:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MN0LTX9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net> <g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  05.12.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  g237r0hcilmnrmsqhi94i57up4ov2b9cjp@4ax.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


RW> The PERFORM ... THRU we're discussing looks like this:
RW>
RW> PERFORM some-paragraph
RW>        THRU some-paragraph-exit.
RW>
RW> some-paragraph.
RW>     ....
RW> some-paragraph-exit.
RW>     exit.

   You might change that easily like this:

   PERFORM some-action

   .
    .
    .
    some-action SECTION.
    some-paragraph.
        .....
    some-paragraph-exit.
        EXIT.



    after having checked, that none of the paragraphs between "some- 
paragraph" and "some-paragraph-exit" are mentioned elsewhere in the  
program.

   This little change already removes one uncertainty.

   If "some-paragraph-exit" is not destination of a GOTO, one might as  
well remove it, together with the associated EXIT.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Wir wohnen in Gï¿½ttingen in Scheiterhaufen, die mit Tï¿½ren und Fenstern versehen sind. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **References:** `<bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <9MR5AAQeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <31lp0dF3d1892U1@individual.net>`

.    On  08.12.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  31lp0dF3d1892U1@individual.net
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>>    No, first because I don't like it -- SECTION is the next level of
>> hierarchy below the DIVISION --, and second, because I'm for peacemeal
>> solutions in this regard.


PD> I couldn't grasp the meaning of your last clause, above.

   I'm sorry ... a typographical error of mine ...

PD> You may have meant "piecemeal" and if that is the case,

   I actually meant that. Maybe it was not just a typo, but a wrong  
translation from sound to writing. The pronounciation would be the  
same.


PD> I don't understand what you mean by the SECTION being a "piecemeal
PD> solution".

   A piecemeal solution to transforming such a program.

   Instead of specifying a range of labels as the object of a PERFORM  
with the THRU clause, give the range of labels a SECTION header and  
specify that SECTION name in the PERFORM statement. This could leave  
the labels (paragraph headers) and their internal relationship within  
that SECTION initially untouched.

   Just one piece of a piecemeal solution.

   Was that last sentence now right, syntactically and semantically  
correct?


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Es gibt manche Leute die nicht eher hï¿½ren bis man ihnen die Ohren abschneidet. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`

```
In article <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> I posted a Demo (in Best Practices) that nested programs two deep. I
> don't find it tough, as you imply, but others do. The concept would be
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> within the SCOPE of nested programs, but it isn't.


Just a difference between Pascal-family style function scope and 
C-family style scope.

If your 'simplified' version was the way to nest, you would introduce 
the implicit concept of file-scope.

With the current version, the scope is always explicit and always 
understood.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 54)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T03:06:51+00:00

irrelevant.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T08:15:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cosd9p$ff4$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <3100r05iul1q335c0tah8k2ak45gcpkkas@4ax.com> <copeeu$9et$1@panix5.panix.com> <ocm2r0ttbp4fisd4rqbvpsp5fd7kjg488u@4ax.com>`

```
In article <ocm2r0ttbp4fisd4rqbvpsp5fd7kjg488u@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 3 Dec 2004 05:17:02 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>We're proud to be dumbasses.'

I'd agree with the first bit, that it would be hard to imagine someone 
agreeing with you... but the expression of pride does not necessarily 
follow.  People use the control structures of PERFORM THRU -EXIT, lots of 
cut-n-paste, numbered paragraphs... and the systems return satisfactory 
results for decades and the companies show profits.

That might be cause for a bit of pride, certainly... but the label of 
'dumbass'?  There I am not so sure.

>
>I know they exist because I work with them every day.

It might be wise, Mr Wagner, to be wary about generating Universal 
Conclusions based solely on the perhaps unique set of accidents which 
compose a single, small life.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>results for decades and the companies show profits.

What an insight. Company profit is determined, in part, by programming
style. We could turn around failing companies by rewriting their
software in '70s-style Cobol. 

You should point that out to your company's management, then ask for
an annual bonus tied to net profit.

>>I know they exist because I work with them every day.
>
>It might be wise, Mr Wagner, to be wary about generating Universal 
>Conclusions based solely on the perhaps unique set of accidents which 
>compose a single, small life.

As they say in the Marine Corps: once is an accident; twice, a
coincidence. If it happens three times, it's a pattern.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T14:40:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412041440.6fe9685f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <3100r05iul1q335c0tah8k2ak45gcpkkas@4ax.com> <copeeu$9et$1@panix5.panix.com> <ocm2r0ttbp4fisd4rqbvpsp5fd7kjg488u@4ax.com> <cosd9p$ff4$1@panix5.panix.com> <rbo3r05ptvaphj67lav876k4b99v171ljo@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> What an insight. Company profit is determined, in part, by programming
> style.

Having a conservative approach is risk reduction. Many companies
prefer to reduce risk on internal systems to maintain profitability.
This enables the IT dept allocate their staff to any system knowing
that each can work equally.

There is no need, for example, to contract OAPs to work on older
systems because the new wiz-kids cannot understand them.

> We could turn around failing companies by rewriting their
> software in '70s-style Cobol. 

Now you show how clueless you are.  It isn't the _actual_ style that
is important (to them), it is the consistency, and risk elimination.

> You should point that out to your company's management, then ask for
> an annual bonus tied to net profit.

It seems that you were the one that left a trail of failing companies.

> As they say in the Marine Corps: once is an accident; twice, a
> coincidence. If it happens three times, it's a pattern.

And the pattern _may_ be that only failing companies will hire you.

Or possibly that companies with 30 year old style will hire
programmers who had worked in the '70s, while more progressive
companies will only hire younger staff and contractors, or ones with a
range of language skills, and those that show a hatred of C and other
languages will be dumped.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

Initio, Cognos and similar, not in Cobol.

>> We could turn around failing companies by rewriting their
>> software in '70s-style Cobol. 
>
>Now you show how clueless you are.  It isn't the _actual_ style that

There is greater risk in falling behind the competition, or the
perception that a manager isn't using modern technology.

>> You should point that out to your company's management, then ask for
>> an annual bonus tied to net profit.
>
>It seems that you were the one that left a trail of failing companies.

They might still be in business if only I'd kept PERFORM .. THRU.

>And the pattern _may_ be that only failing companies will hire you.
>Or possibly that companies with 30 year old style will hire
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>languages will be dumped.

I told Sears to keep their old payroll system, written in '60s
assembly lanaguage. They wouldn't listen. Now they're being acquired
by K-Mart!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T18:30:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412051830.13fa7393@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> That's simply untrue. Companies write Web pages in Java and Cold
> Fusion, not legacy languages. They write data warehousing in Ab
> Initio, Cognos and similar, not in Cobol.

Those aren't the core business systems that the profitabilty relies
on.
 
> There is greater risk in falling behind the competition, or the
> perception that a manager isn't using modern technology.

Business systems are _not_ the business, they are a tool to support
the business. The invoice that the company sends out is _not_ the
product.

> They might still be in business if only I'd kept PERFORM .. THRU.

It seems certain that spending a year rewriting the systems to do
exactly the same job (as you claimed that testing merely needed
regression tests) meant that there was a cost in the IT with no
progress, no new facilities.

> I told Sears to keep their old payroll system, written in '60s
> assembly lanaguage. They wouldn't listen. Now they're being acquired
> by K-Mart!

And you think that is related ?
```

```
On 5 Dec 2004 18:30:16 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

>> There is greater risk in falling behind the competition, or the
>> perception that a manager isn't using modern technology.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>product.

It is for some businesses -- data warehousers (e.g. Reuters,
Bloomberg) and computer outsourcing companies. Some say that
businesses shouldn't try to manage computer systems. They should stick
to what they know and outsource computer processing to professionals.

>> They might still be in business if only I'd kept PERFORM .. THRU.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>progress, no new facilities.

In subsequent years, we were able to maintain and enhance with six
programmers rather fifty. The net saving was $5-10M per year. 

I didn't pull fifty out of the air. That's how many similar-sized
competitors used.

>> I told Sears to keep their old payroll system, written in '60s
>> assembly lanaguage. They wouldn't listen. Now they're being acquired
>> by K-Mart!
>
>And you think that is related ?

It follows from the logical principle Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc.
```

>>>I know they exist because I work with them every day.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>coincidence. If it happens three times, it's a pattern.

As they said for centuries before 10 Nov 1775, Mr Wagner, 'There are more 
things in heaven and earth, Horatio, Than are dreamt of in your 
philosophy.'

DD
```

- **References:** `<lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ocm2r0ttbp4fisd4rqbvpsp5fd7kjg488u@4ax.com> <cosd9p$ff4$1@panix5.panix.com> <9MIoK2teflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p1j9r0thvv1dbaehtk4ctot3kppihdv9d7@4ax.com>`

```
>>  According to others, those habits died out at least two decades
>> ago. You dare to contradict them?

> I'll let other mainframers contradict them.

You frequently seem to have severe difficulty distinguishing between
'all' and 'some'.

If some "LW's others" say that the habit died out a couple of decades
ago in their corner, then some "RW others" cannot contradict that
unless they have been to that same exact corner and seen differently.
Even if their corner still does do that, it doesn't mean that it is
universal.

You seem to only think in binary, if it isn't white it must be black.
You not only don't allow for gray, but you also can't imagine
checkerboards or stripes.

To you all mainframe shops are exactly the same, if something is done
in one then it is done in all of them and they are all dumbasses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OPTINIONS challenged (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T18:40:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lwnsd.249990$SM5.14411@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <1mRrd.6098572$6p.972527@news.easynews.com> <9MAhyfZuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <81s2r092o0ku34bpt7n9qpo38bm7v1qo04@4ax.com> <cosdfl$el3$1@panix5.panix.com> <6do3r0pib90npdvgi1qa94pj56uqaaap7c@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:6do3r0pib90npdvgi1qa94pj56uqaaap7c@4ax.com...
> On 4 Dec 2004 08:18:45 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
<snip>
>>The 'real world', Mr Wagner, or what you see of it?
>
> You could say the same about everyone's reportage. The bias here seems
> to be your selectivity in challenging opinions you don't like while
> passing over the ones you do like.

Robert,
   One more time ....

NO ONE objects to your reporting on what you have seen when you identify it as 
such.  (I may say that I have never or not recently seen it - but I don't 
question that you have).

The problem is that you CONSTANTLY generalize to state
 ... "All mainframe shops ...."
        or
 ...  "The current state of mainframe code is ..."

When you EXPLICITLY post that you have seen XYZ (even XYZ in many shops), that's 
fine.

When you INCORRECTLY assume that what you have seen is universal, generally 
occurring, or otherwise present in shops that you have NOT seen, then you are 
wrong and we (many of us) object.

and YES (again), I/we (some of us) DO expect you to qualify your statements 
EVERY TIME - unless it is a "universal truth"

Example,e:
  Universal TRUTH
    - the '85 Standard (and all compilers conforming to it) allow a VALUE clause 
on an item with or subordinate to an OCCURS

 Opinion (something true in many places but not everywhere)
  - All upper-case source code is hard to maintain

Generalization (that needs to be rephrases
 - mainframe programmers always use PERFORM THRU structures

***
Distinguish between facts and opinions - and personal experience and universal 
usage - and ALL of your posts (IN MY OPINION) will receive better reception.
```
