###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T21:06:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412042106.317544f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <1mRrd.6098572$6p.972527@news.easynews.com> <9MAhyfZuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <81s2r092o0ku34bpt7n9qpo38bm7v1qo04@4ax.com> <cosdfl$el3$1@panix5.panix.com> <6do3r0pib90npdvgi1qa94pj56uqaaap7c@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >>Posters seem to have no idea how bad it is in the real world. I'm
> >>composing a new thread that documents it.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> passing over the ones you do like.

No. It seems that everyone _except_ you recognises that their view of
'the real world' is limited and may at times be distorted and
untypical, though it is still a small part of 'the real world'.

In your case you frequently claim that your tiny distorted corner is
the _whole_ 'real world', or at least the only part that is relevant. 
For example you will claim that there is zero deviation in everything
you see and therefore your small sample represents _everything_.

While you are here claiming that others have 'bias', 'selectivity',
and 'pass over your words' because you think that they should be
treating your words as if from some messiah, in fact those are exactly
_your_ faults.

No one, except you, is claiming that there experiences are the only
valid one, but they are simply expressing that their experiences are
not the one you claim to be universal.

No one is saying that your experiences are not true, they are not
'passing over' them, they are pointing out that your experiences are
not universal, even though you claim to have seen nothing else.

For example you claimed 50 out or 50, which was obviously biased by
your hatred of mainframers, was being very selective by 'passing over'
the evidence presented here.

You have grungingly backed off now to 45/50, but still run around
claiming that others must be selectively ignoring what you see.

Perhaps people think that you are like Chicken Little.
```

>No. I don't think that anyone, except you, thinks you are like that
>child.  Maybe some see you as Don Quixote.

I see myself doing Programming, not the mud slogging that passes for
programming in too many Cobol shops. What's remarkable is the
hostility it evokes. That wouldn't happen in any other language. Cobol
programmers feel threatened by other programming cultures. Their
response is to circle the wagons (US) or retreat to a Fortress (tm
PECD) to fight off the 'invaders'.

Eric Hoffer wrote, "In a time of drastic change it is the learners who
inherit the future. The learned usually find themselves equipped to
live in a world that no longer exists."
```

advertising: "WagnerWorld - How the real world SHOULD have been... Visit
WagnerWorld and get real insight about what IBM mainframe sites do...
(Discount for Java and C++ programmers with families...free calculator
programmed with RobertThink provided to statisticians.... ) Don't miss it!
Visit WagnerWorld now!"

Go Robert... <G>!

Pete.
```

```
Still doesn't make sense when you say you saw *CODE WALK THRUS" in 20 shops in 
the last 6 years.

Remember - that WAS what the discussion was about when you started with your "20 
out of 20" or "50 out of 50"

None of the things that you say "exposed" you to mainframe shops would require 
"walk thrus" - (with comments about numbering paragraphs, all upper-case, 
PERFORM ... THRU". If you think one of the following would require a "code walk 
thru" - please explain:

> As I said in another reply, it is not necessary to be ON a mainframe
> to see mainframe code.

> Some was code developed in other shops and imported after acquisition.

> Some was purchased from third-parties.

. Some were distant shops to
> which I had access. All were within the last six years.

Finally, in your other note you state:

>I said I hadn't worked ON IBM mainframe since 2000. All my work in the
> last six years has been AT mainframe shops.

Which "mainframe"?

For references (sorry for long URL's) to your various statements, you have said:

http://www.google.com/groups?q=worked+mainframe+years+group:comp.lang.cobol+author:wagner&hl=en&lr=&as_drrb=b&as_mind=12&as_minm=5&as_miny=1981&as_maxd=2&as_maxm=12&as_maxy=2003&selm=3e463270.51372086%40news.optonline.net&rnum=1

"Forty years isn't enough? I managed a VSE shop for ten years, where I did heavy
replacement of CICS internals. The 4361, which ran at 1 MIPS, supplied  more
than 100 terminals with .9 second response time, with a standard deviation of
less than .5 sec. You don't get that kinda performance with dead-stock IBM
software.

I've also worked on Burroughs, Honeywell (before GE), RCA and CII-Bull mainframe
platforms, as well as Big Iron MVS. "

From:

http://www.google.com/groups?q=worked+mainframe+years+20+group:comp.lang.cobol+author:wagner&hl=en&lr=&selm=tboqp09nq9rne2ae6ps0f75cinc5kn7mh1%404ax.com&rnum=2

"I worked in modern, progressive sites for 20 years. They weren't so
when I arrived;  they were when I left. I'd love to breathe life into
another medium-sized site."

  AND MOST IMPORTANTLY - your original statement (with no mention of MVS or IBM)

From:

http://www.google.com/groups?q=Y2K+mainframe+group:comp.lang.cobol+author:wagner&hl=en&lr=&selm=40af4e69.121064597%40news.optonline.net&rnum=1

"I'm used to running single batch streams on Unix. Excepting two years during
Y2K, I haven't touched a mainframe in 20 years."
```

```

On  2-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >  Please clarify how you have "seen more than 20" shops where you know
> >  CURRENT
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to see mainframe code.

It isn't uncommon for mainframe shops to have 20 year old code.    I wouldn't
expect that seeing such would indicate what current procedures are.

People who write disposable code don't understand why others have code that
doesn't match new styles.   Just throw away the old code and replace it with
new.    There is something to be said for this attitude - my daughter's TV went
bad and she found it would be as cheap to replace it as to fix it.    But this
isn't ALWAYS the best solution.    Sometimes it is better to keep what's working
going rather than to replace it with something which doesn't have the same
reliability yet.    So mainframe shops have old code in old programs.
```

In article <dcknq0l6j596bg7j4kbpp04p2vildl4l57@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:

> wrote:

How is it circular?  The only argument of mine you quoted above is
the claim that there is no other justification than laziness for
implicit qualification.  It applies equally well if there were no
languages that require explicit qualification.

> >> The Cobol style encourages programmers to describe structural
> >> relationships. Required explicit qualification discourages it.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> verbose.' Their own words indicate a dislike for typing. 

Their error (if it exists) does not excuse yours.

> >Hardly.  You haven't shown that your sample is representative, or
> >that multiple levels of qualification are justified by the structure
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to demonstrate it, as is common in Cobol tutorials.

Many things are easy; that does not compel tutorial authors to
demonstrate them.  It's easy in C to convert between a wide-character
and a multibyte-character representation, as those are defined by the
implementation, but I've never seen a tutorial touch on it.

And you still haven't provided any evidence to show that your
purported survey of tutorials is representative.

> >Here's an equally questionable survey: I just looked at the 61 C
> >headers in one project tree.  ...
> 
> I suspect your samples were written by systems types rather than
> applications programmers. 

I imagine they were.  Most of them were written by me, in fact.  I
don't see how this bolsters your argument, however.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 43)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T16:47:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp9vjg01i1m@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <cofp5p02h9@news1.newsguy.com> <dcknq0l6j596bg7j4kbpp04p2vildl4l57@4ax.com> <col2pm02734@news4.newsguy.com> <a4kvq0d2h6dinbmiu2kaqtihng9aa95cf1@4ax.com>`

```

In article <a4kvq0d2h6dinbmiu2kaqtihng9aa95cf1@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> 
> Ok. In Cobol, one can add a level to the structure without being
> required to change procedural code. In block languages, every
> reference to subordinate elements must be changed. 

Generally true; some of the code in question may be operating on,
say, a pointer to the inner structure, and so wouldn't need to be
changed.  I think that's an advantage of requiring explicit
qualification.  If the data structure visible to the code has
changed, the code should change; that prevents skew between the
data definition and the code that operates on it.

If the code doesn't need to know the definition of the outer
structure, then it should only be operating on the inner structure,
through a pointer to it, or through functions that manipulate it.
When data irrelevant to a section of code is visible to it, that
creates excessive data coupling, which is undesirable, as it
increases fragility.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T00:17:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c50qq05rpmn08pdkof1tt94bpkogm23vn6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com> <c9c848692bec1effd33f424591359187@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>`

```
On Tue, 30 Nov 2004 11:11:37 -0500, "Uphios"
<kgverrell@btinternet.com> wrote:

>Serious answer?? Well it will vary by the machine you are running on

Let's say you're running on a z/990.

>I also seem to
>[recall] that the maths process on the IBM was most effectively carried out in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the brackets.

Thank you for confirming my thesis that pre-optimization is nearly
always counter-productive.  AP (Add Packed) runs about 200 times
slower than AR (Add Register) on modern IBM mainframes. 

As for saving money on memory, 1G of DDR costs $192. By saving one
byte on small numbers, you've saved the client .000000192. If your
hourly rate is $12 (.0033/sec) and you spent more than .017 sec
pondering, the client would have been better off without your
services.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<col708$9ev$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXUmMNeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30m2m3F2vmrnvU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <gnmcq0hrsu1l0802cs2ksas5hlf926781r@4ax.com> <cofe92$inq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <30knq0d8jhipd5p5ukeac33kb90ld0q320@4ax.com> <coi388$63l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <os2qq0p36u9p3detd0ra50vgv6povqtomq@4ax.com>`

```

On 30-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >Part of the initial design should be concerned with efficiency.   How you
> >access
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> priesthood.

Being more efficient might keep our jobs, but priesthood implies we have a
congregation following us.   At any rate, go on pretending I'm a priest, and
coding efficient code helps me keep my work competitive.   How is this
counter-productive?

But I guess I need to ask who efficiency is counter-productive for?   For the
mainframers?   For our companies?   For people who want customers to wait around
while their XML pages load?   For those who want their data mining results
today?

> Modern databases are more efficient than a hand-tuned system. Why?
> Because they parallellism.

Big modern databases are tuned constantly.   Why?   Because that's where the
best bang for the buck comes for tuning.   Parallel processing certainly works
better with tuning.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f9vq09hetk4mkfl1f7nh3oc38ii10vhdo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXUmMNeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30m2m3F2vmrnvU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <gnmcq0hrsu1l0802cs2ksas5hlf926781r@4ax.com> <cofe92$inq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <30knq0d8jhipd5p5ukeac33kb90ld0q320@4ax.com> <coi388$63l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <os2qq0p36u9p3detd0ra50vgv6povqtomq@4ax.com> <col708$9ev$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Wed, 1 Dec 2004 19:45:12 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 30-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>counter-productive?

It is inefficient because it does not obey the Pareto Principle (aka
80/20), which states that only a vital few factors are responsible for
producing most of the problems. Lacking empirical data, designers
optimize activities that *seem* valuable or complicated from a human
perspective. They would do better to build a rough-cut prototype and
let database tools tell them where the problems really are.

>But I guess I need to ask who efficiency is counter-productive for?   For the
>mainframers?   For our companies?   For people who want customers to wait around
>while their XML pages load?   For those who want their data mining results
>today?

All of the above. An day wasted optimizing the wrong thing is a lost
opportunity to speed up user access.

>> Modern databases are more efficient than a hand-tuned system. Why?
>> Because they [use]  parallellism.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>better with tuning.

Databases have multiple layers. Historically, DBAs have directed most
of their effort toward layers that were close to and accessible to
them, server hardware and configuration, and given insufficient
attention to layers offering the best return on time investment -- 
business logic and applications. But again, optimizing applications
requires data, not a wet finger in the wind.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

```
Walter Murray wrote:
> 
> This was in response to my suggested solution to the question of how to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> common COBOL idiom, it should thereafter be known as "Murray's device."  :-)

For what it's worth, I think that's the best solution.  I'm really, 
really surprised there has been so much vociferous opposition to it. 
(It's not like you said that the world didn't evolve or something...  ;> 
  )  It's two statements, quite simple, and a simple comment line would 
elucidate even the most hurried of 2-year-experienced-kids-at-2-am...

*>  This detects truncation
     move A to B
     if A not = B
        ....

It works for all types of fields that may not be compatible.  Thanks for 
sharing it, and know that at least one reader here plans to use it if 
the need occurs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T21:56:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lAasd.15$Yq6.8@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0412022336.50f7e503@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1> <%SOrd.4375$A26.186@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0412022336.50f7e503@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote 
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> For example if A is S9(8)V999 and B is S9(8)V99.

True - but if A = B after the move, the thousandths place dropped was a 
zero, and would have no impact.  If you turned around and used B in a 
compute statement, and it was the most precise item, you might drop a 
decimal point.  But, the 2 lines of code aren't designed to error-proof 
an entire program, just to provide a way of knowing if truncation has 
changed the value of the receiving variable.
```

## Thread: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T05:35:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com>`

```
Just because I haven't seen it mentioned yet, ...

Today, IBM announced the "long expected" CICS TS V3.1,

IBM CICS Transaction Server for z/OS V3.1 offers improved integration, 
application transformation, and enterprise
management (118.2KB)
    http://www.ibm.com/isource/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=204-285

Of particular interest to those involved  with IBM (in this environment) and 
COBOL, is the (*TOTALLY* expected) paragraph:

"OS/VS COBOL:
  As indicated in previous announcements, most recently in the announcement 
of CICS TS V2.3, CICS run-time support for OS/VS COBOL (5740-CB1, 5734-CB4, 
and 5740-LM1) is not provided in CICS TS V3.1. (Refer to the Application 
programming summary section)."

If you have ANY "OS/VS COBOL" still running in your shop (and even if you do 
NOT use CICS, you want to read the "writing on the wall"), I suggest you 
make certain your management know and understand this.

NOTE WELL:
  This is NOT simply a change in "service" type support.  As OS/VS COBOL was 
incapable of producing "truly RENT" code, CICS had software support to 
enable "serially reusable OS/VS COBOL" object code or work in a CICS region. 
This support is NOW REMOVED. and OS/VS COBOL object code simply will NOT RUN 
in a CICS TS V3.1 region (regardless of run-time or anything else).
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T22:35:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com>`

```
Interesting, Bill.

So the days of prioritised timeslicing and serial re-usability are gone
under the new CICS?

COBOL code will now be truly re-entrant, with registers and state being
swapped on every task interrupt, and tasks interruptible at  (almost) any
point?

Wow! and it only took thirty years... :-)

It sems a pity that the new version cannot be backward compatible. After
all, it is a superset of what was there before. You'd think it could easily
include task/state saving at dequeue boundaries, and the 'progress
indicator' used to decide where to 're-enter' and stored in the terminal
dependent buffer (DFHCOMMAREA for CICS, SPA for IMS/DC, or whatever they
call it now...) if memory serves me correctly...or is that all long gone?)
has always been an application responsibility, so it shouldn't impact the
OS. I wouldn't mind betting that programs written for the old environment,
recompiled in the new environment, will run perfectly. Please advise if I am
wrong.

I guess I'm interested to know what specific feature(s) of the new software
precludes compatibility, or is it just.....marketing? (spits through fingers
and turns three widdershins to the East...)

Pete.

announcement

5734-CB4,
> and 5740-LM1) is not provided in CICS TS V3.1. (Refer to the Application

>

do

was

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T09:42:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C136B2.09425501122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com> <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net>,
 "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Interesting, Bill.
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

I'll take a WAG at this.  

With dynamic AORs and CICSPlex, each execution of a transaction can 
occur on a different region -- load modules need to be loaded in both, 
and be unalterable or that does not work.  If you were to allow serial 
reusability, then you would have a region affinity until task completion 
because of the state of the load module.  And given the 
psuedo-conversational nature that CICS sometimes uses, how does it 
exactly know what 'task completion' is?

With truly RENT modules, CICS must only share the state of each instance 
of a program over various region sets instead of sharing instance state 
AND load modules.

Just a WAG...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T16:58:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kLmrd.6018817$6p.961093@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com> <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net>`

```
IBM has had COBOL compilers that could create RENT code since VS COBOL II, 
R1 - introduced in ''84 (or so).  Since then they have been trying to get 
their users OFF of the OS/VS COBOL compiler (which only produced 24, not 
31-bit object code and had lots of other problems).  "Upward compatibility 
been continued for (about) 20 years - and with LOTS of warning, they have 
finally dropped support in this release of CICS TS (and in some cases for 
DB2 V8).   OS/VS COBOL was their (last) '68/'74 compiler.  They are STILL 
providing upward compatibility (for CICS TS) for their 20 year old VS COBOL 
II code.

I think that Joe has provided a better answer than I could on some of the 
recent (and not so recent) improvements in CICS.

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net...
> Interesting, Bill.
>
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T12:36:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Thanks Bill.

No-one has confirmed or denied whether old CICS apps can still run if
recompiled or not.

I understand the need to move on for IBM and I understand the implications
of  RENT as a compiler option.

I'm just wondering HOW the new CICS is different and I don't know whether
Joe's WAG is speculation or informed opinion, becuase I don't know what a
WAG is...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T03:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tewrd.3949724$yk.602222@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com> <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net> <kLmrd.6018817$6p.961093@news.easynews.com> <3172t5F385ingU1@individual.net>`

```
Old applications that do NOT use syntax that is no longer supported, can 
certainly be recompiled and run under the new CICS TS.  The problem is that 
there were TONS of "undocumented extensions" not to mention '68 and '74 Standard 
"stuff" that is no longer supported in the current compilers (and hasn't been in 
any compiler that IBM produced since 1984 !!!).

This is why IBM has been giving so much warning that sites HAD to upgrade to a 
"currently supported compiler".  HOWEVER, there is also no "drop" date for the 
earlier release of CICS TS (that I know of), so shops can STILL keep using CICS 
TS V2 and using OS/VS COBOL applications.  (They must use the LE run-time to be 
supported).

As far as enhancements in the latest CICS, check out the announcement in my 
original note and it describes what is "new and improved".

Does this answer your question (to me)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T07:57:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wVzrd.1015035$Gx4.41093@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3172t5F385ingU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com> <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net> <kLmrd.6018817$6p.961093@news.easynews.com> <3172t5F385ingU1@individual.net>`

```


Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Thanks Bill.
> 
> No-one has confirmed or denied whether old CICS apps can still run if
> recompiled or not.

My shop converted from CICS/Transaction Server 1.3 to 2.2 about a year 
ago.  We have no plans yet to upgrade to CICS/TS 3.1, but we generally 
stay current with supported releases.

I've been programming with CICS since 1988, and I would guess that old 
OS/VS COBOL CICS application programs could be recompiled for TS 3.1 
without too much trouble.  The biggest problem would be COBOL syntax 
changes, converting EXAMINE to INSPECT, for example.

My guess is that it's a lot easier than converting CICS macro-level to 
CICS command-level, which we had to do before we upgraded from CICS/VS 
2.1.2 to CICS/VS 3.3 around 1994.  We shut down our last CICS 
applications that used macro-level code in 1992, but those apps ran on 
a very back-leveled CICS/VS 1.6.1.

> 
> I understand the need to move on for IBM and I understand the implications
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

WAG = Wild-Ass Guess, as you probably know by now.

 From my point of view, upgrading from CICS/TS 2.2 to TS 3.1 is 
probably no big deal, but then we have long since recompiled all our 
CICS programs to at least an LE-enabled COBOL compiler.  Unless we've 
missed something, we don't even run any CICS COBOL programs that were 
last compiled with VS/COBOL II, much less OS/VS COBOL.

There was a limitation on maximum COMMAREA size of 32K passed on an 
EXEC CICS LINK command, which I think can be replaced by a much larger 
"container" in TS 3.1.  I can think of some interesting use we might 
make of that.

If your CICS experience is 20 years old, then you have missed out on 
DB2 in CICS, VSAM data tables, improved inter-region and inter-system 
communications, Java support, html support, threadsafe programming 
with multiple TCB's, and running programs above the line (full 31-bit 
addressing support instead of 24-bit limitations).

With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM, CICS, COBOL, et al

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T22:50:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3186rnF37npnnU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<lKcrd.48693$SM5.1825@news.easynews.com> <315hjoF37mc0vU1@individual.net> <kLmrd.6018817$6p.961093@news.easynews.com> <3172t5F385ingU1@individual.net> <wVzrd.1015035$Gx4.41093@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:wVzrd.1015035$Gx4.41093@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I understand the need to move on for IBM and I understand the
implications
> > of  RENT as a compiler option.
> >
> > I'm just wondering HOW the new CICS is different and I don't know
whether
> > Joe's WAG is speculation or informed opinion, becuase I don't know what
a
> > WAG is...
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> addressing support instead of 24-bit limitations).

Thanks for that, Arnold. Very informative (and interesting.)

It is certainly now 20 years since I worked on IBM mainframes. However, I
have worked with DB2 since it became available, and attended the second
course on it that was run by IBM UK, I think it was 1983. I have worked with
all forms of VSAM (KSDS, ESDS,  RRDS and RBDS as a subset of ESDS) and most
of the other IBM access methods (including ISAM and QSAM and the
teleprocessing ones (BTAM, QTAM, and VTAM), since the days of system 360
(1965 for us in NZ, although some other NZ sites had it earlier. We ran in
1401 compatibility mode for the first 3 months... <G>)). Both DB2 and IMS
used VSAM as the underlying access method and during a relatively short
stint as a DBA I learned something about this. Improved Inter Region/System
communication doesn't ring a bell so I certainly missed that. Same with
threadsafe programming using multiple TCBs (sounds fascinating <G>). Running
above the line I certainly received training on (I  hazily recall something
about XA...extended architecture? It all made perfect sense because for
years before that we had been swapping code in and out with overlaying
(until we learned the wisdom of writing small modules for on-line
environments).

I always had a soft spot for Amdahl's revolutionary architecture (even when
it got trashed in benchmarks against the ICL 1900 <G>)

I never really LIKED CICS. Probably because I worked on IMS/DC first and
later with SHADOW and TASKMASTER which were superb teleprocessing monitors.
There seemed to me to be something 'kludgy' about using a four character
code to start transactions...<G>

But, it looks as if I missed some good stuff in the CICS world and it has
certainly stood the test of time.

Thanks for the useful response,

Pete.
```

## Thread: Is COBOL being widely used? (Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE) — 4 msgs

### Is COBOL being widely used? (Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE)

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T08:15:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9M2Fytk9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
.    On  28.11.04
  wrote  nospam@bisusa.com (JerryMouse)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  E7GdnZY65MStyjfcRVn-qg@giganews.com
  about  Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE


n> Lueko Willms wrote:
>>
>>   COBOL isn't in wide use compared to C, C++, Java or even Delphi,...

n> That's your position and I assume you're sticking to it?

   Yes, although there are certainly no precise statistics. And I am  
thinking about active development or even maintenance, not programs  
written in COBOL which are still in use.

   A friend of mine has a bookshop here in Frankfurt, specialized in  
computer, data processing and some electronics, who told me that he  
might sell 1 (one) COBOL book per month, but several more on Java and  
related stuff per day.

   In job offers, knowledge of COBOL is rarely asked for, but Java  
much more, and C++, too.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er trieb einen kleinen Finsternis-Handel. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL being widely used? (Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE)

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T10:42:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412011042.8c7d09b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9M2Fytk9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>    Yes, although there are certainly no precise statistics. And I am  
> thinking about active development or even maintenance, not programs  
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> related stuff per day.

Books are primarily bought by people who _don't_know_ the language (or
the topic of the book) and want to learn it so that they _might_ get a
job.  You are basing your estimates of how many programmers there are
on the numbers _who_don't_know_ the language.
 
>    In job offers, knowledge of COBOL is rarely asked for, but Java  
> much more, and C++, too.

A job offer relates to an empty desk in an office.  You are basing the
numbers of programmers there are by counting the empty desks - ie the
number of programmers that aren't there.

In fact one may conclude that the people buying books are doing so in
the hope that it will enable them to get one of the jobs being
advertised.  If they do so then it may be discovered that all they
know is what they remember from reading the book and that job winds up
being re-advertised.

The fact is that there is no shortage of Cobol programmers, thus few
jobs get to be advertised.  This may be beacause it is a declining
market.  Java is certainly an increasing market and there is a
shortage.

The actual total numbers involved in each group cannot be determined
from the number of books sold or the shortfall.

For example, as related on 'Top Gear' by Jerremy Clarkson, there is a
queue of 2,500 wanting the new Ford GT40 (he is one of them).  I am
sure that more books on this car are sold than that of most other
cars.  Your argument would conclude that there are more GT40s than any
other car on the roads.
```

r> For example, as related on 'Top Gear' by Jerremy Clarkson, there is a

    There is a certain parallell, COBOL compilers are darn expensive,  
as this new edition of the Ford GT40 is, and the GT40 -- which you  
will know, if you have seen the show -- consumes a giant amount of  
fuel, and is not fit to be parked in public, because one could not  
open the doors of it when other cars are parked on its side.

    But besides that, when you want to use the number of books on a  
automobile model sold, compared to the number of books sold on a given  
computer programming language, than you should not count those coffe  
table books, those books with nice color photos, produced for fans,  
who will never buy such a car, but the number of _maintenance_ manuals  
sold. There will be hardly one "How to repair a Ford GT40 second  
edition" be sold, and that is really in relationship to the number of  
prospective owners of such a useless car.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL being widely used? (Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE)

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T13:19:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412021319.3a081345@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9M2Fytk9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0412011042.8c7d09b@posting.google.com> <9M2LvWruflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>    By the way, my bookshop-friend said that he sells Java books in a  
> 100:1 ratio to COBOL.

That does not surprise me.  The bookshops that I go into currently
have no books on Cobol, though they have dozens on Java.  This means
that if I wanted to buy Cobol books I could not do so.  Also of
course, Java is changing.  Those that bought 'Java' last year now need
to upgrade and buy 'Java 2' now and will need to buy 'Java 5' next
year when those new editions come out.

Java is also much wider ranging: J2EE, JSP, JDBC, AWT, Swing, Java3D,
... are all topics requiring there own treatment, and each has several
competing titles.

Cobol, on the other hand, is more stable.  I would buy a book on the
'02 standard and on definitive OO, but they aren't in the bookshop
(yet?).  For most compilers the documentation provided is adequate, I
have several complete sets of printed manuals, even for Fujitsu 3 and
4. I do have over a dozen Cobol books that are not vendor produced
collected over 30 years, but I already have more than that of Java and
_no_ vendor printed publications.

If I added up all the books in vendor sets that I have the total for
Cobol would be close to 100.

>     But besides that, when you want to use the number of books on a  
> automobile model sold, compared to the number of books sold on a given  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> sold. 

Actually there are many motor books, such as many that I have, that
are neither 'coffee table fan boy books' nor maintenance manuals, they
are books to be read.

> There will be hardly one "How to repair a Ford GT40 second  
> edition" be sold, and that is really in relationship to the number of  
> prospective owners of such a useless car.

The car is not 'use-less' though its uses do not include 'going to the
mall'. As I don't 'go to the mall' that is not a problem for me,
though I don't think that I will get one anyway (and nor does my bank
manager).
```

## Thread: Cobol2Java 2.0 --- COBOL to Java Source Code Translator — 10 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

_Thread started [2004-11](2004-11.md#t-84161a6a2d)._

#### ↳ Re: Cobol2Java 2.0 --- COBOL to Java Source Code Translator

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T09:09:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9M2Fzp4uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6f6dnYpmX8wi8jbcRVn-gw@rogers.com>`

```
.    On  29.11.04
  wrote  tli@corporola.com (Thomas A. Li)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  6f6dnYpmX8wi8jbcRVn-gw@rogers.com
  about  Cobol2Java 2.0 --- COBOL to Java  Source Code Translator


TAL> Corporola Inc. has released Cobol2Java 2.0 with
TAL> Statement-To-Statement translation technology (patent pending) which
TAL> translates one COBOL statement into one and exactly one Java
TAL> statement in source code.

   I have looked at the example PERFORM-THROUGH-GOTO-SAMPLE.

   Carrying over the bad programming of the COBOL program one-to-one  
into a Java program doesn't seem to me to be a good idea.

   And the COBOL program is easier to read...

   I think it would be easier to first change the COBOL program to a  
properly structured one, and then, maybe, maybe, converting it into  
Java, or to rewrite the program in Java from scratch.

   This resulting Java program, so it seems, would be more difficult  
to change into a proper structured one then its original in COBOL.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Der Mann machte sehr viel Wind. B: O nein! Wenn es noch Wind gewesen wï¿½re, es war aber mehr ein wehendes Vacuum. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

d> Easier for *whom*, Mr Willms?

   For me, obviously, for you certainly, and, as my educated guess,  
even for Java programmers who don't know anything about COBOL.

   Using GOTO and PERFORM-THRU is bad enough, but implementing these  
as Java procedures or objects is really strange.

The good news is that there refactoring tools for object-oriented code.

Statement-to-statement is for manual modification. It's not the best
solution, but it is the easiest to understand and control solution. You can
make bad programming better only after we understand it.

The properly structured one is deferent from person to person, how to
implement it in a program?

If anybody can define what a good program is in BNF format, I will implement
it.

Thomas Li

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T12:02:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9M2F-xBPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <9LivcFWuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <oStqd.5877514$6p.944695@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  29.11.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  oStqd.5877514$6p.944695@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE


WMK> In fact, a "widely known" implementation did fractional
WMK> exponentiation by FIRST truncating to a decimal point and then
WMK> calculating the results, e.g.

   which leaves the user with two choices: ask a correction of the  
error and wait or kick the compiler out right away

WMK> That compiler was conforming and even passed the NIST conformance
WMK> tests.

   Does that say more about COBOL as such or about the conformance  
tests?

WMK> Of course (eventually) they fixed their results.

   Ah, you see?


WMK> As far as "more real world" cases go,  there are a number of
WMK> implementation with an EXTENSION of "NOTRUNC" where binary item can
WMK> be larger than the PIC would allow - but that fit in the same
WMK> storage.  I know of at least two different ways in which
WMK>
WMK> Compute Display-Numeric = True-Binary
WMK>     On Size Error
WMK>
WMK> and
WMK>
WMK> Compute True-Binary = Display-Numeric
WMK>     On Size Error
WMK>
WMK> are implemented - and in some cases the "ON SIZE ERROR indicates
WMK> that the value won't fit into the receiving field and in other cases
WMK> where it won't fit into a data item that matches the PICTURE.

   As you said, that depends on that option to check the functioning  
of the SIZE ERROR exception based on the PICture clause or on the  
actual storage location.

   I assume the programmer did know what he or she was doing when  
activating that option, right?


WMK> BOTTOM-LINE:
WMK>     COMPUTE results are NOT portable or predictable in the '85
WMK> Standard and are ONLY predictable in the '02 Standard with
WMK> ARITHMETIC is STANDARD (and for some - not all - cases)

   A compiler which can't evaluate arithmetic expressions properly, be  
it in value assignments or conditions, is of no use. Such a compiler  
should either be corrected urgently by the compiler's developer, or  
dumped.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er liebte hauptsï¿½chlich die Wï¿½rter, die nicht in Wï¿½rterbï¿½chern vorzukommen pflegen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

He was also known as 'Bluff King Hal'  (I suspect he was a mean poker player
<G>) and was the first monarch to introduce the term 'Majesty' as in 'Your
Majesty...'.  Prior to that Kings and queens were addressed as 'Your Grace'.

Henry was nothing if not colourful, and there is much controversy to this
day surrounding his life and times. Indications (from contemporary accounts,
letters and other writings) are that most of the population at the time
considered him a 'Merry Monarch' and a 'Good King', (it is possible, of
course, that nobody would say otherwise for fear of incurring his wrath...),
although I'm sure his popularity must have waxed and waned throughout his
reign, because some of what he did (at least when measured by modern
standards) was unconscionable.

He gave us Elizabeth and, with her a new age of reason and enlightenment. I
guess we should be glad about that, if nothing else.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **Date:** 2004-12-03T16:34:37-08:00

> <G>) and was the first monarch to introduce the term 'Majesty' as in 'Your
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Was it the monarch or the time that gave us reason and enlightenment?
The cosmological anthropic principle would lead us to believe that the
universe is at it is because we could not comprehend it otherwise and,
because we can envisage god then must must exist. So did the monarch
make for the times or did the times make the monarch? I suspect the
people made for the times and the monarch could have had a minor
influence.

I should point out that after three pints of French lager I have had
the greatest trouble coming up with the above which may need to be
read under the affluence of incihol.
```

"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:d487f04c.0412031634.536f93ea@posting.google.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3174f6F37qkeuU1@individual.net>...
> > He was also known as 'Bluff King Hal'  (I suspect he was a mean poker
player
> > <G>) and was the first monarch to introduce the term 'Majesty' as in
'Your
> > Majesty...'.  Prior to that Kings and queens were addressed as 'Your
Grace'.
> >
> > Henry was nothing if not colourful, and there is much controversy to
this
> > day surrounding his life and times. Indications (from contemporary
accounts,
> > letters and other writings) are that most of the population at the time
> > considered him a 'Merry Monarch' and a 'Good King', (it is possible, of
> > course, that nobody would say otherwise for fear of incurring his
wrath...),
> > although I'm sure his popularity must have waxed and waned throughout
his
> > reign, because some of what he did (at least when measured by modern
> > standards) was unconscionable.
> >
> > He gave us Elizabeth and, with her a new age of reason and
enlightenment. I
> > guess we should be glad about that, if nothing else.
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You really should drink more alcohol, Alastair, your insight above is very
thoughtful <G>.

You have taken a slight liberty with the anthropic principle, but everybody
does that so no problem. It isn't that the universe is as it is so we can
comprehend it, more that if the universe was any other way, we wouldn't be
here to observe it. Subtle, but quite different. Leaving that aside, your
point about the times and the monarch is a good one.

Probably events were happening that Henry really had no control over
(outside England) and these would certainly have shaped the feeling of the
people (and the court). Nevertheless, a ruthless monarch with the kind of
power that Henry had, could have provided a very different outcome. Even
during Elizabeth's reign the security of the Realm was in doubt on several
occasions (the most memorable being 1588 ("Some years of late, in '88, as I
do well remember. It was, some say, the 19th of May, and some say, in
September. The Spanish train, launched forth amain, with many a fine
bravado, their, as they thought, but it proved not..., "invincible"
armado."). Yet she found the capacity to lead and motivate.

I would agree with you that the people made for the times, but we diverge as
far as the monarch having a "minor influence". (There was nothing "minor"
about Henry VIII... and Elizabeth carried on the family tradition in a way
that would have made her father proud...<G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T04:27:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1bsd.430657$%k.71480@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<31ckmfF37doe8U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com> <30sfupF33t5ikU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LiqctCPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <d487f04c.0411301012.db1c8fb@posting.google.com> <coig11$csf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d487f04c.0412010656.7ad75339@posting.google.com> <3174f6F37qkeuU1@individual.net> <d487f04c.0412031634.536f93ea@posting.google.com> <31ckmfF37doe8U1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>I would agree with you that the people made for the times, but we diverge as
>far as the monarch having a "minor influence". (There was nothing "minor"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's the fascinating part about this part of English history, and what 
attracts me to it is the personalities. Certainly inter-play with other 
states on the Continent and the rise of the 'serving nobility', the 
advisers to the monarchs. Agreed he would have been proud of Elizabeth. 
But other than her untimely death he might have also approved of first 
daughter Mary.

She deftly managed to survive Edward's reign and his family advisers, 
and with the old guy's tenacity swiftly solved the problem of Lady Jane 
Grey. I don't think Henry was much of an economist, but for the same 
reasons he married a Spaniard, would have seen the logic of his daughter 
doing the same. Seeing as Mary created the first open-air franchise for 
McDonald's, broiling the odd Protestant or two, he might well have approved.

You've got to bear in mind that although he split with Rome, he remained 
a catholic until death, regularly attending Latin masses and disapproved 
of the Bible being translated into English. Interestingly, Eileen came 
back from UK with a glossy on Hampton Court.  Second wife Anne Boleyn 
(Bullen) supposedly haunts the corridor leading to the Royal Chapel. At 
the time she was about to be put on trial with trumped up charges, she 
rushed down the same corridor to plead with her husband. Yeoman forbade 
her entry to the Royal Chapel where Henry was attending mass.

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Mouse traps in NE on a Novell network — 5 msgs

### Mouse traps in NE on a Novell network

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T11:41:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6e1fab7.0412011141.5b053980@posting.google.com>`

```
We are using Net Express on a Novell network.  We recently had some
users upgraded to use XP machines, so we are suddenly having problems
with disappearing mouses (mice?).

MF personnel then advised us to terminate-then-activate the mouse
after every call to X"91".  This actually does seem to work in some
cases, but it does not work in others.  We are continually scratching
our heads trying to get a handle on this problem, which we are willing
to bet is based mostly on our own collective ignorance.

I also have a general-but-related question:  Is it a better
programming practice to only activate the mouse just before each
ACCEPT statement and then deactivate it just after?  Or, is it better
to keep the mouse enabled most of the time and then disable it just
before calls to X"91"?  I am given to believe the former, but
whichever direction we go in will mean a lot of program changes to
deal with.  I would really rather do this right as opposed to doing it
twice.  Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks,
DaveM
```

#### ↳ Re: Mouse traps in NE on a Novell network

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T17:28:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OPWdndENR8aT2zPcRVn-qA@rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0412011141.5b053980@posting.google.com>`

```
In Windows mouse messages are merged for mouse movement. Check out your
mouse message receiving side document for details. I always enable mouse. If
there is no message handler available, the message just is discarded by the
system. If you disable it, when your application get complex, the trouble
will come because you can't find where it is disabled. If a program works
sometimes, it normally the coding problem rather system problem. Make a
minimal program to test for system problem. If you fail, then your code ....

Hope it will help.

Thomas



"Renfrew" <renfrewthejust@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:d6e1fab7.0412011141.5b053980@posting.google.com...
> We are using Net Express on a Novell network.  We recently had some
> users upgraded to use XP machines, so we are suddenly having problems
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> DaveM
```

```
Renfrew wrote:

>We are using Net Express on a Novell network.  We recently had some
>users upgraded to use XP machines, so we are suddenly having problems
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm aware of x"91" routines, some 12 functions and have never used them. 
But I'm puzzled as to how those CALLs relate to use of a Mouse; so 
obviously I'm missing something. Are you using Screen Section or GUIs, 
(either Dialog Editor or Dialog System). If you are using Screen Section 
- are you familiar with the M/F program source ADMOUSE.CBL, which lets 
you move the mouse around the screen, and using a two dimension table, 
click on any cell  ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mouse traps in NE on a Novell network

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T06:00:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1101996037.037321.44680@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0412011141.5b053980@posting.google.com> <Dawrd.391314$Pl.141021@pd7tw1no>`

```
We are using Screen Sections and, in some cases, field by field
ACCEPTs.

The MF fellow advised us that mouse activation does not work very well
in conjunction with calls to X"91", so we have been playing with
different ways of ensuring that it (mouse) is turned off prior to doing
the call.  Results are still mixed, so we are now seeking a better
fundamental understanding of how mouse routines work in this
environment.

We will look for ADMOUSE.CBL for further review. Thanks for the info!
Dave Miner
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mouse traps in NE on a Novell network

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T21:10:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dxLrd.411212$nl.64012@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1101996037.037321.44680@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0412011141.5b053980@posting.google.com> <Dawrd.391314$Pl.141021@pd7tw1no> <1101996037.037321.44680@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
renfrewthejust@yahoo.com wrote:

>We are using Screen Sections and, in some cases, field by field
>ACCEPTs.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Dave,

It probably doesn't differ much from the V3.1. on-line manual, but the 
following link takes you to the V 4.0 manuals - the Character Handling 
manual *might* perhaps have more examples. I note it includes a 
mousesmpl.cbl..

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/nx40indx.htm

In case you have problems accessing ADMOUSE.CBL, I have a copy, so 
e-mail me dropping the second  'J' in my e-mail address. There is 
another source I can give you LOGPER.CBL (have no idea what that means), 
which using the M/F extensions allows you to change colouring, blinking 
etc., on the fly. The latter looks quite interesting but innocent. You 
have to look at it and give some thought to its usage. Coupled with the 
Screen extensions the techniques in logper.cbl allowed me to produce  a 
menu-bar and drop-down menus, even greying certain items. Never tried it 
- (you probably use Line 25 for error messaging), but again the 
logper.cbl concept could probably be used to emulate a Windows message box..

In response to the tail-end question you originally asked, as you well 
know two options on ACCEPTING, either the whole screen or individual 
fields. My approach used to be something like the following - after all 
this time I've forgotten the *true* syntax :-

perform S10-NAME
perform S20-ADDRESS-LINE1
perform ....

S10-NAME.
accept s-name (assuming it goes into a field Cus-Name)
if Cus-Name = spaces
   do an error routine for blank name - with me it was move the literal 
into error-message-text
    then display error-message-text at Line 25. Using Logper.cbl above 
you could colour the
    message for different types (Warning/Informative) and even blink/not 
blink.

However - caveat - I don't know how any of the above relates to the 
Novell issue you mentioned.

Jimmy
```

```
top posting corrected

On Thu, 2 Dec 2004 14:50:37 -0000, "Euromercante" 
<_jose.gomes@euromercante.com> wrote:
>"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:217e491a.0411292035.b2c898d@posting.google.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The following code opens the sheet mentioned on a cmtext field.
I created the form with the following controls, without changing ANY
of the properties apart from the text for the text control which I
default to 1.

1- Excel connection control
   If I leave the "show excel" option selected it will show the
correct sheet when Excel is shown.

2- a text control (where I will enter the sheet number I wish to
display)
3- a table control
4- A command button control with the code below



 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
 DATA            DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 EXCELSHEET-NAME PIC X(260).
 01 ReturnValue PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01 ExcelSheetIndx PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01 SET-DATA PIC X(5).
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
     MOVE "C:\temp\fjexcel\demo.xls" TO EXCELSHEET-NAME.
     move "Text" OF cmtext1 to ExcelSheetIndx
     INVOKE CmExcel1 "OpenBook" USING EXCELSHEET-NAME ExcelSheetIndx
     MOVE "Text"(1 1) OF CmExcel1 TO SET-DATA
     MOVE SET-DATA TO "Text" OF "TableCells"(1 1) OF CmTable1.
     INVOKE CmExcel1 "CloseBook".


There is no problem with the above code and the selected sheet is
always the one being processed.

This is with Excel 2003 Professional English, with Fujitsu V7, on a
Windows XP professional with SP2.

The excel workbook has 10 worksheets each with a different identifier
char on cell 1, column 1 to make sure the correct was was selected.






Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-modified: 2004/10/27
Version: 3.03
URL: http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm 
Maintainer: wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M Klein)
  
                                COBOL FAQ
                      (Frequently Asked Questions)
    
    Last-Modified: Spring 2002
    
    The full (somewhat out-of-date) COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:
    
    http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm > 
   
    If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, 
    please send private email to:
    
        wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
   
   
    This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):
   
     - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
     - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
     - Where can I contact... ?
     - What about OO COBOL? 
     - Books about COBOL
     - COBOL Tools
     - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
     - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
     - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education? 
 		Tutorials? Etc
   
   	 and much, MUCH more
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T13:24:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coo16f$21d6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com> <9LL4Cis9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <XVxod.310652$nl.309168@pd7tw3no> <9LPD9RVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <mdRod.319213$%k.283963@pd7tw2no> <41AF2BE8.4D0F61C8@fujitsu-siemens.com> <44Lrd.411007$nl.399672@pd7tw3no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:44Lrd.411007$nl.399672@pd7tw3no...

> As 'Chief Scribe', clarification from Chuck please. From the J4 minutes,
> you were forwarding the predecessor document to ISO/WG4 for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> in detail) . Or as one can comment, is the topic still up for grabs ?

The Collection Class Library and XML Syntax proposals aren't final and
aspects of them are still being discussed, and they were brought to WG4 by
J4 with the sense "This is the direction we're planning to go.  Is that OK?
Have you any major or minor complaints with the direction?  How about the
details?", not "We think this is done; is this OK with you?"  (there were a
few of those).

"J4/04-0207, Collection classes -- historical discussion" is intended to
capture the history of this proposal (keeps from cluttering up the actual
proposal), and Bob has updated it to include the outcomes of both of the
teleconferences on the subject as well as the WG4 discussion; I suggest you
check it out.

Note that the J4 meeting starts *Monday next*,   As both XML and Collection
Classes are Technical Reports (rather than proposals for the next standard)
that can apply to the 2002 standard as well, they precede the discussion of
the other 2008 changes in the agenda (J4/04-0205) for the meeting
(dynamic-capacity tables, any-length items, date/time formats, structured
constants, etc.)   For that reason, preliminary comments at your earliest
convenience would be appropriate if you want your suggestions discussed at
that meeting!  The next one is in February, and J4 is working really hard to
get the first draft out for international review by next summer in order to
meet WG4's request that the next standard be published no later than 2008.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

Hmm....

>The Collection Class Library and XML Syntax proposals aren't final and
>aspects of them are still being discussed, and they were brought to WG4 by
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not sure Bob's 04-0207 helped much as background. A lot was a rehash and 
as I stepped through, it initially indicated the old class structure. 
Wasn't until I read further on that SortedCollections are spelled out.,  
My view at least is,  that the re-hash as a history only needed  to make 
reference to UnorderedCollecitons by stating that this feature was 
dropped in favour of SortedCollections.

Now not being in the loop - suddenly confronted with something new - 
"Exception Class" - and that is entirely restricted for use with 
Collections, at the moment.

Then there is the reference in the agenda for the meeting you refer to 
below, "take into account comments from WG4....". Again, playing it in 
the dark - what was commented upon. In Russell's case, which Bill 
resurrected,  there was a document showing you where WG4 took kicks at 
Russell's proposal.

>Note that the J4 meeting starts *Monday next*,   As both XML and Collection
>Classes are Technical Reports (rather than proposals for the next standard)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Here we go again -

"Note that the J4 meeting starts *Monday next*...". Rough guess, took me about three months back in 2000. The recent ones, rushed like mad to get as near to your J4 meeting date as possible - didn't make it. The rush resulted in a lot of repetition which I didn't have time to delete editorially.

There's no way  I can beat the clock, nor do I have an overall handle on 
your direction; then throw in Karl's latest editorial comments for 
consideration. I would really like to*actually* do some programming.

 I don't hear any change clinking. OK so apart from J4 you have other 
duties at Unisys. But there's a difference - imagine a visual cash 
register on each of our desks. In Chuck's case that cash register is 
merrily pinging away, regardless of what duties he is performing , 
getting all set-up to post entries to Payroll so that Chuck gets his 
bank direct credit. Even with Christmas coming up, I wont get one single 
jingle on mine :-) .

I'll take a look, to see if I think there are any bloopers, but without 
some *detailed* background, the major part of 04-0206 still looks 
comparatively much like the original. Haven't looked yet, but Bob's 
example methods under 'Concepts' would appear to clarify it somewhat.

On a practical note - and *assume*  you had the TR ready for Jan 2006 - 
as an M/F user, how long before M/F implement. There's an awful lot of 
code out there already written using  their existing  Collections. Even 
if they provide the feature, M/F users are not going to fall over 
themselves to implement the new J4 syntax. There's another problem  - I 
get the feeling that both F/J and M/F would very much like their 
developers to jump to dotNet. Money, whether Pete on F/J or me with M/F, 
roughly about $3,500 each to get up-to-date.

OK so J4 Collections are *not* about F/J and M/F - but which other 
compilers are up and running with this feature.

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Zc5sd.405645$Pl.340771@pd7tw1no...

> Not sure Bob's 04-0207 helped much as background. A lot was a rehash and
> as I stepped through, it initially indicated the old class structure.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dropped in favour of SortedCollections.

J4/04-0207 is intended as a *chronological* history reflecting, in the order
in which they occurred, the additions, improvements, amendments and changes
in direction that took place during the course of the development of the
proposal whose current instantiation is J4/04-0206.  That you had to look
somewhere other than the *beginning* of this document to find discussions
about what took place *subsequent* to the beginning of the development
process should not be a surprise!

> Here we go again -

Who be "we"?  The meeting schedule is well publicized.   It is also well
known that WG4 wanted these Technical Reports all done and in the 2008 draft
by *this month*; we've delayed the process quite a bit as it is!

> "Note that the J4 meeting starts *Monday next*...". Rough guess, took me
about three months back in 2000. The recent ones, rushed like mad to get as
near to your J4 meeting date as possible - didn't make it. The rush resulted
in a lot of repetition which I didn't have time to delete editorially.

I wasn't on the committee back then, and there's not much I can do about it
now.

> There's no way  I can beat the clock, nor do I have an overall handle on
> your direction; then throw in Karl's latest editorial comments for
> consideration. I would really like to*actually* do some programming.

Bob Karlin is producing the document.  I have repeatedly suggested you
*talk* to him.  It is certainly not clear *to me* what your specific
objections *to the proposal are*.   We *have* studied your papers, and I for
one can't figure out *precisely* what it is you think Bob needs to change in
his proposal to satisfy your requests, nor can I figure out how to apply the
suggestions you have made to the current documents to come up with a legible
standard.  I've tried.

>  I don't hear any change clinking. OK so apart from J4 you have other
> duties at Unisys. But there's a difference - imagine a visual cash
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> jingle on mine :-) .

I don't know what I, as a Unisys employee or as a member of J4 am expected
to do about this.  I have repeatedly suggested you *talk* to the author of
the Collection Class document.   His contact information is available from
the J4 website.   I am not in a position to act as an intermediary.

As to the fact that I am indeed employed, although I survived the most
recent round of voluntary and involuntary layoffs at Unisys, I have not all
that much confidence that that will continue to be the case indefinitely.
I'm not sure what involvement in, or responsibility for, your current
employment situation I have!

> I'll take a look, to see if I think there are any bloopers, but without
> some *detailed* background, the major part of 04-0206 still looks
> comparatively much like the original. Haven't looked yet, but Bob's
> example methods under 'Concepts' would appear to clarify it somewhat.

Well, I hope that's progress!  I suggest you *call* Bob with any questions
or comments!

> On a practical note - and *assume*  you had the TR ready for Jan 2006 -
> as an M/F user, how long before M/F implement.

I can't address that, Jimmy; I don't work for Micro Focus nor am I privy to
their corporate strategies.  Heck, I'm not even privy to *Unisys'* corporate
strategies.

> There's an awful lot of
> code out there already written using  their existing  Collections. Even
> if they provide the feature, M/F users are not going to fall over
> themselves to implement the new J4 syntax.

This isn't new syntax, it's more like a support library as I see it.

> There's another problem  - I
> get the feeling that both F/J and M/F would very much like their
> developers to jump to dotNet. Money, whether Pete on F/J or me with M/F,
> roughly about $3,500 each to get up-to-date.

And what exactly do you expect me, as a Unisys employee or as a J4 member,
to do about this?

> OK so J4 Collections are *not* about F/J and M/F - but which other
> compilers are up and running with this feature.

I can't address this.  But as I see it the standard collection class
*library* isn't a part of the compiler itself, it's intended as a basic
standard set of *support* software adjunct to it.

Again, I'm not now, nor have I ever been, in a position to act as an
intermediary between you and the folks who wrote J4/04-0206 and its
predecessors.  I strongly recommend you talk to the authors and see if you
can clarify *to them* what your objections are.   If you don't choose to do
that, don't blame *me* for their failure to rewrite the proposal to suit
your desires!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T14:40:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31cis8F38doeoU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com> <9LL4Cis9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <XVxod.310652$nl.309168@pd7tw3no> <9LPD9RVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <mdRod.319213$%k.283963@pd7tw2no> <41AF2BE8.4D0F61C8@fujitsu-siemens.com> <44Lrd.411007$nl.399672@pd7tw3no> <coo16f$21d6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zc5sd.405645$Pl.340771@pd7tw1no> <cor13k$117m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,

a very fair, reasoned,  and valid response. It's a pity you weren't on J4
earlier. They were sadly lacking in judgement, decision making, and
leadership. If you had been there ten years ago it might have made a
difference. I wish you luck with your current endeavour, even though I
cannot support it.

I considered putting in some comments about the XML additions but I realised
it would be pointless, and I honestly don't have time to waste. J4 will go
wherever J4 thinks it ought to go. COBOL has already been dismembered by
attempts to keep up with the Joneses, and pointless additions to the
language do not improve the situation.

Adding OO to it was a fantastic achievement (a far from pointless adition
(unlike intrinsic XML and Collections)) and it still leaves me gasping at
the people who did it, but look at the reaction: Nothing. The user base
simply didn't DESERVE to have what was produced. Their resistance to change
and entrenched conservatism shot it down before it could ever fly. Why spend
time adding collections as part of the standard, when all they have to do is
go OO, and they can have all the collections they could ever want, of
whatever type they want?

Same with XML. The external free facilities to handle XML are more than
adequate (if you use OO). In fact they are totally rich, and they are free,
for ANY language that implements a COM/CORBA interface, including OO COBOL.
I can't see the point in using valuable resources to provide people with
something that is already provided, if they were prepared to invest in some
training and re-thinking their attitude. How much time, effort, and money is
being spent by J4 to provide these facilities through a standard that will
be far too late, for a language that will be virtually dead by the time this
standard is ready?

If that time and effort was spent in getting the 2000 standard fully
implemented NOW, (maybe even a joint development project between
J4/ANSI/ISO/whatever and a vendor) and promoting the fact that COBOL was
made viable for future development by the addition of these facilities, you
wouldn't hear a peep out of me.

Jimmy,

how long does it take to realise you are flogging a dead horse?

Let it go.

Pete.
(Top Post no more.)

Pete Dashwood wrote:

Chuck,

Although frustrated with the whole shebang, I tried to keep that Irish 
temper in check - hope I was reasonably successful. Let me remind you I 
first got into this game as a SYSTEMS ANALYST  - i.e., being analytical. 
This (still primarily) analyst is not satisfied with the lack of 
information, i.e. what led up to the creation of the document to date 
and some indication, in summary at least, where you are heading - AS AN 
EXTERNAL READER - not in the know.

So one of your agenda items for this current week is to review current 
recipients on your circulation list. The list currently includes Bill 
Klein - fair enough. It also includes Russell  Clarke. Russell Who ? He 
gave up down in Ozzie after his magnificent effort to produce a 
collection paper - and now merrily codes away in Java.

Similarly, you have Ray Obin on the list - one of the 'originals' on the 
OCTG. Can't be sure - but I think he got dumped at M/F Newbury as a 
result of the Merant fiasco.  I can only imagine how enthused he might 
be about COBOL as a topic.

 From Newbury days, (Summer 2000), some ten J4 members now down to six. 
The leavers :-

- Bill Klein (self) - financial/health
- Artur for F/J - health
- EDS - they've taken a financial whack - what "change" were they 
getting by  contributing.
- Wim (self)  Netherlands - retired
*********
- Fujitsu - why ?
- Hitachi - (ITSCJ - Watara Takagi) - why  ?
- John Piggott (self) - why ?
- Stephen Spiro (self)  - why ?

I may well be wrong, but I have the deepest suspicion that the latter 
group didn't like the concept of "The bureaucrats are in charge".

Let me remind you I HAVE written to Bob Karlin  TWICE and you were cc'd 
on one of the e-mails - replies ZILCH !  

>Jimmy,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As the saying goes, "If you can't beat 'em, join 'em". And as I once 
wrote to you, "Like it or not, they are the only game in town".  In a 
mood of, "the cup is half full", I reluctantly went along with current 
set-up. Oh I "realised", quite some time back it probably was futile. I 
regrettably now see the 'cup as only a 1/4 full', and in the long-run, 
empty.

Let's put the dead horse in the bone yard - requiescant in pace.

Positively the *LAST*  from me on Standards - I'll see if I can convince 
myself that threads here with "Infinite Loops" are worth following, (I 
somehow don't think so). Added first item on agenda next Monday, with a 
sip from a cup of coffee, "Whoopee-do! We've disposed of the two 
J4-Bashers, Dashwood and Gavan".

Yes I *was* enthused about Open Source, just like you observed. I can 
say it now - had Open Source been viable, rather than a hobby - it was 
my hope that a sophisticated and developer heavily-supported 
compiler/standard would eventually replace the current carnival.

(Just think we could have had two Open Source documentation standards, 
(1) The Biggie - the version which would have allowed the 'contest' 
between Richard and Robert to continue ad nauseum, and (2) The Short 
version - say 100 pages, that the rest of us could use).

Jimmy.

Don't be too hard on Chuck. He is not he enemy here. As he pointed out, what
can you reasonably expect him to do about it?

If I were in your position (and I could easily have been, except that I
chose some years back not to be <G>) I would address your concerns to the
person who IS responsible. That would be the award  accepting current Chair.
He is quite OK with accepting awards for the non-performance of this group
so maybe it is time he did something to let the rest of see that he is
capable of doing something. (I'm not suggesting that Don is the "enemy"
either, but he should never have accepted an award for being years late and
contributing to the death of COBOL. It isn't personal; I've never met him,
but his doing that made me (as a COBOL user) feel like my  nose was being
rubbed in the dirt by J4 and I've never gotten over it.) Anyway, my personal
feelings aside, you would do well to at least give Don a chance to do
something about your concerns. He has the authority to do so. (Well, he had
the authority to accept the award, so I guess he does...)

Speculation, Jimmy. It could have been any number of reasons. Part of the

plagued by red tape and committees.

> Let me remind you I HAVE written to Bob Karlin  TWICE and you were cc'd
