- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<326ju1F3hl774U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<31cge3F38hd7vU1@individual.net> <41B7372E.79818F67@mb.sympatico.ca> <10rgrqmge3d40b4@news.supernews.com> <31s5rpF3fnmhkU1@individual.net> <cpat0e$n7$1@panix5.panix.com> <31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <R6Hud.6314$ZP5.1939@fe40.usenetserver.com> <321i43F3gpl03U1@individual.net> <cg0vd.6726$ZP5.5891@fe40.usenetserver.com> <3248o4F3fa9s6U1@individual.net> <iU8vd.7062$ZP5.1891@fe40.usenetserver.com> <Qfmvd.503093$%k.410522@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Qfmvd.503093$%k.410522@pd7tw2no...
> LX-i wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> moral dilemmas?"  I found this hurtful in the extreme but now it
doesn't
> >> bother me. I just live my life and apply the rules I believe in.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, but ONLY a little and only because you asked...

> Possibly not, but was your father an aetheist ?

No. Neither was my Mother. My Mother prayed for me every day (especially
when I was away overseas and in less safe places than Tauranga) and I don't
believe it ever did me any harm.

>Assuming he wasn't then
> regardless of whether you went to a secular public school or other,
> inevitably there in the background, as part of NZ society, back then, an
> element of 'Christianity' would have crept through.

I did go to Sunday School and I was baptised as a baby.  At 13 my father
died tragically and I was distraught (so was my Mum - we both adored him).
People were very supportive and the local vicar told me if I wanted to talk
about things he was there. I decided to try and get some answers so I went
to see him. I'm not going to repeat the discussion here, but at the end of
it, I was alarmed to realise he had no more idea about anything than I did.
He trotted out trite answers to important questions, based on rote learning
of scripture, and demonstrating very little understanding. He could accept
it was all an unknowable mystery; I couldn't.

I resolved there and then that I would find answers. First I had to decide
what the questions were, and Kipling had already pointed the way...

I keep six honest serving-men
  (They taught me all I knew);
Their names are What and Why and When
  And How and Where and Who.
I send them over land and sea,
  I send them east and west;
But after they have worked for me,
  I give them all a rest.

I let them rest from nine till five,
  For I am busy then,
As well as breakfast, lunch, and tea,
  For they are hungry men.
But different folk have different views.
  I know a person small-
She keeps ten million serving-men,
  Who get no rest at all!

She sends'em abroad on her own affairs,
  From the second she opens her eyes-
One million Hows, two million Wheres,
  And seven million Whys!

 (This is the reporter's code for newspapermen to this day).

So, over a period of around thirty years, and still continuing on some of
the questions...

Where am I?  Got seriously into Astronomy on this one. By the time I was 17
I was president of the Astronomy club at the College (Teacher Training,
Ardmore) I attended. It fascinates me to this day, and, as I have grown and
my awareness has grown, I have more understanding of it today than I ever
had. I now feel "comfortable" with the Universe and my location in it.

What am I?  Natural Sciences soon revealed the relationship between Humans
and the rest of the organisms on the planet. I also acquired respect for all
life in passing.

How am I? Well, it varies... Good days, bad days <G>. Stimulated an interest
in Philosophy and Logic which was helpful in my career.

When am I? Led me to an investigation of Time and thence to Relativity and
Riemann.

Who am I? The vicar's response had been "You are a child of God, my Son..."
Yeah, right... The search for identity is one of the most driven ones. Today
I know who I am and that also is "comfortable". I arrived there after a 25
year study of comparative religions, philosophies and cults.

I have read the Koran and the Bible,  the teachings of Lao Tze,
Zarathrustra, and Confucius, the life and teachings of the Buddha, and
Bhagavad Gita too. I have had long discussions with Scientologists and
people from Eckankar, and devotees of Guru Maha Raj Ji, and Witches,
Wizards, Freemasons, and Rosicrucians and Theosophists, Oooh and I almost
forgot the Aetherius Society. This particular investigation took me to
monasteries in the Himalayas, Churches in Europe, Temples in the Far East,
and universities in New Zealand and Europe.

I drew no conclusions, just amassed data. After a year or two of thinking
about it I arrived at the conclusions I currently hold.

The only clue you have is my acknowledged atheism. But that could change...I
might have the same catharsis Saul did on the road to Damascus... however,
my personality is not a bit like Saul's, so it is unlikely... <G>

Why am I? This is the biggie. And it is totally personal. I therefore shall
not discuss it further, other than to say that "no particular reason" is
every bit as valid as "because we are working out God's plan".

It would be wrong to assume I am saying I have all the answers (and it would
also indicate a closed mind on my part). I don't, and some of the evidence
is re-evaluated often. Some of it is indeterminate or inconclusive. But I do
have enough to let me live in this place and be comfortable about it.

I don't push it to other people because all of us must "work out our own
salvation with diligence" (the dying words of the Buddha to his friend and
disciple Ananda. Interestingly, he never claimed to be (a) God, but then he
never claimed not to be either <G>). This post would never have been made if
you hadn't actually requested it and I am unlikely to discuss these things
again.


>To wit, your love of
> literature.

Arts and literature have nothing to do with my religious belief or lack of
it. I play music and I write because I HAVE to. These are passions that have
never diminished throughout my life.

>Take phrases like Tennyson's from On Westminster Bridge,
> "Dear God the very houses seem asleep.....".  You and I both know what
> the poet is attempting to describe, even ignoring the word 'God'.
>
Yes. So?

> Even accepting Richard's premise that the Israelite tales are mythical,
> I think we could all agree that the Ten Commandments (excluding the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don't generalise about others, Jimmy. The 10 commandments of the Hebrew God
are indeed sensible, but so are the tenets and teachings of all of the great
teachers. And it is quite possible to arrive at similar conclusions just by
travelling and observing.

> So what was your particular path Pete, and no dispute about your own
> personal morality, how did you arrive at your own set of rules to live by
?
>

A fair question, honestly asked.

I did the above, and I also observed life going on around me (in very
different cultures). I found there are more similarities than differences
between men. There is a universality about the Human condition that makes it
possible to establish rules of conduct that will cause the least problem.
(In other words, without being pious, try and make the world a "better
place"...) It is also necessary to stop and think about things sometimes. I
have never been bored.

I have had a very full and interesting life (not saying it is over <G>, but
it is certainly entering a different phase now. I want peace and tranquility
and the adventuring is probably gone...). I have no regrets, (although there
are certainly things I could have done differently or better) and, like
Robert Louis Stevenson, when the time comes, I can "lay me down with a
will".

The belief that there is no afterlife does not frighten me. I see death as a
normal part of life and I think we should make the most of this tiny window
of awareness that is open for around 70 years... I am very happy for my
atoms to rejoin the Cosmos where they were forged.

Hope this covers it,

Pete.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Even accepting Richard's premise that the Israelite tales are mythical, 
>I think we could all agree that the Ten Commandments (excluding the 
>first), are a pretty wise set of  rules for any human to follow - and 
>are the most challenging set of rules for mankind. to observe. We all 
>break many of them consistently.

It's a little-known fact that Moses was the Israelites' top union
negotiator.  God originally intended to hand down 100 Commandments and
Moses was deputed to try and get Him to be more reasonable.  Moses,
after days of heavy negotiation came back down the mountain with the
following, not often reported, message..  "Boys, I've got some good
news and some bad news..  The good news is, I've got Him down to ten..
The bad news, 'Adultery' is still in.".     :-)
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-11T13:58:06+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31uv17F3fr2g6U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<31cge3F38hd7vU1@individual.net> <i-Kdnc_CI_TikizcRVn-iQ@giganews.com> <1102399619.170434.159340@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10re7uu9kotq24b@news.supernews.com> <41B7372E.79818F67@mb.sympatico.ca> <10rgrqmge3d40b4@news.supernews.com> <41B91B71.A8C19BF@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:41B91B71.A8C19BF@mb.sympatico.ca...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Agreed, if you stumble upon graven tablets in the woods outstide Elmyra,
New
> > York.
>
> I only regret I'm not con-man enough to try that for myself.
>
I read the book. It staggered me that a man had the ability to pull this
off. Either he was severely deluded and honestly believed what he said, or
the events he described really transpired.

There are millions of people around the world who believe the Latter...<G>

Out of respect for all beliefs I shan't take this any further.
>
> >
> > No, unqestioning reliance by a lay person on a written rule is no
substitute
> > for the reasoned opinion of those who've made their life work studying
> > religion or law.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I found your comments here interesting, Peter.

It sems quite valid to me. There is certainly no reason to believe that
anyone else will not take liberties with what was said or written, and
whether they do so consciously or not, it is inevitable that their
interpretation will be coloured by their own life experience and
personality, if nothing else.

After some thought about this, I have decided that I ascribe to the school
that says each of us should arrive at our OWN personal belief. At least that
way we have some responsibility for what we take on board, and if we get it
"wrong" or miss the point that the writer intended, then hopefully as time
goes by, we will be moved to re-evaluate and correct what we believe,
because we will find it wanting.

In a modern society where there are not masses of uneducated illiterate
people who are incapable of evaluating things for themselves, you can
seriously ask, why should people be reliant on the words of priests and
scribes. Maybe the whole edifice of organized religion needs review (but
then, I WOULD say that... <G>).

Pete.
```

```
In article <3204u3F3enroiU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>It is the ones who KNOW that theirs is the only true faith who are dangerous
>to the rest of us.

Oh, my Goedel is killing me... and this is KNOWN to be a tenet of the only 
true faith, right?

(for those who have less English: Goedel ('oe' being a variant spelling of 
'o-umlaut') refers to Kurt G�del's (I tried to cut-n-paste there) 
Incompleteness Theorem and has a pronounciation similar to the English 
word 'girdle', a word which, among other things, describes an undergarment 
sometimes worn by women who wish to appear thinner than they are; a few 
years ago a manufacturer of women's undergarments in the United States of 
America was touting the superiority of their product in a series of 
advertisements beginning with the catch-phrase, said by a woman whose face 
showed obvious pain, annoyance and discomfort, of 'My girdle is killing 
me'... hence, when confronted with a statement which seems to fall under 
the purview of the Incompleteness Theorem in a humorous way, e.g. 
'Everything is relative!' 'Oh... is that absolutely true?' my response 
often begins with 'Oh, my Goedel is killing me...')

(for those who have more English: a joke explained is a joke lost; see 
what happens when one tries to clarify a few words?)

DD
```

```
On  8-Dec-2004, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Think about what the US Constitution actually says about the right to
> bear arms, as opposed to what it's usually taken to mean, and you'll get
> my drift.

You mean:
A well-regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the
right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed; except as to
arms which are deemed insufficiently relevant to the needs of a militia[1], or
those which are deemed excessively relevant to the needs of a militia[2]; or in
such other cases and manners as the Congress, the Legislatures of the several
States, the City Council of Bumfuque, New Jersey, or any other governmental
entity
whatsoever shall decree.

[1] E.g., sawed-off shotguns.

[2] E.g., assault rifles.

================

Congress shall make no law abridging freedom of speech or of the press, unless
such abridgement shall be necessary to prevent unauthorized persons from
influencing the outcome of an election.

Each State shall appoint, in such Manner as the Legislature thereof may direct,
or the Federal Government may order in the interests of choosing the proper
candidate, a Number of Electors.

In all criminal prosecutions, other than that of  Terrorist or Accessory
thereof, the accused shall enjoy the right to a speedy and public trial.


The powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution, nor
prohibited by it to the States, nor being mentioned once in the same
sentence with interstate commerce, are reserved to the States
respectively, or to the people, pending Supreme Court approval.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T22:36:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ri6g0dd7s61ec@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<31cge3F38hd7vU1@individual.net> <i-Kdnc_CI_TikizcRVn-iQ@giganews.com> <1102399619.170434.159340@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10re7uu9kotq24b@news.supernews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:10re7uu9kotq24b@news.supernews.com...
[snip]
> The important thing to remember when dealing with the Bible (or the
> Constitution) is this: It doesn't matter what the text says - what IS
> important is what the text means. Often God (or the "Founding Fathers")
> didn't say what they meant or mean what they said.

With respect to the Constitution and Founding Fathers,
Federalist 33 < http://www.constitution.org/fed/federa33.htm >
Paragraph 6 "... If there ever should be a doubt on this head, the
credit of it will be entirely due to those reasoners who, in the
imprudent zeal of their animosity to the plan of the convention,
have labored to envelop it in a cloud calculated to obscure the
plainest and simplest truths."

As I understand these words, attributed to Alexander Hamilton,
one of the Founding Fathers, the Constitution says what it means,
plainly and simply.

While the letter, published January 2, 1788, was intended to
address anti-federalist sentiments (as explained in paragraphs
one and two), I find no clearer example of "animosity to the plan
of the convention" than President Franklin Roosevelt (in 1935?)
threatening to "pack" the Supreme Court for not passing on
(approving) the constitutionality of his social agenda.

Paragraph 6 also mentions tyrannical use of power and forced
construction, both of which may be seen to have occurred by
regarding the Constitution as consisting of "the plainest and
simplest truths."

All of the Federalist Papers are available through
< http://www.constitution.org/fed/federa00.htm >.
```

```
Man stattete ihm sehr heiï¿½en, schon etwas verbrannten Dank ab. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

<a id="t-5dd6373f60"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Standards (follow-on note 1 of ???) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### COBOL Standards (follow-on note 1 of ???)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T07:09:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wodsd.223921$SM5.12961@news.easynews.com>`

```
To: comp.lang.cobol newsgroup (with various CC's)

I don't know how many notes I will (or won't) post in "response to" (or prompted 
by) current CLC threads about the "entire Standards process" (now, past, or 
future).  I will, however, post at least this one - and let others who are 
posting their general comments and concerns (or even those who haven't voiced 
their opinions in this forum) do as they wish.

NOTE:
  Comments on "past" problems may or may not help "vent spleen" - but I don't 
personally see as very useful for the current situation or the future of COBOL 
(in anything other than an historical aspect).

One current series of comments concern what I would call "accountability" in the 
current process.  (This may or may not also relate to the RELEVANCE of the 
current process - for COBOL at least)

On the current J4 agenda at:
   http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/04-0209.doc

Is the following item:

"9.2       04-0156 - J4 Annual Report to INCITS (Schricker)"

The paper 94-0156 (Covering the Period from June 2003 to August 2004) is 
available at:
   http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/04-0156.htm

If someone "interested in the COBOL standardization process" (PRO, CON, or 
other) felt that report either inaccurate or not reflecting issues that they 
think should be made known to the body to which J4 "reports", then I would send 
a letter (possibly including a soft/email copy - but in hard copy as well) to:

Patrick Morris
Executive Director
c/o Information Technology Industry Council
1250 Eye Street NW, Suite 200
Washington, DC 20005

202-626-5742
 pmorris@itic.org

with a copy to:

Deborah Spittle
Associate Manager, Standards Operations
c/o Information Technology Industry Council
1250 Eye Street NW, Suite 200
Washington, DC 20005

202-626-5746
 dspittle@itic.org

(She is listed as having support for TCs:  CT22, H3, J1, J3, J4, J7, J9, J11, 
J15, J16, J17, J18, L2, T3, T6, T10, T11, and T20).

  ***

In addition, I would copy:

Ronald F. Silletti
INCITS liaison (for J4)
Corporate Program Director of Standards
Intellectual Property & Licensing
IBM Corporation
North Castle Drive, Armonk, New York 10504

phone: 914-765-4373 fax: 914-765-4420
 email: silletti@us.ibm.com

  ***
Finally, I would copy the J4 chair at:

  Don Schricker
  4012 Nancy Margarite Ln
  Las Vegas, NV 89130-2618

  Tel:    (978) 314-6821
  Fax: (702) 515-1737
  Don.Schricker@microfocus.com

     ****

Before sending your comments, you might (or might not) want to review the 
information (status and otherwise) of J4's work at their "public" web page at:

  http://www.cobolstandards.com/

That page includes links to their planned meetings and other relevant sites and 
documents.

To the best of my knowledge (and I could EASILY be out-of-date on this - and 
things might change at the meeting scheduled for next week), J4 still plans on 
having "Public Review" periods of their current TR's (Technical Reports) on 
native XML support and Collection classes during next year (2005).  I believe 
the current hope/expectation is that these will join the  Finalization TR as 
approved (and as an official ISO TR) in 2006  and that the first either formal 
or informal public review of the next full revision will occur in 2005. (With 
expected completion and approval of the next full revision in 2008).

If it helps any, it should be noted that to the best of *MY* (potentially 
out-of-date) knowledge:
 - no vendor currently claims to have a compiler fully conforming to the 2002 
Standard (approved two years ago this month)
 - no vendor has announced a date for having such a conforming compiler
 - several vendors have indicated that they have little or no immediate interest 
in providing such a conforming compiler
 - those vendors that have indicated SOME interest in the 2002 Standard have 
(all - as far as I know) indicated that they are implementing features "one by 
one" (or so) based on current customer demand for SPECIFIC features (without - 
so far as they have indicated publicly) indicating that ANY vendor has received 
sufficient customer demand to provide a business case for EVER providing a fully 
conforming (to the '02 Standard) compiler
 - there is no current "certification test" for the '02 Standard - nor am I 
aware of any governmental or private body developing such a suite (similar to 
the NIST test suite for the '85 Standard).
- there is no current US government requirement for "conformance" to the '02 
Standard (as their was for conformance to the FIPS Standard in the '80s and 
'90s)
 - there is no European (EUC) or Japanese (or any other that I am aware of) 
government body requiring (or expected to require) a conforming '02 COBOL 
compiler
```

## Thread: MicroFocus C-Isam Index Rebuild 9/037 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### MicroFocus C-Isam Index Rebuild 9/037

- **From:** salty_sohal@yahoo.com (Jonathan)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T16:25:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c95bb32e.0412041625.6b7b24b6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi guys (and gals too),

Please excuse my ignorance I'm not a COBOLer, but I do have much
respect for what you do. (I'm lazy I write everything that I could
ever need in PERL)

So my problem is this... I'm dealing this old "Real World" accounting
system that has decided to trash one it's index files. I spent the
last day researching how to rebuild this index. I discovered I needed
to use the REBUILD.exe. According to microfocus online docs I can
restore an non corrupt idx file so that rebuild will know which keys
to build from. Great! (I thought I was homefree!) So I restore an old
idx and run:

xm rebuild PMRWRK00.dat

And I get:

***Error on input file - status 9/037 - records read = 0


After a little more digging I discover that this has something to do
with passwords/authentication. So now I am stumped, I don't know
anything about the system I am messing with except what I've read in
the past 24 hours (a lot but my brain is still indexing it) and as far
as I know there is no password associated with this app. It runs on
one persons computer and there is no security other than OS login.

Can somebody tell me what I need to do, or nudge me in the right
direction? Is there a way to subvert the password without breaking the
APP? (I'm assuming the APP knows the pasword since it writes to the
file)

Thanks alot,

Jonathan

P.S. I don't know if this will tell you anything helpful but I ran

xm rebuild Pmrwrk00.dat /f:c63d5

on the file with the restored index to see what kind of information I
could get...
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus C-Isam Index Rebuild 9/037

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <charlescribbs@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T15:44:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g1Gsd.4597$714.1183@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c95bb32e.0412041625.6b7b24b6@posting.google.com>`

```
What microfocus is telling you is that it can't find the .idx file, which
you already new.  The rebuild utility makes the assumption that you have a
.idx file that has been corrupted and the rebuild will fix it.

If you haven't got the .idx file you are going to have to find out the
original file layout and the various keys that the file used to make up the
index.  Unless somebody has that information you are going to be out of luck
with that file.  What you might do see if they have an old back-up copy of
the data file and the .idx and that might give some clues as to what you
have to do.

If you want the big explanation of what all you have to do e-mail me back
and I can give you the long drawn out version.


"Jonathan" <salty_sohal@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:c95bb32e.0412041625.6b7b24b6@posting.google.com...
> Hi guys (and gals too),
>
…[156 more quoted lines elided]…
>         ******************************************
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus C-Isam Index Rebuild 9/037

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T10:07:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412051007.6f311fe9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c95bb32e.0412041625.6b7b24b6@posting.google.com>`

```
salty_sohal@yahoo.com (Jonathan) wrote 

> xm rebuild PMRWRK00.dat

XM implies that it is DOS runtime.  1996 elsewhere indicates MF Cobol
version 3.4.  These things are useful to help solve the problem.
 
> And I get:
> 
> ***Error on input file - status 9/037 - records read = 0

> After a little more digging I discover that this has something to do
> with passwords/authentication. So now I am stumped, I don't know
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> one persons computer and there is no security other than OS login.

If the backup had been written to CD ROM then the restore may have
left the file 'read only' which could give the 9-037 error.  Check
using ATTRIB or just set it off with ATTRIB -r *.IDX

Otherwise if this in on Windows NT/XP it may be a user permissions
issue.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I am getting the following error while trying to compile the demo.cob
program. I am using MF Server Express 4.0 SP1. Any idea what is
happening here.

Load error : file 'check'
error code: 173, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
173     Called program file not found in drive/directory 

cob64: error(s) in compilation: demo.cob


demo.cob
--------
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  HELLO.
       AUTHOR.      MY NAME.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAINLINE.
       DISPLAY 'Hello world!'.
       STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T07:29:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MIqSBMPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  04.12.04
  wrote  mbhat_peraje@yahoo.com (Muralidhar)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com
  about  MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23


mp> Load error : file 'check'
mp> error code: 173, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
mp> 173     Called program file not found in drive/directory

   While I don't know this MF Server Express, my first guess is that  
there is a file called "check.*" missing. Probably "check.dll".

   This would mean, the compiler is missing one of its parts. Your  
program seems to be completely valid to me.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Belehrung findet man ï¿½fter in der Welt als Trost.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T10:26:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412051026.3ea92ece@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com>`

```
mbhat_peraje@yahoo.com (Muralidhar) wrote 

> Load error : file 'check'
> error code: 173, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
> 173     Called program file not found in drive/directory 

You probably need an environment variable pointing to where the
compiler modules are located.  SET COBRUN="where check.DLL and other"

 
> demo.cob
> --------
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
>        PROGRAM-ID.  HELLO.

You probably want to have the filename match the program-id.  Either
rename demo.cob to hello.cob or change the -ID to DEMO.

While this is not important in this case it becomes so in large
modular applications.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23

- **From:** Wiggy <wignomore@btopenworld.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T02:12:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp0f64$gtd$1@sparta.btinternet.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi.

I suspect your environment may not be set up correctly.

You need to have COBDIR set to the root directory of your COBOL 
installation, PATH set to include $COBDIR/bin, and LD_LIBRARY_PATH set 
to include $COBDIR/lib.

If this is the case, and it's still not working, please invoke 
'mfsupport' from a command prompt, and post the mfpoll.txt output here.

Regards,
SimonT.
```

```
wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>       STOP RUN.

The entire program should contain one line:

display 'Hello world'

All the rest is superfluous. 

You seem to be compiling the program with the name HELLO and calling
it with the name 'check', or maybe 'demo'. Try stripping it down to
one line basics and trying again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T19:13:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b2td.6300230$6p.998057@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com> <duk7r0tc585sekpvkbjlkc4qkbt54t9boq@4ax.com> <9MN0LsM9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Micro Focus does:

 Identification Division header - always optional (per '02 Standard)

Program-ID paragraph optional - if the program name default is accepted

Procedure Division header optional (I can't remember exactly when this is 
allowed and when not)

No paragraph (or section names) required in Procedure Division

   ****

All marked as "extensions" when FLAGSTD or FLAG(ANS) is specified.

This "sample" is often cited as  showing how COBOL doesn't have to be verbose 
<G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF cobol compilation error on HP Itanium 11.23

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T19:50:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8K2td.450538$%k.119950@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9MN0LsM9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<ebb0012b.0412041924.6e6f34eb@posting.google.com> <duk7r0tc585sekpvkbjlkc4qkbt54t9boq@4ax.com> <9MN0LsM9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

>.    On  06.12.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
For starters, Robert's suggestion of cutting the program down to one
line wasn't very helpful. There's no reason the enquirer should have to
do that to get it to work.

Which compiler - Micro Focus for as long as I can remember. I know it
wont turn you on but it does me. I only have a viewing copy of Acrobat
so I can't copy and paste from the Standards document.  The following is
just as applicable to 'Procedural' as it is to OO. The Standard has a
number of source examples to illustrate OO, one I looked at occupied a
complete page. Usual stuff , Class-Id., Repository, Environment
Division, Data Division, blah, blah , blah.

Using the concept of  'cut out the red tape' M/F allows you to leave out
the 'superfluous' - e.g., why put in either Data Division or
Working-Storage Section headers if you use neither in a particular
program/class. (I don't use the UPPER-CASE approach below, so
syntactically I may not be accurate).Take a hypothetical method(s) :-

METHOD-ID. doSomething.
LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION.
01 SOMETHING1            PIC 9(03).
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 LINK1                           PIC 9(03).
01 LINK2                          PIC 9(03).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK1 RETURNING LINK2
MOVE WS-A TO SOMETHING1
COMPUTE LINK2 = LINIK1 * SOMETHING1
EXIT METHOD
END METHOD doSomething.
METHOD-ID. doSomethingAgain.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION.
01 SOMETHING1            PIC 9(03).
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 LINK1                           PIC 9(03).
01 LINK2                          PIC 9(03).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK1 RETURNING LINK2
MOVE WS-A TO SOMETHING1
COMPUTE LINK2 = LINIK1 * SOMETHING1
EXIT METHOD
END METHOD doSomethingAgain.

So first I'll go for LOWERCASE - doesn't that tie in with 'not
SHOUTING', and my style - I like comments as separators

*>------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "doSomething". *> can be quotes/no quotes
*>------------------------------------
01 Something1                  pic 9(03).
Linkage-Section.
01 lnk-1                              pic 9(03).
01 lnk-2                              pic 9(03).
Procedure Division using Lnk-1 returning lnk-2.
move ws-A to Something1
compute lnk-2 =  Something1 * lnk-1
End Method "doSomething".
*>------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "doSomethingAgain".
*>------------------------------------
01 Something1                  pic 9(03).
Linkage-Section.
01 lnk-1                              pic 9(03).
01 lnk-2                              pic 9(03).
Procedure Division using Lnk-1 returning lnk-2.
move ws-A to Something1
compute lnk-2 =  Something1 * lnk-1
End Method "doSomethingAgain".
*>------------------------------------------------------

Note in above I don't need 'Local-Storage Section" - it is understood.
Similarly EXIT-METHOD is superfluous - only required where exiting from
a conditional just like exiting an in-line PERFORM, or, to signal the 
end of the procedure if your method does use Paragraphs. My preference, 
it allows me to focus on the 'guts' without reading verbiage, and the
latter could be an unnecessary cause of compiler syntax errors. I really
am curious that no other compiler vendor picked up on this feature - so
yes, it is an extension, and you do have portability problems  - but I
don't see that happening in OO COBOL in my lifetime !

Look at this one, which while OO is still the same concept   - user
clicks on a push-button to re-sequence a Listbox :-

    *> ByKey is a Level 88 up in global Working Storage
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "PB-SequenceByKey".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
          if not ByKey
             move zeroes to ws-CurrentIndex
             set ByKey to true
             invoke self "createMaterialsCollection"
          End-if
       End Method "PB-SequenceByKey".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------

So yes, and I've done it :-

*>------------------------------
display 'Hello World'
*>------------------------------

Compiles clean and displays - providing your monitor is switched on :-)

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-932b2561f8"></a>
## Thread: Vindication of SECTION (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Vindication of SECTION (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits)

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T13:07:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MIsaWcPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
.    On  06.12.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  31gdvsF3asv3pU1@individual.net
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

   Thank you for writing this:

PD> Without arguing the value (or otherwise) of paragraphs (more
PD> importantly, labels), within SECTIONS, it is certainly true that
PD> SECTION can adequately describe (and document as a descriptive
PD> header) a piece of related, contiguous code, that implements a given
PD> function. If you subsequently add paragraphs or remove paragraphs
PD> from it you do NOT have to check every reference to it as you would
PD> if it was PERFORM...THRU.
PD>
PD> Richard mentioned the requirement to use SECTION when overlaying and
PD> that is no longer relevant. We have discussed this before and I am
PD> fairly persuaded to Richard's argument that there is no technical
PD> need for sections in modern code. I still use them because they are
PD> familiar to me, the benefits I would derive don't warrant changing (I
PD> have NEVER had a program bug caused by sectioning), and because they
PD> appeal to me as part of the language hierarchy that is being lost
PD> sight of.

PD> DIVISION.
PD>      SECTION.
PD>           PARAGRAPH.
PD>                  SENTENCE.
PD>                          STATEMENT.
PD>                                  CLAUSE.
PD>
PD> That makes sense to me and always has done.

   I carried this for long with me to write something about the  
Vindication of SECTIONs in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.

   All divisions are divided up in SECTIONs (exept the IDENTIFICATION  
DIVISION), so the most natural first division of the PROCEDURE  
DIVISION is the SECTION, and within a SECTION maybe one or more labels  
as paragraph headers.

   SECTIONs are also necessary when DECLARATIVES for event and  
contingency processing are written. So, using SECTIONs in the first  
place, one does not have to rewrite the program after the introduction  
of DECLARATIVES (I would have always DECLARATIVES when files are read  
or written in the program, in order to handle unrecoverable file- 
errors).

   Besides, for those programs which still have GOTOs, I recommend the  
rule which I layed out for the COBOL development back in the early  
1980ies, when COBOL-85 with its (late) introduction of proper  
structuring possibilities, namely to use only paragraph labels within  
the section itself as destination of a GOTO, should a GOTO ever be  
unavoidable.

   One case where this was explicitly allowed was the use of a GOTO  
DEPENDING ON, which is a primitive form of a CASE statement, or  
EVALUATE in COBOLese.

   So, one could change a PERFORM para-name-1 THRU para-name-2 by a  
simple PERFORM section-name-1 and introduce a SECTION header for that  
sequence of paragraphs. A first and simple step to gradually reform  
the program.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Man kann wirklich nicht wissen ob man nicht jetzt im Tollhaus sitzt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7711efdd.0412051937.64c30c6f@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm now doing work under W/2K (had been NT 4.0). I am now getting the
following:

LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined

Now, there is only one program which is marked "MAIN", and I am
building to an exe (as opposed to DLL).

The COBOL source compiles clean (well, ok I do get an INFO message in
the ID Division because I'm using an obsolete clause) - there are no
undefined paragraphs, labels, etc. Basically this is a read a line,
print a report type of program.

Again, this is COBOL97 (V4) from Fujitsu.

Regards,
Steve Thompson

ps. Yes, it has been a long time since I've posted. Been quite busy on
NON-computer projects.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error in Build with FJ 4.0 under W/2K

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
sthompson@ix.netcom.com (Steve Thompson) wrote 

> LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
> 
> Now, there is only one program which is marked "MAIN", and I am
> building to an exe (as opposed to DLL).

The error is because you are linking _without_ there being a MAIN program.

See the FAQ on Fujitsu site.
```

```
From an earlier post:

From Fujitsu web site:

"""Windows 2000 and Windows XP Application Compatibility Tips for
Version 3 and 4


If you are using Windows 2000 and Windows XP and have problems making
Version 3 or 4 work, we suggest you check out the following link. We
can't guarantee that they will work for you, but it should give you
the best chance of making things work.


Go to http://msdn.microsoft.com/compatibility/ where you should be
able to find the "Windows Application Compatibility Toolkit".


Download the Toolkit and go from there. The file at time of writing is
named act.exe. After installing it, it will likely enable you to run
several
programs that may not otherwise be compatible with Windows 2000 and
Windows XP.


If this does not work, you will have to purchase Version 6.1. If you
are a
student, there are academic licenses available."""


Note that even with the MS patch it may still not work - according to
Fujitsu.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1102503274.180384.168240@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<39ocr0peom1776e73koosttu4qqrjokusd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7711efdd.0412051937.64c30c6f@posting.google.com> <asudnYYNVMGl5SncRVn-jA@giganews.com> <1102365274.888514.248590@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10r9oq9jtkllpb6@news.supernews.com> <1102398013.979422.275010@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1102452391.299939.106490@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1102470946.607320.71630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <39ocr0peom1776e73koosttu4qqrjokusd@4ax.com>`

```
No, actually the program name was RPGMAIN2 when it should have been
RPG2MAIN.  These things happen when you are forced into developing
tools that you need on your own time, late in the day.

So I actually doubt that I am some newbie learning COBOL that is trying
to get you to do my homework.

I do find it interesting that this error was created on an NT system
that for whatever reason didn't flag it.

Later,
Steve.T
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Build with FJ 4.0 under W/2K

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T08:15:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp6upr$dqu$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7711efdd.0412051937.64c30c6f@posting.google.com> <1102470946.607320.71630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <39ocr0peom1776e73koosttu4qqrjokusd@4ax.com> <1102503274.180384.168240@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1102503274.180384.168240@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
steve.t <sthompson@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>No, actually the program name was RPGMAIN2 when it should have been
>RPG2MAIN.  These things happen when you are forced into developing
>tools that you need on your own time, late in the day.

And lo, there is nothing entirely new under the sun.  From:

<http://groups.google.co.uk/groups?selm=743jpc%24qlr%241%40clarknet.clark.net&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

A decade and a half ago my eldest brother was in the computer lab at a
university where he was studying for his Master's Degree in Business
Administration; the lab had just received a shipment of IBM personal
computers and these were still rather... new in 1983.  Anyway, he heard a
student call out, rather distressed, 'Does anyone here know *any*
FORTRAN?'; my brother walked over, slowly, and said 'Well, I only have
about twenty, maybe twenty-five years at it... what do you need?'

The poor student pointed at his screen.  'I cannot get this subroutine
invoked... and I've been working with this CALL statement for 2 1/2 hours
now and I cannot get it to work!'

My brother glanced at the screen and said 'This might be because *here*
you spell the name with one 'b' and *there* you are spelling it with two?'

Mr Belli, this technique is also called, at times, 'four-eyed
debugging'... it is *very* useful and happens here all the time.  Your
apologies are accepted, your gratitutde is appreciated... but now you have
taken on the burden of giving the same favor which you have received here
to someone else.

Carry this responsibility well, Mr Belli.

--end quoted text

DD
```

```
d>
d> My brother glanced at the screen and said 'This might be because
d> *here* you spell the name with one 'b' and *there* you are spelling it
d> with two?'

   In my last days at Unisys I had to travel to Mannheim, which is  
about one hour's drive away, because my colleague at the new  
installation there did not get the TCP/IP system up and running which  
had to communicate with the tape cartridge robot.

   I had a look at the configuration listing and quite quickly found  
that little dot at the beginning of that line which made the rest of  
it a comment...

   It was a nice Saturday afternoon, with extra pay for that trip.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

Als er am Kirchhofe vorbei ging, sagte er: Die da kï¿½nnen nun sicher sein, daï¿½ sie nicht mehr gehï¿½ngt werden, das kï¿½nnen wir nicht. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
That's one good thing about languages that terminate with ";" instead of 
"." - the semicolon is much easier to see.  (And even less likely to be 
confused with a comma...)
```

```
Lueko Willms <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote:

[snip]

>   I had a look at the configuration listing and quite quickly found  
>that little dot at the beginning of that line which made the rest of  
>it a comment...

Hmmmmmm... wasn't someone here recently advocating a COBOL style using 
free-format source that put the period for the preceding paragraph on the 
same line of the next paragraph's name?  Something like:

 Procedure Division
.First-Para
.   If [cond]
---
    End-If
.Second-Para
.

... or along those lines.  Those who do not learn from the mistakes of the 
past and all that... hee, hee, hee.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Build with FJ 4.0 under W/2K

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T12:53:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MZPy9L9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<7711efdd.0412051937.64c30c6f@posting.google.com> <9MVJOgJeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cp9hhf$p2j$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
.     Am  09.12.04
 schrieb  docdwarf@panix.com
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  cp9hhf$p2j$1@panix5.panix.com
   ueber  Re: Error in Build with FJ 4.0 under W/2K

>>   I had a look at the configuration listing and quite quickly found
>> that little dot at the beginning of that line which made the rest of
>> it a comment...

d> Hmmmmmm... wasn't someone here recently advocating a COBOL style using
d> free-format source that put the period for the preceding paragraph on
d> the same line of the next paragraph's name?  Something like:
d>
d>  Procedure Division
d> .First-Para

   I'm not sure, but I would think that this is not correct syntax.

   But I have always put in my COBOL programs the full stop on a line  
by itself, so that it would stick out, and also so that I could add,  
delete, or move lines without having to add or delete the dot at the  
end of that line.

   And with the proper structuring statements from COBOL-85, I do put  
dots, er full stop, period, only where it is syntactically required,  
e.g. at the end of a section or a data declaration.

   On the other hand, I think that the syntax of that configuration  
file specifying a single dot as a comment marker is not very wise. It  
should be something more obtrusive.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Die Arbeit in weiï¿½er Haut kann sich nicht dort emanzipieren, wo sie
in schwarzer Haut gebrandmarkt wird."     - Karl Marx     12.11.1866
```

```
wrote:

>.    On  08.12.04

>   I had a look at the configuration listing and quite quickly found  
>that little dot at the beginning of that line which made the rest of  
>it a comment...

I once spent an hour staring at a C program, trying to figure out why
a large block of code didn't seem to be executing. When someone else
opened it with a color-coded editor (VisDev), it showed in GREEN.
Duuuh .. the whole 'paragraph' was a comment. 

In C, the mistake is especially easy to make and hard to see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error in Build with FJ 4.0 under W/2K

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T10:57:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102618669.031906.147300@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1ntgr0hpnn0k4l3404rd410u7lhdbrc245@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7711efdd.0412051937.64c30c6f@posting.google.com> <1102503274.180384.168240@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <cp6upr$dqu$1@panix5.panix.com> <9MVJOgJeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1ntgr0hpnn0k4l3404rd410u7lhdbrc245@4ax.com>`

```

> In C, the mistake is especially easy to make and hard to see.

What is easy to see is entirely what one is used to seeing, here you
are simply blaming the tools for your inadequacies (which may simply be
your unfamiliarity).

Many years ago, when I was doing customer support for Cobol compilers,
I would see many experienced programmers blaming the language and the
compiler for such things as NOTE statements and full-stops in the
middle of IF blocks.  Things that they had spent days gathering
evidence to prove it was a compiler fault.

> When someone else opened it with a color-coded editor
> (VisDev), it showed in GREEN.

Adequate tools certainly help.  Was it your own editor that you were
using ?
```

```
> that on the Davox side, two zeroes pad the rightmost portion of

> dollars and sixty-nine cents appears properly on the COBOL output
file
> (1833.69), but after loaded to Davox it looks (so I am told) much
> different (183.3600 in "OVERDUE"; 9 in "INOVERDUE").

probably be able to help you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Take that Linux!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T09:18:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10re6qb7bl88k2b@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<n6mdnZMqYO_E5CncRVn-og@giganews.com> <31ll4rF3bj72lU1@individual.net> <10rbcpkq13jif4b@news.supernews.com> <wimtd.458671$%k.405030@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
> Out of curiosity what's an easy source for kosher/non-kosher foods. I'd 
> check it out with a rabbi - just think of the fun you could have
> pulling the tail feathers out of its ass, while  it sits on your
> plate.

Easy sources for kosher designation:
1. Ask a rabbi.
2. Look for the kosher mark on the wrapper (circle-U, circle-K, and others).
3. All raw vegetables (processing may introduce non-kosher ingredients).

Kosher refers to the food intrinsically as well as how it is prepared or 
slaughtered.

Then there's a whole other thing for "Kosher for Passover."

Interesting thing about birds and kosherness.

The Bible lists seven birds you cannot eat and is silent on the permitted 
ones. So the rabbis set out to discover the clue. Of these seven, some were 
scavengers, some not. Some had two toes forward and two back. Some didn't. 
Some flew at night, others didn't. Oh well.

The rabbis threw up their hands (Oh vey!) and decided that the birds 
sacrificed in the original temple were okay (doves, quail, etc.), and others 
thru tradition (ducks, geese).

A thousand years pass. Every Jew on the planet knows what birds are 
permitted.

Columbus sailed for the new world the day after the Jews were expelled from 
Spain; his navigtor was Jewish. Jews were early in the New World.

They find a critter called a "turkey." **

Letters go back and forth between the New World Jews and the sages of the 
generation:

"Is 'Turkey' kosher?"
"What is "turkey?"
"It's a big bird. Tail feathers like a peacock, but all brown."
"Does it eat carrion?"
"We don't know. It eats corn."
"What's corn?"
and so on.

Ultimately, the rabbis decide a turkey is more like a chicken than a vulture 
and turkey, the animal, is deemed kosher. Everybody is notified.

Another several hundred years go by.

Penguins.

The rabbis refuse to start that ball rolling again. I think there is a 
form-letter all rabbis use when this question comes up, saying, in short: 
"Get a life."

-----------
** There are several opinions as to the origin of the word "Turkey." One is 
it is related to the Hebrew word for peacock, "Tuki."
```

```
JerryMouse wrote:

>James J. Gavan wrote:
>  
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks for that Jerry, it was interesting.

My own association with the word "kosher" was my very first job as a kid 
out of school, working in Portsmouth Health Department as a junior. My 
main and soul destroying job, filing cards for kids alphabetically for 
the city where other staff had taken them out for 
inoculations/vaccinations at different schools, then they were dumped on 
my desk to be re-filed.

Sideline - I was available on phone for the 'City Veterinarian' - not 
sure of correct title - but he was a Scots veterinarian.. Somebody raps 
on my frosted window - I open it and little angry old lady shoves an 
opened can of meat/veggies, whatever, under my nose and says "It 
stinks". I have to endure her tirade and say I'll pass it on to my Scots 
'friend'. Not sure how it worked, but presumably some lab did tests. The 
point about the vet was he inspected abatoirs/slaughterhouses, for 
cleanliness, compliance with regulations etc. Through him I was aware of 
rabbis performing ritualistic killing of cows/bulls, (slitting the 
throat ?), then the meat became 'kosher'.

You've got to love this. A very likeable Jew can't remember his first 
name, (last =  Seftel), just died some few days ago at age 91. Opened an 
hotel-cum-restaurant here back in late sixties with reasonably priced 
fare. Quite popular with the older age groups. We were there some time 
back near Easter. As an additional ploy on the way out you can buy a 
selection of their own home-baked goods. We gave it a try - kosher Hot 
Cross Buns. Nothing like the Jewish entrepreneurial spirit !

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-7f29a177d1"></a>
## Thread: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T22:48:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`

```
Just as a new thread, I wanted to post my observations and thoughts on this 
(most previously posted).

1) It is my impression that the use of (all) SECTIONS is more common in Europe 
and the use of (all) PARAGRAPHS is more common in the US. (I don't know about 
Asia or Pacific or India or Canada, etc).  This observation comes from my time 
working at Micro Focus (and dealing with customers in both regions.)

2) It is my perception that having ALL "Sections" (possibly with a single 
paragraph and/or exit paragraph) *OR* having all PARAGRAPHS is each an "equally 
maintainable" style (and not error prone). While having a MIXTURE of the two is 
not a "good idea" (IMHO).

3) Having numbers as the prefix of EITHER paragraphs or sections is ENTIRELY a 
"style" issue and never (or rarely?) causes any maintainability or enhancement 
problems.  It is USEFUL with some editors (e.g. ISPF) and not useful with 
others.  (I have yet to see any cases where numbered procedures are "bad" - but 
I can see why - in some environments - they would be useless.).  I do know that 
in "large IBM mainframe shops" where I have seen it used consistently (with 
"normal" ranges for various functions) it can make "picking up" someone else's 
code easier to understand or fix quickly.  However, this is probably true for 
ANY "structure" technique that is used consistently within a large shop.

4) Having a SECTION with multiple (other than just an -EXIT) paragraph within
     OR
    Having multiple PARAGRAPHS (other than an -EXIT) in a PERFORM range
is equally error-prone.

5) Some (possibly many) sites that "traditionally" have PERFORM xxxx THRU 
xxxx-EXIT standards *also* have software (or at least procedures) to insure that 
NO paragraph header appears between "xxxx" and "xxxx-EXIT".

6) Using the PERFORM xxxx THRU xxxx-EXIT technique (or using the SECTION with 
an -EXIT procedure) provides the functionality that is provided as an extension 
to the '85 Standard or in the '02 Standard of doing an "immediate exit" from 
within a procedure (i.e EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION).   Although it is POSSIBLE to 
avoid this technique with switches and/or deeply nested conditionals, I think 
that the ability to "exit immediately under some logic situations" is a useful 
tool in COBOL. Furthermore, (IMHO) this ability outweighs any potential problems 
of PERFORM ... THRU - when sufficient safeguards are in effect to avoid other 
types of GO TO and/or middle-of-the range paragraph names.  (I personally prefer 
EXIT PARAGRAPH - as it avoids "potentially error-prone" labels -  but accept 
that it is not generally available.)

7) Just as a "side-note" I usually PERFORM rather than GO TO a "kill/stop the 
application" procedure (such as an ABEND or EXEC CICS RETURN paragraph).  I know 
this isn't ever going to come back, but it is easier to insure that my 
applications do NOT include any "bad" GO TO's.

8) Just as there is debate about how to "modify" OO properties (see separate 
thread), there is also "debate" as to whether doing a "downward" GO TO within a 
single procedure is or is not "structured" code.  It *does* maintain the "single 
entry / single exit" rule - but also DOES a "go to label" statement.   If your 
view of structured coding does NOT allow this structure *and* you feel that 
following "strict structured coding" philosophy is important, then don't use it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T21:17:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch9td.5378$Yq6.3902@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> 3) Having numbers as the prefix of EITHER paragraphs or sections is ENTIRELY a 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANY "structure" technique that is used consistently within a large shop.

I agree.  It can be "bad" (or, for those opposed to the terms "good" and 
"bad", less-than-maintainable) when these numbers are not sequential, 
though, as the reasoning behind numbered paragraphs, if I've been told 
correctly, was to help programmers working with paper listings.  By 
referencing a paragraph number, they'd know whether to flip the stack of 
paper up or down.

One of the toughest programs I've ever worked had a 3-D table in 
working-storage, non-sequenced numbered paragraphs, and program flow so 
tightly controlled by GO TO that you'd think the original programmer 
didn't realized that COBOL has a natural flow to it.  :)  The fix ended 
up being a Pic X field into which they were moving a Pic 9 Comp field, 
then trying to do math on it - a one-character fix.  Sure was a bear 
finding it though...
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T21:13:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102396398.424714.98520@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`

```
Bill et al:

I may have missed some things here, but thought you might be interested
in anecdotal information from the late '60s thru early '80s.

Sections were used, particularly on Burroughs systems, with numbers. I
also recall them being used on some old Honeywell code that I converted
to run under MVS/XA, and I seem to recall them being used with the old
UNIVAC 1100 machines. The number used (not part of the label)
determined if the section was subject to roll-out (paging) or if the
section was "fixed". Seems that OS/360 systems also used this for
overlay. As a result, a lot of code that I saw for many a year had
SECTIONs done where the housekeeping routines were swappable and the
main routines were not. Also, the way the INPUT and OUTPUT SECTIONS
were done allowed those blocks of code to be swapped in or out
depending on where you were in SORT/MERGE.

Later,
Steve Thompson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T05:45:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Trbtd.392774$SM5.23004@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com> <1102396398.424714.98520@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
"priority numbers" were an ANSI/ISO Standard for the "segmentation module". 
This did, in deed, provide information on when "overlays" *COULD* (not required) 
be done.

I don't think this has anything to do with "prefixing" paragraph/section names 
with numbers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T12:30:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MR7BBf9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  06.12.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com
  about  Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics


WMK> 1) It is my impression that the use of (all) SECTIONS is more common
WMK> in Europe and the use of (all) PARAGRAPHS is more common in the US.
WMK> (I don't know about Asia or Pacific or India or Canada, etc).  This
WMK> observation comes from my time working at Micro Focus (and dealing
WMK> with customers in both regions.)

   Well -- I'm from Europe.

   Maybe Chuck Stevens could add from his experiences to this  
observation.

   I also state that the normal hierarchical subdivision of a COBOL  
program, as Peter Dashwood already pointed out, is

   DIVISION
      SECTION
         paragraph
           etc


   and that therefore the first division of the PROCEDURE DIVISON  
should be in SECTIONs.


   My rule was (using COBOL-74, i.e. before proper structuring was  
possible):

   - divide the PROCEDURE DIVISION in separate procedures as SECTIONs  
according to logically separate tasks;

   - SECTIONs may only be accessed by PERFORM <procedurename>, and  
PERFORM may only reference SECTIONs;

   - Paragraphs are labels which may only be referenced by a GOTO  
(should such be unavoidable), and only within the SECTION itself,  
preferably the standad paragraph names ANFANG (english: BEGIN) and  
ENDE (english: END, but which would have to be replaced by something  
which is not a reserved word), which obviously forces qualification of  
that label by SECTION name.

   Labels would have to be used e.g. by a GOTO DEPENDING ON integer1  
which is to be replaced nowadays by an EVALUATE integer1.


   As I had already mentioned in another contribution, I have seen a  
program text where every SECTION was terminated by an EXIT SECTION,  
indented to the left, so that it stuck out from the actual procedural  
statements. I found that very sympathetic.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Gerade das Gegenteil tun heiï¿½t auch nachahmen, es heiï¿½t nï¿½mlich das Gegenteil nachahmen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections, Paragraphs, and related topics

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T14:56:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4gc6$cf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<il5td.372304$SM5.21434@news.easynews.com>`

```

On  6-Dec-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> 1) It is my impression that the use of (all) SECTIONS is more common in Europe
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> working at Micro Focus (and dealing with customers in both regions.)

Mine as well.

And I have to say, even if I don't care for the confrontational way he says it,
my opinion about SECTIONs is very similar to that of Mr. Wagner.


> 2) It is my perception that having ALL "Sections" (possibly with a single
> paragraph and/or exit paragraph) *OR* having all PARAGRAPHS is each an
> "equally
> maintainable" style (and not error prone). While having a MIXTURE of the two
> is not a "good idea" (IMHO).

That is not my perception.   I want to eliminate all drop-through code.   If a
SECTION has multiple paragraphs, I don't like it.   If it doesn't, why not avoid
the opportunity for error and eliminate the word SECTION.

I can't win this argument though until we get compilers with EXIT PARAGRAPH
available.


> 3) Having numbers as the prefix of EITHER paragraphs or sections is ENTIRELY a
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANY "structure" technique that is used consistently within a large shop.

I absolutely agree with you here.


> 4) Having a SECTION with multiple (other than just an -EXIT) paragraph within
>      OR
>     Having multiple PARAGRAPHS (other than an -EXIT) in a PERFORM range
> is equally error-prone.

I have been stuck with an error section in a copy member with my code, so I have
been sticking in a dummy Section name to avoid warning error.   It's sort of
like the olden dayze when we CoBOL SORTs required SECTIONs, and we dutifully put
them in, followed by a dummy REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION to avoid drop-through
errors.


> 7) Just as a "side-note" I usually PERFORM rather than GO TO a "kill/stop the
> application" procedure (such as an ABEND or EXEC CICS RETURN paragraph).  I
> know
> this isn't ever going to come back, but it is easier to insure that my
> applications do NOT include any "bad" GO TO's.

Some optimizers work better when you do this.
```

```
Application to run a machine shop. Old and company out of business. 

When going into certain parts of application. An error occurs.

COBOL I/O error 94, 40  REBUILD\PCLABOR\PCHEADER

The all caps text above is the path and file name.

Anyone know how to fix this error? Any help appreciated. I am lost on
this one. I am not a COBOL person!


TIA
David
```

```
It is telling you to rebuild a corrupted file. Look for a program
named REBUILD.exe. If that doesn't solve the problem, it would be very
helpful to know which compiler was used. Look for a copyright notice
inside the application's executable.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL application with errors.....any help?

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T12:12:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102450320.074763.247620@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<m7aar0l8bbdkcfoi3j1vemrnpilhj5uif5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<m7aar0l8bbdkcfoi3j1vemrnpilhj5uif5@4ax.com>`

```
> COBOL I/O error 94, 40 REBUILD\PCLABOR\PCHEADER

That seems to indicate that the Cobol system you are using is RM Cobol
- Ryan-Mcfarland. This message is the standard format for that
implementation. The '94' file status error has a qualifier of '40',
which is how RM does indicate file-status errors.

The actual error indicated by this is that for that file the primary
key has an 'invalid or mismatched key offset'.  It seems that there is
a mismatch between how the program specifies the keys and how the file
has recorded how the keys should be specified.

This may be that the data file has been corrupted somehow so that this
information does not match.  It would be necessary to know exactly how
the program requires the file to be written in order to know if it can
be fixed.  It may be that if you move the file elsewhere (delete it in
that directory) the program will recreate an empty file.  If it does
this then it may be possible to recreate enough data (by keying it in)
to get that part working again.

The reindexing program is RECOVERY in RM systems:

RECOVERY datafile outputunrecoverablefile

But if the key information is corrupt then I don't know how to fix
this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old COBOL application with errors.....any help?

- **From:** mosnar <locals@spotbeam.net>

- **References:** `<m7aar0l8bbdkcfoi3j1vemrnpilhj5uif5@4ax.com>`

```
Thanks for the response.

I got it going. Rebuild.exe not available in this version. RMCobol was
correct. There were files called recover, recover1 and recover2.COB.
Although trying these were fruitless. Restoring just the one corrupt
file got them back up with limited loss of data.

Thanks again.
```

## Thread: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS — 155 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T04:44:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
These standards apply to all new Cobol code.

.. Fixed format.

.. A period at the end of every non-nested statement.

.. No blank data names. 
    You must code FILLER.
   If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

.. No inline comments.

.. No REDEFINES.

.. No qualification.
   
.. No more than one statement per line.
   Don't put COPY on the same line with 01.

.. File descriptions in copybooks
   One each for Select, FD and record (in working-storage). If a
   file contains multiple record types, for example header and
   trailer, there must be a copybook for each format.
   If two files contain the same format, there must be separate
record copybooks for each file.

.. No REPLACING.
   If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
   six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

.. No common key areas.
   When key fields in the record are not contiguous, you will assemble
   keys in working-storage for two purposes: to sequence check and to
   phase files. There must be three areas per file: current sort key,
   previous sort key and match key. If match key is identical to sort
   key, a separate area is still required. Field names are prefixed
   with the file name and either curr, prev or mtch. Using a single
   key definition with OCCURS is not permitted.

.. No shared File Status code.
   Code a separate pic xx field for each file.
   This implies no common error handling; each file has its own.

.. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
   record areas. 

.. Counters must have unique names.
   Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.
   Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

Procedure Division

.. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
   in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

.. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

.. No backward PERFORMs.

.. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
   get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

.. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than arithmetic.
   For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
   you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
   the records and test whether the counter is zero.

.. For input files, control adds and sequence checking goes in I/O
   paragraph(s). For output files, it goes in business logic.

.. Follow the spec closely.
   For example, if the spec says:
       evaluate true
           when A < B
             ...
           when A = B
             ...
           when A > B
             ...
   write it that way. Do NOT write:
       evaluate A
           when < B
             ...
           when B
             ...
           when > B
             ...

   If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

.. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right
   Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
   Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
             ws-imbalance-2 = zero

.. ZERO is numeric.
   When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.
```

```
Assuming a z/OS environment and *any* compiler that is  currently supported 
there (other mainframes may have different issues/restrictions) ... See below 
for some specific comments:

I have NOT commented on those items that I (personally) think are "good" in 
order to provide ease in "picking up" someone else's code.  (as stated 
elsewhere, consistency "aids" this - not necessarily one decision over another).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T07:57:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ondtd.98517$7i4.43940@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Hpbtd.392658$SM5.22976@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <Hpbtd.392658$SM5.22976@news.easynews.com>`

```


William M. Klein wrote:

> Assuming a z/OS environment and *any* compiler that is  currently supported 
> there (other mainframes may have different issues/restrictions) ... See below 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
 > wmk > inline comments not available with any IBM mainframe compiler

Perhaps what Mr. Wagner meant was a rule that disallows any procedure 
division comments except between the end of one paragraph and the 
beginning of another.  For example:

*******************************************************
* THIS PARAGRAPH READS THE CONTROL CARD FILE AND      *
* AND VALIDATES THE CONTROL INFORMATION               *
*******************************************************

READ-CONTROL-CARD.
     READ SYSIN-CONTROL-FILE INTO WS-CONTROL-CARD
     AT END
         DISPLAY 'CONTROL CARD IS MISSING'
         GO TO BLOW-EM-UP-GOOD.

*******************************************************
*   CHECK FOR VALID CONTROL DATE (THIS IS AN INLINE   *
*   COMMENT).                                         *
*******************************************************
     IF WS-CONTROL-DATE-MM IS GREATER THAN 12
          yada, yada, yada...


With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T18:57:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k2ntd.6367856$6p.1000265@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <Hpbtd.392658$SM5.22976@news.easynews.com> <Ondtd.98517$7i4.43940@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cenbr01bhi8kjja3guupe57l7utgnn7s6l@4ax.com>`

```
As I stated, the extension (to the '85 Standard) of

   *>  this is an inline comment

isn't supported by any current (or past) IBM mainframe compiler.  Other 
mainframes may have implemented this '02 Standard feature already - I don't 
know.

P.S.  Same for Free-form reference format and "split keys"
```

```
On Tue, 07 Dec 2004 18:57:20 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>P.S.  Same for Free-form reference format and "split keys"

Split keys was a reference to flat files. It would also apply to
databases, although it's hard to imagine row-at-a-time sequential
processing in that environment.
```
