###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T21:17:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q5ptd.4276182$yk.644148@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <Hpbtd.392658$SM5.22976@news.easynews.com> <Ondtd.98517$7i4.43940@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cenbr01bhi8kjja3guupe57l7utgnn7s6l@4ax.com> <cp53o0$baa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,
   No IBM mainframe shop would allow this (as specified in the '02 Standard 
*and* as an extension to the '85 Standard by some vendors).  I think you may be 
confusing:

  05  Field-1.
*>  The next field is something
       10  Another-field Pic X.

*versus*

05  Field-1.  *> this is a group item
      10 Another-Field Pic X.

IBM mainframe compilers will accept the former, but the latter (what RW was 
referring to) would cause a compiler error.
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T08:34:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FWdtd.98602$7i4.59383@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
Without going into style wars or rationalizations, I will just point 
out the differences between these shop standards and the ones in my 
mainframe shop.  I didn't write those shop standards, but I am 
comfortable using them.

Robert Wagner wrote:

> These standards apply to all new Cobol code.
> 
> .. Fixed format.
> 
> .. A period at the end of every non-nested statement.

No requirement for this in my shop.  Some programmers are using the 
single period per paragraph style now.

> 
> .. No blank data names. 
>     You must code FILLER.
>    If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

Not such restrictions in my shop standards.  I've seen blank instead 
of filler and FILLER for flag fields that have condition names 
defined.  Due to our data naming standards, a proper condition name 
would look like 88-100-ALL-DATA-PROCESSED.  The original justification 
was so that all condition names would appear next to each other in the 
cross reference listing, but still include the numeric prefix that 
identified the 01 record where they resided, for example, in the 
working storage 100-PROGRAM-SWITCHES record.

> 
> .. No inline comments.

If you mean comments within a paragraph, as opposed to a flower box 
between the end of the last paragraph and before the paragraph name, 
then inline comments are not restricted.

> 
> .. No REDEFINES.

No restrictions againt using REDEFINES.  RENAMES is somewhat 
discouraged, and I have never it used.

> 
> .. No qualification.

Qualification is not restricted, but our naming conventions require 
numbered datanames, with each 01 record having a unique prefix.  That 
pretty much rules out qualification, since there should be no 
duplicated data names to qualify.  But we don't enforce our naming 
conventions strictly.

>    
> .. No more than one statement per line.

Discouraged, but not prohibited.  Rarely seen.

>    Don't put COPY on the same line with 01.

No restrictions, but I've never seen it done in my shop.

> 
> .. File descriptions in copybooks
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> record copybooks for each file.

I have seen copybooks for FD's, but it's extremely rare and it is not 
required.  I've never seen copybooks for SELECT statements in my shop. 
  If a file has multiple record layouts, generally we put all the 
record layouts in one copybook.  Record layout copybooks are preferred 
in working-storage, rather than under the FD, but there is no 
restriction enforced.

> 
> .. No REPLACING.

No restrictions on replacing, but I haven't seen it used much, due to 
our naming conventions for data.

>    If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
>    six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

Copybooks for record layouts are encouraged, but not for SELECT or FD.

> 
> .. No common key areas.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    key definition with OCCURS is not permitted.

No such requirement in our standards.  It's left up to the programmer.

> 
> .. No shared File Status code.
>    Code a separate pic xx field for each file.
>    This implies no common error handling; each file has its own.

Separate file status for each file is preferred, but not required.

> 
> .. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
>    record areas. 

Each 01 record in Working-storage has a three-digit numeric prefix 
common to all fields in that 01 record, and unique to other 01 
records.  Copybook record layouts may have an alphanumeric dataname 
prefix.  Exceptions are not punished.

> 
> .. Counters must have unique names.
>    Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.
>    Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

No such restriction.

> 
> Procedure Division
> 
> .. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
>    in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

Our naming conventions are four-digit prefixes, but I have seen 
five-digit prefixes used.  Older programs (20+ years old) had some 
very weird prefix conventions, six character alphanumeric prefixes 
that no one understands anymore.

> 
> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

No GO TO, no THRU, No exits, No ALTER.  But our standards are not 
strictly enforced, and I have seen a few PERFROM THRU's and GO TO's in 
new production code.

> 
> .. No backward PERFORMs.
> 
> .. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
>    get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

The are no standardized paragraph names, but our style checker 
requires all paragraph names to begin with a numeric prefix, followed 
by an action verb (from a table of 206 action verbs), followed by a 
direct object.

I have seen older programs with an initilization paragraph named 
1000-HOUSEKEEPING.  Most newer programs have something like 
1000-INITIALIZE-THE-PROGRAM.

> 
> .. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than arithmetic.
>    For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
>    you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
>    the records and test whether the counter is zero.

The exact method is left to the programmer's discretion.

> 
> .. For input files, control adds and sequence checking goes in I/O
>    paragraph(s). For output files, it goes in business logic.

The exact method is left to the programmer's discretion.

> 
> .. Follow the spec closely.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>    If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

Again, all these are left up to the programmer.

> 
> .. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right
>    Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
>    Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
>              ws-imbalance-2 = zero

Left up to the programmer.

> 
> .. ZERO is numeric.
>    When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.
> 

ZERO, ZEROS, and ZEROES are figurative constants.  They can be either 
alphanumeric or numeric depending on context.  Our style checking 
program warns for "data type mismatch" (comparing packed decimal to 
PIC X, for example), but the ZERO figurative constant will never 
generate any such warning.

I'm not saying the standards in my shop are better than some other 
standards.  I'm just pointing out the differences.

One other thing is that our shop standards specifically allow the 
project leader to approve any variance.  So if you are on good terms 
with your project leader, you have quite a bit of latitude.

With kindest regards,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T14:39:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4fc5$ste$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <FWdtd.98602$7i4.59383@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  7-Dec-2004, Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> Without going into style wars or rationalizations, I will just point
> out the differences between these shop standards and the ones in my
> mainframe shop.  I didn't write those shop standards, but I am
> comfortable using them.

The only standard that he mentioned that comes close to a requirement here is
that we can't have a period on a line by itself where it won't be seen.  We
encourage the use of periods as we have a pre-compiler that puts in IF logic and
full stop periods are guaranteed to end a sentence.
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T12:18:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  07.12.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com
  about  MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS


   assuming these standards are genuine ...

   Some are OK, some are superflouus and some are really bad.

   E.g. those rules that the program has to implement the program  
specification in relation to nested or serialized conditional  
statements (IF/THEN/ELSE, EVALUATE/WHEN) - what else?

   But the rule on EVALUATE:

RW>    For example, if the spec says:
RW>        evaluate true
RW>            when A < B
RW>              ...
RW>            when A = B
RW>              ...
RW>            when A > B
RW>              ...
RW>    write it that way. Do NOT write:
RW>        evaluate A
RW>            when < B
RW>              ...
RW>            when B
RW>              ...
RW>            when > B
RW>              ...

   is superflouus since the syntax doesn't allow the "< B" and "> B"  
in the second version (if one of those IBM compilers does allow that  
non-standard syntax, the rule should simply state to avoid all those  
IBM extensions to the standard, or list those which are allowed (there  
might be some which make sense).

RW> .. Follow the spec closely.

   BTW, if the one writing the specification goes so deep into  
details, like this:

RW>    If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

   why doesn't that person write the program himself?

   Well, maybe because those rules make writing the program so awkward  
that nobody wants to do it? There is no need for a coder separate from  
the program designer if the rules do not stipulate to duplicate the  
work of the compiler and the program designer by the coder.

   Like this:

RW> .. No REDEFINES.
RW>
RW> .. No qualification.
RW>
RW> .. File descriptions in copybooks
RW>    One each for Select, FD and record (in working-storage). If a
RW>    file contains multiple record types, for example header and
RW>    trailer, there must be a copybook for each format.
RW>    If two files contain the same format, there must be separate
RW> record copybooks for each file.
RW>
RW> .. No REPLACING.
RW>    If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
RW>    six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

RW> .. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
RW>    record areas.

  The above is just foolish, I dare to say.



  while this:

RW> .. No shared File Status code.

              should be self-evident, whereas this:

RW>    Code a separate pic xx field for each file.

    makes me wonder --- it would be equally self-evident to have the  
File Status with all its condition names as a COPY element, but then  
one would have to REPLACE parts of it or use qualification ...

    Then this:

RW> .. Counters must have unique names.
RW>    Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.

   this should depend if the items being counted are actually a  
recurring item, or if there happen to be different items to be counted  
separately.

   But the following:

RW>    Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

   is again quite foolish. If it _is_ a recurring item, it should be  
handled as such.

   This makes me think of that famous beginner in programming, who was  
give the task of writing a program which had to read in and process a  
deck of cards, and who came back after some time asking for the number  
of cards in the deck. Since he got the answer "100", he coded 100  
separate READ statements in his program....


RW> .. No inline comments.

   I understand this to mean introducing comments with "*>" which then  
can be everywhere on a line, not just in position 7.

RW> Procedure Division

RW> .. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than  
arithmetic.
RW>    For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
RW>    you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
RW>    the records and test whether the counter is zero.

   This probably implies that the normal loop reading a file, i.e.  
with a loop which is executed once for every _available_ record, and  
not once more, i.e. for every _attempt_ go fetch a record, is not  
being used. Proper programming to process a sequential sequence with  
unknown number of occurences is
   1. try to get first
   2. IF no success
         leave it
      ELSE
         REPEAT
            process item
            try to get next
         UNTIL no success
      END-IF

   which makes the above rule so natural that it would not have to be  
mentioned.


RW> .. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
RW>    in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

   My dear goodness. If the logical structure is not clear by the  
names of the procedures, then trying to force that into numbers will  
fail.

   One will inevitably have similar or identical named procedure names  
or labels differentiated only by number, which is just confusing and  
generating errors.

RW> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

   My rule was and is, that object of a PERFORM can only be a SECTION,  
while labels (called Paragraphs in COBOLese) may only be referenced by  
GOTOs, and only within one and the same SECTION, and this only if the  
programmer is unable to write the structure properly (well, using a  
pre-1985 COBOL it may have been unavoidable sometimes, and did make a  
lot of sense in case of a GOTO DEPENDING ON some-integer).


RW> .. No backward PERFORMs.

   That makes sense.

   And this:

RW> .. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
RW>    get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

    is just some arbitrary rule, which standardizes naming.

    And the following also makes sense:

RW> .. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right

    but for compound conditions ... not necessarily

RW>    Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
RW>    Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
RW>              ws-imbalance-2 = zero

    And I would always write the boolean operator (AND or OR) _before_  
the expression it applies to, and not at the end of the previous, i.e.
        IF condition-A
        AND condition-B

    The same as I write
        4711
      + 0815

     and not 4711 +
             0815


    The following had already be covered:

RW>    When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.

    Better to use a condition name out of the COPY element to be used  
for every file status (use it for TYPEDEF with a compiler which  
supports it), but this requires qualification ...


   The whole sets of rules reflects someone full of fear of losing  
control to the compiler, trying to be as close as possible to writing  
some assembler, and trying to rule the actual storage bits and bytes  
in the machine instead of abstracting from the machine details and  
concentrating on the actual objects and their symbolic  
representations.

   It is a recipe for bad and inefficient programming producing many  
errors and forcing extensive and long testing and debugging hours,  
days or weeks.




Yours
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Als er am Kirchhofe vorbei ging, sagte er: Die da kï¿½nnen nun sicher sein, daï¿½ sie nicht mehr gehï¿½ngt werden, das kï¿½nnen wir nicht. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:24:25-05:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T19:03:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MRARBiPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cp4fk6$t3d$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
.     Am  07.12.04
 schrieb  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  cp4fk6$t3d$1@peabody.colorado.edu
   ueber  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

>> RW> .. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
>> RW>    record areas.
>>
>>   The above is just foolish, I dare to say.

   I meant all of the above, concerning naming conventions for  
variables, including unique names for each and every variable,  
prohibiting REDEFINES and that stuff.


HB> Foolish in what way?
HB> What kinds of trouble will such a foolish standard lead to?


   Maybe I use this word somewhat differently from those whose first  
language is English. So please don't nail me on differences between  
"foolish" and e.g. "silly" (Silly was a Rock band in East Berlin).


   Coming back to naming variables -- what this shop standard  
stipulates is to manage the machine storage space, finding a unique  
name for each and every storage address, instead of caring about  
identifying _objects,_ real objects or symbolic representations of  
them.

   Main storage space is administered by the compiler and the run time  
system, including the operating system, and should not concern the  
programmer. Otherwise, her or his attention is turned away from the  
_real_ task toward tasks better done by the compiler.

   Look at what this shop standards which we are talking about  
specified for file status and qualification. While I get a shudder  
thinking that anybody could think of specifying the same status  
variable for different files, the file status should not be coded anew  
for each and every program, but included via COPY with REPLACING the  
name, or better by COPYing once the definition as a TYPEDEF and using  
that TYPE repeatedly.

   Like this one which I made up for my use:

000010 01  FILESTAT .
000020*> FILESTAT sollte beim COPY durch passenden Namen REPLACEd werden
000030     88 File-Status-OK                VALUE '00'.
000040     02 Major             PIC X.
000050        88 is-Successful-completed           VALUE '0'.
000060        88 has-reached-at-end                VALUE '1'.
000070        88 has-invalid-key                   VALUE '2'.
000080        88 has-permanent-error               VALUE '3'.
000090        88 has-logical-error                 VALUE '4'.
000100        88 has-record-lock-problem           VALUE '5'. *> ab Standard 2002 und X/Open
000110        88 has-file-lock-problem             VALUE '6'. *> ab Standard 2002 und X/Open
000120        88 has-implementor-defined-condition VALUE '9'.
000130     02 Minor             PIC X.
000140        88 has-no-further-information        VALUE '0'.
000150        88 it-just-didnt-work                VALUE '1'.
000160        88 has-duplicate-key-error           VALUE '2'.
000170        88 has-record-not-available          VALUE '3'.
000180        88 has-boundary-violation            VALUE '4'.
000190        88 is-not-present-file-or-recdesc    VALUE '5'.
000200        88 has-sequence-error                VALUE '6'.
000210        88 has-some-incompatability          VALUE '7', '8', '9'.
000220

    The name FILESTAT has to be REPLACED by the actual name (or, leave  
that name out, and let them write "01 input-file-status COPY .... ",  
also verboten); and then one has of course to qualify the condition  
names for the actual file status, like a "IF has-reached-at-end OF  
input-file-status" or so.

    Whenever the same object occurs in different structures, e.g. in  
different files, in a SCREEN definition etc, it should have the same  
outspoken name, which of course need to be qualified.

    Naming variables is to identify objects, not main storage  
locations. To do it otherwise, one is led astray from the actual task  
of devising a transformation algorithm from one set of symbolic  
representations of reality to a different set. This is best done by  
just specifying the rules governing this transformation, and not by  
worrying about locations in the main memory of the machine which is  
being used to actually execute that transformation.

    Think of that transformation as manipulation of an n-dimensional  
vector space. And don't try to do the work of the compiler.



Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Es sind nicht die Generï¿½le und Kï¿½nige, die die Geschichte machen,
sondern die breiten Massen des Volkes"                - Nelson Mandela
```

On Tue, 7 Dec 2004 14:45:49 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On  7-Dec-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Could you provide examples of how these silly standards force this?

move ABLMNR1-CUST-NO to ABLMNX1-CUST-NO.

A typo in the cryptic filename 'qualifier' causes no compilation
error. Data is copied to the wrong place. Compare to:

move CUST-NO in input-record to CUST-NO in output-record.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T18:17:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 07 Dec 2004 12:18:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  07.12.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

>   But the rule on EVALUATE:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>might be some which make sense).

It is supported by the current Standard ('02) and the compiler in use.

>  while this:
>
>RW> .. No shared File Status code.
>
>              should be self-evident

It's not self-evident. The Standard does not require separate File
Status codes. Consider:

perform varying file-number from 1 by 1 until file-number > 10
    evaluate file-number
        when 1 open input file-1
        when 2 open input file-2
        ...
        when 10 open output file-10
   end-evaluate
   if  file-status-1 not equal to zero
      display 'open error ' file-status ' on file '
                  file-name (file-number)
       perform abend
   end-if
end-evaluate


>RW> .. No inline comments.
>
>   I understand this to mean introducing comments with "*>" which then  
>can be everywhere on a line, not just in position 7.

Yes.


>RW> .. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than  
>arithmetic.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>mentioned.

Like this:

perform read-file
if  end-of-file
    abort
end-if
perform until end-of-file
    process item
    perform read-file
end-perform

Not this:

perform read-file
perform until end-of-file
    process item
    perform read-file
end-perform
if  record-count = 0
    abort
end-if

>RW> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmer is unable to write the structure properly

Even Old Style shops avoid GO TO, except to abort.

>RW> .. No backward PERFORMs.
>
>   That makes sense.

Why? 

>   The whole sets of rules reflects someone full of fear of losing  
>control to the compiler, trying to be as close as possible to writing  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>representations.

Well said. It reflects a way of thinking that solves problems with
brute force rather than abstraction.

>   It is a recipe for bad and inefficient programming producing many  
>errors and forcing extensive and long testing and debugging hours,  
>days or weeks.

A well written program was 900 lines long. Rewritten according to this
standard, it took >2,000 lines.
```

As far as I can tell, both permit this:

Evaluate A
    when = B or C > D

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T20:22:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4hoer0hkdec0fsh8ij91p8v24538nt8h03@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <76ntd.4271806$yk.643505@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Tue, 07 Dec 2004 19:01:23 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>"partial expressons" in EVALUATE are t NOT by any compiler currently available 
>for z/OS - but may be for other mainframes.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the relational operator.

Partial expressions solve the problem of testing for multiple values.
Under '85 Cobol, we wrote:

evaluate A
  when 1
  when 2
  when 3
     perform B
  
I wish I had a nickel for every time I've seen:

evaluate A
  when 1
    perform B
  when 2
    perform B
  when 3
    perform B

Under '02 and current Micro Focus, we can write:

evaluate A
  when = 1 or 2 or 3
    perform B
```

evaluate A

ya'...  :)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T19:09:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com...
> On 07 Dec 2004 12:18:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
>>RW> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The *advantage* of PERFORM THRU (with  a compiler that does not yet support EXIT 
PARAGRAPH/SECTION) is *not* to "abort" (if you mean abort the program) but 
rather to do an "imdeiate exit" from the SPECIFIC procedure, i.e.

Perform 123-ABC thru 123-ABC-Exit

123-ABC.
    If A = X
        If B = Y
            Perform 987-XYZ thru 987-XYZ-exit
            Go to 123-ABC-Exit
         Else
            Perform 456-LMN thru 456-LMN-Exit
         End-If
     Display "Sitl in initial IF TRUE branch"
     Else
        Compute Q = Y + 3
            On size Erorr
                 Perform 999-Some-Error thru 999-Some-Error-Exit
                 Go To 123-ABC-Exit
             Not on Size Error
                 Perform 222-Something-Else thru 222-Something-Else-Exit
          End-Compute
       End-If
       Display "all has gone well"
            .
 123-ABC-Exit.
     Exit

***

Of course there ARE other ways this same "logic" could be structured (and I 
haven't compiled it - so I may have made a type that the COMPILER would have 
caught).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:13:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-37CC18.20133307122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com>`

```
I would call the ability to do an immediate exit a disadvantage.  

It only encourage programmers to take a lazy approach to structure and 
promotes paragraphs that become large and unwieldy.

One memorable error went something like this:

1234-ABC.

   ... dozens of lines ...

      If A = B
            continue
      Else
            Go To 1234-Exit
      End-If

   ... dozens more lines ...

      If A = B
            continue
      Else
           Perform 5678-Do-Stuff thru 5678-Exit
      End-If

   ... dozens more lines ...

1234-Exit
   Exit.      


The critical 5678-Do-Stuff was, of course, never executed.

Early bail-outs only encourage people to write things like the above.



In article <8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com>,
 "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> "Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
> news:30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com...
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> caught).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T07:14:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MVCe4KPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com>`

```
.     Am  07.12.04
 schrieb  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com
   ueber  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

WMK> The *advantage* of PERFORM THRU

   can there be an advantage?

WMK> (with  a compiler that does not yet support EXIT PARAGRAPH
WMK> /SECTION) is *not* to "abort" (if you mean abort the
WMK> program) but rather to do an "imdeiate exit" from the SPECIFIC
WMK> procedure, i.e.

    Whow, another piece of bad programming -- mixing proper structure  
with hopping around in the computer's memory.

   Delete all those GOTO, which are only there "to confuse the  
Russians", but in reality more the programmer her- or himself:

   The exception is the "DISPLAY 'all has gone well'" -- well, one may  
have to think deeper about all those other actions and conditions.


     Perform ABC

     ABC SECTION.
     ANFANG.
         If A = X
             If B = Y
                 Perform 987-XYZ thru 987-XYZ-exit
              Else
                 Perform 456-LMN thru 456-LMN-Exit
                 Display "Sitl in initial IF TRUE branch"
              End-If
          Else
             Compute Q = Y + 3
                 On size Erorr
                      Perform 999-Some-Error thru 999-Some-Error-Exit
                  Not on Size Error
                      Perform 222-Something-Else
                                 thru 222-Something-Else-Exit
              End-Compute
           End-If
           *> ........................................

           Display "all has gone well"



   Do you see any significant difference, except that it is much  
easier to understand, and the sense of the final DISPLAY cannot (by  
me) understood without seeing more of the context?



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de

sondern die breiten Massen des Volkes"                - Nelson Mandela
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T19:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4BItd.487542$SM5.28667@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <8entd.4272126$yk.643554@news.easynews.com> <9MVCe4KPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
My example was contrived and I think (hope) I indicated it could be restructured 
in many other ways.

The most common place where I have seen (IMHO) "good" usage of the "get me out 
quick" technique is in a single procedure that does "field validation" of 
several input fields (from a "character based" screen).  In these cases, the 
FIRST field (usually checked from the bottom of the screen up) that has an error 
causes an error message to be issued - then the program logic "exits" the entire 
routine (as there is no need to check further).  This CAN be done by "deeply 
nested" IF statements - but I (personally) find that LESS maintainable.

This is, however, (again in my opinion) a style matter - not a "best practice" 
(for all programmers and all shops) matter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:16:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp5327$avp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`

```

On  7-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> Like this:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-if

What makes one of these superior to the other?

Lots of times a standard just makes it easier for everybody to do maintenance -
if all the programs have the same style.   Unless there is compelling reason why
the other style is superior, this reason is good enough to follow the standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T01:58:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gbjcr0lrgdalqompoutviun77q6udqm935@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <cp5327$avp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Tue, 7 Dec 2004 20:16:07 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  7-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>the other style is superior, this reason is good enough to follow the standard.

This was a simplified example. Suppose there were four input files,
each having several control checks -- record count, hash, etc. I
prefer to put all control validation in one place rather than
scatterred through the program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T15:00:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102460436.865164.267050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`

```
> It is supported by the current Standard ('02) and the compiler in
use.

I find that argument particularly shallow.

Many of the standards you listed were to eliminate non-standard
extensions and permit portability of code to other compilers should
this be necessary or expedient.

One of the main advantages of Cobol is that it is _Common_.  It is
available in the same way in a number of systems.  None of those
systems implement the whole of the '02 standard yet, and many may not
do so at all.  Using proprietry extensions will make changing to other
systems more difficult (ie lockin).  It is useful to the business'  to
maintain flexability and avoid high-cost conversions or higher cost
lock-in.

For example it may be that in the future they would want to distribute
the system to hundreds of PCs around the country.  If they were locked
into requiring MicroFocus then each would require an expensive
run-time.  If they wanted to switch to Fujitsu and benefit from free
run-times saving hundreds of thousands of dollars then they would be
able to do so easily if all MF extensions were excluded from the code.

Some other of the standards (FILLER, REDEFINES, 1 statement/line) were
to cater for debuggers and other utilities to give the best results
from those.

Just because you have no idea _why_ these are imposed doesn't mean they
are 'bad'.

If you don't like them, don't work there.  If they fail to find anyone
to work under those conditions then they may change them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T01:58:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94lcr05nn5t17hc44pc9gj5r1ipfmrsaeo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460436.865164.267050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 15:00:36 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> It is supported by the current Standard ('02) and the compiler in
>use.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>this be necessary or expedient.

The 'non-standard extensions' (to '85) are in the CURRENT Cobol
Standard, and supported by both Micro Focus and Fujitsu. Porting was
most unlikely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T18:40:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102473619.969188.110890@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<94lcr05nn5t17hc44pc9gj5r1ipfmrsaeo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460436.865164.267050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <94lcr05nn5t17hc44pc9gj5r1ipfmrsaeo@4ax.com>`

```
> The 'non-standard extensions' (to '85) are in the CURRENT Cobol
> Standard, and supported by both Micro Focus and Fujitsu.

No they aren't.  For example the EVALUATE that is not allowed is
rejected by Fujitsu (based on actual testing):

JMN2535I-S  WORD '=' CANNOT BE SPECIFIED IN EVALUATE STATEMENT.

Being in the current standard is irrelevant until compilers for that
standard are generally available and have been moved into production
use.

> Porting was most unlikely.

I didn't realise that you were part of the management policy committee
and were circulated with all the sales proposals.  I thought you were
just a coder being told what to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T05:02:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9n2dr0pftpsht9kad0gp0lvtkr8o9ngk64@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460436.865164.267050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <94lcr05nn5t17hc44pc9gj5r1ipfmrsaeo@4ax.com> <1102473619.969188.110890@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 7 Dec 2004 18:40:20 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> The 'non-standard extensions' (to '85) are in the CURRENT Cobol
>> Standard, and supported by both Micro Focus and Fujitsu.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>JMN2535I-S  WORD '=' CANNOT BE SPECIFIED IN EVALUATE STATEMENT.

You're right. The POINT of the shop standard was don't deviate from
the spec. Evaluate was an example. I should have used a conforming
example.

>Being in the current standard is irrelevant until compilers for that
>standard are generally available and have been moved into production
>use.

Some of the prohibitions have been in place 20 years: COPY ..
REPLACING, REDEFINES, no periods, ctc.
```

>>Standard, and supported by both Micro Focus and Fujitsu.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

Indeed. Is there such a thing as twenty year old code that has never

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T13:35:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpl1ub$f38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460436.865164.267050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <94lcr05nn5t17hc44pc9gj5r1ipfmrsaeo@4ax.com> <1102475278.086314.244340@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1102475278.086314.244340@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...

> Being in the current standard is irrelevant until compilers for that
> standard are generally available and have been moved into production
> use.

This might be regarded as heresy by some, but I'd argue that for a
*particular feature* it's not all that relevant whether compilers
implementing *all* of the standard containing the feature are generally
available; what's relevant is whether compilers implementing *that feature*
are generally available, whether those compilers conform to the rest of the
standard specifying the feature or not.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T15:01:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102460489.223611.190350@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`

```
> It is supported by the current Standard ('02) and the compiler in
use.

I find that argument particularly shallow.

Many of the standards you listed were to eliminate non-standard
extensions and permit portability of code to other compilers should
this be necessary or expedient.

One of the main advantages of Cobol is that it is _Common_.  It is
available in the same way in a number of systems.  None of those
systems implement the whole of the '02 standard yet, and many may not
do so at all.  Using proprietry extensions will make changing to other
systems more difficult (ie lockin).  It is useful to the business'  to
maintain flexability and avoid high-cost conversions or higher cost
lock-in.

For example it may be that in the future they would want to distribute
the system to hundreds of PCs around the country.  If they were locked
into requiring MicroFocus then each would require an expensive
run-time.  If they wanted to switch to Fujitsu and benefit from free
run-times saving hundreds of thousands of dollars then they would be
able to do so easily if all MF extensions were excluded from the code.

Some other of the standards (FILLER, REDEFINES, 1 statement/line) were
to cater for debuggers and other utilities to give the best results
from those.

Just because you have no idea _why_ these are imposed doesn't mean they
are 'bad'.

If you don't like them, don't work there.  If they fail to find anyone
to work under those conditions then they may change them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T02:10:41+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T20:23:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FE8D2D.20231607122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460521.016734.278130@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1102460521.016734.278130@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
 "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Just because you have no idea _why_ these are imposed doesn't mean they
> are 'bad'.

Paragraph numbers help one find ones place in printed listings.

Perform/Thru with forward-only Go Tos allow somewhat structured code to 
be written in a language that doesn't support structured programming.

Line numbers help one if they drop their deck.  

Upper-case only source allows it to be displayed on all terminals.

These are all reasonable and valid standards for the problems they were 
intended to address.

If they remain imposed once the original reason for imposing them is 
relieved, well, then they become arbitrary and pointless.

And arbitrary and pointless are usually considered _not_good_.

And the negation of good is _bad_.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T18:23:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102472591.000633.265860@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FE8D2D.20231607122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <1102460521.016734.278130@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <joe_zitzelberger-FE8D2D.20231607122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```

> If they remain imposed once the original reason for imposing them is
> relieved, well, then they become arbitrary and pointless.

I wouldn't agree with all the standards that were listed.  As you point
out, the reasons for those you picked are obsolete.  However, many of
the others are to preserve portability in current systems and therefore
a _good_thing_.

Some may be good, some may be bad.  Some may be gray, checkered or
striped.

Some of those standards were to impose a consistency of style.  It is
important to be able to make some predictions about code so that it may
be understood more readily.  For example if code is always 'performed
forward' then when reading a program for the first time one can assume
that.

Of course one cannot make that prediction when reading _my_ code, I
perform code wherever it happens to be.  Some would find that
confusing, or at least, annoying.
```

```
.     Am  07.12.04
 schrieb  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com
   ueber  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

>> . Proper programming to process a sequential sequence with
>> unknown number of occurences is
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> mentioned.

RW> Like this:
RW>
RW> perform read-file
RW> if  end-of-file
RW>     abort
RW> end-if
RW> perform until end-of-file
RW>     process item
RW>     perform read-file
RW> end-perform

   Not quite, do it this way, as explained above in an abstract  
notation:

 perform read-file
 if  end-of-file
     tell-user  *> why abort?    A gentle way to pass
                *> the information would be preferred.
 ELSE           *> !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
   perform until end-of-file
     process item
     perform read-file
   end-perform
 END-IF


  See the difference?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T07:09:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MVCdqNuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com>`

```
.     Am  07.12.04
 schrieb  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com
   ueber  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

>> RW> .. No shared File Status code.
>>
>>              should be self-evident

RW> It's not self-evident. The Standard does not require separate File
RW> Status codes.

   Not everything wich the syntax-check lets pass, does make sense.  
The file status is an attribute of the file, not of the program, so  
each and every file should have its own file status attribut,  
separated from all other files.

RW>    if  file-status-1 not equal to zero
RW>       display 'open error ' file-status ' on file '
RW>                   file-name (file-number)

   *> One could as well write

       display 'open error ' file-status(file-number) ' on file '
                   file-name (file-number)

   *> i.e. when file-names are indexed,
   *> one might index the file-status as well.

   *> or
       CALL 'EXPFSTAT'
              USING file-name(file-number) file-status(file-number)

   *> in case table items are allowed as CALL parameters

RW>        perform abend
RW>    end-if


RW> Even Old Style shops avoid GO TO, except to abort.

   at least.

>> RW> .. No backward PERFORMs.
>>
>>   That makes sense.

RW> Why?

   Because there is a top-down hierarchical structure of a COBOL  
program where execution starts with the first executable instruction  
in the first SECTION following DECLARATIVES.  Recursive PERFORMing is  
not allowed.

   So I think it is reasonable to organize the program text in a  
hierarchical fashion, starting with the main SECTION, and putting any  
SECTION being PERFORMed below (in text direction) of the SECTION from  
where it is called.



Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

  "Nach meiner Ansicht besitzt die Presse _das_ _Recht_,
Schriftsteller, Politiker, Komï¿½dianten und andere ï¿½ffentliche
Charaktere zu _beleidigen_. Achtete ich [so einen Angriff gegen mich]
einer Notiz wert, so galt mir in solchen Fï¿½llen der Wahlspruch: ï¿½
corsaire, corsaire et demi [auf einen Schelmen anderthalben]."
                  - Karl Marx   17.11.1860  (Herr Vogt, Kapitel XI)
```

```
On 08 Dec 2004 07:09:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.     Am  07.12.04
> schrieb  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>separated from all other files.

The program can access one file at a time. It will check the status
_immediately_ after the I/O. There is no reason to retain it after it
has been checked.

I've been using one File Status for 20 years. It's never caused a
problem.

>RW>    if  file-status-1 not equal to zero
>RW>       display 'open error ' file-status ' on file '
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   *> one might index the file-status as well.

It's not so easy. When SELECT is in a copybook, it doesn't know which
subscript to use. REPLACING could be used, but why?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T23:43:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab3fr0lh6hirba0sl9rkkboogh7uba6e6m@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <t8hdr0p5lucbaek36th03t4ch3ulb7lpap@4ax.com> <cp6kuf$9ve$1@panix5.panix.com> <uuber0dph5cv5bcr24dng06geraqol0k42@4ax.com> <cp7d77$8sn$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 8 Dec 2004 12:21:43 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <uuber0dph5cv5bcr24dng06geraqol0k42@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>>>>The program can access one file at a time. It will check the status
>>>>_immediately_ after the I/O. There is no reason to retain it after it
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>spend on advertising is wasted... now if I only knew *which* half!'

ESQL returns a single SQLCODE. They could have designed it so each
statement returned a separate one:

select surname into :ws-surname from person where personid =
:ws-personid STATUS IN :ws-status-07;

If they had, Cobol shops would have formed elaborate rules specifying
how to name them, avoid collisions, etc. There would be debates on the
best way to do it. If I said it was pointless, all you need is a
single SQLCODE, people would scoff. They'd say 'We've been receiving
separate codes for 40 years. That proves we're right.'

>  Second request, Mr Wagner: 
>other people have been using multiple File Statuses for *forty* years... 
>does that make their practises twice as 'best'?

Sure it does, in their minds.
```

Mr Wagner, I did not ask you what you thought went on 'in their minds'...

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T19:24:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZxItd.6424636$6p.1014073@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <9MVCdqNuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <t8hdr0p5lucbaek36th03t4ch3ulb7lpap@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:t8hdr0p5lucbaek36th03t4ch3ulb7lpap@4ax.com...
> On 08 Dec 2004 07:09:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,
  You also implied (or I inferred) that you didn't like a standard that required 
that you couldn't have multiple statements on the same line.

However, you have previously indicated that you "didn't like"

Open Input File-1 File-2
         Output File-3

which is perfectly VALID COBOL - and would cause problems with having a single 
file status field.

You would also have problems with a SORT or MERGE statement - without 
Input/Output Procedures.

Obviously, you have never used either of these (or haven't checked file status 
fields) in the last 20 years - which is fine for you, but is restrictive (IMO) 
and not something that I would REQUIRE others to follow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T00:28:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d25fr05jn00dq46hr7aapehdc73au1kli2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <9MVCdqNuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <t8hdr0p5lucbaek36th03t4ch3ulb7lpap@4ax.com> <ZxItd.6424636$6p.1014073@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 08 Dec 2004 19:24:41 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>that you couldn't have multiple statements on the same line.

I wasn't thinking of multiple I/O statements.

>However, you have previously indicated that you "didn't like"
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>file status field.

Would you also advocate:

read file-1
read file-2
read file-3

if  file-1-status not = zero
   display 'Something wrong with file-1'
end-if
if  file-2-status not = zero
    display 'Something wrong with file-2'
    ....

If not, what's the difference?

>You would also have problems with a SORT or MERGE statement - without 
>Input/Output Procedures.

I'd use a sort utility.

>Obviously, you have never used either of these (or haven't checked file status 
>fields) in the last 20 years - which is fine for you, but is restrictive (IMO) 
>and not something that I would REQUIRE others to follow.

I don't advocate requiring either style. The standard REQUIRES
separate fields. 

The only advantage of "Open Input File-1 File-2" is that it saves
typing the word Input. Let's see which style really saves typing:

perform varying f from 1 by 1 until f > 9
  evaluate f
    when 1 open input File-1
    when 2 open input File-2
    when 3 open input File-3
    when 4 open input File-4
    when 5 open input File-5
    when 6 open input File-6
    when 7 open output File-7
    when 8 open output File-8
    when 9 open output File-9    
  end-evaluate
  if  File-Status not equal to zero
      display 'Error' File-Status ' opening File ' f
      perform ... 
  end-if
end-perform

Line count: 17

Open Input File-1 File-2 file-3 File-4 File-5 File-6
         Output File-7 File-8 File-9
if  File-Status-1 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-1 ' opening File 1'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-2 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-2 ' opening File 2'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-3 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-3 ' opening File 3'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-4 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-4 ' opening File 4'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-5 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-5 ' opening File 5'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-6 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-6 ' opening File 6'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-7 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-7 ' opening File 7'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-8 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-8 ' opening File 8'
    perform ....
end-if
if  File-Status-9 not equal to zero
    display 'Error ' File-Status-9 ' opening File 9'
    perform ....
end-if

Line count: 38
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T08:56:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BF5DFE.08565509122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <ZxItd.6424636$6p.1014073@news.easynews.com> <d25fr05jn00dq46hr7aapehdc73au1kli2@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
In article <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
 l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> .    On  09.12.04
>   wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> files at once. Could you explain?

Just to chime in with an example -- my shop gets a lot of help from 
parallel jobs.

We have almost half a billion accounts on file.  So to make processes 
run faster we will often hash the files into groups based on the IMS 
area it falls into.

Then separate jobs can be started for each IMS area to accomplish the 
processing.

The reverse is also needed.  Sometimes we take group output and merge it 
all together...

When doing this via Cobol, one needs to allow for up to 240 areas which 
is some old limit of IMS.  The current versions support 2000 areas, but 
the process wasdesigned before they did.
```

JZ> Just to chime in with an example

  thank you

JZ> -- my shop gets a lot of help from parallel jobs.

  I would understand the concept of "parallel" jobs to mean that  
several jobs run in parallel, each processing a file. In COBOL itself,  
parallell processing is not possible, at least by the standard (I know  
of two implementations which do implement what they call "asynchronous  
processing").

JZ>
JZ> We have almost half a billion accounts on file.

  A phone company?

JZ> So to make processes run faster we will often hash the files into
JZ> groups based on the IMS area it falls into.

   If I only knew what an IMS area is. ...

JZ> Then separate jobs can be started for each IMS area to accomplish the
JZ> processing.
JZ>
JZ> The reverse is also needed.  Sometimes we take group output and merge
JZ> it all together...

   More a SORT/MERGE-process?

JZ> When doing this via Cobol, one needs to allow for up to 240 areas
JZ> which is some old limit of IMS.  The current versions support 2000
JZ> areas, but the process was designed before they did.

   Again, what is meant by these areas?


Yours,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T00:00:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-B69038.00005410122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-BF5DFE.08565509122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <9MZQU30eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
In article <9MZQU30eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
 l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> .    On  09.12.04
>   wrote  joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> processing").

Once the split is performed, then 1 job per group is kicked off.  They 
run on different LPARS or different machines and then the results are 
available as output files that can be merged back together.

> JZ>
> JZ> We have almost half a billion accounts on file.
> 
>   A phone company?

Financial services.

> JZ> So to make processes run faster we will often hash the files into
> JZ> groups based on the IMS area it falls into.
> 
>    If I only knew what an IMS area is. ...

An IMS area is a 'physical' VSAM file.  Many of these are joined by IMS 
to make a 'logical' database.
```

- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <ZxItd.6424636$6p.1014073@news.easynews.com> <d25fr05jn00dq46hr7aapehdc73au1kli2@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 09 Dec 2004 07:14:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  09.12.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>files at once. Could you explain?

Many many cases.

Let's see one example that comes to mind.

Insurance software.
1- Links file (holds the relation between diff numbers used on the
other files.
2- Master Person file
2- Person insurance details 
3- Person physical details (address and so on)
4- Scheme file
5- Scheme Category file
6- Policy file
7- Coverage details file
8- Coverage amounts file
9- Policy fund selection file
10- Unit totals file
11- Unit movements file
12- Fund switch file
13- Fund calculation file
14- Generic tables file
15- Accounts file
16- Error reporting file
17- Exception reporting file
18- Softlock file
19- Unit exclusion file

This particular program still has more files but I don't remember
them.

The program isn't even medium size, just around 7/8 thousand lines
excluding file layout copybooks.
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Das Buch, das in der Welt am ehesten verboten zu werden verdiente, wï¿½re ein Katalogus von verbotenen Bï¿½chern. -G.C.Lichtenberg

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T23:24:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e4nhr0h3j7sbitmbpfvdc7mle49n9scv8i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 09 Dec 2004 21:48:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  09.12.04
>  wrote  real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com (Frederico Fonseca)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>   That is a database.
Only in the sense that a database is a collection of tables with
related information. Nothing else.
>
>   But why is this database in individual flat files which are kept  
>together only by the programs working on them, instead of in a real  
>database system which can take care of referencial integrity and so  
>on?
Because this is not a database. It is an application fully developed
in COBOL and as such it works with indexed files.
This application had over 100 main files, and around 3000 programs.
Converting this to a full database would be very costly, and no real
gain was visible to the company.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T13:58:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31sal9F3fpbhjU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <e4nhr0h3j7sbitmbpfvdc7mle49n9scv8i@4ax.com>`

```

"Frederico Fonseca" <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote in message
news:e4nhr0h3j7sbitmbpfvdc7mle49n9scv8i@4ax.com...
> On 09 Dec 2004 21:48:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
> wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The conversion cost is in the 3000 programs, not the database. I have tools
that could convert the data for under $US3000, including indexes,
constraints, and third normal form, with OCCURS clauses in record layouts,
converted to separate linked tables.

The problem is in converting the programs that process the current files.
That would be expensive because it can really only be "computer assisted"
not fully automated, and 3000 programs is a significant number. Even given a
tool that claimed to do the conversion in the programs 100%, it still needs
manual intervention to check it.

Nevertheless, the benefits which were "not visible" would be as follows:

1. Reporting and downstream feeds (interfaces with other systems) without
having to write a COBOL program.
2.  Data entry requirements can be easily met without requiring COBOL
programs.
3. Any standard software can access the data. It is not locked in to the
COBOL system.
4. The database can be easily scaled and even moved to a different platform
if it made sense to do so economically. More options are available.
5. Standard backup recovery replication makes these functions simpler and
more reliable.

The life of this application will be limited by it NOT being converted.

However, in the meantime it is doing the job and everybody is happy with it.
(I'd start planning for its replacement NOW, if I were you... <G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T08:37:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pdnir09ldpf309jet9r7ncna669lfcoqbg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <e4nhr0h3j7sbitmbpfvdc7mle49n9scv8i@4ax.com> <31sal9F3fpbhjU1@individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 10 Dec 2004 13:58:11 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Frederico Fonseca" <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote in message
>news:e4nhr0h3j7sbitmbpfvdc7mle49n9scv8i@4ax.com...
snip
>
>1. Reporting and downstream feeds (interfaces with other systems) without
>having to write a COBOL program.
Not a problem for two reasons.
1-Although the programs consider the files as Indexed files these are
AS400 physical and logical files and are therefore available to all
other applications out there that can access DB2.

All generic reporting (e.g. letter to customers) are do using a
VB/Word application, all others can be done either with Query/400 or
with Crystal Reports.
There are a few COBOL reports but not that many.

>2.  Data entry requirements can be easily met without requiring COBOL
>programs.
Interfacing has now moved to a Java application. All programs were
kept unchanged, including the entry ones, as these are "called" from
Java.
>3. Any standard software can access the data. It is not locked in to the
>COBOL system.
See above.
>4. The database can be easily scaled and even moved to a different platform
>if it made sense to do so economically. More options are available.
>5. Standard backup recovery replication makes these functions simpler and
>more reliable.
What has backup anything to do with it!!
>
>The life of this application will be limited by it NOT being converted.
>
>However, in the meantime it is doing the job and everybody is happy with it.

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no...
> Lueko Willms wrote:

<snip>>
> Then no doubt you will get a different answer. :-)  Or, having answered

No such justification is possible, apart from: "That's the way it evolved;
that's the way it is."

(OK, I'm teasing... but you have to admit, if it wasn't apparent before, it
certainly is now, that the advantages of  DBMS go a long way beyond I/O
performance...Locking your application into the COBOL file system is like
saying: "Nothing else is ever going to communicate with this system, it is
never going to communicate with anything else, and if you need to know
anything stored in it you can bloody well write a COBOL program..." As the
COBOL star wanes, this argument has less and less weight and power...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T02:12:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete,
   HISTORICALLY (pre- "PC" and outside of Unix), the "COBOL file system" often 
(usually???) was the Operating System "file system".  This meant that data 
"stored" in COBOL "files" could EASILY be accessed by any and all other 
applications (including those not in COBOL - even those just using "O/S 
utilities").  Or at least that is my impression.

Therefore, although I certainly agree that there are advantages to "databases" 
(and often - not always - advantages to Relational over Network and Hierarchical 
databases) accessibility from non-COBOL was not (historically) one of them.
```

- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  10.12.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS


WMK>    HISTORICALLY (pre- "PC" and outside of Unix), the "COBOL file
WMK> system" often (usually???) was the Operating System "file system".
WMK> This meant that data "stored" in COBOL "files" could EASILY be
WMK> accessed by any and all other applications (including those not in
WMK> COBOL - even those just using "O/S utilities").  Or at least that is
WMK> my impression.

   More or less true.

WMK> Therefore, although I certainly agree that there are advantages to
WMK> "databases" (and often - not always - advantages to Relational over
WMK> Network and Hierarchical databases) accessibility from non-COBOL
WMK> was not (historically) one of them.

   The difference is that with flat files, as being used in the very  
elaborate example presented here, the relationships between those  
different files are maintained only by the programmed logic of the  
programs accessing this database.

   In a proper database system, relations, contraints, referential  
integrity is defined in the database schema, and enforced by the  
database system.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch muï¿½ erst ausgedroschen werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-11T12:04:04+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com...
> Pete,
>    HISTORICALLY (pre- "PC" and outside of Unix), the "COBOL file system"
often
> (usually???) was the Operating System "file system".  This meant that data
> "stored" in COBOL "files" could EASILY be accessed by any and all other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Therefore, although I certainly agree that there are advantages to
"databases"
> (and often - not always - advantages to Relational over Network and
Hierarchical
> databases) accessibility from non-COBOL was not (historically) one of
them.
>

My argument was not historical, it is the present. But I'll respond to your
points above, anyway...

In the time you are talking about, (pre-1983), there WAS no alternative to
the OS file system, OTHER than network or hierarchic database
implementations that incurred overheads serious enough to make them suitable
only for major applications.

ISAM worked fine to provide both random and sequential access for smaller
files where there were few relationships and ordering requirements, so
alternate keys were minimal. The only real downside was the necessity to
periodically reorganise ISAM files due to overflow (and second level
overflow on some systems).

When VSAM was released it quickly replaced ISAM because of its
self-reorganizing capability. (I still believe VSAM  (IDCAMS if we consider
all VSAM services) is the finest data handling access method that has ever
been developed. I like it better than BTRIEVE, although there is not much
between them...) It is no surprise to me that even today mainframe database
implementations  utilize VSAM as the underlying stroage handling mechanism.

Yet, even then, the Corporate solution was a database. I remember building a
totally VSAM based database which worked very efficiently and effectively
for a major corporation in london in the late 70s. It was replaced within 3
years (to the chagrin of my Management, who had backed it to the hilt) by
IMS which was less efficient, but more acceptable to the 14 European
countries where the system had to run. (I wasn't bothered. I got well paid
for it and did a lot of tripping around Europe...)

Acessibility from non-COBOL didn't matter because there was no other
"standard" software, such as we take for granted today. The whole gamut of
PC software which has revolutionised the way people use computers was not
invented yet.

As time went by, PCs came along, newer and different ways of doing things
evolved, Codd and Date released the Relational model (early eighties)  and
the efficiency of DBMS (including RDBMS) improved many thousands of times.

Today, I wouldn't consider using ISAM on a PC (or VSAM on a mainframe) for
serious application development. The RDBMS implementations offer me so many
facilities it would be silly not to use them. I can design and build
applications using ACCESS or MySQL and know that they will scale perfectly
to whatever platform I want to run them on. (In fact, in a mainframe
environment, while I was waiting for the DBA group to approve the use and
creation and allocation of test databases, I'd already have them running and
under test on my PC, shared with my colleagues. It is a good way to parallel
process the politics <G>) Not only that, but standard software such as
spreadsheets can get to the data without any effort on my part. I can know
that I don't have to build interfaces to all my other systems; the databases
integrate easily and seamlessly simply by adding linked tables.

I believe your thinking may be coloured, Bill, by the fact that IBM have
always provided excellent support for IDCAMS and it is one of the best
things in the IBM arsenal. DBMS use in the mainframe environment is hindered
by the politics of getting DBA approval to do anything that touches
Corporate databases, and the difficulty this imposes on application
developers makes them look for other ways. It is easier for developers to
simply use IDCAMS and handle the data that way. If you were free of those
encumbrances (as people developing in a PC environment are) you would
probably see things differently.

It is gratifying to me to see that when I advocated people should move off
the COBOL file system in the last century, I received vitriolic attack and
abuse, but now people say: "Well of course. " (Apart from people like
yourself, of course, and that is why we are having this discussion... <G> )

Bottom line:

Application developers who are using Client/Server are most likely to use
RDBMS because it is simple, efficient, and brings with it a number of useful
facilities that would otherwise need to be written.

Application developers working in mainframe environments have been raised on
IDCAMS, know it well, don't usually need permission to use it, whereas DBMS
involves political manoeuvering with interface through a DBA group that can
be tiresome and time consuming. They are therefore less likely to use DBMS.

Both groups are being driven together by the corporate need for free data
flow and interfacing to all corporate data systems. This factor weights the
dice in favour of DBMS in the long term.

I should mention in closing that the new breed of users and programmers who
have grown up with PCs wouldn't know what ISAM was... they know SQL and when
they need to store data they knock out a database in minutes.

Pete.
```
