###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T00:38:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3204h3F3hf550U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com> <31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net> <9Mgc1KK9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9Mgc1KK9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  11.12.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Was that IBM, Lueko? Was it recently?

Certainly IBM sites today are much more amenable to using RDBMS because many
sites have streamlined the DBA function and, in some cases, distributed it
so that divisions are responsible for their own data, and ensuring it can be
accessed by other divisions of the company.

However, I'm sure there are many IBM mainfame programmers here who are
facile with IDCAMS and use it daily for intermediate files, extracts,
interfaces, organising Generation Data Groups and so on. I believe that most
of them would agree that their access to databases on the mainframe is in
conjunction with major applications and not as a matter of course. Of
course, many of them will also have desktop computers that can run local
databases (like ACCESS), and spreadsheets for data transfer, as I described.

Perhaps some of the IBM mainframe people would be able to confirm or deny
whether this is still a valid description of what goes on, or whether their
experience is different and I am describing a past era?

>    This explains my astonishment about that programmatically
> maintained database of that insurance company.
>
>
There was a time when that was the only way. And it was not a bad way. I am
extremely sceptical about the prices that Frederico quoted (sorry, I'm not
suggesting he lied, just that the companies bidding were certainly
sandbagging the quote. People who didn't want the system changed would
encourage this also...)

At the end of the day, the system Frederico described MUST be meeting the
needs or it would HAVE to be replaced/upgraded. I believe the problems will
arise as it moves towards the end of its life cycle but time will tell. Even
adding Java front ends and so on will prolong the life for a finite period
(as opposed to "indefinitely"). Eventually the underlying engines will wear
out or become over maintained or there simply won't be the skill available
to maintain them.

I worked for some time for IBM on the HUON solution which is their standard
"insurance package". It is written in COBOL, runs on mainframes and has been
around for a very long time. It is getting harder and harder to find people
who can maintain it. I also worked in Norwich Union for several years both
as a lead programmer and as a Project Manager. My experience of insurance
systems is that as various parts (functions) need to be replaced or added,
the design is likely to move towards a DBMS. You can still use the VSAM
files. The two are not mutually exclusive. There is nothing to stop a COBOL
Program from accessing files and databases at the same time. I would see the
life cycle (for an IBM mainframe site (and that is not the case for
Frederico) as:

1. Write the system using ISAM to provide random and sequential access.
(1960)
2. Move the ISAM access progressively to VSAM. (1970)
3. Normalize new functionality and use RDBMS for it. Gradually replace the
existing VSAM access with Relations (Tables) (1990)
4. Replace application functionality in COBOL programs with RDBMS based
code, using RDBMS tools. (2010)
5. New technology replaces all existing systems as electricity replaced
steam (2020). The company has either outsourced or bought a standard
package. Higher probability is outsourcing for IT, and refocusing internally
on the insurance business. Departmental processing is carried out entirely
by corporate portable computers (notebooks and handhelds) using wireless
links, which all staff will have. Data transfer between these machines and
to the outsourced or packaged main system will be totally transparent to the
people using them. Only IT companies will still be using mainframes, and
desktop PCs will be antiques.

This is, of course just one hypothetical scenario, but I believe it is not
far from the truth.

Pete.
```

```
This customer was a branch of a multinational. Due to legal contraints
they had already spent a huge amount of money getting a package
adapted to their needs, not only the legal ones, but also in terms of
products. Then the brains from head-office decided that it would be a
good idea of moving them from this package to a Oracle one, and have
both this branch and one in another country using the same package.
Prices were indeed on the region I mentioned, and the size of the team
involved on this for 2 years was supposed to require 70 analysts and
programmers.



>
>At the end of the day, the system Frederico described MUST be meeting the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to maintain them.
Application is still being improved and new functionality is being
added to it in terms of new products. In many cases to add a new
insurance product is only a question of modifying a few(not that
few!!) tables, and add new product rules to product files.
New development is required because there are things that the product
does not/did not do. I for instance added Claims functionality to the
product when I was there. But even this did not add all type of
claims, and when I left automatic calculation of Death claims was
still not possible due to the business not be willing to spend x
amount of money to develop the required new screens/files to hold the
extra data required when the volume of death claims was very very
small.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```
FF>> Application is still being improved and new functionality is
FF>> being added to it in terms of new products. In many cases to add
FF>> a new insurance product is only a question of modifying a few(not
FF>> that few!!) tables, and add new product rules to product files.

PD> That is terriffic, and exactly what you need. But neither Lueko nor
PD> myself could deduce that when it appeared that the system was
PD> comprised of ISAM files.

   Actually it was my perception that this system of files looked like  
a well designed database, except that the fetching of related  
information from several tables had to be organised "manually", i.e.  
by extracting the key information from one file's record and use this  
to START and READ the corresponding record from another file, instead  
of getting all needed fields from all tables by one SELECT.

   And I would not be astonished at all if the people who have  
developed that system had developed their own CALLable procedures  
which emulate such a SELECT from multiple tables.


Yours,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T12:39:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hsdor0d6qulhov1is5nhk81jgnmj1o71gc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <lnplr05m7vud0qa525itbjritr1u84q7an@4ax.com> <321f45F3h65spU1@individual.net> <9MkhnTZ9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 12 Dec 2004 10:26:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  12.12.04
>  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>of getting all needed fields from all tables by one SELECT.
The way the application works is really 
read main file, 
   start/read next the others.
     for each of the others
         start/read next some others

Due to the way the application was originally desiged it would be very
hard to replace the logic with one select.

Just to give an example.
A group scheme can contain several policies. It can also contain
several "persons" and several categories.
So processing of a scheme would be
scheme file 
   get scheme details (2 files)
   links file
     get policies attached
         get product details (12 files)
         links file
            get coverages attache
                get coverage specifics (a few files here)
            get persons attached
                get person details (3/4 files)
            get category attached
                get category details (2 files)
     get categories attached
         get category details (2 files)
     get scheme persons attached
         get person details (3/4 files)

If developing a new system it would be feasible to incorporate some of
the above on a single SQL, but not all.
>
>   And I would not be astonished at all if the people who have  
>developed that system had developed their own CALLable procedures  
>which emulate such a SELECT from multiple tables.
All processing is done using I/O modules, with some exceptions where
ESQL was used for some batch programs due to speed contraints.

The whole system is highly parameterizable, and as I said before many
new things can be done just by changing/adding values on tables/files,
no code needed.

I would like to be still working there as my current customer is a
"crap" site compared to that one (sigh||).

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Das ganze Zeitungs-All.  -G.C.Lichtenberg



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```
Frederico Fonseca wrote:
<snip>

>Application is still being improved and new functionality is being
>added to it in terms of new products. In many cases to add a new
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't envy you and Pete getting involved with insurance applications one 
bit :-)  . I'd hazard a guess, they are the most complex even 
outstripping stocks and shares.

Back in Radio Shack days a Dickensian office manager ( the brother to 
Scrooge), thought he could maintain his insurance system on a Model II. 
Background, but sketchy, couple of guys set up a local insurance agency, 
tied into the 'nationals' for coverage. Went bankrupt - so controlled by 
one of the national accounting firms as 'managers in bankruptcy', 
(whatever the correct legalese is).  Although no longer 'selling', still 
had to maintain existing policies, monthly payments in and due payments out.

Well ! He tried to start explaining what was going on, the ins and outs, 
why they had done this, why they had done that. This is Policy Type A, 
this is Policy C which is Policy A with a soupï¿½on of Policy B..........I 
valiantly tried to start capturing it with Decision Tables. He never had 
success, nor did I. Even as a proponent of OO - I think even that would 
be a challenge. My gut feeling would be both classes and DB Tables, 
built in an hierarchial format. No I'm not interested, I still think the 
topic is dead BORING.

Norwich Union - I think both insurance and mortgages ??? Had a friend 
who moved up to Norwich as Chief Programmer - back in, errr - late 
sixties/early seventies.  Not particularly dynamic, and doesn't appear 
to have lasted there very  long. Went contracting on his own. Last job 
down near Heathrow airport where he suffered a fatal heart attack.  
Although working for NU never had a penny of coverage on his mortgage - 
put his wife and kids out on the street !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT:Insurance WAS Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T12:48:50+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3243n6F3gtp9kU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com> <31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net> <9Mgc1KK9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3204h3F3hf550U1@individual.net> <lnplr05m7vud0qa525itbjritr1u84q7an@4ax.com> <CV1vd.497230$%k.347997@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:CV1vd.497230$%k.347997@pd7tw2no...
> Frederico Fonseca wrote:
> <snip>
>
<snip>. No I'm not interested, I still think the
> topic is dead BORING.
>
Funny that. I don't recall ever being bored in my life. There is too much
going on in my head and I have a wide range of interests. I think BORING is
dependent on the person, not the circumstances. But that's just me... (Not
personal, Jimmy, just an observation.)


> Norwich Union - I think both insurance and mortgages ???

And general insurance (as opposed to Life), and Pensions, retirement
funding, and recently diversification into general investment.

They are an excellent company and I have good memories of my time there.

As a general rule I have no truck with Insurance companies (except where
legally required to, of course.)

As a young man in my first marriage I dutifully took out policies to protect
the wife, as you do. When the marriage broke up and I was on the bones of my
arse (5 years later), I tried to get some cash against the policies and they
wouldn't touch it. I ended up surrendering them for a fraction of what I had
paid in. They shafted me good. Later, when things got better and I was in
the top 8% of the country for earnings, single, and healthy, I was besieged
by Life Insurance salesmen trying to sell me policies... (they have
insidious ways of targeting people, like vultures...). I kept resisting but
one guy in particular would not take "no" for an answer.

I finally agreed to let him buy me lunch (I have always been a sucker for
good food...). He did his spiel with all the plastic wrapped tables and the
tax savings and so on, and I listened politely.

"So, I pay you X amount a month, and if I die you pay out Y to my estate?"
"Yes."
"OK, how about this...YOU pay ME X a month, and if I die, I'll pay YOU Y?"
Shocked incredulity....
"No, Peter, it doesn't work like that. Let me go over it again..."
"No, I understand what you described. I just thought it would work better
for me this way."
"We can't do that."
"So you are offering me a deal that you and your company would not do?"
"Well...er...not exactly..."
"I'd have to be pretty stupid to go for something like that, don't you
think?"
"But, it is a standard policy..."
"Not for me it isn't. Thanks for the lunch."

I never heard from him or any of his cronies again. Good result.

Most of us have had some experience with the standard "weasel" clause that
insurance companies insert into things like car and general accident
policies. It's in the very fine print, or sometimes embedded as a microdot
that looks like a full stop under paragraph 361 of section 500 in Appendix
40...

"If the policyholder is less than 6 foot tall and has a green shirt  (on
their person or in any other place whatsoever) OR the Policy holder blinks
more than 5 times a minute OR (ad nauseum)...then this policy shall not
apply."

For me it was a very agreeable surprise to find that Norwich Union actually
make a point of paying claims as quickly as possible, have no weasel clauses
and try to treat people fairly. When you hear some of the abuse they get it
is really commendable. And some of the scams people try on them.

I have my minimal (car, house, general) cover with their New Zealand
subsidiary, State Insurance.

> Had a friend
> who moved up to Norwich as Chief Programmer - back in, errr - late
> sixties/early seventies.  Not particularly dynamic, and doesn't appear
> to have lasted there very  long.

Like all big corporations there was a certain amount of stress from internal
politics and there was some friction between the people using "New
Technology" and the entrenched mainframe people. I shouldn't think that is
still true as the lines are much less clearly drawn now.

>Went contracting on his own. Last job
> down near Heathrow airport where he suffered a fatal heart attack.

That is pretty tragic. So many IT people get stressed out and this is not
the first fatality I have heard of. I had one guy working on a team I
managed and he was going off to Spain with the family for his holiday.
Collapsed and died while boarding the plane. I often wondered if there was
anything I could have done to prevent that. Maybe didn't see the signs of
overstress. Yet he seemed fine and was looking forward to his break.

> Although working for NU never had a penny of coverage on his mortgage -
> put his wife and kids out on the street !
>
That is horrific. A contractor at that time (I was one) would be earning
significant money and there is really no excuse for not providing for the
family.(Not necessarily via insurance, but definitely some way...)

Pete.
```

```
.    On  12.12.04

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  3204h3F3hf550U1@individual.net
  about  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS


>>    The applications which I worked with on mainframes were all using a
>> database (with one exception at a customer site with a very static
>> master file and a high volume of current data entered batchwise in
>> quarterly or monthly intervals, processed and never accessed again).
>>

PD> Was that IBM, Lueko? Was it recently?

   No, and no.

   It was on Sperry Univac, and it was in the early 1980ies. Sperry  
Univac started the development of a Business Accounting System (named  
SUBAS) which was probably thought to compete with SAP. The system was  
based on DMS/1100, a CODASYL or network database system (with INVOKE  
SUBSCHEMA ...) and organized as online transactions, plus a stint at  
another project with a forwarders software (where I learned the famous  
phrase "The customer wants it so"). Later, in 1994, I worked at a  
Unisys customer with RDMS/1100, a relational database.


PD> At the end of the day, the system Frederico described MUST be meeting
PD> the needs or it would HAVE to be replaced/upgraded.

   Sure, and just looking at the descriptive names of the files, it  
looks like a well normalized system.

   But while with an RDMS, one would do one single
      SELECT Name FROM PERSON_MASTER,
               Address FROM Person_Details,
                 ContractNumber FROM Insurance_Details
          WHERE Person_Details.Person = Person_Master.ID
             AND Insurance_Details.Person = Person_Master.ID


whereas in that system being presented one would have to find the  
linked records in the other files all manually, SEEKing and READing,  
etc.

   But once all that has already be programmed and is working ... who  
wants to change a running system?



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Als er am Kirchhofe vorbei ging, sagte er: Die da kï¿½nnen nun sicher  
sein, daï¿½ sie nicht mehr gehï¿½ngt werden, das kï¿½nnen wir nicht. - 
G.C.Lichtenberg
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T08:56:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<326af8F3ibebdU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com> <31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net> <cpk9lv$jfm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cpk9lv$jfm$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 10-Dec-2004, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> VSAM pretty much duplicated what was available on other computers.   But
only
> KSDS became popular, replacing ISAM.   IBM wanted VSAM to replace relative
and
> flat files as well.
>
Disagree Howard. That's not how I remember it. VSAM/KSDS may have duplicated
the functionality of ISAM but there were no other systems around at the time
(with the possible exception of BTRIEVE, and that was largely Academic) that
had the capability to reorganize themselves with CA/CI splits and reclaim
freespace. I worked on many other mainframes around this time (IBM and the
BUNCH) and NOBODY as I recall had this capability. Burroughs (later Unisys)
had an access method called Indexed Random that periodically reorganized
itself in the background by sorting the latest additions to a file and
chaining them. When you closed the file the whole thing was sorted ready for
the next use. On Control Data Cybers the SCOPE operating system allowed
files to be managed across drives (if you allowed it to) and would write
data wherever the next track was available, then remember where it had put
it (a bit like FAT tables on a PC). NCR required a separate reorg utility as
did Honeywell at the time. I can't speak for Univac as my experience of it
was limited.

VSAM was quite revolutionary in it's capability. The fact that it is still
used today without major changes to it, testifies to its success.

VSAM/RRDS was a very good implemenation for COBOL  relative files, and I
remember writng an access method called RAM which used it, for the South
London branch of a major international Bank.

As for replacing flat files I don't think so.  VSAM/ESDS was never going to
do that and I don't believe IBM saw it as a competitor for QSAM or even
BSAM. Obviously, our experiences and memory differ here.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T15:26:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpn0ns$9k5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <326af8F3ibebdU1@individual.net> <cpktpb$1v3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9MotNb4uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

On 13-Dec-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> HB> In order to keep functionality, I had to make all of my files
> HB> VSAM.   These were mostly flat and relative files though.
>
>    What does that tell to someone like me who has never used a VSAM
> file?

VSAM files are created by a utility called IDCAMS and have their own file
management that isn't the default IBM.   Access goes through the VSAM software.
   There are three types of VSAM files (each with their 4 letter acronym),
handling flat, indexed, and relative files.   Because IBM's existing ISAM
(indexed) file system had inefficiencies, customers were quite willing to go
through this extra step to replace them.   But flat and relative files don't
need the system to do much, and users didn't see a big need to change to VSAM
for them.
```

```
.    On  14.12.04
  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
```

- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MotNb4uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cpn0ns$9k5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9Mt4M5cuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cppk28$5pt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Wed, 15 Dec 2004 15:08:18 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 15-Dec-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>It was 20 years ago, and the details escape me.

Possibly for backup, where file names keep changing. For example,
names containing a date or generation. Under VSAM, files can be placed
in UCAT (user catalog), which can be backed up with a single command.
If they were QSAM, you would have needed a script or program to create
a list of file names.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS (VSAM)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T02:53:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SAsvd.1089328$Gx4.509542@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<326af8F3ibebdU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com> <31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net> <cpk9lv$jfm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <326af8F3ibebdU1@individual.net>`

```


Pete Dashwood wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:cpk9lv$jfm$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

VSAM ESDS (VSAM sequential files) are used very commonly in CICS for 
writing transaction logs.  That's because using QSAM files in CICS is 
a bit...difficult.  A VSAM ESDS file can easily be open for both input 
and output in CICS.  Not so for QSAM which requires CICS DCT entries 
(Destination Control Table).

In CICS you can specify in the FCT entry that a VSAM file is a data 
table.  You must specify the maximum number of records.  The entire 
file is cached in memory, which means access is much faster, but the 
programs need to be recompiled.  There are two types of VSAM data 
tables - CICS maintained, where CICS periodically flushes buffers to 
the disk, and User maintained, where the file is never copied back to 
disk.

I support applications with both CICS maintained and User Maintained 
VSAM data tables.  For User Maintained tables we define an extra FCT 
entry that points to the same file, without defining it as a data 
table.  We have "sweeper" programs that run at timed intervals to read 
from the UMT and write to the disk version of the file.

It's still a good idea to reorg VSAM KSDS files periodically, 
especially if there's a high volume of insert and delete activity, but 
  you can go for quite a while before performance degrades.

I have never needed to use a VSAM RRDS file, and I'm not sure if RRDS 
supports variable length records.  Obviously, variable length records 
are well supported for VSAM KSDS and ESDS files.

And as was stated elsewhere, I can easily define my own VSAM file, but 
I cannot get a new DB2 table created.  Only a DBA can do that for me.

With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS (VSAM)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T03:23:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W0tvd.4642625$yk.694047@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <YB7ud.563876$SM5.33457@news.easynews.com> <31uobbF3dunluU1@individual.net> <cpk9lv$jfm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <326af8F3ibebdU1@individual.net> <SAsvd.1089328$Gx4.509542@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message 
news:SAsvd.1089328$Gx4.509542@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
<snip>
> I have never needed to use a VSAM RRDS file, and I'm not sure if RRDS supports 
> variable length records.  Obviously, variable length records are well 
> supported for VSAM KSDS and ESDS files.
>

Yes, current z/OS VSAM *does* support variable length RRDS (relative) files. 
Interestingly enough (hisotical trivia), this support was FIRST available for 
VSAM under VSE.  At the time that IBM introduced VS COBOL II, R3.0 for MVS (and 
CMS), VSAM did *not* support variable length relative files.  Therefore, IBM 
needed to provide the SIMVRDS (Simulate Varaible Relative Data Set) run-time 
option - which they  continue to support today - in order to provide "full" '85 
Standard conformance.  I don't remember the exact year, but a couple  of years 
later variable length RRDS files became available for MVS and can be used today 
(or SIMVRDS files using KSDS "structures" can still be used).
```

```
"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:SAsvd.1089328$Gx4.509542@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>KSDS became popular, replacing ISAM.   IBM wanted VSAM to replace
relative
> >
> > and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Disagree Howard. That's not how I remember it. VSAM/KSDS may have
duplicated
> > the functionality of ISAM but there were no other systems around at the
time
> > (with the possible exception of BTRIEVE, and that was largely Academic)
that
> > had the capability to reorganize themselves with CA/CI splits and
reclaim
> > freespace. I worked on many other mainframes around this time (IBM and
the
> > BUNCH) and NOBODY as I recall had this capability. Burroughs (later
Unisys)
> > had an access method called Indexed Random that periodically reorganized
> > itself in the background by sorting the latest additions to a file and
> > chaining them. When you closed the file the whole thing was sorted ready
for
> > the next use. On Control Data Cybers the SCOPE operating system allowed
> > files to be managed across drives (if you allowed it to) and would write
> > data wherever the next track was available, then remember where it had
put
> > it (a bit like FAT tables on a PC). NCR required a separate reorg
utility as
> > did Honeywell at the time. I can't speak for Univac as my experience of
it
> > was limited.
> >
> > VSAM was quite revolutionary in it's capability. The fact that it is
still
> > used today without major changes to it, testifies to its success.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > As for replacing flat files I don't think so.  VSAM/ESDS was never going
to
> > do that and I don't believe IBM saw it as a competitor for QSAM or even
> > BSAM. Obviously, our experiences and memory differ here.
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> Beware of the licence model of mySQL -- when your application is  not
open source, licence fees apply.

There are several other Open source databases available. I prefer
PostgreSQL, at release 8 there will be a native Windows version for
those that still run that.  Ingress has just been made available, and
MaxDB was SAPDB/Adabas.  There is also FireBird which used to be
Borland Interbase and is not to be confused with Firefox and
Thunderbird from Mozilla.

Using ODBC and unixODBC, programs can access whichever of these is most
suitable and so it is up to the customer to select and install the DB
which can avoid any issues with licencing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ PC COBOL Environment - WAS : MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T19:41:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GZmud.473203$nl.302093@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9McVvaBuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <aF7ud.470251$nl.311297@pd7tw3no> <9McVvaBuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

>.    On  10.12.04
>  wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Frankly from what I have read on Net Express and dotNet, (a detailed 
book from Will Price), I'm not exactly thrilled with dotNet, which tends 
to lock you into the Microsoft approach.

 In the real world, when you change from one environment to another, 
there are *always* fairly significant changes. Then over and above a new 
machine, there's the problem of another approx $3,500 to bring me 
up-to-date switching from Net Express V 3.1 to V 4.0. (To clarify that, 
the switch would have been automatic had I still been on an upgrade, 
with periodic Fixpacks, covered by an annual Technical Support fee - I 
believe about $1,000 - that represents getting the 'latest and greatest' 
on an instalment payment plan).

As it is, I must reload N/E V 3.1 from the CD on to the new machine, and 
of course ask for a new Request Key to use it. Now I'm missing the first 
two Fixpacks which I had while paying for Tech Support - hopefully 
somehow I can retrieve those. (Can't be sure, but I'm fairly certain one 
of the two Fixpacks introduced the SQL Assistant - my 'dream baby' that 
I use for testing SQL statements and then copy/paste into COBOL 
programs/classes - absolutely bullet-proof SQL code !).

As examples of switching to COBOL dotNet using N/E, and both *my* 
examples are COBOL 2002 compliant  :-

*>------------
FACTORY.
*>------------
Factory methods..........
*>--------------
END FACTORY.
*>-----------------
OBJECT.
*>---------------
Object/Instance  methods.........
*>----------------
END OBJECT.
*>-----------------

For whatever reason, for dotNet, Micro Focus has substituted the word 
'STATIC' for 'FACTORY' above. Not that it's a realistic scenario - but 
'bye bye' any portable code.

Currently I will code a method :-

*>---------------------
Method-id. "calculate".   *> could write this without the quotes
*>---------------------
some source code

End Method "calculate".    *> could write this without the quotes
*>---------------------

Micro Focus takes the above method-name and either converts it to 
Upper-case/Lower-case for passing messages - I *think* - Lower-case.

The dotNet examples from Will's book :-

*>---------------------
Method-id. Calculate as "Calculate".
*>---------------------
some source code

End Method Calculate as "Calculate".
*>---------------------

Now Will's text on the above, (and really can't clarify it without a 
compiler) :-

 >>>>"In the previous section's description of the class's method, the 
importance of using the "as" phrase in the Method-ID is emphasized. You 
can demonstrate this for yourself by removing it - that is, change the 
following :-

Method-id. Calculate as "Calculate".

to :-

Method-id. Calculate.

Compile the program and you will see the following message :-

        Could not find method 'Calculate' with this signature

The reason, as mentioned earlier, is that Net Express had catalogued the 
subject method as CALCULATE and NET. access to the method is case 
sensitive.....".>>>>>

BUT - Net Express is *case sensitive* even in V 3.1 - as I indicated above.

Exception handling - using dotNet facilities covering validation, 
file-status/SQLCode and System errors - N/E dotNet introduces the new 
syntax :-

TRY.......
CATCH.......
END-TRY......

For COBOL 2002 - a non-standard, and hardly likely it takes into 
account  the existing 2002 Exception Handling  names - which to my 
knowledge haven't yet been implemented by anybody.
 
The key to using dotNet is Microsoft's CLR (Common Language  Runtime). 
Billy Boy is the master mariotineer in charge of pulling the strings for 
all the other languages. It's about two years ago, Bill Klein enthused, 
"Hey take a look at this - Bill Gates says COBOL is a great language".
 
We knew it was and so *did* Bill Gates - but he needed the missing piece 
for dotNet - one additional  puppet - COBOL. Fujitsu kindly obliged by 
coming up with F/J dotNet.

(Told to a friend, by a friend, who told a friend etc., .... or so I am 
told. Micro Focus always  had a good relationship with Microsoft, way 
before F/J entered the scene. The very last versions of Microsoft COBOL 
that I used were re-badged versions of the Micro Focus compilers. They, 
(M/F) were first approached by MS about dotNet. Story goes, "No thanks, 
but we don't think we are interested.....".  Subsequent to introduction 
of F/J's dotNet - for marketing, they had to play quick catch-up and 
introduce N/E with dotNet features).

Components and other tools and languages you referred to above. Prior to 
dabbling with Treeviews in COBOL, because I can see a very good use 
where I can visually give the user an insight to what is available from 
the hierarchical tree - I went googling. Lots of really sophisticated 
stuff on Treeviews. The better ones of course more pricey than the 
low-end items. And just like you observed above, even *prior* to dotNet :-

(also available as components for Delphi,  
C++Builder, and Kylix)

Go looking now and the same components are listed for dotNet versions of the tools/languages. Again more money to get VB, Delphi, C++ or whatever.
Note, quite a number of components are tool/language specific. The assumed dotNet fix is not a quick handle between COBOL and other languages via Microsoft - a decision is required from you which tool/language you are going to follow - then pay your money upfront. Compare that to Pete's non-dotNet approach to Components currently - certainly some built using existing non-dotNet F/J, for which he provides the accompanying F/J runtime free, coupled with a helpfile on methods and parameters to be used by *any* other language - he would have to confirm the last.

Having looked at Treeviews - I've done it entirely in OO COBOL. But I would like to be able to add checkboxes to the Treeview - then I could indicate at a summary Level, 'this one's been actioned', "this needs action". I doubt there's a particularly easy approch to adding checkboxes, regardless of language - you have to have an appreciation of how the Treeview API calls interface.

Computing is real easy. Gershwin, "It ain't necessarily so.....".

Jimmy
```

```
Richard wrote:

so did Leuko "wrote" :- 

>> Having got royally clobbered on my Win 98 with viruses,

portal to another dimension. (There was a TV series based on it too). At 
one stage Russell and Spader get separated from the rest. Russell's 
comment :-

"Just my luck, to get stuck with the dweeb !"

Loosen up guys ! This rocket scientist wants to do the following :-

1. use COBOL
2. Use Net Express OO
3. Use Net Express GUIs
4. Use Net Express SQL Assistant
5. Use Net Express Java connection - possibly in future
5a. #'s 1 to 5 above represent an all-in-one deal.
6. Distribute to users who have WINDOZE - LINUX is still not common for

/usr/cs/program -t, x, -r ....... or whatever the gibberish was - FORGET

 >> Reformt and install Mandrake, or SUSE.

Reformat the new one which would come with XP already loaded- whoa - if

 >>Get OpenOffice.org, you can open MSWord documents in those. At least

Well that one looks OK - they have Windows versions. Besides which I

I've given up on that now...........

 >> Do not ever use Outlook or IE, they are the conduit by which you get

Never purposefully use Outlook, although some sites will automatically 
bring it up when responding to your request. The IE (Internet Explorer) 
- understand what you are saying, but creating Treeviews in NE OO, 
surely the API calls must be accessing the IE and certainly if I use the 
Class Filename with a wildcard, that accesses the IE.(Treeview), somehow.

Is there an equivalent from SUSE or MANDRAKE to the hierachial view you 
get with the Treeview from the MS IE ?

 >>>You also need to dump all the Spyware that has installed itself, 
use  both AdAware and
 >>> SpyBot to get rid of all that too.

OK downloaded both as a result of the virus problem. (Probably read 
about them in c.l.c.). Sure got rid of a *lot* of junk. I think the 
sequence is correct - I use  AdAware and then go into the Spybot screen 
- just freezes on me. But again would pursue with new machine.

MYSQL - in response to Leuko's warnng - watch out for the distribution 
licensing fees. Not quite the same thing, but currently using MS Access 
- User may *not* yet have MS Access - so has to go out and *buy* it,  
latest version -  which could be later than the version I'm currently 
using. Plus, always upgrades to MS products. I suspect upgrades for 
shareware play out slightly easier. At least at this stage I can 
download MYSQL and play suck it and see. If it does the trick for me 
without too much hassle - then it negates the need for MS Word, Access 
and Excel.

Primarily for Donald - but others can comment. I nipped around a couple 
of retailers but opted for FutureShop. Logic applied - if I have 
problems they are *physically* available. Could of course have gone Dell 
(Canadian site) or even MDG.  They all up fropnt only think WINDOZE .
One of those or perhaps a small local retailer with a good reputation - 
perhaps I could get them topartition the disk for Windows and Linux - if 
that's what is needed. (I currently have the Penguin - an early version 
of Red Hat Linux, (nevert installed) - but probably now way out of date).

 >>and have no problem connecting to other windows systems using PC 
Anywhere,......
 >>Pain in the ass to learn, but then they all are.  Not doing any Cobol 
on it yet.

Be just my luck Donald - I'll finish up with PC Nowhere. Back to Xenix 
days - that was a slightly simpler pain in the ass. Just learned 
sufficient to make RM/COBOL tick.
 
Do you guys want to go over this in a little more detail - do I need to 
partition the disk ? There's really no rush on my part, as I haven't yet 
parted with any money. Spot price was $1,400 - then added a flat screen 
plus GST came to $2,185. Talked to the salesman yesterday - now we are 
down to $1,800. Chop out the MS software - could probably be close or 
even below the $1,400.

(That damned GST -  Retail $100, add Provincial Tax (zero in Alberta) + 
GST @ 7% = $107 - in UK Retail Price = 107,  the VAT is *inclusive*, 
regardless of the actual percentage).

Given a choice between Meccano (bits of metal, nuts and bolts etc), or 
Leggo (just plug 'n play - that is, squeeeze the pieces together), then 
I'll accept the challenge of the most difficult one - Leggo :-)

Jiimmy
```

```
still freezes boot into Safe Mode and try again.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:aF7ud.470251$nl.311297@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> >"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> >news:6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no...
<snip>>
> Solution new desktop Windows XP .
XP Pro is a good operating system. The first one from MicroSoft that I have
been truly satisfied with.


> (Hey, watch it there on the beach.
> Just read to-day a laptop can cause infertility :-) ).

I think it is too late for me now. I have started seeing a new girlfriend
(who is an absolute delight) and she has an 8 year old. Maybe my parental
experience will be vicarious. <G>

> Here's the
> luverly bit - the guys who sold it to me gave me Access, Word, Excel
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is fairly standard for OEM copies ot be pre-installed. You can still
request a CD for backup and re-installation purposes.

> Word - every damn bit of correspondence you get, seems you need Word.

Outlook allows you to use Word as your mail editor. You don't HAVE to.
> Excel - could do without it, (although I had it in mind to do some very
> simple graphing). Access - that's the route I went - and as I've
> indicated previously, from the M/F IDE I generate SQL syntax and do a
> copy and paste into COBOL source.
Sure, that's a viable approach. It sounds like they have installed an OEM
verison of Office XP (I use that). You won't save anything by not having
Excel as it is part of the package.
>
> I haven't yet plonked down the money, salesman was off sick with flu,
> then he thought I would contact him, and I thought he would contact me -
> bloody human communication. So I could still back off on MS Access and
> go MySQL - if that's what it is called.

You can whether you have ACCESS or not. Might be useful to have both. (I
find it so...).


>(With the exception of Word,
> not using the MS stuff could save me $300-400 approx). But even with
> your background in DBs you did indicate it was a little tricky getting
> started. I'd have to check with M/F but assuming  'MySql' can be added
> to the ODBC list and I can still generate my SQLs as copy and
pastes -------
> Any thoughts ?
>

You go to the MySQL site and download MySQL and MyODBC. MyODBC installs
itself as a driver and appears under your ODBC32 drivers when you create a
new DSN. (So, instead of selecting "MS ACCESS DATABASE" as the driver for
your new DB, you select "MySQL driver 4.01" or whatever. The BIG advantage
that MyODBC adds is the ability to place your database on a Web Server so it
can be accessed remotely by other people as well as yourself (you give them
the server details, of course). This means you can share it over the
Internet. The MS drivers don't do that.(At least, when I tried to use a
remote server as a data source they wouldn't accept it.)

Enjoy the new system!

Pete.
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Word - every damn bit of correspondence you get, seems you need Word.  
> Excel - could do without it, (although I had it in mind to do some very 
> simple graphing).

You might want to look at http://www.openoffice.org - it's an 
open-source office productivity suite.  Currently, it can read and write 
Microsoft formats, and you'd get replacements for Word and Excel, along 
with a pretty nice drawing tool (I've used it to create database 
diagrams at work - the folks there couldn't believe it was done with a 
free tool).  They have versions for Windows and Linux (and Mac too, I 
think), so if you switch operating systems, you still have the same tools.

> Access - that's the route I went - and as I've 
> indicated previously, from the M/F IDE I generate SQL syntax and do a 
> copy and paste into COBOL source.

MySQL has some new tools that let you develop the query against your 
database - then, I imagine, you could do the same thing with 
cut-and-paste.  (You'll still have to add the INTO clause and your host 
language variables - I'm not familiar with M/F's IDE...)

> I haven't yet plonked down the money, salesman was off sick with flu, 
> then he thought I would contact him, and I thought he would contact me - 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the ODBC list and I can still generate my SQLs as copy and pastes 

Go to http://dev.mysql.com - in the last year, they've brought out a 
couple of GUI-oriented tools that you can use to set up your MySQL 
server, create databases, etc.  You can take a look around - it's all 
free!  :)

If you've got any MySQL questions, feel free to shoot 'em to me - I've 
not yet used it in the context of COBOL, but I've used it just itself, 
and through ASP, VB, and PHP.
```

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <aF7ud.470251$nl.311297@pd7tw3no> <hhHud.6316$ZP5.272@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
LX-i wrote:

Thanks for the info Daniel. However, unless there is some *major* 
advantage, I really don't want to swap GUI tools. I've got a reasonable 
handle on the Micro Focus approach and that integrates, via things like 
Collections, throughout their whole OO concept.

> James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well to clarify. Pete has produced his own DECLGEN, which already does a 
nifty job. He reads your MS Access Tables, (or others) and generates 
copyfiles giving you COBOL formats - from memory,  primarily pic 
s9(09)v9(09).

The M/F tool takes it a stage beyond that - generating a copyfile in 
three parts - (1) the format required by the SQL Preprocessor, (2) the 
COBOL format - same as Pete's above, and (3) also used by the 
preprocessor a set of NULLs. (So currently I extract # 2 as a separate 
copyfile which I can use in 'Business Logic' programs/classes).

Register the appropriate DB against the Windows ODBC and then you can 
access it through the ESQL Assistant. Select your DB then a specific 
table. Now select your type of query, select all columns or specific, 
and selection criteria - on screen it builds the SQL statement. You can 
even do a test run against your DB, directly from this tool, against 
your DB to test results. Once you've got it figured, now copy/paste the 
above SQL statement into your COBOL program/class - bullet-proof syntax, 
even handling NULLs, based on your DB set-up. Of course get the query 
logic wrong and although it will run, GIGO !

Haven't tried it yet - wasn't aware it could be done. In setting up the 
query above, I can do Table joins.

End of post.....

Jimmy

>> I haven't yet plonked down the money, salesman was off sick with flu, 
>> then he thought I would contact him, and I thought he would contact 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<jR5vd.7051$ZP5.3914@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<_g2vd.473990$Pl.193433@pd7tw1no>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZNO$neflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <pd3hr05k0s989uqh9mfcug5f6bk29ici39@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no> <31sb3bF3h21qoU1@individual.net> <aF7ud.470251$nl.311297@pd7tw3no> <hhHud.6316$ZP5.272@fe40.usenetserver.com> <_g2vd.473990$Pl.193433@pd7tw1no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Well to clarify. Pete has produced his own DECLGEN, which already does a 
> nifty job. He reads your MS Access Tables, (or others) and generates 
> copyfiles giving you COBOL formats - from memory,  primarily pic 
> s9(09)v9(09).

Yes - I've seen an evaluation copy of that software.  :)

> The M/F tool takes it a stage beyond that - generating a copyfile in 
> three parts - (1) the format required by the SQL Preprocessor, (2) the 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> query above, I can do Table joins.

If it uses ODBC, it should work with MyODBC or MySQL Connector/ODBC 
(whatever it is they're calling it now).  Depending on the SQL it 
generates for the JOINs, the 4.1 series may or may not support it.  I 
can't remember if subqueries (in my opinion, its one deficiency) are in 
4.1, or if they're in 5.0, now beta.

They've also taken over the SAP database, and called it MaxDB by MySQL. 
  They recently released a graphical admin console for it.  It's the 
other side of the coin - tougher to set up, but supports about 
everything you could think of.

> End of post.....

For some reason, this reminded me of the Master Control Program from TRON...

END OF LINE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T07:26:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9McVvLv9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MZSySVPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no>`

```
.    On  09.12.04
  wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  6c5ud.473437$%k.409029@pd7tw2no
  about  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS


JJG> Lueko, your question was :-

>>>  I wonder about that scenario where it would make sense to open 9
>>> files at once. Could you explain?

JJG> And he gave you an answer.

   An enlightening one. But even with such a database without database  
management system, I would think that the files do not have to be  
opened all at once, but according to their logical relationship, when  
the files are needed. I.e. for many programs, the address related to  
an account is not necessary, for others one doesn't need the insurance  
details when only the current account is of interest, etc.

   Also, I don't need to open an output file when I can't access the  
input file.

   This reasoning is behind my question.

JJG> Now if you want to ask :-

>>>  I wonder about that scenario where it would make sense to open 9
>>> *TABLES* or *DBs* at once. Could you explain?

   That is done automatically by the database system. The program  
interfaces those tables thru the database management system, not  
directly.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch, das in der Welt am ehesten verboten zu werden verdiente, wï¿½re ein Katalogus von verbotenen Bï¿½chern. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T19:33:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpkqsa$d0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <9MR7AwfuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30rbr0hhhukth2unir0s3kamicavlqk010@4ax.com> <9MVCdqNuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <t8hdr0p5lucbaek36th03t4ch3ulb7lpap@4ax.com> <ZxItd.6424636$6p.1014073@news.easynews.com> <d25fr05jn00dq46hr7aapehdc73au1kli2@4ax.com> <cpk94r$j3g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j2prr0dbf53f8m8g7kbb0qe22tsqip70sc@4ax.com>`

```

On 13-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >> >You would also have problems with a SORT or MERGE statement - without
> >> >Input/Output Procedures.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why write a Cobol program containing no logic?

Huh?

Typical CoBOL program with a SORT statement:
Read database, calculate result, sort on result, write report.

Same type of logic as a program without a SORT statement, except the output is
sorted.
```

```
On 13-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >> >> >You would also have problems with a SORT or MERGE statement - without
> >> >> >Input/Output Procedures.
...

> The reading and calculating would be in the Input Procedure; report
> printing, in the Output Procedure.

I was reading this to be without "either an input or an output procedure"
instead of "without either an input nor an output procedure"

I do have one case where I would consider not having either.   That is where
some data or user parm determines the sort key.   The data can even be
calculated  e.g.   If the sales amount is greater than $1,000,000 then sort the
file by division.
```

```

Robert Wagner wrote:
> These standards apply to all new Cobol code.

[snip]

> .. Counters must have unique names.
>    Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.

Perhaps this explains the example that I once cited from Bentley's
_Programming Pearls_ (p 23) where a program designed to tally numbers
from 1 to 500 contained 500 counters, 500 if statements and 500 display
statements.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T03:03:30+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31lrhpF3fbrjlU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <1102427068.417283.287150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```

<epc8@juno.com> wrote in message
news:1102427068.417283.287150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
> > These standards apply to all new Cobol code.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Although somewhat mathematically challenged myself, I can tell you
immediately, without a program or calculator, the answer is: 125250

Friedrich Gauss figured this out when he was 8 years old. He "folded" the
outer limits (1 +500) and multiplied that by the mid-point (250). He
realised that this was equivalent to: 1 + 500 = 501, 2+ 499 = 501, 3 + 498 =
501... 250 times...

Fiendish cunning these Germans... <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** epc8@juno.com
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T11:52:29-08:00

```
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

+ 498 =

Sorry, input was not just the intgers from 1 to 500 but data from a
file of numbers ranging from 1 to 500. The desired output was the data
needed to produce a frequency histogram - the number of 1s, 2s, etc. In
this case, it's a lot easier to use a table (with all entries
originally set to zero) as a counting array.
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T09:10:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4dkg$7qv$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
In article <1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>These standards apply to all new Cobol code.
>
>.. Fixed format.

IBM mainframe here.

>
>.. A period at the end of every non-nested statement.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No blank data names. 
>    You must code FILLER.
>   If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No inline comments.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No REDEFINES.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No qualification.

Not at my current site.

>   
>.. No more than one statement per line.

Not at my current site.

>   Don't put COPY on the same line with 01.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. File descriptions in copybooks
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>record copybooks for each file.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No REPLACING.
>   If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
>   six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No common key areas.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   key definition with OCCURS is not permitted.

Not too sure what is intended here... but on my curent site there are no 
resrictions (outside of what the architeture imposes, of course) regarding 
key structure or manipulation.

>
>.. No shared File Status code.
>   Code a separate pic xx field for each file.
>   This implies no common error handling; each file has its own.

Not at my current site... although it is my habit to code separate 
statuses.

>
>.. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
>   record areas. 

Not at my current site.

>
>.. Counters must have unique names.
>   Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.
>   Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

Not at my current site.

>
>Procedure Division
>
>.. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
>   in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. No backward PERFORMs.

Not at my current site... but this is one that I try to adhere to, 
probably out of superstition more than anything else.

>
>.. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
>   get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than arithmetic.
>   For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
>   you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
>   the records and test whether the counter is zero.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. For input files, control adds and sequence checking goes in I/O
>   paragraph(s). For output files, it goes in business logic.

Not at my current site.

>
>.. Follow the spec closely.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>   If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

I see these as two facets of the same phenomenon; it smells like a classic 
Cover Your Ass scenario:

'Why did you code it this way?'

'Because the spec said so.'

>
>.. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right
>   Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
>   Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
>             ws-imbalance-2 = zero

Not at my current site.

>
>.. ZERO is numeric.
>   When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.

Not at my current site.

So, Mr Wagner... our experiences are different, is the immediate and 
reasonable conclusion that your experiences are incorrect?

DD
```

```
>Not at my current site.
>
>So, Mr Wagner... our experiences are different, is the immediate and 
>reasonable conclusion that your experiences are incorrect?

They are correct according to the dictionary:

1 : conforming to an approved or conventional standard

Definitely correct.

2 : conforming to or agreeing with fact, logic, or known truth

Fact: yes. Logic or known truth: I think not, but what do I know?
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T14:34:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4f2n$ssd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```

On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> These standards apply to all new Cobol code.
>
> .. Fixed format.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. A period at the end of every non-nested statement.

I've seen some people who dislike this, but they have been unable to convince me
that this is bad.

> .. No blank data names.
>     You must code FILLER.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

>    If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. No inline comments.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. No REDEFINES.

I agree that this limits the code a lot.    Interesting that this standard is
not a left over from some previous era.

> .. No qualification.

This limits the code some.   I wonder if there was a procedure to break the
standard when applicable.   (Most shops allow appeal of standards)

> .. No more than one statement per line.
>    Don't put COPY on the same line with 01.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. File descriptions in copybooks
>    One each for Select, FD and record (in working-storage). If a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> record copybooks for each file.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. No REPLACING.
>    If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
>    six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

Odd, that.    This could make things more difficult.

> .. No common key areas.
>    When key fields in the record are not contiguous, you will assemble
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    key definition with OCCURS is not permitted.

Odd, but: Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. No shared File Status code.
>    Code a separate pic xx field for each file.
>    This implies no common error handling; each file has its own.

I think I agree with this.   Hiding methods makes errors harder to trace down.
If I look at this dump, I can see every File Status of every file.

> .. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
>    record areas.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. Counters must have unique names.

I think I agree with this for the reason I gave above.

>    Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.
>    Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

I can see advantages both ways.    But my reason for saying something is good
code or bad code isn't how easy it is to write.

> Procedure Division
>
> .. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
>    in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

I had to 15 years ago working for EDS, and I objected then.   My objection will
have more strength though when I work with compilers that allow EXITING from
performed paragraphs.    Meanwhile, those advocating this style have a valid
argument in that maintenance can be easier using the "least impact" style of
making changes.   This style is designed for ease of maintenance.

> .. No backward PERFORMs.

Odd, but:  Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
>    get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.    Creative names do obfuscate a bit.

> .. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than arithmetic.
>    For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
>    you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
>    the records and test whether the counter is zero.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. For input files, control adds and sequence checking goes in I/O
>    paragraph(s). For output files, it goes in business logic.

I'm not sure I follow.

> .. Follow the spec closely.
>    For example, if the spec says:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>              ...

That's not a coding standard, that is not trusting the programmer.   I hate
shops that virtually write the code in the specs and require that the coder not
be a programmer.

>    If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

See above.    They must not have trusted the coder.

> .. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right
>    Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
>    Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
>              ws-imbalance-2 = zero

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.

> .. ZERO is numeric.
>    When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.

Who cares?  it doesn't make the code bad.   This code is very slightly better -
not enough that I'd make it a standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T22:28:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3qvtd.5655$Yq6.3808@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> These standards apply to all new Cobol code.

And these replies refer to the Unisys 2200 UCS COBOL compiler, and my 
shop standards in my current shop.  :)

> .. Fixed format.

Which is the only option...

> .. A period at the end of every non-nested statement.
> 
> .. No blank data names. 
>     You must code FILLER.
>    If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

I disagree with these two, but I've maintained programs that followed 
those rules.

> .. No inline comments.

If you're talking about *> the rest of this line is ignored
stuff like that, we don't have that.  If you're talking about comments 
within paragraphs, I disagree (especially with complex structures - why 
not have a comment that says what it does?).

> .. No REDEFINES.

Not really necessary, since we have reference modification (and I didn't 
see it prohibited in these rules), but a meaningful name breakout can 
really help a maintainer understand what the program's doing.

> .. No qualification.

We don't have this standard, but we have one against Move Corresponding. 
  There are few instances where I'd really be *for* Move Corresponding, 
and qualification is a pain.  However, for our "direct" database 
records, the compiler generates something like...

01  UVR-KEY.
     03  PAGE-NUM        PIC 1(18) BINARY-1.
     03  RECORD-NUM      PIC 1(18) BINARY-1.

, so there's no real way to eliminate qualification there. 
System-provided copy procedures have similar names, so qualification is 
a must there as well.  I did code one Move Corresponding recently, and 
it was agreed that since the individual underlying items were not 
reference on their own, the reviewers allowed it to pass.  (It was an 
error handling routine, and I moved "corr" the status code to the 
displayed status line - broke it up quite nicely.)

> .. No more than one statement per line.
>    Don't put COPY on the same line with 01.

This just makes it more readable.  I have been known to code error 
handling like so, though...

evaluate error-num
   when zeroes      continue
   when "0013"      set notFound to true
   when other       go to 900-dml-error
end-evaluate

But, if any of those had not fit, or had more than one line, there 
wouldn't have been an imperative statement on the same line as a 
conditional one.

> .. File descriptions in copybooks
>    One each for Select, FD and record (in working-storage). If a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> record copybooks for each file.

This one hasn't really been an issue - all of our FDs are in the 
programs themselves.  (But, our system doesn't really share files either...)

> .. No REPLACING.
>    If there are six files, there must be six select copybooks,
>    six FD copybooks and at least six record copybooks.

Not a standard in our shop, probably because they didn't even know about 
replacing (the free-format kind, anyway) until I brought it up to them. 
  We do have procs that had $$$-VAR-NAME that *have* to have a replacing 
clause on the proc if they are to compile properly - that's been that 
way since before I got there.

> .. No common key areas.
>    When key fields in the record are not contiguous, you will assemble
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>    This implies no common error handling; each file has its own.

Not standards in our shop.

> .. All working-storage names must be prefixed with ws-, including
>    record areas. 

Minus the record areas, this one is.  We've approved ws without the dash 
(i.e. wsMyVar).  The way our database is laid out, this helps - if you 
see EQPP-EQUIP-ID, you know it comes from the EQPP-158 record.  If 
someone redefined that in working-storage as EQPP-EQUIPMENT-ID, you'd 
expect that data item to exist in that record as well - but it doesn't.

> .. Counters must have unique names.
>    Do not use OCCURS. If there are 120 counters, write 120 names.
>    Do not use OCCURS on report lines with columns of numbers.

If the counters are counting unique things, then they need unique names. 
  If you've got an array of counters (for example, days of the month or 
year), occurs would be fine.  We have no standard about this one way or 
the other.

> Procedure Division
> 
> .. Paragraph names must begin with a five digit number, which are
>    in sequence and indicate logical hierarchy.

This one was recently changed to a suggestion.  :)

> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

Specifically prohibited per our standards - thru is discouraged, but if 
it's used, it's only to be used to the exit of that paragraph, and not 
to encompass multiple paragraphs.

> .. No backward PERFORMs.

Seems to be common sense - but, also not addressed in our standards.

> .. Main paragraphs must be named -init, -process and -wrap. Don't
>    get creative with names like beginning, middle and end.

Nope.

> .. Control checking logic must be procedural rather than arithmetic.
>    For example, when the spec says 'abend when the input is empty',
>    you must check for end of file after the first read. Do not count
>    the records and test whether the counter is zero.

Also not addressed - but wouldn't a read/test be quicker than counting, 
especially if you've got 10,000 records in the file?

> .. For input files, control adds and sequence checking goes in I/O
>    paragraph(s). For output files, it goes in business logic.

We have a standard that says all file I/O *should* be in a separate 
paragraph.  In practice, it's not enforced.

> .. Follow the spec closely.
>    For example, if the spec says:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>    If the spec has nested IFs, do not change them to EVALUATE.

If my spec had that level of detail, it'd get a NO GO, telling the 
functional to give me what it's supposed to do, and let ME do the design 
and code (which happens to be what I, not they, are paid to do).  (And I 
have done so, recently...)

> .. In conditionals, the literal goes on the right
>    Wrong: if zero = ws-imbalance-1 and ws-imbalance-2
>    Right: if ws-imbalance-1 = zero and
>              ws-imbalance-2 = zero

Not addressed in our standards.  Personally, if ws-imbalance-1 was 
numeric, I'd prefer IS zero over = zero.

> .. ZERO is numeric.
>    When checking file status, use '00' rather than ZERO.

Shop standard is silent, but I prefer the word "zeroes".  If the 
variable is numeric, I'll check "is zero" - if it's alphanumeric, but 
could contain zeroes, I'll check "= zeroes" (see my example of error-num 
above - it's defined by the database as pic X(4)).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T13:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9MVFroU9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com> <3qvtd.5655$Yq6.3808@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  07.12.04
  wrote  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  3qvtd.5655$Yq6.3808@fe40.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS


>> .. All PERFORMs must be THRU nnnnn-name-EXIT, not nnnnn-EXIT.

l> Specifically prohibited per our standards - thru is discouraged, but
l> if it's used, it's only to be used to the exit of that paragraph, and
l> not to encompass multiple paragraphs.

   But then it _does_ encompass multiple paragraphs, at least two.

   The exit of a paragraph is the last line before the next SECTION  
header or paragraph label, and that is then a next paragraph on its   
own. The only relationship these paragraphs have, is that there is not  
another paragraph between them.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

Wir wohnen in Gï¿½ttingen in Scheiterhaufen, die mit Tï¿½ren und Fenstern versehen sind. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: MAINFRAME SHOP STANDARDS

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T23:37:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cyvtd.24336$l%5.1088800@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com>`

```
Best laugh I've had all week.

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T13:27:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpl1fq$e27$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:1laar0hcgea9dt2rchgh69m4p24inmp8rj@4ax.com...

> .. No blank data names.
>     You must code FILLER.
>    If all operations are via 88 level, don't use FILLER, name it.

Without the 2002 features "VALUE WHEN SET TO FALSE" and "SET Level-88-item
TO FALSE", I can see your point -- but if an implementation supports this
feature, I see no reason to preclude its use save portability to a
less-progressive compiler.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-ced60e12c3"></a>
## Thread: Alternatives to Pan Valet for Blind Programmer — 5 msgs

### Alternatives to Pan Valet for Blind Programmer

- **From:** JGH <johnheim@nospam.tds.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T19:14:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
I'm blind and I need to write a COBOL program for an IBM mainframe. I 
haven't written COBOL in years but that's another story...

Because I'm blind, I need to edit the source code on a machine with a 
screen reader. Often on linux or unix machines, I can map a share via samba 
and use the editor on my Windows PC. But sometimes I edit a file locally 
and use FTP to post changes to the target machine.

What options do I have on an IBM mainframe?
```

#### ↳ Re: Alternatives to Pan Valet for Blind Programmer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T19:32:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dzntd.4272946$yk.643692@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
I am visually impaired and not blind. I simply use a (very) LARGE screen.  I 
have sent your note to someone in IBM who might be able to help.  I'll let you 
know what I hear back.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Wntd.454769$nl.337013@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<dzntd.4272946$yk.643692@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu> <dzntd.4272946$yk.643692@news.easynews.com>`

```

>I am visually impaired and not blind. I simply use a (very) LARGE screen.  I 
>have sent your note to someone in IBM who might be able to help.  I'll let you 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Good on you Mr. Helpful.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Alternatives to Pan Valet for Blind Programmer

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T19:06:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-318697.19065907122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
In article <cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu>,
 JGH <johnheim@nospam.tds.net> wrote:

> I'm blind and I need to write a COBOL program for an IBM mainframe. I 
> haven't written COBOL in years but that's another story...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> What options do I have on an IBM mainframe? 

One of the things you might look at is the ISPF Workstation Agent.

It can be configured to make "edit" commands route to a workstation and 
fire up the editor of your choice -- when you save the file it is 
updated back on the mainframe.

It was a really nice integration idea from OS/2 that IBM did absolutely 
nothing with -- but it still exists and is available for Windows and 
Unix.
```

#### ↳ Re: Alternatives to Pan Valet for Blind Programmer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T01:39:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FXstd.447632$SM5.26563@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cp4ve9$hel$3@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
The following is the reply that I received from my "source" at IBM,

Bill,

Well, using a tool like PCOM (IBM Personal Communications) provides access
to mainframe for visually impaired people, and WSED is fully approved for
'accessibility' for impaired people. The mainframe itself does not have 
anything,
but hardly anyone uses mainframe stuff for communicating with mainframes,
they use TCP/IP, browsers, workstation emulators, etc. Who has a 3278 anymore?
```

```

function (using the FUNCTION-ID paragraph) in one program, and then
making that function available in another program?

I've scoured the group and all of the references I can think of for a
good working example, but I cannot seem to find any.

and make those functions available to all other programs on the system.
Thanks in advance!

Chris
```

## Thread: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions — 2 msgs

### Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T13:58:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102456712.216630.183780@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

and make those functions available to all other programs on the system.
```
