```

#### ↳ Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T06:26:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102602383.000047.225260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1102456712.216630.183780@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1102456712.216630.183780@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
All - thank you all very much for your input. I have found this group
to be invaluable for finding creative - sometimes alternative (thanks
Jimmy) - ways to approach different scenarios I have encountered. With
that in mind, I wanted do my part to contribute and post the solution,
as provided to me by Micro Focus. It is two-fold:

1) As Bill pointed out in this thread, trying to pass/return the same
variable in the function was causing problems. You need to use two
different variables for this.
2) I was using the REPOSITORY directive improperly. Also, when the
REPOSITORY directive is used properly, you can also specify the RDFPATH
as the target for the repository information, otherwise it will default
to the source directory.

The following code samples work under MF Server Exress 4.0 SP1. No
special command line options are needed during the compile, simply use
cob <file>.

<myfunctions.cbl>

$set repository(update on)
identification division.

function-id. mydouble.
environment division.
data division.
linkage section.
01  in-number  pic  9(2).
01  out-number  pic  9(2).

procedure division using in-number returning out-number.

compute out-number = in-number * 2 end-compute
exit function
.

end function mydouble.

<myfunctiontest.cbl>

identification division.
program-id. myfunctiontest.

environment division.
configuration section.
repository.
function mydouble
.

data division.
working-storage section.
01  num-1     pic  9(2).
01  num-2     pic  9(2).

procedure division.

move 5 to num-1
compute num-2 = mydouble(num-1) end-compute
exhibit named num-2
         goback returning 0
         .
```

---

Chris,
   What compiler are you using?

feature of the '02 Standard.  When available, you would want to look at the 
REPOSITORY paragraph to make you "common" user-defined functions generally 
available.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T15:41:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102462892.728327.208390@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<VQptd.437946$SM5.25511@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1102456722.731239.184810@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <VQptd.437946$SM5.25511@news.easynews.com>`

```
Bill,

Thanks for the response. I am using the latest version of Micro Focus
Server Express 4.0 SP1 - for HP-UX 11.11, in 64-bit mode.

The source code I am using looks like this:

myfunctions.cbl

identification division.

function-id. mydouble.
environment division.
data division.
linkage section.
01  a-number  pic  9(2).

procedure division using a-number returning a-number.

compute a-number = a-number * 2 end-compute
exit function
.

end function mydouble.

When I compile myfunctions.cbl (cob function.cbl), I get MYDOUBLE.int,
MYDOUBLE.idy - which is what I would expect.

Then I have a secondary program - myfunctiontest.cbl:

identification division.
program-id. myfunctiontest.

environment division.
configuration section.
repository.
function mydouble .

data division.
working-storage section.
01  num-1     pic  9(2).
01  num-2     pic  9(2).

procedure division.

move 5 to num-1
compute num-2 = mydouble(num-1) end-compute
exhibit named num-2
goback returning 0
.

When I compile this program (cob myfunctiontest.cbl), I get the
following compile error:

18   compute num-2 = mydouble(num-1) end-compute
*1304-S**************************
**
**    Method not found in the repository.

If I try this:

compute num-2 = function mydouble(num-1) end-compute

I get the same compiler error.

Micro Focus' documentation is not very thorough, so pehaps someone -
like yourself, whom I've noticed from previous posts - that is
extrememly knowledgeable and well versed on the standards could point
me in the correct direction.

If it works, I plan on building a single "function library" that can be
used throughout application development and thus eliminate a lot of
repitition.

Thanks in advance!

Chris
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T21:33:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jqKtd.441329$Pl.224988@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1102462892.728327.208390@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1102456722.731239.184810@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <VQptd.437946$SM5.25511@news.easynews.com> <1102462892.728327.208390@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

Bill - reaction ?

Bearing in mind, regardless of what is in COBOL 2002, Chris is working 
to N/E V 4.0 .
He had the following code :-

>The source code I am using looks like this:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
**** Chris - following line will get you into trouble with OO - probably 
same applies with
UDFs - coding "using x returning x" = unpredictable results. Play it 
safe, coding, "using A returning B".

>procedure division using a-number returning a-number.
>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Without delving into UDF syntax which somewhat parallels OO Method 
syntax, all the LRM gives is :-

COMPUTE = an *arithmetic* expression

unless somewhere under UDFs that "arithmetic" bit is expanded.

Now look at his code :-

compute num-2 = function mydouble(num-1) end-compute

It looks a bit like a bastardization of an OO in-line invocation where 
'mydouble' is the function-name being invoked and num-1 is being passed 
to the receiving function as a parameter. Can't see it easily in the M/F 
on-line manuals. Is that a feature allowed in COBOL 2002  ? (I would 
have anticipated seeing either CALL or INVOKE to get at a function - but 
without the compiler, who knows).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T22:13:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g0Ltd.4331537$yk.651620@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1102456722.731239.184810@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <VQptd.437946$SM5.25511@news.easynews.com> <1102462892.728327.208390@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <jqKtd.441329$Pl.224988@pd7tw1no>`

```
The word "function" for BOTH an intrinsic and a user-defined function is 
OPTIONAL in the '02 Standard (and I would guess in MF) *if* the proper 
"repository paragraph" option is used.

e.g.

Compute Numfield = Function NumVal (ABC)
    is the same as
Compute NumField = NumVal (ABC)

If

  Repository.
      Function ALL Intrinsic.

appears in the Environment Division / Configuration Section.

Similarly,

 Compute XYZ = Function User-Defined-with-Numeric-Returned (ABC XYZ)

is the same as

  Compute XYZ = Function User-Defined-with-Numeric-Returned (ABC XYZ)

if you code

 Repository.
    Function User-Defined-with-Numeric-Returned.

*and* you created a "function prototype" for User-Defined-with-Numeric-Returned

   ***

Does this answer your question Jimmy?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions

.    On  08.12.04
  wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  jqKtd.441329$Pl.224988@pd7tw1no
  about  Re: Using FUNCTION-ID to create user-defined functions


JJG> compute num-2 = function mydouble(num-1) end-compute
JJG>
JJG> It looks a bit like a bastardization of an OO in-line invocation
JJG> where 'mydouble' is the function-name being invoked and num-1 is
JJG> being passed to the receiving function as a parameter. Can't see it

   Yes, and I see it as a step forward by this 2002 standard.

JJG> to get at a function - but

   No, it is a function, because a function returns a value, which can  
be used in any expression.

- **References:** `<1102456722.731239.184810@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <VQptd.437946$SM5.25511@news.easynews.com>`

```
Bill,

Thanks for the response. I am using the latest version of Micro Focus
Server Express 4.0 SP1 - for HP-UX 11.11, in 64-bit mode.

The source code I am using looks like this:

myfunctions.cbl

identification division.

function-id. mydouble.
environment division.
data division.
linkage section.
01  a-number  pic  9(2).

procedure division using a-number returning a-number.

compute a-number = a-number * 2 end-compute
exit function
.

end function mydouble.

When I compile myfunctions.cbl (cob function.cbl), I get MYDOUBLE.int,
MYDOUBLE.idy - which is what I would expect.

Then I have a secondary program - myfunctiontest.cbl:

identification division.
program-id. myfunctiontest.

environment division.
configuration section.
repository.
function mydouble .

data division.
working-storage section.
01  num-1     pic  9(2).
01  num-2     pic  9(2).

procedure division.

move 5 to num-1
compute num-2 = mydouble(num-1) end-compute
exhibit named num-2
goback returning 0
.

When I compile this program (cob myfunctiontest.cbl), I get the
following compile error:

18   compute num-2 = mydouble(num-1) end-compute
*1304-S**************************
**
**    Method not found in the repository.

If I try this:

compute num-2 = function mydouble(num-1) end-compute

I get the same compiler error.

Micro Focus' documentation is not very thorough, so pehaps someone -
like yourself, whom I've noticed from previous posts - that is
extrememly knowledgeable and well versed on the standards could point
me in the correct direction.

If it works, I plan on building a single "function library" that can be
used throughout application development and thus eliminate a lot of
repitition.

Thanks in advance!

Chris
```

Hello,

Forgive me if this is the wrong group for my question. I am a sys admin
supporting a group of developers. They have reported this error to me
which occurs when they attempt to run a script which at some point
executes a compiled cobol program.

ASLM: Semaphore failure. Rerun as root
ASLM: Errno 1013

The product is Microfocus Server Express running on sparc Solaris. The
compiler environment was setup before I got here so I'm pretty lost but
from some google searches it seems that this may be a runtime licensing
issue. Could anyone point me in the right direction to troubleshoot
this error.

TIA,
Vic

#### ↳ Re: Semaphore failure. Rerun as root

- **From:** "Bill Yarnall" <Bill.Yarnall@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T10:33:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpa5k1$hde$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<1102536131.508631.110250@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
This is an error that occurs when attempting to 'check-out' a Micro Focus
Application Server (Runtime) license. Here's a possible explanation:

This error message may be caused by a missing semaphore file (aslmfsem) in
/var, or if the permsissions on the file, or on /var are inadequate.
Since the file is zero length, it can be simply created using the following
command, having cd'ed into /var.


   touch aslmfsem
The file should have R/W permissions for all, and should be chowned to root.
If the file is present, with the correct permissions and the problem
persists, check that the permissions on /var itself allow users to access
this file.

<vic@summerseas.com> wrote in message
news:1102536131.508631.110250@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
> Hello,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-3234859bb4"></a>
## Thread: Style - OO vs User-Defined Functions — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Style - OO vs User-Defined Functions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T22:56:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`

```
We (CLC) are having SO much fun (???) talking about "style" issues, I thought 
that I would start a new thread on another "style" issue.

First, let me state that I *do* believe that what I am talking about in this 
note is a CHOICE without a "right or wrong" answer, but it is also an issue that 
MIGHT be worth thinking about (sooner than later).

The question is:
    Given a FULLY CONFORMING 2002 COBOL compiler (a thing that doesn't exist 
today - that I know of), then what would be the factors that one might consider 
in deciding when a piece of new "procedural logic" should be created in OO and 
when should it be a user-defined function?

What I hope to do in this note is to state some of the things that *I* think 
might be such factors.

1) First thing that comes to mind is the fact that (to me) there may be a 
difference in
    - the code (and requirements for the programmer) who creates the new 
"procedural logic"
        versus
    - the code (and requirements for the programmer) who USES the new 
"procedural logic"

Specifically, creating a "user-defined function" is EASY for anyone who knows 
how to "code" a subprogram.  In fact, the only real difference between a 
subprogram's source code and a user-defined function is the use of FUNCTION-ID 
paragraph and END FUNCTION marker versus PROGRAM-ID and END PROGRAM.  There may 
be *some* semantic implications (last-used state - for example) that need to be 
considered and some "outside" things like function prototypes and repositories, 
but BASICALLY the coding is the same.  In fact, many subprograms CAN be made 
into a user-defined function (assuming they have the '02 Standard "RETURNING" 
phrase) simply via a COPY REPLACING or REPLACE statement.

On the other hand, creating an OO class requires LOTS of "new" syntax and 
understanding.  (Different and "more complex" source structure - or at least 
"more complex" until you have used it - IMHO).

2) Clearly, the "design" issues of user-defined functions (which SHOULD be a 
single procedural task) versus creating an OO class (with data, methods, to 
encapsulate ALL of the "relevant" attributes/actions of an "object") is a very 
different task.  To me, user-defined functions require no new "design 
methodology" while OO done withOUT new methodology is a "bad"idea - if not 
doomed from the start.

3) Assuming (big assumption !!!) that someone has already created a Class (or a 
class library) or a function (or function library), then EITHER seems (to me) to 
be pretty easy to use.

  OO
    - INVOKE (out-of-line)
    - :: (in-line)
  User-Defined Function
    - <function>  (in-line only, use CALL and subprogram for out-of-line)

4) What about interfacing with other languages:
     Interestingly enough, there doesn't seem to be much "literature" (that I am 
aware of) about using "user-defined functions" from non-COBOL libraries .... 
EVEN THOUGH functions (in such languages as C) have been around for years 
(decades).  I think - but won't swear to it that this is because this has been 
available "transparently" to COBOL vendors with a RETURNING extension for years. 
No one really thought about this as something needing to be "documented".  For 
example ...
    Call "Some_C_Function"
           Using ABC
           Returning XYZ
...
    But now (possibly?)
       Move Function "Some_C_Function" (ABC) to Other-Field  *> with FUNCTION 
now optional word

(of course the new '02 "CALL-CONVENTION" may be relevant here too).

On the other hand, there is LOTS of discussion of using non-COBOL OO Class 
libraries (including inheriting from them and overriding their methods) from 
"new" OO COBOL.

My guess (based solely on impression - no fact) is that *TODAY* there is more 
NEW development of non-COBOL "class libraries" than there is of "general purpose 
functions" (API's).  However, there are certainly PLENTY of "functions" 
available in many operating systems and environments.

5) If one has an "experienced" COBOL programmer who is NOT familiar with coding 
EITHER OO or user-defined functions, then there are questions of:
   - are they picking up one or the other "for the learning experience"?
   - which is likely to be more portable to other COBOL compilers (with PARTIAL 
implementations of the '02 Standard)
   - is this a "one-off" or something that is the start of a new 
shop/application approach (and if so, which will meet the greater set of 
application needs)

6) Are there issues (for this compiler and Operating System) concerning "late 
binding" versus "early binding" (link-editing, whatever) and if so, are there 
testing and "audit trail" implications for one or both of the approaches?  (i.e. 
if one is creating a "general purpose user-defined function" or new OO Class, 
then how easy will it to be to "maintain" with adequate but not burdensome 
restrictions for other/all systems using the new "code").

7) COBOL does *not* have easy ways to define truly "arbitrary" user-defined 
function input parameters (i.e issues with the ANYLENGTH, OPTIONAL, - not to 
mention different classes, categories, and PICTUREs).  On the other hand, OO can 
(with KNOWLEDGEABLE coders - IMHO) handle a variety of "inputs" to the same (or 
similar) methods.  (Personally, I don't think there is any way in STANDARD COBOL 
to create the ORD-MIN intrinsic function - for example - in COBOL.  Creating an 
ORD-MIN-OO "method" for DIFFERENT object types would, however, be if not simple, 
possible - I think.)

    ****

There are probably LOTS of other factors that one might consider for this STYLE 
choice, but I thought others might want to think about (talk about) this.

I guess that my SUGGESTION would be that (assuming that BOTH features were 
available *and* that you had equally skilled programmers available) then ...

   If you have a SINGLE procedural logic "snippet" that you want to make 
"generally available" *and* it has well defined (and not variable) input, then a 
user-defined function would be a good choice (and would have SOME advantages 
over a subprogram)

  If, on the other hand, you have a "series" of similar actions that you want to 
perform on a specific (or set of similar) "objects" then creating an OO Class 
would be a good choice.
```

#### ↳ Re: Style - OO vs User-Defined Functions

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-08T21:35:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cKPtd.5705$Yq6.4676@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I guess that my SUGGESTION would be that (assuming that BOTH features were 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be a good choice.

Sounds like a good plan to me.  :)  Of course, I'm likely a long time 
away from being able to do either...

#### ↳ Re: Style - OO vs User-Defined Functions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T05:10:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<27Rtd.444755$Pl.394845@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>We (CLC) are having SO much fun (???) talking about "style" issues, I thought 
>that I would start a new thread on another "style" issue.
>
>  
>
<snip>

>On the other hand, creating an OO class requires LOTS of "new" syntax and 
>understanding.  (Different and "more complex" source structure - or at least 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Let me jump in here. Stick with Procedural, consider UDFs or go the OO 
route. Without question, OO does introduce new syntax, but the 'mystery' 
part  is  getting a handle on  concepts and objects.
If you go UDFs then logically you want to build a library. In a large 
installation you could have everybody going off doing their own thing, 
multi re-inventions of the wheel, unless *co-ordinated*.
Plus logically you want a library of UDFs. Not dramatically different 
but in the *long-term*, OO  lets you build a library of like functions 
for say Date manipulation as a series of methods within one class - in 
this sense Date Class = Date Library functions.

Selfishly perhaps, but I'm thinking of Chris and his N/E approach to 
getting a handle on  User Defined Functions - and immediatley he runs up 
against 'Repository'. Well that's new, and without V 4.0 source, I would 
hazard a guess functions, (UDFs)  to some extent draw-off exisiting M/F 
OO routines.

What I had intended illustrating to Chris :-

1. He can use Procedural with his own UDFS
2. He can use Procdural with his own OO (purely 'textual' Pic 9/x)
3. Then hopefully, at some stage, he says to himself,  "Hey this OO jazz 
isn't a monster, afer all" - AND - starts dabbling with the full OO 
model. (I bet you've got it Lueko - just in case - "dabbling' is not 
related to'dab-hand' - the former = fiddle, experiment etc.)

So really bare bones OO in the following :-
1. I don't have "MyClass inherits from Base" - first time I tried it, 
but on an error when coding it
still jumped up to Base displaying a "doesNotUnderstand"  message"
2. The exisiting "compute" statement  from Chris is no doubt slicker; I 
don't have in-line invocaton :: - otherwise I could have given it a try.
3. As is, subject to changing 'Repository' syntax, fair chance this will 
work with Fujitsu, even early versions.
4. I've tried to indicate, in a very small example, that the OO code is 
flexible. (There will be endless permutations I haven't given thought to).
5. I have *not* used the words OBJECT or OBJECT REFERENCE in the code.

Here goes :-

       *>------------------ FunctionTest.cbl---------------------------

       *> This (N/E V 3.1) doesn't need any Directives.
       *> To make it 2002 compliant for N/E V 4.0 subsitute Repository
       *> synatax for the "Class-Control" entries.

       Program-id.         FunctionTest.

       Class-Control.      MyDouble    is class "mydouble"
                           MyFunction2 is class "myfunk2"
                           .
       Working-Storage Section.
       01 charx                          pic x.
       01 num-1                          pic 9(03).
       01 num-2                          pic 9(03).
       01 num-3                          pic 9(03).

       Procedure Division.

       *> Two possibities :-

       *> 1 - could invoke "new" for what I want, returning os-Someobject.
       *> So here I have created an "instance" then invoke os-Someobject
       *> os-Someobject "dothis".......

       *> If anybody is curious why I use os-SomeObject, instead of say,
       *> SomeObject - old habits die hard. In N/E V 3.1
       *> "Working-Storage Section" is "Object-Storage Section" in order
       *> to create 'instances' - so my standard prefix is 'os-'

       *> 2 - trying to stay 'faithful' to UDFs I'll keep the methods in
       *> the FACTORY only, therefore no "instances"

        move 5 to num-1
        invoke MyDouble "mydouble" using num-1 returning num-2
        invoke MyFunction2 "myFunction2" using num-2 returning num-3
        display num-2
        display num-3
        accept charx
        STOP RUN.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Class-id.               MyDouble.

       Class-Control.          MyDouble is class 'mydouble'
                               .
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "mydouble".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       linkage section.
       01  a-number  pic  9(03).
       01  b-number  pic  9(03).
       procedure division using a-number returning b-number.

       *> It's really a No-No in OO to use same variable for using/
       *> returning - unpredictable results. Probably the same
       *> applies to UDFs
       *> You really only need Exits when you are jumping out of a
       *> function/method resulting from a conditional test

       compute b-number = a-number * 2 end-compute
       .
       *>EXIT METHOD.
       End method "mydouble".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *>OBJECT.
       *>END OBJECT.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END CLASS MyDouble.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Class-id.               MyFunction2.

       Class-Control.          MyFunction2 is class 'myfunk2'
                               MyDouble    is class 'mydouble'
                               .
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "MyFunction2".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       linkage section.
       01  a-number  pic  9(03).
       01  b-number  pic  9(03).

       procedure division using a-number returning b-number.
       *> Could invoke self to replicate the 'mydouble' method,
       *> invoke self "theDouble" using a-number returning b-number
       *> OR :-
       *> invoke directly the function class MyDouble

       invoke MyDouble "mydouble" using a-number returning b-number

       End method "MyFunction2".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *> Method-id. "theDouble".
       *> same code as for "MyDouble
       *> End Method "theDouble".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *>OBJECT.
       *>END OBJECT.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END CLASS MyFunction2.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Style - OO vs User-Defined Functions

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T11:59:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31s3mjF354rq9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com>`

```
Bill, I know yours is a considered post, but there are things here that
really don't make sense to me.

I'm not going to be drawn into a debate on this because it isn't right or
wrong, and minds are seldom changed in this forum anyway.

However, I am responding to your post and this will probably be the only
response from me.

See below.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:iELtd.4332339$yk.651716@news.easynews.com...
> We (CLC) are having SO much fun (???) talking about "style" issues, I
thought
> that I would start a new thread on another "style" issue.
>
> First, let me state that I *do* believe that what I am talking about in
this
> note is a CHOICE without a "right or wrong" answer, but it is also an
issue that
> MIGHT be worth thinking about (sooner than later).
>
Agree with that, but I don't see it as an "issue", and I don't think it
should be thought about in that context.

There is a place for functions and there is a place for OO. They are NOT
mutually exclusive and one is NOT a substitute for the other.

> The question is:
>     Given a FULLY CONFORMING 2002 COBOL compiler (a thing that doesn't
exist
> today - that I know of), then what would be the factors that one might
consider
> in deciding when a piece of new "procedural logic" should be created in OO
and
> when should it be a user-defined function?
>
First off, the existence of this compiler is hypothetical and there is no
evidence to suggest it will be available any time soon. I really wish the
2002 standard could be removed from discussions here because it is like "jam
tomorrow", and it is just plain silly to try and have a sensible discussion
around hypothetical ideals. By all means discuss features of it that ARE
implemented by SOMEBODY. The rest of it is just vapourware.

Second, the majority of people who read this forum will not be considering
an OO solution. If shops were going to seriously consider OO they would have
done it long ago. They didn't; COBOL missed the boat.

Even a dyed-in-the-wool OO user like myself is now considering the value of
using COBOL for it. I love COBOL but even with a free runtime it is
unattractive to end users. How anybody ever sells applications with a
chargeable runtime is beyond me. For mainframes the runtime is not so much a
consideration, but if you were to implement OO it would be nice to be able
to create and use components with it.. (I know IBM have been looking at this
but the Websphere/Java solution seems to be as far as it has gone. I know of
no mainframe support for COM or even CORBA. Please enlighten me if I am
wrong about this...)

> What I hope to do in this note is to state some of the things that *I*
think
> might be such factors.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Specifically, creating a "user-defined function" is EASY for anyone who
knows
> how to "code" a subprogram.  In fact, the only real difference between a
> subprogram's source code and a user-defined function is the use of
FUNCTION-ID
> paragraph and END FUNCTION marker versus PROGRAM-ID and END PROGRAM.
There may
> be *some* semantic implications (last-used state - for example) that need
to be
> considered and some "outside" things like function prototypes and
repositories,
> but BASICALLY the coding is the same.  In fact, many subprograms CAN be
made
> into a user-defined function (assuming they have the '02 Standard
"RETURNING"
> phrase) simply via a COPY REPLACING or REPLACE statement.
>
> On the other hand, creating an OO class requires LOTS of "new" syntax and
> understanding.  (Different and "more complex" source structure - or at
least
> "more complex" until you have used it - IMHO).
>
Yes, OO COBOL requires more than writing a user defined function.

But it is like saying that  Building a car is more complex than building a
soapbox derby racer. (We call them "trollies" in NZ and most kids here have
built one with assistance form Dad at some time in their lives.

Just because you have a car doesn't mean you can't enjoy a trolley, or the
fun of building one. Just because you have a trolley doesn't mean you don't
need a car.

UDFs and OO may look as if they serve a similar purpose but there is much
more to it.

If you decide to run the kids to school or do the shopping, the trolley is
not going to be much help. If you just want the fun of movement down an
incline without being surrounded by technology, the trolley is fine. Only a
true optimist would consider there was any hope of replacing the functions
of the car with a trolley. I believe the same is true for UDFs and OO. My
position is there is a place for both and they are not in any way mutually
exclusive, neither can one replace the other.

> 2) Clearly, the "design" issues of user-defined functions (which SHOULD be
a
> single procedural task) versus creating an OO class (with data, methods,
to
> encapsulate ALL of the "relevant" attributes/actions of an "object") is a
very
> different task.  To me, user-defined functions require no new "design
> methodology" while OO done withOUT new methodology is a "bad"idea - if not
> doomed from the start.
>
Yes, agreed.

> 3) Assuming (big assumption !!!) that someone has already created a Class
(or a
> class library) or a function (or function library), then EITHER seems (to
me) to
> be pretty easy to use.
>

Anything created by anybody needs to be easy to use for it to have any
chance of success.

>   OO
>     - INVOKE (out-of-line)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The way and ease with which these things are implemented is irrelevant to
the use of them. You use a function because you NEED a function. You use OO
because you NEED OO. Trying to emulate OO functionality with something like
a UDF, because it is easy to write and procedural programmers can understand
it, is just another excuse for not doing the hard yards and learning OO. If
people had bitten this particular bullet some years ago, there would be
fewer unemployed COBOL Programmers and they would not be seeing their jobs
disappearing. And the hard yards are not so hard. I taught myself Java in a
few weeks part time and in the process learned more about OO than I had in
months of trying to understand OO COBOL.

Your argument here, Bill, reminds me of Abe Lincoln who once posed the
question: "If you call a horse's tail a leg, how many legs has a horse?".

(the answer is four. Calling something a leg, does not make it one...)

Calling a UDF OO does not make it so.

> 4) What about interfacing with other languages:
>      Interestingly enough, there doesn't seem to be much "literature"
(that I am
> aware of) about using "user-defined functions" from non-COBOL libraries
....
> EVEN THOUGH functions (in such languages as C) have been around for years
> (decades).  I think - but won't swear to it that this is because this has
been
> available "transparently" to COBOL vendors with a RETURNING extension for
years.
> No one really thought about this as something needing to be "documented".
For
> example ...
>     Call "Some_C_Function"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>        Move Function "Some_C_Function" (ABC) to Other-Field  *> with
FUNCTION
> now optional word
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> libraries (including inheriting from them and overriding their methods)
from
> "new" OO COBOL.
>
> My guess (based solely on impression - no fact) is that *TODAY* there is
more
> NEW development of non-COBOL "class libraries" than there is of "general
purpose
> functions" (API's).  However, there are certainly PLENTY of "functions"
> available in many operating systems and environments.
>

This is a valid point and very true. We NEED general purpose functions. We
need them within many different contexts. That doesn't mean that OO
components are then irrelevant. (In fact some components will certainly
utilise UDFs. Just as they currently utilise intrinsic functions.)

> 5) If one has an "experienced" COBOL programmer who is NOT familiar with
coding
> EITHER OO or user-defined functions, then there are questions of:
>    - are they picking up one or the other "for the learning experience"?
>    - which is likely to be more portable to other COBOL compilers (with
PARTIAL
> implementations of the '02 Standard)
>    - is this a "one-off" or something that is the start of a new
> shop/application approach (and if so, which will meet the greater set of
> application needs)
>

Again implies that they are mutually exclusive. They aren't.
BOTH of them will meet application needs. If you only pick up UDFs, that's
fine. In your procedural COBOL shop it may be all you need. Don't then start
thinking you have all the advantages of OO design and implementation. A tail
is NOT a leg.

> 6) Are there issues (for this compiler and Operating System) concerning
"late
> binding" versus "early binding" (link-editing, whatever) and if so, are
there
> testing and "audit trail" implications for one or both of the approaches?
(i.e.
> if one is creating a "general purpose user-defined function" or new OO
Class,
> then how easy will it to be to "maintain" with adequate but not burdensome
> restrictions for other/all systems using the new "code").

If you are writing functions OR OO components that NEED maintenance, then
you really haven't understood what OO is all about. You are still thinking
procedural solutions that are dependent on source code. At this point I bow
out of the discussion because it is of no further interest to me.

>
> 7) COBOL does *not* have easy ways to define truly "arbitrary"
user-defined
> function input parameters (i.e issues with the ANYLENGTH, OPTIONAL, - not
to
> mention different classes, categories, and PICTUREs).  On the other hand,
OO can
> (with KNOWLEDGEABLE coders - IMHO) handle a variety of "inputs" to the
same (or
> similar) methods.  (Personally, I don't think there is any way in STANDARD
COBOL
> to create the ORD-MIN intrinsic function - for example - in COBOL.
Creating an
> ORD-MIN-OO "method" for DIFFERENT object types would, however, be if not
simple,
> possible - I think.)
>
>     ****
>

No comment.

> There are probably LOTS of other factors that one might consider for this
STYLE
> choice, but I thought others might want to think about (talk about) this.
>
> I guess that my SUGGESTION would be that (assuming that BOTH features were
> available *and* that you had equally skilled programmers available) then
...
>
>    If you have a SINGLE procedural logic "snippet" that you want to make
> "generally available" *and* it has well defined (and not variable) input,
then a
> user-defined function would be a good choice (and would have SOME
advantages
> over a subprogram)
>
>   If, on the other hand, you have a "series" of similar actions that you
want to
> perform on a specific (or set of similar) "objects" then creating an OO
Class
> would be a good choice.

The last two paragraphs are a complete over-simplification of the design
choices in using functions and OO. The only bit I can agree with
unreservedly is that an UDF is certainly preferable to a subprogram
(usually).

There is much more to this than has been covered here.

I believe it could help if people think about when they might use a current
intrinsic function in COBOL, rather than write their own. When they are
happy with that, it makes sense to start thinking about having the same
facility, but "bespoke" (customised to your requirements). That would be a
UDF. The consideration of when you would employ an OO Class is something
else again, and if you are working in a procedural COBOL shop, it is
unlikely to even be come up. If your shop is seriously going OO and you have
developed a few OO applications or even components, you will know when you
need a Class and when you need a function.

Pete.
```

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 7.6 version.
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in
every operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS
and EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/oldinvoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
Remember to read the Hachette Filipacchi Media U.S. project at
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/archives/v4/v40603.asp

What's new in this version
Solved problems with Simplified Chinese and Korean fonts.
Minor bug corrected.

Test txt2pdf 7.6!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a COBOL for Apple Mac?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T11:31:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FE1356.11315410122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9McVvq3PflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
In article <9McVvq3PflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
 l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

>    Just out of curiosity I would like to know if there is or ever was  
> a COBOL compiler and/or development system for Apple's Macintosh  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The GCCCobol and/or OpenCobol could be built for Mac OS X easily.  But 
last time I checked, they were both in early early early alpha stage.  
Once they are integrated with the standard GCC tree, there will be a 
Cobol for mac.

TinyCobol builds and runs on OS X the last time I tried, but it only has 
an Intel back-end.

Kobol, from the Kompany, keeps promising a Mac OS X version 'soon' on 
it's web page, but it has been two years in vaporware state.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a COBOL for Apple Mac?

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@nobloodyspam.nospam.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-10T23:56:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n9vud.125$8%6.50@fe51.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9McVvq3PflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-FE1356.11315410122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

 > ...
 > The GCCCobol and/or OpenCobol could be built for Mac OS X easily.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
 > be a COBOL for Mac.

By GCCCobol, I think you are referring to GNU COBOL, which used a an old
version (0.12 I think) of the OpenCOBOL LEX/YACC front end and run-time.

AFAIK, no further development is planned for GNU COBOL.

OpenCOBOL (1) generates C code, so in theory it is platform independent.

It does require the helper libraries, ncurses, GMP and BDB. But these
should be available for the Mac OS X.

It does pass the NIST tests for COBOL 85 compatibility, so it is fairly
functional.

So OpenCOBOL should be able to compile COBOL programs on Mac OS X.


 > TinyCOBOL builds and runs on OS X the last time I tried, but it
 > only has an Intel back-end.

TinyCOBOL generates GNU assembler (ATT style), then uses GCC to create
an executable.



1) OpenCOBOL
hppt://open-cobol.sf.net/
```

On 10/12/2004 2:35 AM, Lueko Willms wrote:
>    Just out of curiosity I would like to know if there is or ever was  
> a COBOL compiler and/or development system for Apple's Macintosh  
> computer.

<a id="t-a6f0b260b0"></a>
## Thread: SCREEN SECTION processing in RM/COBOL — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### SCREEN SECTION processing in RM/COBOL

- **From:** Buck Black <Buck.Black@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-11T17:14:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com>`

```
I am currently involved in a project to convert a suite of RM/COBOL
programs to a SCREEN SECTION based format. Currently all screens, and
screen handling routines, are implemented entirely within the
procedure division.  

I would like to be able to assign attributes to screen section items
dynamically (colors, for the most part) and to be able to determine
which field in the screen has control on accepts and displays.

Any ideas on how to do this in RM/COBOL?

I would be extremely grateful for any suggestions. 


TIA
```

```

wrote:

>I am currently involved in a project to convert a suite of RM/COBOL
>programs to a SCREEN SECTION based format. Currently all screens, and
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

First suggestion is DON'T DO IT.

If you need to convert the programs then convert them to something
else like SP2 from www.flexus.com or eventually COBOL-WOW from
Liant(RM) www.liant.com.

Apart from the above it would help if you told us what you expect to
achive with this conversion to Screen Section, and also whether you
are staying with the RM/COBOL compiler or if you are swapping to other
vendor.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com

<real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:

>On Sat, 11 Dec 2004 17:14:31 GMT, Buck Black <Buck.Black@virgin.net>
>wrote:

An example of the kind of functionality we are looking to implement is
to redisplay a screen when a value entered via that screen fails a
validation check; to return control *directly to the field containing
the errant value* (possibly changing the color attributes to white
text/red background, for example).

We would also like to implement a moving bar menu *without accepting*
the screen at any point, prior to selecting a menu option thru an
exception key press.

I know its possible to do any of this very easily using Acucobol
products.

Unfortunately, this is not an option. We're stuck with the RM system.

Thank's for your suggestions.
```

If you use either of the products I mentioned you can do this.

I personally prefer SP2 from Flexus, but COBOL-WOW is also good.
Preference has to do with the way you code with them.

With SP2 you can have your programs working in either character mode
or GUI mode, and both products allow you to use a web/think client
mode.

You can download SP2 from www.flexus.com (assuming you have a windows
runtime), and you can ask your COBOL representative for an evaluation
of COBOL-WOW.

>
>Thank's for your suggestions.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION processing in RM/COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T19:42:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1b1vd.473739$Pl.377269@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<thfmr0h7qgmn51o1mfn23fuu1ss0l8ungt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com> <t9dmr0p5ses3kr2dp5h5p50de16hasmtss@4ax.com> <thfmr0h7qgmn51o1mfn23fuu1ss0l8ungt@4ax.com>`

```
Buck Black wrote:

>On Sat, 11 Dec 2004 18:01:05 +0000, Frederico Fonseca
><real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK so you are staying with RM/COBOL.  I just double-checked the Liant 
site and see that when you refer to Screen-Section you are talking 
something very close  to the current 2002 Standard. From the RM examples 
I see that you can test for function and Escape key etc.

Background - I used a very *old* version of RM both on Radio Shack and 
PCs, (now no longer supported by Liant). My statements were just 
ACCEPTS, DISPLAYS with White on Black. Screen colouring was achieved by 
screen painting tools, the latest being Flashup - but that just 
'painted' a screen image - your data still showed as White on Black..

How far do you want to go with this - just 'tart' up your screens to 
highlight errors, etc., or are you after further flexibility. 
Suggestions already made - use WOW or SP2. Plus - *even more 
importantly* - from Pete - segregate you Screens into separate CALLs to 
ancillary programs.. Back when I used it my Procedures were an 
inter-mingled mess of Business Logic and screen instructions. I've 
learned better since :-) .

Depends, as I said above, how far you want to go. If you just want to 
'add a bit of colour' - and you will have to check with Tom Morrison of 
Liant  - my old compiler had a colour routine, which I never used :-

O/S : MSDOS 2.n,  RM/COBOL V 2.0  and the Doc # CT032, which is about 
routines written in Assembler. Example COBOL code :-

CALL 'COLORCNG.COM' USING FOREGROUND-COLOR, BACKGROUND-COLOR.
CALL 'BORDERCNG.COM' USING BORDER-COLOR

If still available and compatible with your latest RM compiler, (and O/S 
of course) - that might do the trick as a 'quickie'. Failing that, then 
I would suggest you most certainly are looking at new code to 
accommodate either WOW or SP2. Whichever route you go - consider hiving 
off your screen routines.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Either of these two suggestions would do the job.

Another would be to abandon the 'screen' and embrace the 'browser' (and/or 
web services) and in the process remove the user interface entirely from 
your COBOL legacy applications.  You may accomplish this by using the 
RM/COBOL based application server:

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
