#### ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION processing in RM/COBOL

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-11T10:12:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102788744.131307.311140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com>`

```
> I would like to be able to assign attributes to screen section items
> dynamically (colors, for the most part)

I am not sure that one can with SCREEN SECTION.  I always did
field-by-field processing just using ACCEPT/DISPLAY AT and changed
colour by routines at appropriate places.  Typically I would have:

IF ( Field-Number = 4 )
PERFORM Screen-In
DISPLAY field4     AT field4pos
ACCEPT field4     AT field4pos
PERFORM Screen-FG
DISPLAY field4    AT field4pos
if exit-key move 99 to field-number
else if back-key subtract1 from field-number
else
check field4 ......
if ok add 1 to field-number
end-if end-if
END-IF

This was suitable for being code generated from a text layout file.
The Screen-In, Screen-FG, Screen-BG, etc would change the colour being
used by a DISPLAY or ACCEPT to whatever the user had chosen for his
settings for that system.  It is still very useful for mult-company
sites because the users set up different colours for each company.

This is implemented different ways depending on the version of Cobol,
terminal types etc, and goes back to when I was using serial terminals
on Concurrent-DOS (where the 'colours' may be bold, half-bright,
inverted etc) so the termiinal type still includes ANSI, VT52. I think
it uses CALL x"A7" for IBM screens with MF, for others it DISPLAYs a
control string.

> and to be able to determine
> which field in the screen has control on accepts and displays.

If you are accepting several fields at once then with MF you could use
CURSOR IS Cursor-Position in SPECIAL-NAMES and this can be set to the
cursor position required at the start of an ACCEPT and it reports where
the cursor was at the end of the ACCEPT.  It may be available with RM.

This doesn't prevent other fields being entered, but it does put the
cursor where required.
```

#### ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION processing in RM/COBOL (LONG with sample code)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T16:00:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<321qk8F3ha1gkU2@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com>`

```
"Buck Black" <Buck.Black@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:qmamr01v2abddsm38vj40dgc10s1ra46n2@4ax.com...
> I am currently involved in a project to convert a suite of RM/COBOL
> programs to a SCREEN SECTION based format. Currently all screens, and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You have had some good suggestions from Richard and Frederico.

Your post made me think back to the days under DOS and Win 3.1 when I used
Screen sections 15 years ago.

I've decided to make this code public domain because I believe anyone still
using Screen Sections needs all the help they can get <G>.

FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLES:

1. Remove screen processing from the Business logic. (Multi-tier design).
This means that application code should simply see a buffer containing the
variables from the screen and it should manipulate those then put them back
(code provided below does this)

2. GENERATE the screen formats by "painting" them. I have a tool I wrote
called ACEPAINT. You draw the fields you want, indicating what is protected
and what is variable, and the colours you want. ACEPAINT scans the screen
and generates definitions as follows:

1. MFS type 1 buffer for IBM mainframe.
2. CICS Map for IBM mainframe.
3. Screen Section for the PC.

These principles then allow the same screen to run on both mainframe and PC.
It is also a fairly simple matter to add other hardware support.

DETAILS (this is long, but it may be of use to somebody...It is taken from a
document I sent to someone in 1994, who had requested very much the same
thing you have, but for a HP environment):

Hopefully, the following will be of some help...please excuse the "old"
COBOL; I wrote this stuff 7 years ago and blush when I look at it now.
Nevertheless, it is fully debugged and working and does the job.

The scenario is as follows:

1. For each screen that the system can display, we will create an
encapsulated module consisting of a screen section which defines the screen,
a linkage section which allows us to pass a corresponding message, and a
procedure division which processes either input or output and calls a module
to deal with dynamic attributes (in the PC case, only cursor placement. MF
ADIS cannot handle true dynamic attributes). These screen handling modules
will be identically coded; the only difference is the screen and linkage
sections.
A copy of the skeleton code I use is included below. Although I have a
generator which I wrote myself (ACEPAINT) to do this for me, it is a simple
matter to do it manually. (If you are likely to be doing  a lot of this,
I'll rebuild ACEPAINT and you can have it for $50)

We'll start with the Calling program's view. There's a message buffer with
all the variable fields used in the Screen Section of the Screen Handling
module for the screen we are concerned with. Each variable field in the
buffer  is preceded by a 2 byte field which may contain a dynamic attribute.
These are "control bytes" which control the way things are displayed on a
screen. The ones described below pertain to the mainframe IBM 3270 and are
accessed by a system called "MFS" (message format services).  As I like my
code to run on both PCs and mainframes, I use these attributes. You may have
different ones for HP.
MicroFocus COBOL only allows cursor insertion as it cannot dynamically
modify attributes. I have organised things so that the same attribute which
inserts
the cursor on the mainframe, also does it on the PC...
(Please select the sample code and apply font courier 10 to it...)
    61*
ACE00061
    62********************  DYNAMIC  ATTRIBUTES  ********************
ACE00062
    63*
ACE00063
    64*
ACE00064
    65*   MFS ATTRIBUTES CAN BE DYNAMICALLY MODIFIED AS FOLLOWS:
ACE00065
    66*
ACE00066
    67*   1. MOVE 128 TO 'DYN-ATT-WORK'.
ACE00067
    68*
ACE00068
    69*   2. ADD THE VALUE(S) FROM THE TABLE BELOW WHICH CORRESPOND(S)
ACE00069
    70*      TO THE ATTRIBUTE(S) YOU REQUIRE INTO 'DYN-ATT-WORK'.
ACE00070
    71*
ACE00071
    72*   3. MOVE 'DYNAMIC-ATTRIBUTE' TO THE TWO BYTE ATTRIBUTE FIELD
ACE00072
    73*      RESERVED IN THE MFS MESSAGE DEFINITION AT THE START OF
ACE00073
    74*      THE FIELD BEING MODIFIED.
ACE00074
    75*
ACE00075
    76*        ***  TABLE OF DYNAMIC ATTRIBUTE VALUES  ***
ACE00076
    77*
ACE00077
    78*                 ATTRIBUTE                         VALUE
ACE00078
    79*                 =========                         =====
ACE00079
    80*
ACE00080
    81*       FORCED RETURN (MDT PRESET)                    1
ACE00081
    82*
ACE00082
    83*       LIGHT PEN DETECTABLE                          2
ACE00083
    84*
ACE00084
    85*       NON DISPLAYABLE (INVISIBLE)                   4
ACE00085
    86*
ACE00086
    87*       HIGH BRIGHTNESS                               8
ACE00087
    88*
ACE00088
    89*       NUMERIC FIELD (0 THRU 9 , . - +)             16
ACE00089
    90*
ACE00090
    91*       PROTECTED                                    32
ACE00091
    92*
ACE00092
    93*       AUTO-SKIP (PROTECTED NUMERIC)                48
ACE00093
    94*
ACE00094
    95*       REPLACE THE DFLD ATTRIBUTE                   64
ACE00095
    96*       (IF NOT SET, THE VALUES SELECTED WILL BE
ACE00096
    97*       LOGICALLY 'OR'ED WITH THE EXISTING ATTRIBUTE
ACE00097
    98*       VALUES DEFINED IN THE DIF/DOF DFLD. FOR EXAMPLE,
ACE00098
    99*       IF THE DFLD SPECIFIES 'NUMERIC' AND THE VALUE OF
ACE00099
   100*       8 IS ADDED TO DYN-ATT-WORK, THEN THE FIELD WILL
ACE00100
   101*       BE NUMERIC HIGH-BRIGHTNESS. IF THE VALUES OF 64
ACE00101
   102*       AND 8 ARE ADDED TO DYN-ATT-WORK, THEN THE FIELD
ACE00102
   103*       IS ALPHA (DFLD OVER-RIDDEN) HIGH-BRIGHTNESS.)
ACE00103
   104*
ACE00104
   105*       INSERT CURSOR (PLACE CURSOR ON THIS FIELD)   49152
ACE00105
   106*
ACE00106
   107 01  DYNAMIC-ATTRIBUTE-VALUES USAGE COMP.
ACE00107
   108     12 DA-FORCED-RETURN      PIC 9(8)          VALUE 1.
ACE00108
   109     12 DA-PEN-DETECT         PIC 9(8)          VALUE 2.
ACE00109
   110     12 DA-NO-DISPLAY         PIC 9(8)          VALUE 4.
ACE00110



* Micro Focus COBOL Version 3.1.35   L2.4 revision 004 14-Sep-94 00:29 Page
3
*                                        ACEPNT.COB
   111     12 DA-HI                 PIC 9(8)          VALUE 8.
ACE00111
   112     12 DA-NUM                PIC 9(8)          VALUE 16.
ACE00112
   113     12 DA-PROTECT            PIC 9(8)          VALUE 32.
ACE00113
   114     12 DA-AUTO-SKIP          PIC 9(8)          VALUE 48.
ACE00114
   115     12 DA-REPLACE            PIC 9(8)          VALUE 64.
ACE00115
   116     12 DA-INSERT-CURSOR      PIC 9(8)          VALUE 49152.
ACE00116
   117 01  DYNAMIC-ATTRIBUTE-WORK-FIELDS.
ACE00117
   118     12 DYN-ATT-WORK          PIC 9(8) COMP VALUE 128.
ACE00118
   119     12 FILLER REDEFINES DYN-ATT-WORK.
ACE00119
   120        15 FILLER             PIC XX.
ACE00120
   121        15 DYNAMIC-ATTRIBUTE  PIC XX.
ACE00121
   122*
ACE00122
   123*   EXAMPLE OF USE:
ACE00123
   124*
ACE00124
   125*   THE FOLLOWING CODE CHANGES AN ATTRIBUTE DEFINED AS
ACE00125
   126*   NORMAL ALPHANUMERIC, TO HIGH-BRIGHTNESS NUMERIC AND PLACES
ACE00126
   127*   THE CURSOR AT THE BEGINNING OF THE FIELD.
ACE00127
   128*
ACE00128
   129*    COMPUTE DYN-ATT-WORK = 128
ACE00129
   130*                           + DA-REPLACE
ACE00130
   131*                           + DA-HI
ACE00131
   132*                           + DA-NUM
ACE00132
   133*                           + DA-INSERT-CURSOR.
ACE00133
   134*
ACE00134
   135*    MOVE DYNAMIC-ATTRIBUTE TO FIELD-ATTRIBUTE.
ACE00135
   136*
ACE00136
   137*
ACE00137
 Note that although the above code is fine for mainframes, the only
attribute that can work on the PC is DA-INSERT-CURSOR.

The screen handler will call a module to check these attribute bytes and
ensure that the cursor is placed correctly on the PC screen

Heres the source code for the module which places the cursor (This is called
from the screen handler (see skeleton code below)) ....

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                 DYNATR.
       INSTALLATION.               PRIMA COMPUTING.
       AUTHOR.                     PETER E. C. DASHWOOD.
       DATE-WRITTEN.               NOVEMBER 90.
      *    *******************************************************
      *
      *    MODULE TO CHECK THE ATTRIBUTE BYTES IN AN MFS FORMAT 1
      *    MESSAGE (AS USED BY ACE/PC) AND DETERMINE WHERE THE
      *    CURSOR IS TO BE INSERTED BEFORE THE SCREEN WRITE.
      *    THIS IS THE ONLY DYNAMIC ATTRIBUTE WHICH CAN BE SUPPORTED
      *    FOR ACE/PC, AS IT IS NOT CURRENTLY POSSIBLE TO CHANGE
      *    ATTRIBUTES ON DEFINED SCREENS USING ADIS.
      *
      *    INTERFACE TO THIS MODULE IS AS FOLLOWS:
      *
      *    NOTE: NO RETURN CODE. ERROR IS NOT POSSIBLE- WE EITHER
      *          FIND AN ATTRIBUTE OR WE DONT.....
      *
      *
      *    1. THE SYMBOLIC CURSOR FIELD (LINECOL)
      *
      *    ON EXIT, THIS FIELD WILL CONTAIN THE LINE AND COLUMN ON
      *    THE SCREEN WHERE THE CURSOR IS TO BE INSERTED. THIS WILL
      *    ALWAYS BE THE START OF A VARIABLE FIELD.
      *    IF NO DYNAMIC CURSOR INSERTION ATTRIBUTE IS FOUND THIS
      *    FIELD WILL BE SET TO ZERO WHICH CAUSES THE CURSOR TO BE
      *    INSERTED IN THE FIRST UNPROTECTED FIELD ON THE SCREEN.
      *
      *
      *    2. THE TABLE OF VARIABLE FIELDS GENERATED BY ACEPAINT AND
      *       INCLUDED IN THE SCREEN HANDLING MODULE BY GENSCR.
      *
      *    THIS TABLE HAS THE LINE AND COLUMN OF EACH VARIABLE ON THE
      *    SCREEN AND THE OFFSET OF THE ATTRIBUTE BYTE WHICH CORRESPONDS
      *    TO THIS FIELD IN THE MESSAGE BUFFER. IN THIS WAY ATTRIBUTES
      *    IN THE MESSAGE BUFFER CAN BE CHECKED.
      *
      *    3. THE MESSAGE BUFFER, AS GENERATED BY ACEPAINT AND
      *       CONTAINING A 2 BYTE ATTRIBUTE PRECEDING EACH FIELD.
      *       (SAME AS IBM'S MFS FORMAT 1)
      *
      *
      *    THE CODE TO CALL THIS MODULE IS INCLUDED IN THE FORMAT.SKE
      *    MEMBER IN \ACEPCSRC\COBLIB. THE MODULE IS INVOKED
      *    IMMEDIATELY BEFORE WRITING THE SCREEN.
      *
      *    *******************************************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
      *    DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  OFFSET-ZERO            PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  LEN                    PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 2.
       01  ATTR-WORK              PIC XX.
       01  BIG-BIN.
           12 FILLER              PIC XX VALUE LOW-VALUES.
           12 ATTR-BIN            PIC XX.
       01  CUR-BIN REDEFINES BIG-BIN PIC S9(7) COMP.
       01  OFFSET-WORK            PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  FINISH-FLAG            PIC X VALUE ZERO.
           88 FINISHED            VALUE '1'.



       01  DUMMY    PIC X.

       LINKAGE SECTION.

       01  LINECOL.
           12 CUR-LIN             PIC 999.
           12 CUR-COL             PIC 999.

       01  VAR-FIELDS.
           12 VAR-FLD-ENTRIES OCCURS 450
                              INDEXED BY  X1.
              15  VF-CUR-LIN      PIC 999.
              15  VF-CUR-COL      PIC 999.
              15  VF-OFFSET       PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  MESSAGE-BUFFER-FULL.
           12 FILLER              PIC X(6).
           12 MESSAGE-BUFFER      PIC X(1920).
      **********************************************************
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
                          LINECOL
                          VAR-FIELDS
                          MESSAGE-BUFFER-FULL.
       MAIN-LINE SECTION.
       MAIN-001.
           MOVE ZERO TO LINECOL
                        FINISH-FLAG.
      *
      *    SCAN THE VAR FIELDS AND CHECK THE ATTRIBUTE FOR EACH
      *    VARIABLE FIELD IN THE MESSAGE BUFFER.
      *
      *
      *
           PERFORM SCAN-VAR-FIELDS
              VARYING X1
                 FROM 1
                   BY 1
                UNTIL
                    FINISHED
                       OR
                    VF-CUR-LIN (X1) = 999.
       RETURN-TO-CALLER.
           EXIT PROGRAM.
      ************************  SUBROUTINES ****************************
       SCAN-VAR-FIELDS SECTION.
       SVF.
      *   GET THE OFFSET INTO THE MESSAGE BUFFER FROM THE VAR TABLE
           MOVE VF-OFFSET (X1) TO OFFSET-WORK.
      *   MOVE THE ATTRIBUTE FROM THE MESSAGE BUFFER TO A WORK AREA.
           CALL 'AVMOVE' USING
                                MESSAGE-BUFFER
                                OFFSET-WORK
                                ATTR-WORK
                                OFFSET-ZERO
                                LEN.
      *   CHECK THE ATTRIBUTE FOR 'INSERT CURSOR'.
      *   TO SAVE TIME WE CAN EXIT WHEN IT'S FOUND AS THE CURSOR
      *   CAN ONLY BE ON 1 FIELD. FOR FULL MFS COMPATIBILITY WE
      *   SHOULD REALLY SCAN ALL ATTRIBUTES AND USE THE LAST
      *   'INSERT CURSOR' WHICH IS FOUND. IF THIS IS REQUIRED
      *   REMOVE THE LINE WHICH SETS THE FINISH FLAG.
           IF ATTR-WORK NOT = SPACE
              MOVE ATTR-WORK TO ATTR-BIN
              IF CUR-BIN NOT < 49152
                 MOVE  VF-CUR-LIN (X1)   TO CUR-LIN
                 MOVE  VF-CUR-COL (X1)   TO CUR-COL
                 SET FINISHED            TO TRUE
      *   RESET THE ATTRIBUTE IN THE MESSAGE BUFFER...
                 MOVE SPACE TO ATTR-WORK
                 CALL 'AVMOVE' USING
                                      ATTR-WORK
                                      OFFSET-ZERO
                                      MESSAGE-BUFFER
                                      OFFSET-WORK
                                      LEN.

Note that the above code uses a table of offsets to the variable fields.This
is produced automatically by my generator (ACEPAINT). It would be more
 tedious to have to do it manually, but it is the only way to get cursor
placement correct in the MF environment. (MicroFocus requires the line
and column to place the cursor, so it is therefore necessary to equate each
variable field with a line/column).

The AVMOVE routine can be replaced by reference modification. It is an
Assembler routine I coded for both the mainframe and the PC, which
allows a totally flexible COBOL MOVE facility. It has been superceded by the
arrival of Reference Modification.
AVMOVE has the following parameters:

1. The source field
2. The offset into the source field (starting at zero)
3. the target field
4. The offset into the target field (starting at zero)
5. the length

Now let's have a look at the screen handling module skeleton:

There will be one of these for each screen you intend to display. Each
module will have a corresponding message buffer to allow input/output of the
fields
defined in the screen section as variable. In my system each of these fields
is preceded by a 2 byte attribute field to support cursor insertion as
you saw above, but if you are happy to always insert the cursor on the first
variable field on the screen (MF default), you could eliminate this. (What
about
when errors are detected and it is necessary to place the cursor on the
offending field?)

The skeleton code for the screen handling module is as follows:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.     $.
      *
      *    ACE/PC SCREEN HANDLING MODULE FOR FORMAT: $
      *
       AUTHOR.           PETER E. C. DASHWOOD.
       INSTALLATION.   PRIMA COMPUTING LTD.
       DATE-WRITTEN.   MARCH 1990.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
         CONSOLE IS CRT
         CURSOR IS LINECOL
         CRT STATUS IS AID-BYTES.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  LINECOL               PIC 9(6).
       01  GENERAL-FIELDS.
         12 AID-BYTES      PIC 999.
         12 FILLER REDEFINES AID-BYTES.
            15 AB-1              PIC 9.
            15 AB-2              PIC X.
            15 AB-3              PIC X.
         12 BIN-WORK.
            15 FILLER      PIC X VALUE LOW-VALUES.
            15 AB-2-LOD    PIC X.
         12 AB-2-COM REDEFINES BIN-WORK  PIC S999 COMP.
         12 AB-DISP              PIC 99.
         12 AB-DISP-X REDEFINES AB-DISP PIC XX.
         12 SDATE          PIC X(8).
         12 STIME          PIC X(8).
         12 SLTERM               PIC X(8) VALUE 'LTERM001'.
         12 EDIT-MASK     PIC XXBXXBXX.
         12 FILLER REDEFINES EDIT-MASK.
            15 FILLER     PIC XX.
            15 MASK-1     PIC X.
            15 FILLER     PIC XX.
            15 MASK-2     PIC X.
            15 FILLER     PIC XX.
         12 EDIT-FLD REDEFINES EDIT-MASK PIC X(8).

       01  MESSAGE-LENGTH        PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  INOUT-MESSAGE.
      *
      *   THIS IS THE MESSAGE DEFINITION WHICH WILL BE WRITTEN TO
      *   THE FORMAT DEFINED IN THE SCREEN SECTION AND DISPLAYED.
      *
      *   THE SAME FIELDS ARE UPDATED WHEN THE FORMAT IS ACCEPTED,
      *   AND THEN PASSED BACK TO THE USER.
      *
         12 INOUT-MESS-LL       PIC S9(4) COMP.
         12 INOUT-MESS-ZZ       PIC S9(4) COMP.
         12 INOUT-MESS-AID      PIC XX.
      *
      *   COPY THE MESSAGE DEFINITION HERE
      *   (FILE $.MSG)
      *
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
%MSG.TMP
      *
      *
       01  VARIABLE-FIELDS.
      *
      *   COPY THE VARIABLE FIELD IDENTIFIER TABLE (DYNAMIC ATTRIBUTES)
      *   (FILE $.ATR)
      *
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
%VAR.TMP
      *
      *
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  LINK-MESS.
         12 FILLER               PIC X(1926).


       SCREEN SECTION.

      *
      *   MS/COBOL SCREEN DEFINITION FOR MFS FORMAT: $
      *
      *    BLACK   =  0
      *    BLUE    =  1
      *    GREEN   =  2
      *    CYAN    =  3
      *    RED       =  4
      *    MAGENTA =  5
      *    BROWN   =  6
      *    WHITE   =  7
      *
      *
      *
       01  $-SCREEN.
      *
      *   COPY THE SCREEN FORMAT FOR $ HERE.
      *   (FILE $.FMT)
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
%FMT.TMP
      *

       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
                    LINK-MESS.
       MAIN SECTION.
       MAINLINE.
         CALL 'ACEDSCR' USING SDATE.
         ACCEPT EDIT-MASK FROM TIME.
         MOVE ':' TO MASK-1 MASK-2.
         MOVE EDIT-FLD TO STIME.
         MOVE LINK-MESS TO INOUT-MESSAGE.
         MOVE ZERO TO LINECOL.
         CALL 'DYNATR'
                   USING
                   LINECOL
                   VARIABLE-FIELDS
                   INOUT-MESSAGE.
         DISPLAY $-SCREEN.
         ACCEPT  $-SCREEN.
         PERFORM HANDLE-AID.
      *
      ********** LOWER CASE TO UPPER CASE CONVERSION *******************
      *
      *   THE COBOL SUBROUTINE 'LCTUC' WILL CONVERT A BUFFER OF ASCII
      *   CHARACTERS FROM LOWER TO UPPER CASE. (BUFFER LENGTH UP TO
      *   64000 CHARACTERS, LENGTH PASSED AS 2 BYTE BINARY FIELD)
      *
      *   IF YOU WISH TO HAVE LOWER CASE INPUT PASSED TO YOUR PROGRAM
      *   AS LOWER CASE, THEN COMMENT OUT THE FOLLOWING CALL TO LCTUC.
      *   INPUT WILL BE PASSED TO YOUR APPLICATION AS LOWER CASE. MOST
      *   MAINFRAME SYSTEMS CANNOT HANDLE LOWER CASE AND IT IS TEDIOUS
      *   TO HAVE TO CHECK EACH RESPONSE IN A PROGRAM FOR UPPER OR LOWER
      *   CASE (AND DOING SO MAY RENDER YOUR COBOL UN-TRANSPORTABLE)).
      *   THE CALL TO LCTUC ENSURES THAT YOUR APPLICATION ONLY HAS TO
      *   DEAL WITH UPPER CASE USER INPUT.
      *
         MOVE INOUT-MESS-LL TO MESSAGE-LENGTH.
         IF MESSAGE-LENGTH > 1920
            MOVE 1920 TO MESSAGE-LENGTH.
         CALL 'LCTUC'
            USING
                 INOUT-MESSAGE
                 MESSAGE-LENGTH.
      * IF THE MESSAGE LEN IS BETWEEN 97 AND 122 LCTUC WILL CHANGE IT...
      * SO IT MUST BE RESTORED FOR THE USER.
      *
         MOVE MESSAGE-LENGTH TO INOUT-MESS-LL.
      *
      *
         MOVE INOUT-MESSAGE TO LINK-MESS.
       RETURN-TO-CALLER.
         EXIT PROGRAM.

      /
       HANDLE-AID SECTION.
       HA.
      *
      *   THIS SECTION ANALYSES THE TERMINATE (ATTN) KEY WHICH WAS
      *   PRESSED AND SETS THE AID (ATTENTION IDENTIFIER) IN THE
      *   MESSAGE FOR THE USER.
      *
         IF AB-1 = ZERO
      *   "ENTER" KEY OR LAST ON-SCREEN AUTO-SKIP...
            MOVE SPACES TO INOUT-MESS-AID
            GO TO HA-OUT.
       HA-PF-KEYS.
         MOVE AB-2 TO AB-2-LOD.
         IF AB-2-COM > 40
            MOVE '99' TO INOUT-MESS-AID
            GO TO HA-OUT.
       HA-MOD-AID-BYTE.
         IF AB-2-COM > 12
            SUBTRACT 10 FROM AB-2-COM
            GO TO HA-MOD-AID-BYTE.
         MOVE AB-2-COM TO AB-DISP.
         MOVE AB-DISP-X TO INOUT-MESS-AID.
       HA-OUT.
         EXIT.


As you can see there are 3 "tags" in the above program which tell the
generator where to insert the message buffer (%MSG.TMP), the table which
equates variable
fields to a LINE/COL for MF COBOL (%VAR.TMP), and the physical screen
structure defined in the screen section (%FMT.TMP). The AID (Attention
Identifier)allows us to handle PFKeys (Program Function Keys). For example
if the user presses PFK1, the AID byte returned in the message header will
contain a 1.
Again, most of this has been coded for compatibility with IBM Mainframes. It
is important to remember that even if we are running solely on a PC, these
functions
are still a necessary part of the processing, and the above code has been
tried and tested for many years in a production environment without failing.

Here's an example of the skeleton code after the generator has finished with
it...

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.     NADIA002.
      *
      *    ACE/PC SCREEN HANDLING MODULE FOR FORMAT: NADIA002
      *
       AUTHOR.         PETER E. C. DASHWOOD.
       INSTALLATION.   PRIMA COMPUTING LTD.
       DATE-WRITTEN.   MARCH 1990.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CONSOLE IS CRT
           CURSOR IS LINECOL
           CRT STATUS IS AID-BYTES.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  LINECOL                 PIC 9(6).
       01  GENERAL-FIELDS.
           12 AID-BYTES            PIC 999.
           12 FILLER REDEFINES AID-BYTES.
              15 AB-1              PIC 9.
              15 AB-2              PIC X.
              15 AB-3              PIC X.
           12 BIN-WORK.
              15 FILLER            PIC X VALUE LOW-VALUES.
              15 AB-2-LOD          PIC X.
           12 AB-2-COM REDEFINES BIN-WORK  PIC S999 COMP.
           12 AB-DISP              PIC 99.
           12 AB-DISP-X REDEFINES AB-DISP PIC XX.
           12 SDATE                PIC X(8).
           12 STIME                PIC X(8).
           12 SLTERM               PIC X(8) VALUE 'LTERM001'.
           12 EDIT-MASK           PIC XXBXXBXX.
           12 FILLER REDEFINES EDIT-MASK.
              15 FILLER           PIC XX.
              15 MASK-1           PIC X.
              15 FILLER           PIC XX.
              15 MASK-2           PIC X.
              15 FILLER           PIC XX.
           12 EDIT-FLD REDEFINES EDIT-MASK PIC X(8).

       01  MESSAGE-LENGTH          PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  INOUT-MESSAGE.
      *
      *   THIS IS THE MESSAGE DEFINITION WHICH WILL BE WRITTEN TO
      *   THE FORMAT DEFINED IN THE SCREEN SECTION AND DISPLAYED.
      *
      *   THE SAME FIELDS ARE UPDATED WHEN THE FORMAT IS ACCEPTED,
      *   AND THEN PASSED BACK TO THE USER.
      *
           12 INOUT-MESS-LL       PIC S9(4) COMP.
           12 INOUT-MESS-ZZ       PIC S9(4) COMP.
           12 INOUT-MESS-AID      PIC XX.
      *
      *   COPY THE MESSAGE DEFINITION HERE
      *   (FILE NADIA002.MSG)
      *
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
           12  FLDAT001V            PICTURE XX.
           12  FLD001              PICTURE X(0004).
           12  FLDAT002V            PICTURE XX.
           12  FLD002              PICTURE X(0001).
           12  FLDAT003V            PICTURE XX.
           12  FLD003              PICTURE X(0030).
           12  FLDAT004V            PICTURE XX.
           12  FLD004              PICTURE 9(0004).
           12  FLDAT005V            PICTURE XX.
           12  FLD005              PICTURE X(0079).
*     *   TOTAL LINES GENERATED FOR COBOL   = 0010
      *
      *
       01  VARIABLE-FIELDS.
      *
      *   COPY THE VARIABLE FIELD IDENTIFIER TABLE (DYNAMIC ATTRIBUTES)
      *   (FILE NADIA002.ATR)
      *
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 001.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 014.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0000.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 006.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 011.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0006.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 017.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 011.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0009.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 019.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 011.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0041.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 024.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 002.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0047.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 999.
           12 FILLER  PIC 999  VALUE 999.
           12 FILLER  PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0005.
      *
      *
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  LINK-MESS.
           12 FILLER               PIC X(1926).


       SCREEN SECTION.

      *
      *   MS/COBOL SCREEN DEFINITION FOR MFS FORMAT: NADIA002
      *
      *    BLACK   =  0
      *    BLUE    =  1
      *    GREEN   =  2
      *    CYAN    =  3
      *    RED     =  4
      *    MAGENTA =  5
      *    BROWN   =  6
      *    WHITE   =  7
      *
      *
      *
       01  NADIA002-SCREEN.
      *
      *   COPY THE SCREEN FORMAT FOR NADIA002 HERE.
      *   (FILE NADIA002.FMT)
      *   IF YOU ARE EDITING MANUALLY, REMOVE THE LINE WITH % IN COL 1
           12 BLANK SCREEN BACKGROUND-COLOUR 00 FOREGROUND-COLOUR 02.
           12 LINE 02 COLUMN 04
           PIC X(0008)  FROM   SLTERM.
           12 LINE 01 COLUMN 71
           PIC X(0008)  FROM   SDATE .
           12 LINE 02 COLUMN 71
           PIC X(0008)  FROM   STIME .
           12 LINE 01 COLUMN 04  VALUE
           'SCR=A002 '
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 01 COLUMN 14
           PIC X(0004)  USING  FLD001
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 01 COLUMN 23  VALUE
           '***  PRIMA COMPUTING   (NADIA)  ***'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 03 COLUMN 23  VALUE
           'NAME AND ADDRESS INFORMATION ACCESS'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 04 COLUMN 23  VALUE
           '_(VIEW/CHANGE/DELETE NAME/ADDRESS)_'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 06 COLUMN 03  VALUE
           'ACTION:'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 06 COLUMN 11
           PIC X(0001)  USING  FLD002
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 06 COLUMN 13  VALUE
           '<=='
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 06 COLUMN 17  VALUE
           '1.'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 06 COLUMN 20  VALUE
           'VIEW   NAME/ADDRESS'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 07 COLUMN 17  VALUE
           '2.'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 07 COLUMN 20  VALUE
           'CHANGE NAME/ADDRESS'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 08 COLUMN 17  VALUE
           '3.'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 08 COLUMN 20  VALUE
           'DELETE NAME/ADDRESS'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 10 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'ENTER THE SURNAME IN FULL OR IN PART. IF MORE THAN ONE RECOR
      -    'D HAS THE SAME'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 11 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'SURNAME, USE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 11 COLUMN 15  VALUE
           'PF8'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 11 COLUMN 19  VALUE
           'TO PAGE FORWARD UNTIL THE REQUIRED RECORD IS REACHED.'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 12 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'THE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 12 COLUMN 06  VALUE
           'SEQUENCE NUMBER'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 12 COLUMN 22  VALUE
           'CAN BE USED TO IDENTIFY A RECORD UNIQUELY. FOR EXAMPLE,'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 13 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'IF YOU HAVE 10 PEOPLE WITH THE SURNAME "SMITH", ENTERING "SM
      -    'ITH" IN THE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 14 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'SURNAME FIELD AND "0005" IN THE SEQUENCE NUMBER WILL IMMEDIA
      -    'TELY RETURN THE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 15 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'FIFTH SMITH ON FILE.'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 3.
           12 LINE 17 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'SURNAME:'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 17 COLUMN 11
           PIC X(0030)  USING  FLD003
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 17 COLUMN 42  VALUE
           '<==ENTER FULL OR PARTIAL SURNAME HERE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 19 COLUMN 02  VALUE
           'SEQ. NO:'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 19 COLUMN 11
           PIC Z(0004)  USING  FLD004
           HIGHLIGHT.
           12 LINE 19 COLUMN 16  VALUE
           '<==SEQUENCE NUMBER MAY BE SPECIFIED ONLY IF FULL SURNAME WAS
      -    ''
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 20 COLUMN 19  VALUE
           'ENTERED ABOVE'
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 7.
           12 LINE 24 COLUMN 02
           PIC X(0079)  FROM   FLD005
           BACKGROUND-COLOR 4
           FOREGROUND-COLOR 6
           HIGHLIGHT.
      *

       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
                          LINK-MESS.
       MAIN SECTION.
       MAINLINE.
           CALL 'ACEDSCR' USING SDATE.
           ACCEPT EDIT-MASK FROM TIME.
           MOVE ':' TO MASK-1 MASK-2.
           MOVE EDIT-FLD TO STIME.
           MOVE LINK-MESS TO INOUT-MESSAGE.
           MOVE ZERO TO LINECOL.
           CALL 'DYNATR'
                         USING
                         LINECOL
                         VARIABLE-FIELDS
                         INOUT-MESSAGE.
           DISPLAY NADIA002-SCREEN.
           ACCEPT  NADIA002-SCREEN.
           PERFORM HANDLE-AID.
      *
      ********** LOWER CASE TO UPPER CASE CONVERSION *******************
      *
      *   THE COBOL SUBROUTINE 'LCTUC' WILL CONVERT A BUFFER OF ASCII
      *   CHARACTERS FROM LOWER TO UPPER CASE. (BUFFER LENGTH UP TO
      *   64000 CHARACTERS, LENGTH PASSED AS 2 BYTE BINARY FIELD)
      *
      *   IF YOU WISH TO HAVE LOWER CASE INPUT PASSED TO YOUR PROGRAM
      *   AS LOWER CASE, THEN COMMENT OUT THE FOLLOWING CALL TO LCTUC.
      *   INPUT WILL BE PASSED TO YOUR APPLICATION AS LOWER CASE. MOST
      *   MAINFRAME SYSTEMS CANNOT HANDLE LOWER CASE AND IT IS TEDIOUS
      *   TO HAVE TO CHECK EACH RESPONSE IN A PROGRAM FOR UPPER OR LOWER
      *   CASE (AND DOING SO MAY RENDER YOUR COBOL UN-TRANSPORTABLE)).
      *   THE CALL TO LCTUC ENSURES THAT YOUR APPLICATION ONLY HAS TO
      *   DEAL WITH UPPER CASE USER INPUT.
      *
           MOVE INOUT-MESS-LL TO MESSAGE-LENGTH.
           IF MESSAGE-LENGTH > 1920
              MOVE 1920 TO MESSAGE-LENGTH.
           CALL 'LCTUC'
                USING
                     INOUT-MESSAGE
                     MESSAGE-LENGTH.
      * IF THE MESSAGE LEN IS BETWEEN 97 AND 122 LCTUC WILL CHANGE IT...
      * SO IT MUST BE RESTORED FOR THE USER.
      *
           MOVE MESSAGE-LENGTH TO INOUT-MESS-LL.
      *
      *
           MOVE INOUT-MESSAGE TO LINK-MESS.
       RETURN-TO-CALLER.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

      /
       HANDLE-AID SECTION.
       HA.
      *
      *   THIS SECTION ANALYSES THE TERMINATE (ATTN) KEY WHICH WAS
      *   PRESSED AND SETS THE AID (ATTENTION IDENTIFIER) IN THE
      *   MESSAGE FOR THE USER.
      *
           IF AB-1 = ZERO
      *   "ENTER" KEY OR LAST ON-SCREEN AUTO-SKIP...
              MOVE SPACES TO INOUT-MESS-AID
              GO TO HA-OUT.
       HA-PF-KEYS.
           MOVE AB-2 TO AB-2-LOD.
           IF AB-2-COM > 40
              MOVE '99' TO INOUT-MESS-AID
              GO TO HA-OUT.
       HA-MOD-AID-BYTE.
           IF AB-2-COM > 12
              SUBTRACT 10 FROM AB-2-COM
              GO TO HA-MOD-AID-BYTE.
           MOVE AB-2-COM TO AB-DISP.
           MOVE AB-DISP-X TO INOUT-MESS-AID.
       HA-OUT.
           EXIT.

If you remove the call to 'ACEDSCR' which does some date handling and
formatting, you should be able to compile and animate this source. If you
then enter data into the screen you will see how it is returned into the
defined message buffer (try pressing various PK Keys and see what happens to
the AID byte.)

This module would be called from a main program which would pass the initial
setup for the screen in the message buffer (if required) and would receive
back a message buffer filled with the user-entered data and an indication of
the PFKey which was pressed (if any). The same screen format would run on an
IBM mainframe and use the same message buffer to get and put the same
information.

You can relace the call to LCTUC with a call to the equivalent MF function.
(I use LCTUC because I wrote it myself in COBOL and it is therefore
transportable to any environment)

So, the above is ONE architecture for handling screens in the PC
environment. If your HP system already has a screen handling module, maybe
you could write something which would convert the existing HP screens into
MF screen section format. Wrap a skeleton around it (something like the
above) and replace the HP calls to the screen Handler with a call to your
own control module which would call the appropriate screen format and return
the message buffer.

End of Document

HTH,

Pete.
```

---

<a id="t-95aabc8726"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) - Build Fails — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) - Build Fails

- **From:** "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T19:43:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102909407.265351.117530@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
Anyone with experience using Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) on W/2K? I'm looking
for tips or tricks to get this to work for batch oriented code, not
OOCOBOL.

What is happening is, my compiles run clean, but the Linker apparently
fails to output an .EXE. What I get is a message about "the respository
file" nor "source information analysis information file" was found.
"Database file generation is terminated."

I have installed the M/S Tool kit. I can't seem to determine why the
system "thinks" I'm doing OOCOBOL and not giving it any class info (or
whatever).

Again, what I am doing is building a batch oriented program.

Now it is interesting that I am using W/2K that has recently been
upgraded to SP4. Apparently prior to this upgrade, batch oriented
compiles had worked. [Meanwhile, Fujitsu says that V4 won't install on
W/2K and will complain that SP3 hasn't been installed. However, it did
install, and it did work fine several months ago when I was actively
working on developing a different COBOL based application.]

Again, if anyone has any tips as to what I need to feed the Linker or
change in the compile options, I'd be glad to hear it.
Regards,
Steve.T
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) - Build Fails

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-12T21:15:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102914925.514546.12900@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1102909407.265351.117530@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1102909407.265351.117530@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
> I have installed the M/S Tool kit.

Perhaps it has installed a new version of LINK.EXE that is incompatible
with the Fujitsu Cobol system.  Try changing the PATH in the
environment so that it uses the Fujitsu supplied LINK.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) - Build Fails

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T23:55:57+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<325aq1F3h4ommU1@individual.net>`

> Anyone with experience using Fujitsu V4 (COBOL97) on W/2K? I'm looking

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You ARE doing OO COBOL. It is an OO COBOL compiler...(The fact that your
source uses no OO COBOL constructs doesn't change it.)

You need to build this using the Project Manager so it can create the
necessary supplementary files it needs for proper linkage. If you run the
compile and the link as separate standalone activities it cannot be
guaranteed to work.


> Again, what I am doing is building a batch oriented program.
>

Are you "building" it? If you are, can you post a copy of the output from
the build?

Do you have the debugging option set? Is there a .SVD file produced?

> Now it is interesting that I am using W/2K that has recently been
> upgraded to SP4. Apparently prior to this upgrade, batch oriented
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I could probably help you, but I need to see more information.

Pete.

DEL OSPRPG2.OBJ
C:\FSC\COBOL97\COBOL32.EXE -iC:\FSC\COBOL97\COBOL.CBI -M OSPRPG2.COB

## Thread: Cobol's hanging by a thread... — 13 msgs

### Cobol's hanging by a thread...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T11:42:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello Everyone,

I need your kind help again -- what is the difference between
a Module, a Thread and a Process when programming for windows?
case in point: if I make a GUI cobol program (Kellie.exe) that
utilizes the use of win32 API functions, Is that a Module,
a Thread or a Process when I execute the program?
thanks for the kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol's hanging by a thread...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T20:53:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<djnvd.503254$%k.344584@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM wrote:

>Hello Everyone,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Li'l bit tricky, because folks put different meanings on the words. 
Perhaps once a year I might refer to it - knew it would come in handy 
sometime - so from the Microsoft Press Computer Dictionary :-

 >>MODULE :

A collection of routines and data structures that performs a particular 
task or implements a particular abstract type. Modules usualy consist of 
two parts: an interlace, which lists the constants, data types, 
variables and routines that can be accessed by other modules or 
routines, and an implementation, which is private (accessible only to 
the module) and which contains the source code that actually implements 
the routines in the module........<<

Back when I started designing for mainframes we would talk about the 
'Invoicing System" or 'Sales Ledger System";  I don't recall we ever 
used the words 'application' or 'project'. We also used to refer to it 
as the "Sales Ledger suite of programs", to convey a 'grouping' of 
programs. Now I visualise an application - Accounts Receivable 
Application, with a Master Menu from which the user selects a 'module' 
to execute - say Edit Customers, Print a List etc. Sure enough my 
approach to 'modules' follows the above meaning using a three-tier 
System design approach (1) Business Program (Problem Domain) - which is 
the 'Controller', (2) DBI (Database Interface) be it COBOL files or an 
(R)DBMS and (3) UI = User Interface = GUI

THREAD :

As you aren't clear - it's for sure you are using SINGLE THREAD  :-)

 >> A process that is part of a larger process or program. In a tree 
data structure, a pointer that identifies the particular node and is 
used to facilitate traversal of the tree.....<<

 >> Multi-threading - The running of several processes in rapid sequence 
(multitasking) within a single program. See also 'thread'.....<<

Not too helpful. As background look at the N/E 4.0 on-line books - 
there's one I believe devoted entirely to Threading.

PROCESS ;

 >> A program or a part of a program; a coherent sequence of steps 
undertaken by a program <<

---------------

My own suspicion is that KELLIE.EXE  is *ONE* program ? If so you've 
broken the cardinal rule - look at Module and three-tier systems design 
above.

Have a nice Christmas down there.

Jimmy, Calgfary AB
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On 14-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> that to be a limitation so far.

When you say "modify", do you mean to change or replace the vendor's
code?  Or to build reports etc. that the package doesn't provide?  

If you can "replace" a function or report by making the program loader
find your version first (i.e., by setting the path to look in your
homegrown directory first) then it's probably OK to do so; but if you
modify the vendor's code I'd say the vendor is justified in refusing to
support you - as Howard Brazee points out.

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol's hanging by a thread...

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T10:16:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<326f5tF3ir827U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

<KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
> Hello Everyone,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Kellie, your .exe is a PROCESS. It is a single run unit in COBOL terms and
it will utilize a single THREAD.

If you wrote it as a .DLL that could have various functions loaded by
calling (or invoking, for OO) those would be MODULES.

Easy way to remember:

You run a PROCESS. (which might, though not usually in COBOL, spawn other
process THREADS)

You CALL or INVOKE a MODULE.

HTH,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol's hanging by a thread...

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-13T21:41:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c2sr0lpm604pcr4cnelu6456c0i5k6lvb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 13 Dec 2004 11:42:14 -0800, KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM wrote:

>Hello Everyone,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>thanks for the kind help, Kellie.

It's all three. Your one-process program could launch other processes
to run in parallel with it. Each would show up on the Task
List/Processes with a PID (Process ID) assigned to it. Threads are a
similar concept. A one-thread process can launch additional threads to
run in parallel within a single process id. An example would be
background recalc of a spreadsheet. 

If you're just using the Win32 API, you're not using multi-process nor
multi-thread. If you're using COM, there's a chance it is using
multi-threading, but you don't need to worry about it.
```

- **References:** `<1102966934.640140.134700@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7c2sr0lpm604pcr4cnelu6456c0i5k6lvb@4ax.com>`

Just thought that some of your would find this to be of interest.

People can say what they want.  Carnegie Mellon University studied
operating systems for 4 years and arrived at the results as shown on
this web site:

http://news.zdnet.com/2100-1009_22-5489804.html

This certainly confirms in my mind that Linux is probably one of the
safest server-based operating systems available.  Possibly one of the
safest all-around operating systems available.  Despite "conventional
wisdom" Linux is not going to go away...particulary now that IBM has
embraced it.



Bob Wolfe

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T16:20:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103070057.930458.91050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com>`

```
> IBM shed it's PC business.

IBM has formed a partnership for its desktop systems. It hasn't been
profitable and it has tied contracts with both Intel and Microsoft
which it wants to dump.

It is most likely that it will be coming out with a 'Business Desktop'
that is Power5 based, or even Cell based, and will be manufactured
entirely within IBM rather than being assembled from bought in parts,
and will run Linux.  The PC is a 23 year old design now, it is about
time that it was replaced with something modern.

> I'll bet, if you run the pro-rata numbers, there are vastly more
security
> breaches on Linux systems than on Windows.

You may bet that, but I doubt you would win.  There are vastly more
Linux servers running the Internet than Windows servers.  Yet it is
Windows servers that are breached and taken over by virus worms.

It is estimated that 60% of the spam comes from 'pwn3d' Windows
machines with the user completely unaware what his machine is doing
(except it runs slow).

A recent test put several new machines on the Internet.  Within 4.5
minutes the Windows XP (SP1 admitedly) was 'pwn3d' and started
outputting spam.

http://it.slashdot.org/article.pl?sid=04/11/30/1932245&tid=220&tid=172&tid=201

> You must believe that free software is the spawn of the devil, ...

'Free' is as in 'Freedom'.  It is Free Software because you are allowed
to do with it what _you_ wish to do.  In many cases it also without
cost, but you can sell the software, or your own added value, or
support, as you wish (that is why it is called Free).

It is the Microsoft EULA that is the 'spawn of the devil'.

> ""Linux is a knock-off of a 40-year-old operating system ...

One that was designed from the ground up to be secure, unlike Windows
which simply has security thinly added as yet another layer of bloat.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

>> I'll bet, if you run the pro-rata numbers, there are vastly more

"The CERT results for "Microsoft" returned 250 entries, with the top two 
entries containing the severity metric of 94.5...." and "The CERT results 
for "Red Hat" returned 46 entries. The top entry has a severity metric of 
108.16...."

Within a few percent of each other.

>
> It is estimated that 60% of the spam comes from 'pwn3d' Windows
> machines with the user completely unaware what his machine is doing
> (except it runs slow).

I'd bet the percentage is even higher. But, again, most car wrecks are 
caused by drunk drivers, not the cars themselves.

>
> A recent test put several new machines on the Internet.  Within 4.5
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is the Microsoft EULA that is the 'spawn of the devil'.

You're free to do whatever you wish with the software you write. If you use 
something I wrote, you'll do it on mutually agreeable terms.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-15T10:33:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103135606.992088.47570@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10s0gka4bog6j4d@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com> <1103070057.930458.91050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10s0gka4bog6j4d@news.supernews.com>`

```
> Properly configured boxes, either Windows or that other one (can't
think of its name)
> don't get compromised. Further:

The vast majority of Windows boxes are not 'properly configured'. Most
home users for example don't know what SP2 is or are still running
Windows 98 or ME.  Many still run Outlook and IE because it is there.
With Outlook you can get a security breach merely by selecting an EMail
message unless the user has done something deliberate to stop that
happening, such as getting an update.  With IE you can get a security
breach merely by visting a site and using the scroll bar.

> But, again, most car wrecks are caused by drunk drivers, not the cars
themselves.

That comparison is entirely spurious.  With Windows one can buy a
machine at a retail store, connect it to the internet and, with no
action at all from the user, it could be breached within a few minutes.

This is equivalent to buying a car and putting it in the driveway and
having a tree fall on it.

Actually, these days, it is _not_ like a tree falling on it, it is like
someone attaches a trailer and gets a free ride. A recent survey of
several thousand machines found an average of 29 spyware and adware
items per Windows machine.

Yes, with Linux a direct attack can cause the system to crash if it
isn't configured properly, but it doesn't get silently 'pwn3d' (owned
in text speak).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-15T11:14:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103138074.423578.44710@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10s0gka4bog6j4d@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com> <1103070057.930458.91050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10s0gka4bog6j4d@news.supernews.com>`

```
> You're free to do whatever you wish with the software you write.

Yes, I can.  Writers using proprietry software may find that they are
restricted in what they do with their software.  The EULA is a contract
not a licence and this may impose restrictions. For example it may say
that I may not use this product to develop a product that competes with
any product from the suppier.  When a market succeeds, MS announces a
products and then prevents developers from 'competing' with theirs:

""" ---------------
First Microsoft encourages fleet tracking companies to grow the market.

Second they add confusing language to the EULA which seems to restrict
use for Tracking. (But they don't seem to enforce it. Hmmm, I wonder
why?)

Third, they come out with a product that is directed right at business
users, which is the core business of the fleet tracking companies.

Finally the coupe de grace, (this is my guess) Microsoft targets Fleet
Tracking companies clients. (They even know who the MapPoint users
are.) They starts enforcing the EULA and within 1-2 yrs, they are the
only company left providing fleet tracking with MapPoint.

Ever feel like you've been taken?
---------------------- """
'Freedom' means not having to put up with that sort of crap.
```

I'm not privvy to such numbers.

Jerry....perhaps you misundestand my intent.  I think some people may
get the wrong impression that I am suggesting replacement of Windows
with Linux, when in fact, I was merely suggesting that Linux is a very
safe server environment.  

I really do like Windows XP Pro....but I also speak with people who
suggest that Linux is inferior to Windows.  I disagree with any such
blanket statement that one is better than the other.  They BOTH have
their share of strengths as well as weaknesses.

It is my personal opinion that the best client/server environment is
one which uses a UNIX server based environment combined with a Windows
client environment.



Bob Wolfe

#### ↳ Re: Linux and security

- **From:** "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-14T17:05:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103054484.579094.76800@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com>`

```
As was pointed out by others on the "review this by readers" postings,
this server software was compaired against Windoze. But what about
OS/400, or z/VSE, or z/OS?

It is interesting that before z/OS is released, it goes through
regression testing, and then cert testing. I don't know that Linus is
going to that level when he releases a kernel. From the number of bugs
(security exploits) reported, M/S sure doesn't appear to.

And then, lines of code, means what? If I develop in ALC and you
develop in C++, while another company develops in FORTRAN, and still
another company uses VBS, how does the debugged lines of code stack up
against each other? After all, a line of code is not necessarily a line
of code (FOR, DO, PERFORM, translates into how many machine commands?
And doesn't that vary depending on the optimizer?) once you get down to
what is actually done.

And again, all of this varies depending on the CPU architecture the
language is targetted toward. An MVCL may need a loop in MASM, and may
need a subroutine in C++.

Comparison of apples to apples is very difficult in this world we live
in.

Later,
Steve.T
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux and security

- **From:** Scott Moore <samiam@moorecad.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-19T07:34:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U9axd.218615$V41.37774@attbi_s52>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:
> Just thought that some of your would find this to be of interest.
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Facinatng that everyone here simply took it for granted that they were
finding valid bugs. Read the description:

=========================================================================
The conclusion is the result of a four-year research project conducted
by code-analysis company Coverity, which plans to release its report on Tuesday

...

Code-analysis tools typically use software-design principles to analyze a
program's source code and flag any possible problems
===========================================================================

I've used a code analizer tool. Its a "super lint" that flags such items
as:

while (c = 'a') { ... }

Which certainly looks like an error, but isn't necessarily so.

In any case, all the article says is they are assuming the output of their
code analisis tool is a "bug" count. All it really means is it found less
constructs it didn't like.

A "bug" is a verifiable software problem. There is no automated tool that
can do this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

- **From:** "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-19T11:10:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103483421.829327.197410@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<U9axd.218615$V41.37774@attbi_s52>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <U9axd.218615$V41.37774@attbi_s52>`

```
Scott:

Valid point and observation.

One should find it very interesting that only M/S and Linux are being
reported on / compaired.

Meanwhile, have most people, beyond the techie types, found it very
interesting that malicious code seems to be targetted toward the
systems easiest to compromise?

The answer is no, because marketing of Windoze is so heavy, that most
people have no idea that OS/2 used to be available. And most have no
idea what Linux is.

So when I have discussed this with supposed technical types, the answer
was that if the other systems were more prevalent (having more
exposure) you would see malicious code targetted to them.

It is my conjecture that since most of the reported attacks are against
M/S, it would seem that they have the most exposures that are the
easiest to exploit. If M/S actually starts doing regression testing and
the like, and closes holes in designs and implementation, the crackers
will move on to other systems looking for easy exploits.

The end result should be much tighter code and system by all software
companies.

Later,
Steve.T
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-19T15:06:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103497613.041365.271040@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1103483421.829327.197410@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <U9axd.218615$V41.37774@attbi_s52> <1103483421.829327.197410@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

steve.t wrote:

> It is my conjecture that since most of the reported attacks are
against
> M/S, it would seem that they have the most exposures that are the
> easiest to exploit.

The problem with Windows is that most of the exploits are not of bugs
(though buffer overruns have been exploited) but of _features_.  With
outlook, opening an email can cause the attachment to be executed
allowing virusees to be loaded.  The default for Windows is to _hide_
the file type because the typing is based on DOS, and thus an
attachment that is innocent.jpg.exe is shown as innocent.jpg and
appears to be an image but will execute when clicked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-20T09:35:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103564102.032344.94960@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1103497613.041365.271040@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <U9axd.218615$V41.37774@attbi_s52> <1103483421.829327.197410@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1103497613.041365.271040@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
A poorly designed and/or implemented feature is still a bug - logic
based, but still a defect or bug. Anytime you have incorrect-output,
you have a bug. And undesireable output that is because of a security
exposure is the worse kind.

Let me give an example: In the MVS world, IDCAMS (utility) is in the
books. However, because of what it can be used for, it detects if *you*
are authorized to perform functions. If it did not, you could use it to
delete/copy/modify files you are not authorized to use or see.

If you give that kind of power to common users (as M/S does), and bad
things happen, you can't really say that this is a feature and not a
bug. Yet it is precisely this kind of thing that people want to gloss
over and call a feature -- and not a glaring security error (bug).

Let us look at another issue. It is my opinion from doing D/R work that
the M/S Registry is a LARGE bug. Consider what happens if you have a
drive crash. Upon doing a restore, does the registry get correctly
restored? If it doesn't, software that has Windows API calls seem to
crash because information needed isn't in the directory (well the
registry data points to "D" but since the new drive was large enough to
hold all the data...). Talk about a DoS! This is a poorly thought out
situation that has come to bite several entities when they have had to
restore a system after a hard drive crash.

No other O/S that I know of and/or have used have such glaring
problems. Yet we all take these as "features" from M/S and allow them
to get away with it.

Later,
Steve.T
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-20T18:13:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cq74qd$ai0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <1103564102.032344.94960@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

On 20-Dec-2004, "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> No other O/S that I know of and/or have used have such glaring
> problems. Yet we all take these as "features" from M/S and allow them
> to get away with it.

The philosophy was one of offering complete power to the user.   This worked
when there was one user per machine who owned and controlled everything on the
machine.   Everybody was a power user (and we do like being power users).

But the Internet changed that.   We no longer can guarantee that only our
decisions change our computers.   That philosophy that was so seductive doesn't
work anymore.
```

> Kill/murder - call it what you want. My conscientious objector friend 
> had it right, "War is an obscenity". But unlike him or Bill, I wouldn't 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> another hangar on the airfield. Bit late then.

I agree with you, war is indeed an obscenity.  But unlike your friend or 
Bill, I hardly think it is the obscenity man has created.

Far worse obscenities exist -- targeting jewish/gypsy/gay people for 
death -- slavery, either state or individual owned -- war is hardly the 
worst invention of mankind.

I don't fault your fear, in fact I agree with it.  But I don't see any 
bravery in doing nothing in the face of greater obscenity.  The worst 
creation of mankind is to sit and watch while his fellow man is robbed 
of his liberty and human dignity.
 
The worst obscenity is for good men to do nothing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-15T14:27:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpqdpb$12h8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-76A770.22534814122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cppl0f$6h0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Cz%vd.501940$Pl.220207@pd7tw1no> <cpq3g5$rp8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Ud2wd.502970$Pl.488331@pd7tw1no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Ud2wd.502970$Pl.488331@pd7tw1no...

> Guess my 'ape' experiences are limited to Dianne Fossey and Jane
> Goodall.

Diane Fossey's research was on gorillas; Goodall's on chimpanzees.

> It's also a sure bet neither Sister Mary Ignatius, (so she's a
> female Jesuit ?), nor PBS, would be thrilled to have 'Queer as Folk' on
> that channel - either the original UK or US versions.

BTW, I've seen about ten minutes of one episode of the US edition of Queer
as Folk and wasn't really impressed.  Looked like a soap opera to me, and
I'm not that into soap operas.

PBS did, however, air a program sometime before 1983 entitled "Being
Homosexual", and that in prime time with lots of publicity.  I saw it before
I moved from Texas.   This was during the period in which Showtime (or was
it HBO?) was airing "Brothers", so far as I know the first openly-gay-themed
series (and it might have been the first series developed by Showtime as
well).

The issue as to whether Sister Mary Ignatius (or for that matter the Rev.
Fred Phelps) would be thrilled to *see* such a program on PBS is quite
different from whether PBS would be thrilled to *air* it; my suspicion is
that the latter is actually quite likely.

> Aw hell - let's just throw it in. As you know with young Oprah rapidly
> becoming the future 'star',

No, I don't know.  I haven't seen an Oprah program in a very, very long
time, nor have I a particular interest in doing so.

"Cops" I watch.

> old man Phil Donahue could see the writing
> on the wall, so he had to go provocative - but he still went off the
'tube'.

Nor a Phil Donahue program.

Guess I gotta rely on "ignominious fish-wrappers" like the March 1995 issue
of "Scientific American", in which the article "Bonobo Sex and Society"
appeared!

Some points from that article:

A bonobo skull in a Belgian colonial museum (in the Belgian Congo?) was
originally thought to be a juvenile chimp, until a taxonomist decided in
1929 it was an adult, and probably a pygmy chimp subspecies of some sort, in
1929.  It was decided that it was a separate species sometime in the 1930's.
This is one of the last large mammal species to be found by science.

The behavioral differences were apparently not discussed in print until
1954, and it appears that the observers were so concerned about the shocking
nature of their findings that they wrote them *in Latin*.  *In 1954*.
Extensive field research on bonobos didn't really crank up until the
mid-1970's (but that's only about a decade after similar research on chimps
cranked up).

> One example, a program from the London Palladium on homosexuality where
> the audience was stacked, and dear old Gandalf (LORT), was up on the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> hesitation, "No, not in all my years of observing them".

I can't say that bonobos are particularly *homosexually* inclined; from what
I gather, it's more that they're *pansexually* inclined.    They're not all
that inclined to form *pair bonds* in the first place (the strongest
long-term familial bond appears to be between a male bonobo and his mother).
There are other nonhuman species that don't preclude "homosexual
inclination" or even homosexual lifelong pair-bonding -- this is
particularly common among bird species; swans are well known for this -- but
bonobos don't happen to be among them.  Pair-bonding, in fact, isn't so much
part of the repertoire of chimps *or* bonobos to begin with.

Male chimps pretty much treat their mates as property, intimidate or beat
them into submission, and use them as objects to be exploited for their own
pleasure; I'm not convinced that just because this behavior exhibits itself
heterosexually this should be considered somehow more "civilized" than the
friendly behavior of the bonobo!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

majority in Calgary - but this is Bible-totin' country folks !

Jimmy

#### ↳ Re: utility to read cobol data files?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-15T15:09:59+00:00

"John Smith" <hard.ware@gmx.net> wrote in message
news:cpp5fu$om4$1@fegnews.vip.hr...
> Hello,
> does it egzist?
> I need to read some data from files generated by program in Cobol.
> I don't know structure of data files.
> Pleas, it's urgent.

If you do not  know the structure of the data files, the answer is, no, no
such utility exists. And that's not only true if the files are created by a
COBOL program, it's true all the time.

Best you can do is dig out a hex editor/viewer and try to figure out what
the data mean.

MCM
```

- **Message-ID:** `<MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<B03wd.222$iC4.158@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<cpp5fu$om4$1@fegnews.vip.hr> <btYvd.5851$%s7.1335@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <B03wd.222$iC4.158@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Tom Morrison wrote:

>Actually, have a look at this:
>http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/data-viewer.html
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Looks interesting Tom. But as we all now recognize the different formats 
have been a pain in the butt. Just out of curiosity, history, although 
you may only have been young, bright-eyed and bushy-tailed at the time.

As you indicated quite some while back RM was into a whole bunch of 
compilers. I think about the same time Micro Focus was also active, 
although I was unaware of the name. From reading Pete appears there was 
a very simple sub-set for file handling in say 1960-ish, so possibly 
what we now know as ISAM, Relative and Sequential were firmed up in 
COBOL '74. Then the dreaded phrase 'implementer defined' was introduced 
- each to his own - "This is how my internal file structures and 
indexing work, (but I ain't gonna tell you exactly)".

Way back when, RM was very much involved in the Standards process. To 
your knowledge, was there ever an attempt to go beyond the broad 
definition of COBOL file handling, i.e., the access VERBS and come up 
with a common format, or was it a case of, "No way !  We want to retain 
our customers, thank you very much". If the latter was even considered, 
did our friends at Big Blue screw it up - wouldn't be the first time, 
from what has been related to me.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: utility to read cobol data files?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-16T22:25:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Xnwd.775$iC4.193@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<cpp5fu$om4$1@fegnews.vip.hr> <btYvd.5851$%s7.1335@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <B03wd.222$iC4.158@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com> <MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no...
[snip]
> Way back when, RM was very much involved in the Standards process. To your 
> knowledge, was there ever an attempt to go beyond the broad definition of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> related to me.

Jimmy,

I personally was never on the COBOL committee except as an alternate that 
never attended a meeting (I did serve on X3S34, though).

I don't think that there could have been too much progress on common 
formats, etc.  This would have been considered 'specification' which was not 
the business of the committee.  The work product was 'standardization' and 
not 'specification.'  And, true enough, anything beyond that would most 
likely have been too contrary to the corporate goals of the committee 
members' employers.

So...that being the case, the behavior of the file system was described (and 
it has served us very well) and the vendors are left to there own devices. 
Some chose to license existing technology (C-ISAM, e.g.) and some chose to 
build their own.  Most of the work in the mid-1980s used B+ trees as 
described in Knuth, since it was (relatively) easy to do garbage collection 
on unused space.  RM completely reimplemented its file system to improve 
performance and meet the emerging demands of the mircoprocessor-based 
revolution.  We provided conversion utilities for our customers (to assist 
the upgrade), but there was really no tremendous proprietary advantage. 
Liant has adaptors for several of the popular file systems that provide 
'properly behaved' COBOL I/O, and we of course publish Relativity which 
provides SQL (ODBC) access to these files (thereby opening up the data, as 
it were) for RM files and for those of a large competitor.

And, of course, Siber and others (Data Junction) have reverse engineered or 
licensed mechanisms to gain access to the data as well.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

Reading a PDS (or PDSE) which includes "load modules" is MUCH more difficult 
than reading a PDS that includes source code (or other "fixed length text 
data").  (Or by "jobs" to you mean JCL?)

For sample source code to "read" a PDS (directory and members), see:

  http://www.mannyjuan.com/mvstools/mvstools.htm

and look for the section starting

 "READPDS - program to read a PDS and display its members"

That example only (I think) works for PDS - not PDSE's.
```

#### ↳ Re: Read a pds file whit cobol

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-16T09:40:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-AED158.09400416122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cea79078258ee73b8b7e7810671fd9f0@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>`

```
In article 
<cea79078258ee73b8b7e7810671fd9f0@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>,
 "Betocent" <hcenturion@biycsa.com.ar> wrote:

> Hi, sorry about my English, I need to know, how can I do to read a PDS file
> in environment MVS, I need read all PDS File, not only a member, Thank's to
> everyone


If you want an easy way, and space is not an issue, then dump the PDS to 
a temporary IEBUPDTE file.  It will be a QSAM segmented like so:

   ./ADD MEMBER=MEM1
   blah
   blah
   blah
   ./ADD MEMBER=MEM2
   blah
   blah
   blah
   ./ADD MEMBER=MEM3
   etc

You can scan for the './' member separations in the first 2 columns.
```
